Posted: November 23
Sekiro isn’t just a great game—it’s a triumph of design, storytelling, and craftsmanship. FromSoftware took the soul of their signature difficulty and forged it into something faster, tighter, and more exhilarating than anything they’d made before. The result? A game that doesn’t just challenge you—it transforms you.

From the moment you pick up the sword, Sekiro becomes a dance. Every clash, every deflection, every perfectly timed parry feels like a spark of lightning in your hands. Unlike other action games, Sekiro doesn’t let you hide behind shields or overpowered builds. It demands mastery, discipline, and patience. And when you finally get it, the game opens up in the most rewarding way possible. Few gaming moments compare to breaking an enemy’s posture and landing that final, devastating deathblow—it’s pure satisfaction.

The world of Ashina is equally stunning. From misty temple forests to burning castles and mythical mountains, every area feels alive and purposeful. The vertical movement, grappling hook traversal, and stealth options give the world a sense of depth that makes exploration genuinely thrilling. You don’t just walk from place to place—you swoop, climb, stalk, and strike.

Sekiro’s story, though quieter than most, hits with surprising emotional weight. Wolf’s journey is simple on the surface—save your young lord—but the bonds, betrayals, and moral choices woven into the narrative give every moment gravity. It’s a tale about loyalty, honor, and the weight of legacy, told with elegance and restraint.

Bosses are where Sekiro truly becomes legendary. Each one is a test of everything you’ve learned: Genichiro tightening your fundamentals, the Guardian Ape shocking you with unexpected chaos, Isshin pushing you to your absolute limits. They’re not just fights—they’re iconic encounters that stay with you long after the credits roll.

Sekiro is not an easy game, but it is a fair one. It respects the player. It teaches without words. And when you finally overcome the impossible, it rewards you with a sense of triumph few games can match.

It’s not just one of FromSoftware’s best—it’s one of the greatest action games ever made. A masterpiece of precision, passion, and unforgettable moments.

Posted: November 23
Sekiro is honestly the darkest nightmare I’ve ever loved. This game feels like it was designed just to test my patience, my anger level, and my skill, everything at the same time. Sometimes I die so many times that even I start doubting myself, but then when I finally win… that feeling is unbeatable.
The world, the boss fights, the atmosphere, everything hits different. It’s beautiful, but it never shows mercy. every enemy teaches you something, every mistake punches you in the face, and every victory feels like you earned it after fighting your own limits.
Sekiro is the greatest and toughest game I’ve ever played. It frustrates me, scares me, challenges me, but still, I keep going back because no other game gives this level of satisfaction. It’s pain, patience, and pride all mixed together.
for me, Sekiro will always be that one game that broke me and built me at the same time.

Posted: November 23
Sekiro is one of my favourite games ever. The combat, music, environments, lore, characters; everything is absolutely incredible from top to bottom. I've played through it multiple times and 100%'d it years ago, but I still come back periodically just to replay some of the boss fights and I'm always impressed with just how much I remember from pure muscle memory alone.

I've seen some people call Sekiro a rhythm game, owing to how you sometimes wait for the enemy to attack to deflect, but I have to admit that's only half the battle. The game actively rewards you for putting pressure on the enemies with the posture system. Enemies can deflect and counter just like the player, making the combat feel more like the enemies react to your moves rather than simply dishing out their attacks in the same pattern every time you fight them. Even bosses react if you manage to cut off their combos with an attack of your own, or even move out of the way; Many of the bosses even follow up on their combos differently depending on if you deflect, block, Mikiri Counter or even jump counter their final attack. It's honestly really hard to put into words how cool I think it is that enemies react so varyingly just by how YOU react.

Compared to its Souls cousins, the game is far more linear and personal with its story, and if you expect more than some minor variation of katana combat, you'll probably be disappointed expecting something as diverse as Dark Souls or Elden Ring. Despite that, the Combat Arts the game gives you are each extremely cool and add some extra spice. I still would've liked some kind of hotbar or selection wheel to switch between these mid-combat instead of having to go to the menu to switch them out.

Sekiro also doesn't overstay its welcome, being just concise enough to have every location be relevant while also being expansive enough to reward you for exploring them thoroughly with cool items and even new prosthetic tools and NPCs (we love you Kotaro). You do however revisit some of the locations, but the game manages to freshen them up to make it interesting still. I also can't overstate how cool the music is, Yuka Kitamura absolutely killed it.

I honestly could go on and on about how much I love this game, but I feel like it would just be a wall of text, more than the one I've already written. All in all I obviously recommend you get the game. There's a good reason why this game won GOTY, even while developed alongside the much bigger Elden Ring (which also won GOTY 3 years later).

Hard to recommend this.

E.g.
- The game is designed in a way to make it very difficult to learn. You have to get good at parrying, and weirdly it offers you very little opportunity to really do that.
- Far too much BS, and far too many situations encouraging cheese.
- OP consumables that trivialise fights, but extremely rare to find so tough ♥♥♥♥ if you didn't kill the boss the time you used snap seeds.
- Punishes the dying and learning cycle with dragonrot mechanic.

I did get to a point where I enjoyed the game, but FromSoft really did make it as difficult as they could to get to that point. I'd recommend Lies of P or Khazan over it any day.

One word for this game - Masterpiece ✨

The story is good but isn't the main selling point here, it's the gameplay it provides. It's easy to learn but hard to master, just parry with perfect timing and boom...enemy is down for the deathblow, you don't even need to attack, just parry. The fights feel very cinematic specially with those satisfying perfect sword parry sound.

The game might feel unfair at first (I rage quitted myself on the first mini boss which was optional) but once you get a hang of mechanics, the game starts getting interesting. You will die 8352 times to a boss just before inally defeat him , you will understand that all the previous attempts were just preparing you for the real battle.

So just don't hesitate to loose, because as you all know..."HESITATION IS DEFEAT!".

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is an intense and deeply rewarding experience. With precise combat, immersive world-building and a progression system focused entirely on player skill, this game stands as a masterpiece within the action-adventure genre. Every encounter feels meaningful, thanks to an exceptional posture-based combat system and brilliantly crafted enemy design. The world is rich in atmosphere, offering stunning visuals, interconnected areas and a story that unfolds subtly through exploration and dialogue. While its difficulty is notorious, it maintains a fluid combat dynamic and a strong sense of growth as you master each technique and boss fight.
To put it bluntly, if you enjoy games with striking art direction, challenging but fair combat mechanics, and a world filled with secrets and lore, this game is for you.

Pt-BR
Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice é uma experiência intensa e extremamente recompensadora. Com um combate preciso, construção de mundo imersiva e um sistema de progressão totalmente focado na habilidade do jogador, este jogo se destaca como uma verdadeira obra-prima dentro do gênero de ação e aventura. Cada encontro é significativo graças ao excepcional sistema de combate baseado em postura e ao design brilhante dos inimigos. O mundo é rico em atmosfera, com visuais impressionantes, áreas interconectadas e uma história que se revela de forma sutil através da exploração e dos diálogos. Apesar da dificuldade famosa, ele mantém uma dinâmica de combate fluida e uma forte sensação de evolução conforme você domina cada técnica e cada chefe.
Sendo direto, se você gosta de jogos com direção de arte marcante, mecânicas de combate desafiadoras porém justas, e um mundo cheio de segredos e narrativas, esse jogo é pra você. =)

Posted: November 17
The combat system is neat. Its enjoyable for a bit but gets really repetitive and drawn out on some encounters.

A lot of bosses have really cheap tricks that they throw at you that feels like it breaks the flow of the combat and I don't find the cheapness fair or interesting. Most of the bosses and mini bosses can kill you in just one or two hits, which makes learning them a painful chore, but of course you need to get about 50 in so that their posture bar doesn't deplete so fast in order to kill them.

Player dropoff is almost 50% by the first major boss. For the rest, a lot of players resort to cheese tactics to beat a lot of the encounters, which I think goes against the intent of the game. I get being tough, but I find this game doesn't always play by its own rules.

Despite picking it up a few separate times to give it a fresh approach, each time I find I just hate it.

I love exploration, good combat, digestible story, and fun bosses. Heavy on exploration, the game gives you options to explore accessible places and rewarded you from exploring.

Every time you have to go forward, there's something behind you to check out before progressing the story. To be fair, the only thing that makes exploring really hard is that somehow i'm under-level where at some point i'm just grappling around to get to the loot--skipping enemies. And that's where I'm really annoyed about the game. The death mechanics punishes you for dying and I hate grinding skill points. Grinding is repetitive, exhausting, and takes time. Not only you will die several times on bosses, the points they gives is not as much as when you died countless times. That's why I grind at the late game, therefore at mid to early game, I'm having a hard time at bosses. And that's just because of ridiculous death mechanic.

Another concern would be the fact that it gives you no map, making you fully immersed as Sekiro getting lost at the snow, swamp, cave, or castles. But, because of it, I will simply just miss something I forgot to check again and making the exploration is not totally to the fullest! There's also a consequences of not knowing something I should've thought of causing me to lose a chunk of content AND you couldn't get it. I'm just saying, the first impression is everything to me and playing NG+ are just for achievements, it's not the same.

With that being sad, I love everything about it. The story, is not confusing and straightforward where during the progress the game will reveal more lore and secrets about the plot, making it more robust, comprehensive, and better world-building. Good combat, where I can have my own playstyle with various items, various tools, and combat art making the game just more complex. The combat is smooth and grounded where it became kind of a problem where my character has to turn around to deflect, and that couple miliseconds really that matters on later hard bosses. The characters, I'm just glad they're interesting, cool, some are ironic, sad, cool MC, and good dialogue because without it, the plot won't be that good with the climax. Fun bosses, where some takes a couple times, some are first timed, and some are unfair until you find out what you have to do.

I love the ambience, it's thrilling, some monsters/enemies made me jolted of jumpscares, i love the characters, I love Sekiro, Kuro, Divine Child, Emma, Sculptor, Isshin, and I think they are all interesting with various personality, role, and you know what, immersive voice acting. Let's not forget about the heavy aspect I really love, the exploration in the game was super fun, and the fact that if some enemies are too hard, I can just turn around and explore somewhere else until you're ready. 8,5/10.

BEFORE YOU READ: THIS REVIEW CONTAIN SOME SPOILERS.

When I first played this game, My expectation was this game is going to be hard to complete or infuriating or even insufferable judging by what people has said about this game and those sekiro no-hit bossfight videos. But no one told me that even though this game is hard for beginners, when you start to getting the feels of this game, It feels rewarding. And somewhat satisfying.

One of the aspect that i really enjoyed from this game is of course: the Deflect Mechanic where you can not only deal tons of posture damage to the enemies, you are also will be rewarded by the satisfying sound of "TING" and that sound alone made me want to learn to deflect more often. And this deflect mechanic is really really useful throughout the game when youre encountering enemies or bosses. Hell the game even encourage you to use this mechanic to the fullest so that you can enjoy the full potential of this game. (except youre that kind of person who does things differently which i respect, srsly tho no offense youre cool sauce.)

Though I do have some critics for this game and it's mainly for the bosses and some areas that i don't quite enjoy. One of the bosses that i really am not keen to it is the Guardian Ape and the other Ape that you'll meet in the Poison Pool, I really don't know why they decided to put TWO giant bosses that has hard to predict moveset and they deal a lot of damage to you, And not only that, they throw sh*t at you lol. and the areas in this game are somewhat easy to just run pass through everything since you has unlimited stamina, which makes exploration a bit weird? not to mention theres hordes of enemies in some areas like at Mibu Village.

Another thing i liked in this game is the Shinobi Prosthethic where you can fully utilize these tools to beat the enemies, one of my favourite is the umbrella one and the firecracker. They both are very useful if you know how to use it making each fights feels strategic and very engaging to do. And if you've leveled enough skill points you can do sick combos with the Prosthethic making you feel like an actual Shinobi.

And lastly, the last boss. Pure Cinema. Just 1 on 1 between You and the leader of Ashina. In the field of silvergrass, wind dusting through your ears. It's so cinematic. The fight starts and all you hear is just swords clashing each other with you focusing when to use your tools, when to time your deflect, when to attack.. AARGGH This whole bossfight is so enganging!! I have finished all of soulsborne (except bb and DeS ofc) and the last boss of Sekiro just beat all of them generally, in term of visual, soundtrack, and the fight itself is so fantastic and well made. I really REALLY adore this bossfight to my heart. it's that good!!

Would 100% Recommend. 9,6/10.

Posted: November 15
For people who are fans of very hard games, this might be it for you. But be warned I've played Silksong and Dark Souls and liked them just fine.

For me there's just too many frustrating issues with this game.

___GAMEPLAY____

First off, there's very little choice in how you engage with the combat or the world. It's one of the most linear games I've played in a long while.

How the game GENERALLY work is as follows, you, the player, will enter an area, where there's a set number of enemies, you can't really attack multiple enemies at once, since they'll just gang up on you immediately, so 1-on-1 is definitely preferred, occasionally you can deal a couple of the weaker enemies at the same time, but generally you definitely want to sneak around and stealth kill.

The stealth is half baked and feels on rails, sometimes enemies can spot you a mile away and run ALL the way to you, other times you can shank a friend of theirs 5 meters to their right and they'd be none the wiser. It really depends on if you stealth kill the enemies in the correct sequence. This can be a very lengthy process and you're gonna do this A LOT in each area.

The combat is too much waiting and either parry/jump/dodge (except dodge is super nerfed in this game) depending on the attack animation. You have very little agency in controlling the battles you are almost only reacting and never acting, it's like one long randomized QTE you have to endure. This is to the point where the miniboss battles I did endure to beat was not enjoyable or fun but just a chore.

Miniboss? you ask, why yes, there's two kinds of hard enemies, afaik, minibosses and then regular bosses, the regular bosses will give you a small boost to your attack stat (btw, there's no allocation of rpg stats in this game, so forget about "builds") now minibosses are probably gonna take up most of your time, they're basically very difficult enemies with a series of attack patterns that you need to learn 100% or else you'll just die. Mostly you need to learn to deflect when the correct animation plays (which is the vast, VAST majority of combat) to the point it feels like you're playing a rhythm game.

Now a lot of the combat is supposed to be about reading the animation and reacting accordingly, but do you know what's constantly blocking the camera from clearly seeing the enemy that you're up close to? that's right the player character itself. Then there's the camera who will spazz out when you're in tight corners. and sometimes just not lock on to the enemy RIGHT NEXT to you, and in an easier game I could forgive that, totally, but not when the punishment for death is as high as it is.

Whenever you die, and you will die, a lot, you lose half your money and a lump portion of xp, which can't be retrieved, it's just gone. Then there's the dragonrot where if you die enough times, which you will, npcs will stop helping you, so you have to spend extra money to fix it in order to progress a story line.

____STORY AND LORE____

Then there's the story and lore, I do think there's something interesting about this world and how so many of it's inhabitants are obsessed with immortality, especially the undead monks, were really cool.

But I don't care about the main character and his lord (a twelve year old boy with magic resurrection blood), really none of the characters are of any interest to me, except maybe the Carver and the undead training dummy, they're kindda funny.

____Overall____
And that's really the issues I have overall with the game, I'm not interested in getting better in a game, that I have so little stake in. I don't care about the characters or what's gonna happen next to them, the world itself is kindda cool, but not enough to warrant the ungodly amount of hours I'd have to invest in order to "git gud".

It's good. Uhh, I don't really know what else to say or where to start. Normally I review beat'em'ups and simpler stuff. Haven't really played many Souls-like games before this one.

The tactical swordplay is very good here. The sense of adventure too. The story of Ashina is very engaging in its history and the various mysteries surrounding the setting.

It's hard for me to praise just one thing. Focusing on any one thing would do an amount of injustice to the rest. I sound like the dumb quotes on the back of book covers saying that, but it's pretty legit.

I only have two complaints. One being they don't explicitly tell you how the window on the parry works, they trust you to feel it out. Which is fine, but I became way better after learning it's a half-second window which decays dramatically if you mash it within the same second. Hesitation is defeat, and you're rewarded for being decisive instead of panicky in a very literal way here.

Second, because of not knowing this, I'd developed a terribly gimmicky play style of totally dodging around enemies with no lock-on engaged, getting chance hits on them, which worked about around until it didn't. I tried using techniques I'd learned from various beat'em'ups and it was auspiciously effective far too often despite me basically not really engaging with the enemies properly.

For context, beat'em'ups often expect you to flank or out-maneuver opponents, and their techniques would be about catching you. There was little interaction with attack strings or properties. I'd also grown up playing the very braindead-until-they're-masochistic RPGs like Kingdom Hearts, where there basically was no such thing as "directly interacting with enemies," which I'm sure Kingdom Hearts experts would debate me on. My problem is-- most Kingdom Hearts enemies are basically obstacles, which you either use a cutscene attack to deal with, or you meticulously memorize the encounter like it was a boss itself to know when to guard, reflect, or where to walk.

Really, my problem with Kingdom Hearts is that its skill expression is extremely top-heavy and Expert existing feels almost unintentional in an otherwise condescendingly easy experience. If you're not getting your balls crushed in the ball crushing factory, you're casually drifting through the shallow puddles that are the standard enemy's hitpoints. Also, the memorization-focused nature of it all is really monotonous.

So I tried playing Sekiro in that way, half because that's how I was raised essentially, and half because I wanted to be a contrarian. I didn't want to guard at all, I wanted to see how playable the game was with just evasion. It almost seemed like the game meant for me to think that for a while because I was clearing challenges that way. Plus, not knowing about the parry's window duration, I didn't come to trust it very much either even when it became clear I had to use it.

It was a little arrogant of me to do that on my first run-through, considering I really didn't fully understand the guarding mechanic yet. Looking back, if I really wanted to be cheeky, I would've guarded without parrying and played in a weirdly passive way. Which, like, seems fully intended for you to be able to do, but something amuses me about how cursed it seems compared to constantly putting pressure on the enemy. Awkwardly enough, it seems just as viable, since this strategy ends up with the enemy exposing themselves more often while trying to pressure you.

Now, I'm not going to blame the game entirely for me coming at it from a somewhat unnatural perspective, but if it had said or done something to help me actually trust the parry (which really is the core mechanic of the entire game) then I probably wouldn't have developed that weird initial play style. I'm sure the general public might disagree with me about how much of a difference knowing the literal parry timing is, but I instantly trusted it far more when I learned. Why-- because it spoke to me how intentional the mechanic is. When they made the parry timing dynamic proportional to how wishy-washy or cowardly you were being, I can tell they meant it. Also, the 0.5s window is pretty generous isn't it? That's actually pretty long, so you can really preempt some moves.

I guess I was waiting for the game to come out and say something sorta passive-aggressive about the virtues of guarding and the follies of not facing danger head-on, or some early enemy who does nothing but check your guard. An experienced player might say, "isn't that all of them?" And I'd reply, "not if running in a circle around them while taking potshots works."

Lastly one might be reading this and going, "hey if Kingdom Hearts has bosses, and Sekiro has bosses, and memorizing them was a low point in Kingdom Hearts, what about Sekiro?"

It's night and day. Kingdom Hearts is basically all about learning how to avoid interacting with enemy moves because "interacting" generally means "taking damage." Mashing out Reflect is a last resort for something you can't simply avoid because Reflect uses your MP up, which is a weird bar that also has a cooldown timer when it runs out and it never feels intentional because it takes what feels like two minutes to come back. Am I just not supposed to be guarding when it's gone? Am I not supposed to be guarding at all?

Sekiro bosses inspire you to learn about their behavior. They aren't just spamming animations. Moves and techniques often have follow-ups that are simultaneously recognizable but not predictable, some only having a chance to come out, and others being things you can bait out with your behavior. Some bosses won't use some moves if you guard instead of parrying or vice-versa. Some bosses have moves they only use if you run away or do something specific in the middle of a technique.

There's also a very particular way the game gets you excited about its engine quirks so that you can hit the boss AI with techniques and mix-ups. In other words, this is basically the best iteration of both Punch-Out and Castlevania bosses. Also, I'd say Sekiro is far, far more forgiving than Critical Mode Kingdom Hearts, and it's a much better game for it. Difficulty is not always a good challenge, and a good challenge is not always meaningful or satisfying.

Posted: November 8
The flames of war lick once again at Ashina Castle, the region corrupted by immortality’s lure. At your lowest point, a mysterious letter persuades you to climb up from the depths and rediscover what you fight for. From Software's Japantastic get-beaten-up-'em-up presents most of the Soulslike staples in a leaner, twitchier game, with an addictive new combat system, and is one of the rare games I've replayed straight after finishing.

Probably the biggest such staple is their level design, and especially with so few poison swamps I have no complaints here. FromSoft have easily turned their perverse, beautiful fantasy stylings from gorgeous gothic to superb sakurapunk. Everywhere you look is an iconic bridge, looming cliff, striking Buddha statue, ornate pagoda or slightly nauseating cave where that giant freak nearly turned you inside out. Every locale’s distinct identity sticks crystal-clear in your mind, and though it shouldn't be, it's easy to accept how they interlink with each other and the castle. Levels are full of verticality, which you can enjoy via jumping (!) and a grappling hook. Hook points are annoyingly easy to miss sometimes, but make exploration joyously fluid, and From Software are brilliant as usual at hiding little secrets and shortcuts for you. Exploration emphasises stealth and use of the environment; you can kill unaware enemies instantly and goriously, but combat with multiple enemies tends to be a losing game. I would have liked to see combat skills integrate more with the hook, as well as more vertical boss encounters, though those are tough enough already.

Sekiro’s battles test your reflexes above all else. Each awesome, intimidating new boss, several of which are now infamous classics, had me genuinely wondering if it was beyond my ability (these are blessedly quick to restart). Deflecting an attack (guarding just as it lands) negates all damage and helps to break the enemy’s posture. The timing window is quite forgiving and it's much more intuitive than Dark Souls’ parry - but there will be a lot of attacks so you’ll need to deflect a lot. Once the enemy’s posture is broken, you can perform an ultra-satisfying deathblow, which wipes out their health regardless of how much is left. I think the intended combat style is to stay close, focus on deflecting and strike when you spot openings, but this is only lightly incentivised. Many enemies can be button-mashed into submission. Mashing the guard button against an unfamiliar attack gives you a good chance of a deflection, whatever the game tells you. Many bosses will let you run around well out of range, pop back to poke them occasionally and slowly claim a very dull victory. Hitting a balance of patience and aggression is by far the most interesting way to play, though.

Building your character is very, very straightforward. You are committed to an agile, glass-cannon-ish build with a katana as the primary weapon. You can equip up to three prosthetic tools which help against different enemies, and two active skills that help you in combat. Killing awards "XP" for purchasing active and passive skills, as well as money. You need skill far more than big numbers to beat enemies (damage numbers aren't mentioned anywhere), and rather than traditionally levelling up you're given fixed power upgrades after certain milestones. As a result, this is a tough game, with the final boss taking me far more attempts than anything in Dark Souls 3, but you won't take as long as me if you utilise everything Sekiro gives you.
Certain items cut the difficulty of fights significantly, and you are told very bluntly what counters what. Sometimes an item will completely defang a miniboss, which is disappointing. Most of the game can reasonably be beaten with just "a katana and a dream", which is exceedingly satisfying but not intended, and some fights are just not practical to beat without items. Sekiro's worst minibosses are 2v1s: a significant difficulty increase in general, but the minibosses are practically impossible. A certain item lets you effortlessly remove one enemy, but the remaining enemy then becomes a pushover. Some kind of middle ground approach to these fights would have been good. Pre-fight pop-ups, and the many loading screens you'll see when you die, contain useful advice that might tip the scales for you - not as satisfying as learning through gameplay, but it gets the job done. The lore happens to be mostly comprehensible too - though it has its moments. Overall information is conveyed very clearly, apart from two crucial things I don't remember being told: talk to all NPCs until they repeat themselves, and you can deflect damn near anything you want. You're welcome…

I have enough praise for this game to fill a second review but I’ve hit my character limit. This beautiful, punishing tale certainly isn't for everyone but I would easily recommend it for lovers of fast-paced action, and anyone who didn't think Dark Souls was hard enough.

This game is absolutely amazing and changed the way I see combat in games. When I started it was rough, like really rough. I died to basic enemies regularly, couldn't get through the easiest minibosses and I almost refunded on the spot, but that's not what you should do. I gave it one more try, I got through the chained ogre and the game rewarded me with one of the best experiences I've had in gaming.

I've never seen such a great combat, at first it may seem simple, parry or attack but there is much more to it and you need to figure it out. You can jump on enemies while they are trying to crouch or slash too low to damage their posture, you can step on their blade when they try to stab you, your left can be practically anything, from axe prosthetic to f**king umbrella, then you have combat arts ranging from slashes around you to literally 1000 cuts in one second. Posture system is amazing, as long as you deflect perfectly, it never breaks.

Of course there are some minor downsides but they are not that bad once you get used to them. The worst ones are:
1) Camera angles can often f*ck you up, even if you are locked onto enemy, you can accidentally run into wall and you can't see anything, beware of that
2) Hitboxes can sometimes be wierd. For example you slash through the enemy, or enemy missed you and you still get hit, this isn't often but when it happens it can completely ruin your bossfight

Even with these problems the game is absolutely 10/10 and a must play. However be prepared to die a lot on your first playthrough, you don't level up your character but yourself. Remember, deflecting is more important than attacking.

Thank you for reading this, definitely buy the game and have a nice day.

Posted: November 6
Good parts: i commend them for releasing a game that isn't a soulsborne. All enemies are new which surprised me and i don't expect originality coming from fromsoft. The game does feel good to play when it all works, and it looks like there is a cool idea beneath the bad stuff.

Bad stuff: 60 fps locked in current year. If a mod can easily bypass this, then this can be added by devs. For me this is enough of a dealbreaker on its own. The option menu is abysmal, and although performance is not as atrocious as Elden Ring, it's unacceptable for a game that looks as this one does.

Posture system is a neat idea but in reality it doesn't. Your own posture barely matters, most enemies can't punish a posture break and it takes less than 2 seconds to recover by just holding block. On bosses it matters but not as much as damage. You can kill a boss by depleting the posture, but for posture to not deplete fast, you need to deal damage first. So it ends up feeling like posture damage doesn't matter for a good chunk of the fight, and then damage doesn't matter. I get the idea but it's badly executed.

Farming is waaaay too encouraged. If you have a ton of experience you are encouraged to farm till your next level or you risk too much if you die. Gold pouches seem like a system that only feels bad. You don't feel smart for paying the 10% tax to save your gold, losing it feels bad, using the pouches feels bad. It's not like gold is super necessary, it's just there to make you feel bad about dying. More than a soulslike. XP tree is bloated with things just to encourage farming. Shinobi tool upgrades require a ton of materials that are rare drops on specific mobs. There are literally consumables to help you farm. Why not like, remove this entirely from the game? It adds nothing and makes everything feel worse. You even have to farm your revives, so you'll be attempting a boss, you don't have your 2/3 revives, you need to go out, kill some trash to farm your revives, to go back to the boss and lose the xp and gold you gathered while farming.

Which gets us to the next point. The trial and error is replaced by a easier game where you are punished a lot for failing. Consumables are super op, tools are op, revives are op, you get too much healing. You can pretty much bulldoze your way through the game as intended; or you can try to learn bosses, try to defeat them cleanly and meanwhile feel that the game is angry at the way you want to enjoy playing it. At least dragon rot was partially removed from the game, ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥, what a dumb mechanic, it's like these people forget all the mistakes they already made, insane that it didn't get completely removed.

Quests and story as incomprehensible as ever, but if you like following a guide to get the endings power to you. Exploration is a bit messed up with places getting up to 3 different states and loot shifting around, but that's not a biggy.

Bosses are pretty good for the most part, really low amount of ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥, even if you are trying to properly learn them. Barring a couple of bosses that go against the main design of the game and are just tanks you have to hit 300 times and you are discouraged from parrying. It's like they have a dumb ideas for bosses quota and they need to hit it.

So yeah, i know these games gather the most undesirable people whose only form of self validation is being able to beat a kinda hard game and they can't have a different opinion pop up on the steam reviews. So kindly, ♥♥♥♥ off.

If you're hesitating to get the game due to the difficulty, I encourage you to still get it.

Elden Ring was my first Souls game. And I think most people agree that Elden Ring is one of the easier entry of this type of games. But Sekiro caught my eye, due to it being the hardest Souls-like game (according to others). And indeed it was hard. I was stuck at the first major boss for a whole week, and I'd attempt to fight him at least an hour or two a day. But after that, everything just got easier. Of course another boss made me quit the game for a while but I came back and beat him, and at the end I managed to beat the final boss only after 11 tries.

Story wise it isn't that strong, but I still find it interesting nonetheless. Combat is top notch and feels so smooth and satisfying. The game feels fair 95% of the time, although there is an optional boss that kinda throws away everything you've learnt so you have to improvise when fighting him. While in other Souls game you can cheese bosses or get overleveled and a super broken weapon to beat them, in this game you can't do all that. You actually need to get good and at least learn the moveset. I did cheese one of the bosses but that still requires you to look at his moveset and time your attacks. At the end it took me around 10-15 minutes to cheese him. If you're willing to learn and willing to be patient, this game will feel so rewarding.

Again I went from the easiest Souls-like to the hardest entry with no experience. And if I can beat this game, so can you.

This game feels like a dance. Every fight is this intense back-and-forth where timing and rhythm matter more than anything. Once it clicks, it’s incredibly satisfying.

I love how every enemy, even the small ones, can destroy you if you don’t know what you’re doing — but once you learn their moves, you just flow through the fight. Beating bosses (and even mini-bosses) feels like a real achievement.

What really makes Sekiro special is how it’s not about getting better gear or new spells — it’s about you getting better. Each boss teaches you something new, and by the end you realize just how much you’ve improved as a player.

The atmosphere is amazing too — beautiful, eerie, and full of tension. Every area feels alive and dangerous.

Sekiro doesn’t hand out wins. It makes you earn them. And that’s what makes every victory feel so damn good.

Sekiro delivers one of the most satisfying combat systems ever made — every duel feels sharp, precise, and earned. The posture mechanic forces you to truly master your opponents rather than just out-damage them, and once it clicks, the flow of battle is incredibly rewarding.

The only real pity is that the game isn’t longer. Its world, though rich and immersive, leaves you wanting more once you’ve mastered its bosses and secrets. A DLC or a true sequel would be a dream — especially if FromSoftware adds a PvP component to test those hard-earned skills against real players.

From the story and stealth mechanics to the refined swordplay and atmosphere, everything comes together flawlessly. Sekiro stands as a near-perfect action RPG — challenging, beautiful, and unforgettable.

A lot of people really like this game but I was not really a fan. It feels like From gutted the RPG experience in a bid to have better action than ever before, but because of how parry-focused this game is, the action actually feels like a dumbed down rhythm game. The exploration and level design is also a lot more confusing and frustrating in this game because of the verticality given to the player. In the Souls games, you pretty much could just follow every path to its end and you would be pretty unlikely to miss that much. In this game, you need to keep your eye out for random tree branches to grapple to which could appear above or below where you're standing on a cliff face hundreds of feet away, or walls to climb that sometimes aren't very clearly signposted, which can lead to you getting lost even just trying to complete the main story.

That being said, the combat definitely still has the potential to be quite satisfying. The final boss of the standard route is one of the most exhilarating fights From has ever made, so there's definitely fun to be had. But be aware that your experience with this game might vary, especially depending on your expectations. You are not guaranteed to love the game just because you loved Dark Souls or Elden Ring.

Wish I could give this game something in between recommended/not recommended, but most of my experience with Sekiro was negative so it gets a "not recommended" from me. That doesn't mean there's no chance you'll enjoy it, or that if you had fun playing the game your feelings are somehow invalid. Just understand that this game is not for everyone, and know that not being for everyone can be a good thing.

Posted: November 1
Let's start with the reason for this review being negative: I don't believe this game deserves this high of a score in Steam's community rating system. Personally, yes, I do reccomend this game, but the issues with new player expierience are too glaring for this game to be Overwhelmingly Positive.

I won't be focusing on the positives too much here, there's plenty of other reviews about that. And they're right, mind you, the combat systems *is* that good, the world *is* that cool.

So what are my issues with the new player expierience? Two things, mainly: consumables and penalties. Your Shinobi Prosthetics use a consumable resource that you have to farm if you run out while learning a boss. There are enemies that are extremely hard to fight without a specific item. Ran out while learning? Welp, go farm.
Another thing - Dragonrot. That's a union between consumables and penalties. And I know Dragonrot doesn't actually matter, that you have to intentionally try to make it a real problem because there are enough items to cure it, but that doesn't change the fact that it feels awful.
Just like the next thing - loss of exp and money on death. Like, why? I died, I don't need extra penalties to know I've failed. The only thing this system incentivizes is roaching out whenever you feel like you might die for real. And what do you do after that? Run to the bonfire. Which s the same result as death. It also hinders exploration because if you see anything that resembles a boss arena and you're currently holding a lot of exp or gold you just can't enter it. So you have to go farm enough exp or gold to buy what you want, and only then can you progress. Not cool imo.

And so all of that amounts to game punishing you for learning. Which seems real weird considering that also seems to be the main way of playing it. It also doesn't help that you are the weakest at the start of the game, both in terms of resources and personal skill. You lack many useful upgrades, your healing is very limited, your health and attack power are low.

A few side things:
- stealth is quite a big part of the game, so it's a bit weird how the enemies are dumb as ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ bricks. Their vision and hearing are terrible, unless the game says "nu uh" on a few occasions and gives them supersight and hearing. There's one ninja that has cheats and will lock onto you the moment you enter his zone or kill the dog that alerts him. The only way to kill him with stealth is to drop from 100 meters above him. Ranged enemies have generally better eyesight, so I guess that make sense but still feels weird compared to their dull melee kin. Oh, did I mention they have ZERO information spread? If you throw a shuriken at an enemy talking to another enemy the one you hit will lock onto you, but the other one will be just midly concerned. There can be situations where an eneme is chasing you while all the other ones in the are are just going about their business.
- the game's paths feel forced at times. The ever-useful grapple can only connect to points the developers put by hand. So sometimes there's 50 of them all around, but then suddenly when it'd be really useful and help you skip something bam, not a single point, even if it feels like it should be there by game's logic. I don't recall the exact circumstances but I've encountered a rocky wall a child could climb but since it wasn't designed so it was impossible for my nimble shinobi to go up it. The point is that the clash between a nimble shinobi and a character that has to follow a set path oftentimes exposes the game for what it is.
- the deatblow animations, while extremely cool, seem like a bit of an overkill for bosses' first healthbar. I put a hole in a normal guy's throat and he just shrugs it off the first time around. It's more of a nitpick but it does feel funny/weird.


Like I said, in the end I enjoyed this game. I got 100%, did a charmless run, beat all 4 gauntlets. I reccomend it but I just want people to know that it's not just "git gud" that stops new players.

Introduction
I came into the souls-like genre relatively late, starting with Lies of P (9/10) and later The First Berserker: Khazan (9.5/10). Both taught me patience and respect for the genre while proving that even a brutal challenge can be beautiful.
When I began Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice, I felt I was stepping into something legendary. It was also my first completed game from the masters at FromSoftware, which made the whole experience feel even more meaningful.

It’s clear that the game comes from the PS4/XONE era, yet its visual direction, lighting and artistic style completely transcend its age.
The cutscenes are captivating, the characters and story pull you in right away, and the atmosphere is soaked in unmistakable Japanese spirit. Among Japanese games from that era, Sekiro’s story stands out as one of the best — powerful, symbolic and full of moments that stay with you long after the credits roll.
I fully understand why Sekiro is considered a “holy grail” among action games.
And yet, I can’t shake the feeling that it’s slightly overrated. Not in terms of quality, but in how uncompromisingly some fans worship it without acknowledging its extremes.

Pros
• Stunning environments and art direction. The fusion of feudal Japan and spiritual mysticism works beautifully, and every location feels like a painting.
• Outstanding cinematic presentation. A film-like rhythm, attention to detail and characters that truly leave a mark.
• Unique combat system. The parry-based rhythm and posture mechanics are demanding but brilliantly designed and deeply satisfying.
• Excellent PC optimization. Smooth performance, fast loading times and virtually no bugs.
• Immense sense of reward. Defeating a boss that once felt impossible is pure adrenaline and relief.

Cons
• Extreme difficulty and frustration. Not everyone has the patience for repeated boss fights; sometimes it feels more like a test of endurance than fun.
• Limited RPG flexibility. There’s little room for experimentation; success relies entirely on skill and reflexes.
• Certain mechanics punish the player too harshly, like the Dragonrot system or the unforgiving XP economy.
• Pacing issues. Some sections feel repetitive or less inspired compared to the game’s best moments.
• Intriguing yet cryptic story. It’s engaging, but much of it remains unclear without diving into external lore.
• Traversal mechanics like grappling, jumping, swimming and diving feel more like add-ons than natural gameplay elements. They’re used only superficially and make the world feel more linear than it initially appears.

Reflection
Playing Sekiro today, after modern titles like Lies of P and Khazan, feels different. The genre has evolved — technically, stylistically and in how it respects player choice.
Khazan, for instance, carries Sekiro’s DNA but refines it further, offering multiple difficulty options and smoother pacing.
Players who once viewed Sekiro as an untouchable pinnacle might benefit from revisiting it today — not to diminish its greatness, but to see how much the genre has grown since 2019.

Conclusion
Sekiro is like a master duel — sometimes unfair, sometimes brilliant. It’s not a game you simply play; it’s one you endure, learn from and ultimately overcome. When you finally do, it leaves behind a strange calm after the storm.
Not all of its design choices worked for me; some, like the grappling, swimming and diving, might have benefited from a simpler and more focused approach. Still, its true strength lies in the fact that even years later, it stays with you.

I might return to Sekiro someday. It offers multiple endings and a New Game+ mode, and there’s something about its world that keeps calling you back. But for now, this is how it felt after my first journey through its storms and victories.
For me personally, it’s a game I admire more than I love — and maybe that’s exactly what makes it special.

Score: ★★★★☆ (8/10) – iconic, stylish and unforgettable, yet far from flawless.

UPDATE – after returning to the game:
The more time I spend with Sekiro, the more I realize how unique and defining this game truly is. Even after frustration, even after moments where the camera or difficulty pushed me near my limit, this is the one souls-like I kept coming back to again and again. Sekiro doesn’t just challenge you – it teaches you. It reshapes the way you think about combat, timing and discipline, and when it finally clicks, the whole experience becomes something extraordinary. After returning to the game and reflecting on what truly stayed with me long after finishing it, I’m raising my score to 9/10. It’s not flawless, but it is iconic – and no other game in this genre grew in value the way Sekiro did.

Sekiro is a great game that suffers from the syndrome known as being apart of the Soulsborne Legacy. That is to say, everything that Sekiro does right feels like an evolution of the Darksouls formula, but fails to let go of aspects that makes it miserable. The game play is excellent- when you already know exactly what to do. The map is well designed - when you know exactly where to go. The game is fun to play - after you've already mastered it. If that sounds nice too you, get it, but I can't recommend a game like that in good faith.

Pros (which there are a lot of)
- Fast and Interesting evolution of Souls-Borne combat
- Excellent Boss-gated Leveling system
- Amazing atmosphere
- Interesting Bosses

Cons
- Weird tacked on Souls-mechanics that detract from the Experience (Abhorrent camera, Weird hitboxes, Souls-esk Stumbling and Uncharacteristic clumsiness, occasionally inconsistent rules)
- Lack of proper tutorialization/introduction to mechanics (Lightning attacks, Shielded Enemies, Archaic actions required for certain areas of progression.)
- Very forgettable story with mediocre characters
- Beginner unfriendly, not encouraged to experiment (Very low heath at game start, Potential to be soft-locked out of using an XP mechanic due to death mechanic, Items made to ease difficulty implemented frustratingly poorly.)

I'd say this game is a time sink, where if you spend the time, you WILL enjoy it, but after 40-some hours, I know for me this is not something I'd recommend to others, nor is something I'd complete without a guide in hand, cheesing every boss I had the mispleasure of facing for the first time.

Clashing of swords at its finest.
It was a bit overwhelming in the very beginning for me (and I've never played a Souls game before) but after some time I got used to the flow. And when you get the rhythm, it becomes the dance.

Fast-pacing engaging combat which requires your concentration and punishes you for your every mistake. It plays fair and teaches you to fight perfectly and if you learn your lesson it will be the most satisfying combat ever. Very fluid animations and clashing sounds which become the music. But katana is not your only weapon, you can use tons of tools fit into the prosthetic arm, both offensive and defensive. Or you can ignore them completely and only use your sword. It doesn't have the variety of customization like in an RPG, but what it has, it is made perfect.

Yes, it's hard, it requires you to 'git gud', and you will die a lot and suffer. I had so much rage during some of the fights, yet I have never even considered a rage quit. The learning curve of the difficulty is quite balanced: once you start to feel comfy, it gives you another challenge. So you'll never get bored, even after beating the game: you can go for additional NG+ playthroughs and make the game harder (yes, harder). Just give up Kuro's charm and ring the demon bell ;D

But besides the combat, this game has some more to offer. The stunning visuals of mountainous Japan, filled with gorgeous castles and temples, detailed armor of human warriors and fascinating creatures, which, as I suspect, are inspired by Japanese mythology. The game on the whole feels very Japanese, but it doesn't require you to know almost anything about their culture beforehand. The story is clear and quite linear, yet you can have 4 different endings. There are not so many characters, but each one of them has a story to tell. Plus some pieces of lore can be found in item descriptions and environment. If you pay attention the lore becomes more deep and complex.

Easily one of the best (or even the best) game I've ever played. Well done, FromSoftware.

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice | Brutal Lessons, Unmatched Highs

I have never played another game like Sekiro, and I probably never will again. I hated it for the first ten hours. I would install it, play a few hours, get stonewalled by a boss, quit for months, then try again. One holiday, I forced myself to push through. Sekiro will teach you more about yourself, patience, determination, and persistence than any other game I have found. You do not really level up your character so much as level up yourself. Everyone should do at least one playthrough and experience what it means to master something. As the game teaches you, hesitation is defeat.



The Good
Combat that is its own genre. The posture system makes every fight a duel of nerve. You are aiming to break posture for a deathblow while deflecting strings at the precise tempo. It feels closer to a rhythm game than a brawler once it clicks, and the flow state is very real.
Vitality versus posture is a clean conversation. Chip vitality to slow posture recovery, then keep the pressure. The rules are readable and fair, which is why you can feel yourself improving.
Telegraphing you can trust. Audio tells, spark showers, and enemy cadence all train you. Hanbei’s practice arena is an honest classroom. When you die, you know what you missed.

























Sekiro is a test of you as much as it is of your character. It will make you angry, then it will make you better, and then it will make you unstoppable. If you want multiple viable builds and flexible difficulty, this is not that game. If you want a pure, surgical duel system with the cleanest parry loop in the business, nothing else feels like Sekiro.

A Masterpiece of Its Own Kind

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is a truly great game. It’s not your typical Souls-like experience, yet it’s incredibly fun and satisfying in its own unique way. I enjoyed every moment of it.

-The Myth of “Too Hard”

Many players often say Sekiro is “too hard” or “not a good starting point for new Souls players,” but I completely disagree after playing it myself. Yes, this game is hard — but the difficulty doesn’t come from unfair enemies or bad design. It comes from mastering its core mechanics.

-Skill Over Stats

Unlike other Souls games where you can rely on builds or stats, Sekiro is all about timing, parrying, deflecting, posture breaking, and learning boss patterns. Since there are no traditional RPG stats or build paths, your skill becomes your true weapon. Once you get used to these systems, the gameplay feels incredibly smooth — and I promise, you’ll start to love it.

-A Message to New Players

For newcomers to the Soulsborne genre, hear me out: you can absolutely start with Sekiro — as long as you tell yourself, “Yes, I can do this,” and embrace the challenge. If you enjoy tough, fast-paced, skill-based combat that truly tests your focus and patience, this game will hit hard — in the best way possible.

-A Word of Caution

However, if you prefer to play casually and have no prior Souls experience, I wouldn’t recommend starting with Sekiro. It can feel brutally punishing for absolute beginners. I’d suggest playing and finishing at least one other Souls game first to understand the general mindset — then dive into Sekiro for an even greater experience.

-The Beauty of Deflection

And don’t worry too much about parrying! Sekiro’s deflection system feels more fluid and forgiving than in other Souls games. Once you understand the rhythm, it becomes second nature, and the combat starts to flow beautifully.

-Short but Powerful

The game may not be the longest one out there, but every moment feels meaningful. The combat is deeply satisfying, and every victory — especially against the bosses — feels earned through pure skill.

-Final Thoughts

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is not just about fighting — it’s about learning, adapting, and mastering yourself. Once you overcome its initial wall, you’ll realize it’s one of the most rewarding and unforgettable experiences ever made.

-Fun Fact

Despite the name “Shadows Die Twice,” trust me — you’ll die way more than twice. 😄

-My Ratings

🎮 Gameplay: 10/10
🎨 Art Design: 10/10
📖 Story: 9/10
🌏 World Design: 10/10
🎤 Music: 7/10

I would like to start off by saying this game is really hard, in my opinion.

It's not a traditional Souls-Like Game, and instead its combat mechanic leans heavily on perfect-parrying and weapon clashing to "break" your opponent's posture to deliver a fatal blow. Each fatal blow allows you to deduct one posture point from the opponent. Once all posture points are depleted, you can deliver the killing-blow. Bosses and mini-bosses in this game have two or more posture points to break, and at times can even have multiple boss phases. Another part of the combat would be the sweeps, thrusts and grab attacks that you can't parry, indicated by the "危“ that appears in red about a half-second before it happens. These attacks rely on you jumping or dodging to avoid the attacks. If the opponent breaks your posture, you're basically cooked.

Character customization does not really go a long way in this game, as there are no armors or weapon classes. However, you can customize your prosthetic arm with 3 different utility items that you get from collecting Shinobi Tools around the world. These Utility Items are for occasional use during combat or sneak attacks. Additionally, there are skill points you can use for heavier swipes of your blade, but at the end of the day, what matters is that you do not hesitate in combat. Also, it is recommended to use sneak in unfamiliar zones with higher monster density to kind of get your bearings in this game.

The level designs in this game go a little more "vertical" in a sense that sometimes when you hit a roadblock, you may have to use your grappling hook to jump up a building or look towards the cliffs for branches you can hook to swing over. This makes the level design feel more "open" than the traditional Dark Souls formula. Speaking of which, this game has a Stealth mechanic and at times, you can also approach a guarded zone however you like. This includes sneak attacking and hiding, using the Gachiin Sugar item to become temporarily invisible, use plunging attacks from roof tops, throwing Shurikens from your Utility arm, or straight up just tanking everyone in the zone (although this is not recommended).

Overall, I do recommend the game, as it really feels like its own niche in Souls-Like Games.

A Masterpiece of Samurai Storytelling and Combat


In my opinion, this game is one of the best ever made by FromSoftware. It mixes a deep and emotional story set in the samurai era with the challenging and rewarding style that fans of the Souls series love. The whole experience feels intense, meaningful, and full of character.


The story takes place in Japan during the late 1500s, a time of war and samurai honor. You play as a loyal shinobi named Wolf, who fights to protect his young master. The narrative explores themes like loyalty, sacrifice, and redemption, which fit perfectly with the spirit of the samurai. In my opinion, this historical setting makes the story feel powerful and memorable.


What really stands out is the combat. Unlike other action games, it focuses on timing, precision, and posture instead of just dodging or blocking. Every battle feels like a duel where you need patience and skill to win. The parry system is smooth and satisfying, turning every fight into a real test of focus. I think it’s one of the best combat systems ever made.


To sum up, this game is much more than a difficult challenge — it’s a true journey through samurai culture and discipline. With its strong storytelling, amazing atmosphere, and perfectly designed combat, I honestly believe it’s a masterpiece worth playing.

★★★★★ “Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice — The Game That Taught Me How to Rise”

Most games are played.
Sekiro is endured.
It doesn’t just test your reflexes — it tests your spirit.

⚔️ Thematic Brilliance

Sekiro isn’t about glory. It’s about duty, loss, and quiet resilience.
You’re not a chosen hero. You’re a broken shinobi clinging to a dying promise , to protect your lord, to uphold purpose in a world collapsing into ash. It’s a story soaked in melancholy: loyalty beyond reason, death without dramatics, and grace found in stillness.

Where Dark Souls asks you to embrace despair, and Elden Ring celebrates boundless freedom — Sekiro demands focus. It’s intimate. Surgical. There’s no build variety, no stats to grind. Just you, your blade, and the truth that skill cannot be bought — only earned through pain.

⚔️ Combat — The Purest Form of Discipline

The combat system is not “hard” in the cheap way other games are.
It’s precise — like a dance where hesitation means death. Every deflect sings like a note in a symphony of steel. You don’t win by dodging; you win by facing everything head-on. The game rewards fearlessness, not safety.

It’s the cleanest combat FromSoftware has ever crafted.
Where Bloodborne is aggression and Dark Souls is endurance, Sekiro is clarity.
Every boss fight becomes a conversation between intent and execution — a duel of patience and nerve.

Genichiro teaches humility.
Guardian Ape teaches adaptation.
Isshin, the Sword Saint, teaches transcendence.
By the time you beat him, you realize the game was never about killing him — it was about killing your own weakness.

💀 Personal Reflection — My Own Return by Death

When I played Sekiro, I was in pieces.
Illness had stripped my strength, life had stripped my peace, and I was just surviving day to day. The world felt indifferent.

Sekiro didn’t comfort me. It confronted me. It told me, “You will die. Again. But you will rise again too.”
Every failed parry mirrored every failed day I tried to rebuild my life. Every boss became a reflection of my inner demons — my fear, doubt, and resentment.

When I finally beat Isshin after countless deaths, I sat in silence.
Because that wasn’t just a victory in a game — it was a mirror.
It was me proving to myself that no matter how many times life destroys you, you still get back up. You still swing. You still fight.

🌸 Final Thoughts

Sekiro is not a game for everyone — but for those who walk through it, it changes you.
It teaches the kind of patience no meditation app ever could.
It humbles you, sharpens you, and teaches you that mastery isn’t given — it’s forged through countless failures.

“Shadows die twice” — but the soul that endures death twice learns how to live.

Thank you, FromSoftware.
You didn’t make a game.
You made a mirror.

Oh boy Sekiro in the year of our lord 2025.
I cant say much that hasn't already been said so if you're looking for a quick review here it is.
Its good.,,mostly. My favourite from software game perhaps.

My years of stubbornly parrying in any game that allows the mechanic have paid off.
The flow and the rhythm of each battle have become etched into my muscle memory. Each boss has a unique rhythm and style. It can get very addicting to keep perfect parrying and attempt hit-less runs.
Most bosses felt like brick walls but they did a good job making them feel fair. If I was dying to a boss, it was because I was getting greedy for one extra hit or I was panicking...in other words I was hesitating.

The game maintains its difficulty and skips quite a lot of tedium ive come to expect from these titles.
The game is laser focused on trying to get YOU the player up to mark in skill and not roleplay as a strong knight or a mage.

In every fight you are punching above your weight. Each boss is a skill check not an XP check.

This game isnt for everyone though...especially not for dodge roll and attack enthusiasts. The combat does not let you do this. You must stay aggressive and push bosses into following a strict tempo. The second you sit back and play reactively instead of proactively, the game pummels you into the ground.

Heres what I did not enjoy about the game:

1)Gimmick fights where the easiest way to win is some one time mechanic. This happens quite a few times and eve though I read the item descriptions, its not clear that this is what is meant to be done,

2)Any beast type boss. I felt like they dont fit the combat style of this game.

3)Artificial difficulty by swarming weak enemies around a strong one.
There are areas where the games has you clear out foot soldiers, essentially feeling like an unskippable cut-scene. Every time you die to the tough fight, you have go clear these mob characters again.
Im also gonna put ganks in this entry. (Nothing as bad as Godskin Duo from Elden Ring)

4) Spirit emblems. They can be quite useful but they are super hard to keep in early game and way too easy in late game. If you keep dying to the same boss, you run out of spirit emblems and have to go grind for more.

5)Stealth. This might be a me thing. I just dont enjoy stealth very much but we're playing as a Shinobi so its par for the course. The stealth in the game also feels wonky at times.

6)Camera. Quite a few times, the camera was the biggest op. Losing track of enemies I was locked on to, getting stuck on an angle where I cant see, locking on to random things or turning me away from the enemy when I try to lock.

These issues can be annoying at times but they don't frustrate you nearly as much as you will frustrate yourself.

You cannot cheese. You must get good.
Hesitation is defeat.

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is an exceptional game, a masterclass in combat design, atmosphere, and storytelling. Every fight is a brutal dance of precision and timing, where each victory feels genuinely earned. The environments are breathtaking, from serene temples to war-torn castles, all brought to life by a powerful soundtrack that perfectly underscores the game’s intensity and beauty. The level design is outstanding, seamlessly connecting areas in a way that encourages exploration and rewards skillful navigation. The narrative, subtle yet deeply impactful, ties everything together with elegance. However, the quest progression system is where the game shows its age, sticking too closely to the old Souls formula. Objectives aren’t always clear, and advancing certain storylines can feel confusing or opaque without external help. Aside from this, Sekiro stands as a masterpiece: challenging, rewarding, and unforgettable.

Rating: 9.5/10

TL;DR: Flawless combat system, incredible atmospheric setting, wish it were possible to achieve all the endings without a guide



Hands down the best melee combat I have ever played. Knowing that every time I die was 100% my fault due to missed parries or poor timing just feels so good. There are bosses where I'd start out by thinking, "There is no way it is even possible to beat this guy" and then beating them on the 10th or so attempt just feels so incredible. This is the second time I've beat the game, the first a year ago, and beating bosses on my first try when on my first attempt took upwards of 45 minutes to an hour is just a feeling that can't be replicated. I love how when you get to that moment when the combat system just "clicks" and you start beating enemies you haven't faced before on a first attempt... just so satisfying. And yes I did beat the and I will absolutely put that in my review because I want to show off.

The only thing keeping this game from being a perfect 10/10 for me is how they handle the multiple endings. I really appreciated how in this game as long as you pay attention to what the characters are saying and reading item descriptions you will know where to go next and how to get to the end of the game (something that is sorely missing in Dark Souls I and II, I have not finished III yet). Only for FromSoftware to decide that the best way to get the different endings is by arbitrarily I mean wtf is that? I don't think any alternate endings should've been easy or obvious to achieve, but I really don't appreciate the fact that the only way to get all of them is by looking at the wiki. Also, the ending that unlocks even more content is one of the the most convoluted ones to get which makes it even worse. So it was frustrating to feel like after paying close attention to the characters, learning their backgrounds, and to understand the story better that I still had no chance at finding the "true" ending on my own felt pretty lackluster.

With that being said, they still deliver a fantastic tale that is firmly rooted in ancient Japanese folklore, full of symbolism and mythology that gets more interesting the more you delve into it. A common theme in all Fromsoftware games that I've played is the price one must pay to cheat death/achieve immortality, and the way they use that theme here and embed it in game mechanics as well as Japanese mythology is some of the best work they've done.

As i was new to Souls-Games, the first run was really hard. So hard, that i spent hundreds of hours to complete it the first time! It was a gift from a friend, because i really enjoyed watching him being a bad ass Shinobi.

The Game has it´s pros and cons.

Pro:
- really nice setting with enjoyable view points (Japan around 1500s)
- extreme skilled and balanced combat mechanics (as a souls-like, be perfect at your style, NOTHING can stop you, make one mistake you DIE! That´s the challenge AND the reward)
- If you love to play it, you want to get EVERY achievement!
- at your first run, you will die a lot. But this is how it works. Learning the moveset and attacks from your enemy brings your further.
- beautiful effects and really satisfying to finish enemies and bosses.
- nice lore and thrilling turns in the game as you progress.
- NO STAMINA as in other Souls Games (which doesn´t mean, that it´s still hard like a diamond to play through)
- limited ressources to encourage you to be a real Shinobi or at least a really good swordsman.
- there is almost always a way to sneak through enemies and stealth kill them ;)

Cons:
- you need a lot of resilence to get through the game. nothing for players with low frustration tolerance.
in other words: if you wanna play just a casual game, thats nothing for you.
- Mistakes in fighting or react wrong to an enemies attack result always in death or near death, no matter how much power or "upgrade" your Character has.
- Simplicity in comparison to other games from fromsoft. You have your katana, a few "tools" and that´s it. You can´t really "customize" your fighting style. That said, you have to focus on what you have.

Summary:

Greatest and bad ass game i ever played and still satisfied even if i have almost everything achieved. And still i wanna get the platinum trophy, to unlock every achievement. I really missed a game like that since the Tenchu Series, and this is what i got and still love.

If you´re someone, who is not willing to give up in real life, and go through life whatever comes, that game is for you! :)

Sayonara!

Critical Analysis: Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice - The Sword of Mechanical Perfection and its Minimalist Narrative

Technical Sheet and Context:

Developer: FromSoftware

Director: Hidetaka Miyazaki

Publisher/Distributor (in the West): Activision (under license from Bandai Namco)

Release Date: March 22, 2019

Platforms: PlayStation 4, Xbox One, Microsoft Windows

Graphics Engine: Proprietary to FromSoftware (an evolution of the engine used in Bloodborne and the Dark Souls series).

Notable Award: The Game Awards 2019 Game of the Year, beating titles like Control and Death Stranding.

Introduction: The Meeting of Two Giants

The collaboration between FromSoftware, the studio revered for its relentless design and interconnected worlds, and Activision, an industry giant with massive expertise in polishing and commercializing experiences for a broad audience, generated huge expectations. Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is the fruit of this unusual alliance, a title that, while drawing from its developer's DNA, marks a radical turning point in its gameplay philosophy.

Gameplay: A Perfected Dance of Swords

This is where Sekiro reaches its zenith and justifies its Game of the Year award.

Abandoning RPG for Pure Action: Unlike the Souls series or Bloodborne, Sekiro completely eliminates stats, character builds, and multiplayer. Progress is not measured in strength levels, but in the player's pure skill. This design decision is fundamental and divisive: you either master the combat system, or the game defeats you. There is no option to "farm" to make the task easier.

The Posture System and Deflection: The heart of the game. It's not about dodging and attacking, but rather a rhythmic dance of deflections (perfect parries) that break the enemy's posture to deliver a deathblow. This system elevates the challenge to a level of reflex and muscle memory demand rarely seen, rewarding calculated aggression and the study of enemy attack patterns.

Gimmicks and Vertical Mobility: The Shinobi Prosthetic and the grappling hook are not mere additions. They are integral tools that transform exploration and combat. The hook grants spectacular mobility, turning the environment into a three-dimensional battlefield. The prosthesis, with its various tools, offers tactical solutions to specific problems (the Flame Vent against beasts, the Sabimaru against the Okami), adding a layer of strategic depth.

Narrative and World: Beauty and Brutality with a Sparse Script

"Simple" Plot vs. Deep Lore: It is accurate to say the main plot is straightforward: "save your young lord." However, as is tradition with FromSoftware, the true narrative is hidden in the atmosphere, NPC dialogue, items, and level design. The story is not "short," but rather condensed and environmental. The loop—the cursed immortality, the decay of Ashina—is the central theme, not a defect.

Aesthetics and Themes: The game adopts a dark and fantastic Japanese mythology. The decision to present characters with unpleasant appearances (the Sculptor, the Senpou Priests) and villages plagued by monsters (the Sunken Valley, Hirata Estate) is not accidental. It reflects the corruption that the pursuit of immortality and power has inflicted upon the land and its inhabitants. It is a visceral representation that, in this world, humanity has lost its grace. Figures like Emma or the Divine Heir are the beacons of purity amidst that corruption.

The "Plot Holes": This is a recurring and valid criticism from a traditional narrative perspective. Sekiro prioritizes the ludic experience over narrative exposition. While Expedition 33 (presumably a reference to a medium that entertains in a more direct way) prioritizes plot, Sekiro prioritizes the sensation of discovery and mastery over the game's systems. The "gaps" are deliberate spaces for the player to fill with their own interpretation and curiosity. It is not that the story is incomplete, but that it is told in an unconventional way.

The Influence of Activision: A "Facilitated" FromSoftware?

This is a crucial point. It is true that, compared to the absolute opacity of Demon's Souls or the first Dark Souls, Sekiro offers clearer guidance through its tutorials and mechanics.

The "Resurrection Point" allows for one mistake without having to return to a checkpoint, reducing frustration.

The boss patterns, although demanding, are often more "pure" and less dependent on randomness or character builds.

However, labeling it as "more simple and easy" is misleading. Activision did not soften the challenge, but made it more accessible by presenting it more clearly. The learning curve is just as steep, but the path to climb it is better lit. The game is brutally difficult, but it rarely feels unfair. Its difficulty lies in execution, not in ambiguity.

Final Verdict: A Specialized Masterpiece

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is not just another FromSoftware title; it is the purest evolution of its design philosophy towards the action genre. It is a game that sacrifices the flexibility of an RPG for the depth of a psychological fencing simulator.

For the casual player: It is an insurmountable barrier. Its demand for perfection makes it one of the most challenging games in the mainstream.

For the FromSoftware or Hollow Knight veteran: The challenge is familiar in spirit, but the execution is completely new. Mastering Dark Souls does not prepare you for Sekiro; an "unlearning" of habits is necessary.

Legacy: Sekiro stands as a cult title and a watershed moment in melee combat design. It is not a game for everyone, and its environmental narrative will not win over those seeking a cinematic story. But for those seeking a pure, challenging, and immensely rewarding gameplay experience, it is an irreplaceable masterpiece and a worthy Game of the Year 2019. It does not entertain like Expedition 33; instead, it demands and rewards like few others.

+Fast paced ninja stealth
+Fun and intense parry mechanics
+A plethora of tools and moves that complement your katana

-Less replayability compared to other FS games

-In terms of leveling up(and item gathering), mind-numbingly grinding for exp is more efficient because bosses yield insufficient exp points
No online PvP, only messages(but you can pause the game now :D )
-Unseen help( a % chance of keeping your money and exp after you die) doesnt really help
-Some enemy attacks (mostly grabbing) have way too big hitboxes
-A certain ending can lock you out from visiting the end game area
-Incredibly BS final boss
-Persistent Activi$$ion pricing

Sekiro is probably the best action-stealth game in the market, and definitely one of the greatest games From Software has ever made. Its definitely another From Software hardcore classic. 9,3/10

*In case you are on windows and the game stutters, but you have tried every known method online to fix it and the stuttering is still there, Im sorry to tell you this its a shader compilation problem that Sekiro's game engine has.
If you can, try to run Sekiro on linux. Vulkan recompiles the shaders before the game starts, and that fixes the stuttering. Moving Sekiro from Windows to Arch linux, worked for me.
