I have strangely mixed feelings about Sekiro.
I am a Souls games veteran and I have couple hundreds of hours in those games.
It took me incredibly long to finish Sekiro. I have hit a number of brick walls that seemed insurmountable. I had many breaks from it and it just made it go stagnant in my mind.
And yet I cannot complain about the story or the combat.
The bossfights are the most cinematic in any souls game and are very high on the list in general.

I generaly don't like parries in souls games. I am a spellcaster mixed with a bonk stick and I stray away from parry and riposte mechanics as much as possible. But Sekiro in this regard feels more like God of War 4 (That's the closest example I have on mind), where the parry window is more generous and the combat revolves around it. Thing is in most games when a boss is hard you can go grind around so it becomes easier. Not so much in Sekiro - xp gives you more skills, but those stop being much of a power increase after mid game. Gold can buy you consumables, but that is not a thing you want to grind for - as it is not a permanent power increase.

Also the death system does not have the classic soul recovery upon finding your body which makes many deaths way more frustrating.
In Dark souls once you die with a lot of souls you feel like you need to get them back - if you die again that is a quit moment.
In Sekiro you don't even get the luxury of getting to try to get things back.

Some bosses also have very bloated health pools and it makes fighting them feel insurmountable especially if the key to defeat them is a hard-to-grind consumable.
It feels like a lesson that was supposed to be learned from DS2 with its hollowing effect - punishing players for defeat feels horrible. Sekiro at least did not continue with Bloodborne's blood vials but instead with the Estus flask system, but you still need to grind "quicksilver bullets" called Sen. Again to me that feels like a pointless mechanic that makes you grind more when all you want is to finally beat the boss.

Now this all feels like heavy criticism, but in the end I really enjoyed the game.
Most bossfights were the most memorable of any Fromsoftware game I played thus far.
The shinobi prostetic is exceptionaly cool and your entire kit is used brilliantly in combat.

Level design is gorgeous. Vistas look stunning. Enemies are placed perfectly.

But despite the praise I don't feel like I want to play Sekiro ever again.
It is a chapter that I closed and I am done.
I have to recommend it. And I advise you to continue even if it feels pointless. You can always get up again, give the boss another try. Its time to git gud and don't you dare to go hollow.

English version (french below) :

Best gameplay From Software has made : fast, fluid, challenging, but extremely satisfying to master.
The areas are stunning, and most of the bosses are very well designed, including probably one of the best final bosses in all of video game history.

Sure, the tools aren’t particularly useful (or only rarely), basic enemies are often harder than they probably should be (though most can be dodged), and, while this is subjective, I wasn’t particularly blown away by the music, even though it fits the overall atmosphere perfectly.
The thing is, the gameplay is so smooth that it more than makes up for these minor negatives.

Be prepared, though, this game is really tough and doesn’t forgive mistakes !

For completionists, 100% takes a while because it requires a lot of farming to get all the skills, and multiple playthroughs to gather some specific resources in enough quantity to fully upgrade your character (though you’ll need to do all the endings anyway, so it’s manageable).

The game is gorgeous and very cool to play, so enjoy it to the fullest if you get the chance (and if you have the patience) ! :-)

Meilleur gameplay des From Software, c'est rapide, rythmé, difficile mais plaisant à maitriser. Les zones sont magnifiques, et les boss sont très réussi pour la plupart, avec porbablement un des meilleurs boss de fin tous jeux vidéo confondus.
Certes, les outils ne servent pas à grand chose (ou très rarement), les mobs de base sont bien plus difficiles qu'ils ne devraient l'être pour des mobs de base (mais la plupart peut être esquivée), et, même si ce n'est que subjectif, je n'ai pas vraiment été emporté par les musiques du jeu, même si elles correspondent totalement à l'ambiance générale. Le truc, c'est que le gameplay est tellement fluide que ça contrebalance beaucoup de ces points négatifs.
Par contre, soyez prêts parce que le jeu est vraiment dur, et il ne pardonne pas !

Pour les complétistes, le 100% est un peu long parce qu'il nécessite beaucoup de farm pour avoir toutes les compétences, et de refaire plusieurs fois le jeu pour avoir quelques ressources précises en nombre suffisant pour améliorer le personnage au maximum (mais il faut de toute façon faire toutes les fins du jeu donc en soi, ça va)

Le jeu est vraiment beau et agréable à jouer donc profitez à fond si vous en avez l'occasion (et les nerfs) ! :-)

Posted: October 14
In a sea of games that often blend together, Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice stands alone — a singular, defiant masterpiece. It's not just a game; it's a journey that tests your patience, willpower, and resilience. But more than that, it's a story that lingers in your bones long after the final boss falls. For those willing to meet it on its own terms, Sekiro offers something few games ever dare to: meaning through hardship.

At its heart, Sekiro is a tale of loyalty, loss, and redemption. You play as Wolf, a shinobi bound by duty to protect a young divine heir. But what begins as a typical rescue mission quickly becomes something deeper. As you travel through war-torn Japan — facing grotesque beasts, fallen warriors, and your own past — the game slowly peels back layers of story and character, revealing a haunting world on the edge of ruin. Yet even in the darkness, there's beauty. The quiet, snowy moments in Ashina. The eerie calm of the Sunken Valley. The ancient rot of the Fountainhead Palace. Every place you visit feels lived in, broken, but still clinging to something sacred.

What truly sets Sekiro apart, though, is its uncompromising gameplay. Unlike other FromSoftware titles, there's no character building or stat grinding to lean on. There's no summoning help or hiding behind shields. It's just you, your blade, and your timing. Every enemy, from the smallest grunt to the towering bosses, demands your full attention. You don’t level up your way out of trouble — you grow by learning. You fail. You adapt. You try again.

And when you finally land that perfect parry against a boss who’s killed you 20 times? It’s not just a victory — it’s catharsis. Sekiro teaches you patience and trust: in the game’s design, in the rules it lays out, and in yourself. Every triumph feels earned because it is. It’s a game that believes you are capable of greatness, even when you don’t.

But perhaps the most profound thing about Sekiro is how personal the journey becomes. Everyone who plays it ends up telling a story — not just of what happened in the game, but what happened to them while playing it. The boss that nearly broke you. The moment you thought about quitting. And then… the moment it clicked. That story becomes yours, not because it was given to you, but because you fought for it.

In a world where so much entertainment is designed to be accessible, easy, or immediately gratifying, Sekiro dares to be different. It asks you to commit. It asks you to suffer. And in doing so, it offers something rare: growth. Not just of your skills, but of your mindset.

It reminds you that true mastery is earned. That loyalty has a cost. That letting go of fear is the only way forward.

And that is why Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is not just a great game. It’s one of the best games ever made.

Because when it’s over — when the sword is sheathed and the silence returns — you’re not the same person who started.
You’ve changed.
You’ve risen.
And like the wolf in the shadows,
You endured.

Insanely overrated game. Boring, tedious, and repetitive. It boggles the mind to think that quick-time-events are unanimously hated in the gaming community (and rightfully so), but this game is universally praised as a masterpiece. Essentially, every fight is a punishing, lopsided QTE simulator that requires quick reflexes and enemy attack pattern memorization. Block/parry most attacks, dodge/jump when it's something like a charge, sweep, grapple, etc. Succeed and you get a brief window to chip away at the enemy, maybe 3-4% of their health. Fail and you get hit for 50-75% of your own health.

It's Guitar Hero, but with ninjas. F*ck off.

It’s like being trained by an ancient, wise samurai master. Calm, disciplined, and endlessly patient. He teaches you the way of the sword, how to breathe, how to strike, how to become one with battle. You respect him. You admire him. You think, “This man will make me great.”

Then, every time you mess up, even by a hair, he cuts off your arm. Every. Single. Time.

You bow, thank him for the lesson, reattach your arm, and try again. And again. And again. Until somehow, you start seeing the rhythm of the fight. Every swing, every parry, every death feels like notes in a song. You stop panicking. You flow.

And when you finally slay your master, it’s not joy you feel. It’s quiet respect. You’ve been broken a thousand times, but rebuilt sharper than any blade.

Sekiro isn’t a game. It’s spiritual violence, beautiful, painful, and perfect.

Initially, I was skeptical of the combat, but over time, it grew on me. Upon nearing the end of my playthrough, I had changed my whole opinion of Sekiro. Shifting my mindset by treating it as a rhythm game rather than a Souls game was a big game changer. You have to get into this precise and rhythmic combat that looks deceptively simple.

The bosses' difficulty and placement are so well designed. Based on my personal experience, the early game was hard, the mid-game was easy, and the endgame became hard again. If Genichiro is the gatekeeper, Isshin the Glock Saint was the final test to see if you have mastered the combat system.

Sekiro is the literal "git gud" game, as it cannot be brute forced. You cannot outlevel and outscale your enemies; you have only 1 main weapon with your limited shinobi tools and arts. In other games, you have many options to deal with enemies/bosses in the form of other weapons and builds. In other games, your character grows stronger. In Sekiro, you as the player are the one that's growing stronger by increasing your skill level in mastering the combat system and reading your enemies.

Overall, Sekiro has ruined action combat for me, as it's so unique. Nothing can compare to it, even up to this day. It's a timeless game that teaches you learning and mastery through its skill-based combat system. If you like action combat games, I highly recommend this game, as you will have an unforgettable and fulfilling experience. Hesitation is Da Feet! IKUZO, SEKIRO!!!

Amazing game that took me 3 attempts over a few years to even start. This game is extremely difficult and simple at the same time, other than movement at any time there are essentially 4 options at any given time, attack, block/parry, dodge, jump. But the difficulty comes from the amazing movesets that the bosses and even trivial enemies have. There is incredible detail put into each boss, miniboss, and even enemies, many of them possess weaknesses, elemental or otherwise that you can gleam from conversations, notes in the world, and even item descriptions. Many also have special interactions with their environments, or your prosthetics and skills. These workarounds can alleviate some of the difficulty in tough fights, rewarding the player for investing their time and resources into these upgrades. But none of these tricks are at all essential, every single fight is learnable down to a T. But I and other players appreciate the attention to detail that deepens not only the in game mechanics, but the lore of the world. Sekiro probably has one of my favorite combat systems ever. There are so many moments that make you feel like an absolute badass, if you have the time or the will to push through this games challenges, it will reward you with an unparalleled feeling of accomplishment, and I can't recommend this game enough.

Posted: October 7
Incredible action-adventure game that foregoes concepts like stamina bars to allow for high aggression both from enemies and the player. The game primarily focuses on good usage of dodges and parrying to defeat enemies rather than simply hitting them until their health bar is empty. As a result the game requires good reaction time for the player, though the parry window is fairly generous to make it easier.

The game does a poor job at explaining how its combat mechanics work so I will briefly summarise it: Enemies, bosses and the player have a 'posture bar' that fills up when their attacks are parried or they take damage. When it is full, you can kill them in one attack. How ever, the rate at which it depletes is tied to how much health they have. If their health bar is still high, you are expected to dodge attacks to create windows to hit them. When their health bar is lowered a bit, you are expected to parry attacks to build up the posture bar so you can kill them faster.
Parrying and blocking, though blocking an attack causes a lot of posture build-up, does not cause you to take damage. Since the posture mechanic applies to you too, striking a balance between how much health you have left and if you need to heal just so your own posture bar can empty out is also important.

The game also features three 'special' type of attacks that have a delay and causes a red Japanese symbol to appear over the enemy's head and is typically reserved for bosses and mini-bosses.
These attacks have special conditions that causes extra posture damage if you fullfill them, or become undodgable, unparriable and unblockable if you don't. These 3 attack variants are a Grab (Must be dodged), Sweep (Must be jumped over, jumping again in mid-air will cause the player to jump on the enemy and deal extra posture damage) and a Thrust (Must be parried, much smaller timing window.).

A beautifully haunting story in an absolute devil's gauntlet of a game.

Many years ago, I tried either the first or second Dark Souls game. After 3 hours of dying to one of the first 3 or 4 enemies in the game, I swore off all "Souls-like" games forever. Then, FromSoftware decided to make one set in Japan. And I do like games set in Japan. I resisted the urge to buy and play it, knowing I would hate myself. But after 5 years of resisting, I bought it. And I hate to admit that I thoroughly enjoyed it.

After 83 hours, I've beat the final boss and reached an end. And I have to say, the difficulty is one of the beautiful things about this game. Everything is punishing. But if you keep at it, slowly, you'll start to see the patterns, and even more slowly, you'll get better at the parry-attack-dodge dance that is Sekiro. This game isn't for everyone, I don't think. But if you, like me, believe it's not for you, try it. It may surprise you.

A fundamentally challenging, interesting and for me personally, the weakest game compared to Dark Souls franchise. However, it is without a doubt, a great souls game with its very own identity.
My Score: 8/10

For my personal taste, it is the weakest game from FromSoftware. The main strength and at the same time, the main emphasize is the incredible challenging gameplay. My main gripe is not necessarily the difficulty, but limited kill animations and limited sword creativity. More below.

The Good:

+ The setting and atmosphere is alright
+ Kills, especially stealth kills can be satisfying
+ Explorations can be rewarding and lead to mini bosses, great loot etc.
+ Give credit where credit is due: In majority of times, the save location is close to a major battle. MANY times
+ There is a better connection and motivation within the storyline compared to Dark Souls. The main protagonist has a clear "mission" and you can get a better sense on the connections between him and his lord and father
+ My favorite time with this game was exploring the huge Ashina castle complex. It has so many levels, areas and places. It was genuinely fun.

Also the "monkey area" where you had to find and kill 4 monkeys was VERY entertaining and a very thoughtful mission design.
+ Great voice acting
+ Overall better endings compared to Dark Souls xD
+

What I didn't like:
- Coming from NIOH, I notice the lack of "sword creativity" and stances. YES, you have special abilities with your sword and your arm can be used as an extended weapon but again, compare this with Nioh (2017) and Sekiro (2019), the latter lacks.

- Your sword or katana is your main weapon. As such I expect it should have more weight to it but NO.
Different katana looks with engravings - NO
Give your katana more special effects - NO
You can't infuse your katana directly with special effects, only to the main protagonist and your arm as mentioned above

- There is literally just 1-2 kill animations from behind where you put the katana in the throat. Like THAT's IT?! When you sneak behind an enemy or when the enemy has depleted his health, you can execute a "finisher". Now you kill all sorts of regular enemies and you will see the same boring kill animation over and over again. (NOTE: There are some special kill animations only for BOSSES)

- The skill tree is nothing special. As mentioned before: You CAN select a special katana attack skill which you can unlock. You can only select ONE, yes ONE skill ONLY!!!!!!
Many similar games got this similar LB + RB button combo to executive your skill. My point is, the katana is so pristine, beautiful and your MAIN WEAPON but you can only execute one skill… this is such a bummer for me!!!
There is no LB+X, LB+Y etc where you can execute multiple katana skills and styles, NOTHING

No stances, no different katana styles, the same boring kill animations. NO decapitation, except for some bosses. Because you only have one sword, why does it lack so much creativity?

In my opinion, Dark Souls 3 and Elden Ring are superior in all areas except story. Sekiros lore was a lot better!
NIOH takes the crown, with different stances, weapons styles and abilities and a large arsenal.

The reason why I keep mentioning NIOH was simply the fact that it got released BEFORE Sekiro and it offers possibly anything that a gamer can wish for as I mentioned above.

Sekiro is a unique sword focused souls like game. I recommend this game who seek a nerve thrilling challenge with a Japan setting.

I’ve played plenty of FromSoftware titles — Dark Souls, Bloodborne, Elden Ring — and I get what they’re about: tough but fair combat, deep world-building, and a sense of reward when you finally conquer a challenge. But Sekiro? It just doesn’t hit the same.

The biggest issue is that Sekiro feels punishing for all the wrong reasons. FromSoft’s other games give you freedom to build your character, to approach a fight your way. Here, you’re boxed into one playstyle. No magic, no heavy armor, no alternate weapons. Just a sword, a prosthetic arm, and the developers’ insistence that you “learn the parry timing or get wrecked.” It’s not challenging — it’s repetitive and frustrating.

The combat is flashy, sure, but after a while, it’s like doing a rhythm-game boss fight where missing a single beat means instant death. And the camera? Still awful, maybe worse. Try fighting a giant in a tight space and tell me that’s good design.

Then there’s the story — vague but without the intrigue that Dark Souls and Bloodborne pull off. The characters feel flat, and the world lacks that eerie, mysterious atmosphere that made those earlier games so memorable. It’s beautiful, yes, but it feels hollow once the novelty wears off.

At the end of the day, Sekiro feels like FromSoftware forgot what made their games special. They traded creative freedom for rigid precision, and while some people love that, I just found it exhausting.

To preface this negative review, I no longer have as much free time as I did. I'm about average when it comes to soulslike, I highly enjoy Nioh games, probably my favourite soulslike.

I got this game as nearly everyone says this is the best game From Software made next to Bloodborne, and went as far as the Ashina Castle rooftops, got killed by the kamikaze guy once and I didn't mind that. What I do however mind about this game, is that every time I feel like I'm starting to have the tiniest bit of fun, the game just ♥♥♥♥♥ on it and throws some BS my way. You want to stealth kill, well here's this ranged enemy you couldn't have seen having perfect view of your stealth kill and shooting you in the back, calling in everyone on you. Alternatively shoots a mofo cannon at you. Said cannon somehow going through the mountain. Supposed to dodge the grab attacks by side stepping? Nope, get grabbed. Google it out, oh, jump will dodge it. Nope, still get grabbed. Solution? Just restart until the enemy stops spamming the grab moves. Brilliant.

In the past soulslikes there would've been ways to deal with enemies, reliable ones, not just pray you get good RNG on their moves. In the past I would've also just grit my teeth, got through it somehow and that would be it. Nowadays however I value my free time quite a bit more than before, and as such I am not willing to waste any more of my time on being annoyed, angry, and what not.

Skill issue on my part? Very likely, but that's not the problem here. Problem is I can't even adjust the game to my skill level. I am forced to deal with this BS whether I like it or not, and I'm simply tired of games that do not respect someone's time in such a way. I loved the OG Dark Souls for the fact it was always fair. It pretty much always felt like I died entirely due to my stupidity, not because the game tried to screw me over in every way imaginable. This is something entirely missing from Sekiro. I don't even feel satisfaction when I get through this BS in Sekiro. Doesn't help the fact that the controls have this weird lag to them, something I feel in From Software games ever since Dark Souls 2.

So if someone is looking for an overall experience more akin to the OG Dark Souls, this ain't it chief. If someone is looking for a lot of stuff to get annoyed at, you couldn't have found a better pick. Well, maybe outside of PvP lobbies in some games.

Posted: October 4
Let's get one thing out of the way first: This game isn't a souls-like, it's its own thing that borrows *some* aspects of FromSoft's other games. There's no co-op, no "builds", and your only variety in "weapons" comes from the different prosthetics you can unlock, which can serve offensive, defensive, or utility purposes. If you come into this expecting a souls-like, you're likely going to be just as disappointed as I was.

This game focuses on a very narrow set of skills, and it expects you refine them to a fairly high degree of mastery. You'll be performing more or less the same sets of moves for a majority of the game's run time, barring a few skills thrown in here and there for spice, and bosses don't generally do anything crazy that breaks the "rules" you learn fairly early on (bear in mind, at time of writing I still haven't seen the last three non-optional bosses of the normal route). Every boss very much has an "intended" way of being fought, although with sufficient skill and/or practice you can work other strategies as well. If that sounds appealing to you, ignore the red thumbs down and give it a try.

Each enemy very much has their own unique rhythm to them, and even the strongest opponents eventually becomes a matter of going through motions like clockwork, meaning the fights will play out more or less the same, depending on movepool rng. Personally, I just found that this meant that once I "mastered" a certain enemy, combat in that area became incredibly boring. This is eased somewhat by using stealth to avoid or eliminate enemies outside of combat, but many stronger enemies are purposefully placed in ways that make them difficult to stealth kill. This also becomes a problem with the many (mostly optional) mini-bosses you'll encounter across the maps, almost all of which are recycled at least once. Simply put: if the combat doesn't "click" with you, it's going to get repetitive and tedious very quickly.

Moving on to the areas themselves, I personally found them much less visually interesting than the usual FromSoft fare. There are some lovely views in the more mountainous areas, but nothing that really awed me or made me sit and stare for a while. The layout of the areas also feels much more obviously video game-y, especially with the way the grappling hook points are almost always lined up to perfectly direct you towards you current objective. The levels are much more open and vertical than other pre-Elden Ring FromSoft games, but it's marred by the subpar platforming. Disappointed as I am in this game, I'll not say one ill word about Sekiro's combat mechanics. It functions entirely as intended and has a good weight to it. The platforming though? Atrocious. Wolf's jumps are incredibly float and just feel bad. The "green" zone for grappling hooks also feels oddly inconsistent, especially for the ones that slingshot you instead of bringing you to a new platform, since it's possible to be on the "wrong side" of it without any way of knowing. Ultimately, though, if you're just going in to the game for combat alone, these are minor annoyances.

Finally, I'll touch on the bosses. The bosses are the part of this game that confuse me the most. Clearly, the main draw for this title is the combat, and the combat in this game works best when you're having an uninterrupted 1v1, so bosses should be where it shines the most, right? Yes and no. There are some bosses that definitely demonstrate a more intricate and compelling dance of combat, like Owl, Corrupted Monk, and Lady Butterfly, but there are others like Guardian Ape that suddenly turn away from the strengths of the combat. And you have to fight him twice, unless you do Corrupted Monk first. Moreover, some bosses (but moreso the mini-bosses) tend to have gimmicks that are countered by consumables. These range from just being convenient time-savers (using snap seeds to dispells Lady Butterflies illusions immediately) to significantly altering the difficulty of the fight (using divine confetti and the purple gourd against any Headless miniboss). Often times, these bosses appear before those consumables are easily accessible, which means its possible to fail to kill the boss in the first few tries and then be left at a disadvantagous in subsequent attempts. BUT, I will say that most of the unique interactions are either completely spelled out for you, or are at least obvious if you take the time to read what your consumables actually do.

I'll probably still finish the game, out of stubbornness if nothing else, but I definitely feel underwhelmed after all the massive hype I constantly hear about the game, so I'll leave that as the lesson for this review: regardless of if you agree with me or not or end up liking the game or not, don't just immediately buy into a game because it's well regarded and well liked. Do you research, try to dig into what people are actually saying, and make sure it's money well spent.

Posted: October 2
As someone who HATES souls games for having too much of an emphasis on rolling, I absolutely loved this game and would put it on my top ten games of all time.

My criticism of other From Software Souls games is that the bosses get to do all the cool moves while the player character just rolls around and attacks the dude's toe for like fifteen whole minutes. Sekiro has cool mobile anime bosses but gives the player ample counter-play options, which gives the game a really fast-paced, hectic feeling where you kill bosses fast but one wrong move kills you. The story is fun, the pacing is perfect, the levels don't overstay their welcome, and the combat is one of the most fleshed out systems I've ever played.

The only gripe I had about the game is this one boss that clearly takes inspiration from the other Souls games, because it's boring and a slog to get through. Fortunately, this boss can be skipped with a nice little jump.

how to make delicious Butter Chicken.
-----------------------------------------------------------
Ingredients for 4 servings,
2 lb boneless, skinless chicken breast (910 g), cubed
salt, to taste
pepper, to taste
2 teaspoons chili powder, divided
½ teaspoon turmeric
6 tablespoons butter, divided
1 ½ cups yellow onion (225 g), diced
3 teaspoons garam masala
1 teaspoon cumin
1 teaspoon cayenne pepper
1 tablespoon ginger, grated
3 cloves garlic, minced
1 cinnamon, 3 inch (8 cm) stick
14 oz tomato sauce (395 g)
1 cup water (240 mL)
1 cup heavy cream (240 mL)
rice, for serving
fresh cilantro, chopped for garnish.

Preparation
In a large bowl, season the chicken breast with salt, pepper, 1 teaspoon of chili powder, and the teaspoon of turmeric. Let
sit for 15 minutes to marinate.
Melt 2 tablespoons of butter in a large pot over medium heat. Brown the chicken, then remove from the pot.
Melt another 2 tablespoons of butter in the pot, then add the onion, garam masala, remaining teaspoon of chili powder,
the cumin, ginger, garlic, cayenne, cinnamon, salt and pepper. Cook until fragrant.
Add the tomato sauce and bring to a simmer.
Add the water and cream and return to a simmer.
Return the chicken to the pot, cover, and simmer for another 10-15 minutes.
Stir in the last 2 tablespoons of butter and season with more salt and pepper to taste.
Serve the chicken over rice and garnish with cilantro.
Enjoy!

This game is a large step from other fromsoftware games, which is perhaps the reason why it doesn't have many of the flaws I often see in other soulsborne games. The combat is very fluid and intuitive when you master it, the story is amazing but less convoluted then other soulsborne games. Encounters are all balanced to your progress at that point of the game, so you don't have to worry about going in to over/underleveled. The main complaint others often talk about is the difficulty. But while I agree that the game has a very steep learning curve, I also think that the difficulty is more fair than in other soulsborne games. Once you learn one aspect of the combat system you can apply it in all scenarios, so once you learn how to fight one boss you can transfer those skills to any fight (except one boss, but that is an optional fight). In the end it became, at least for me, the easiest and most intuitive soulsborne game. I have completed the gauntlet of strength: Mortal journey, and I still think it is easier and less bs than Promised consort Radahn and some other bosses form Elden Ring: Shadow of the Erdtree dlc. In conclusion, I think that Sekiro is the best balanced soulsborne game with the least flaws. For me it is one of the best games of all time and I would recommend it to anyone who is even in the slightest interested in action RPG's with a focus on combat.

First of all, the PC port of Sekiro has some regrettable choices: a 60 fps cap (despite not being tied to physics; there would be no downside to unlocked fps), no widescreen support, and HDR is forced on every boot despite being turned off both in Windows and in game.

Laughably, Xbox button prompts are defaulted to even when playing with keyboard and mouse and no controller is connected. It's clear little thought was put into the port.

Sekrio starts off promising. Basic gameplay seems manageable and interesting, perhaps even too easy, but it soon becomes clear that this game does not have a learning curve: it has a sheer learning cliff. You are expected to learn the game, exactly as it wants to be played, with no compromises, and that's that. It's a brutal process made worse by several factors:

* There are no other weapons to find, no spells to cast, and no alternative builds to experiment with. You have to play Sekiro one way and one way only. If you don't like the playstyle, too bad.

* Regular schmucks are laughably easy to dispatch. You can simply spam attack against them until their posture breaks and then death blow them for free. They offer no real training or incentive to get better at the mechanics.

* Miniboss enemies are your first real challenge but are several orders of magnitude tougher then basic enemies. They have huge health pools and must be death blowed multiple times, and they have attacks that come out so fast and with such variation that they need to be memorised. You'll die a lot to these, memorising patterns, but that leads to...

* The paths to miniboss enemies from checkpoints are filled with hordes of schmucks that need to be cleared every time you make an attempt. But fighting them yields no worthy practice or experience that would help you fight the minibosses; it's like being sent back to your room to do five minutes of chores on each death before you're allowed to continue learning.

It feels repetitive and boring and so you're likely to run past as many of them as you can avoid. But then again, minibosses are often surrounded by hordes you'll have to clear to fight the miniboss properly anyway.

* You are almost guaranteed to lose 1/2 of your resources on each death, with no way to recover lost currencies. Normally clearing basic enemies over and over would at least net you experience used for upgrades, but not here. Further, in what feels like an insult, dying too many times also infects friendly NPCs with a disease that locks you out of interacting with their quests.

* Finally, there's no difficulty setting. So there's no opportunity to tone down enemy damage to give yourself more "time in the lab" to get good and learn patterns, and then bump up the difficulty again later when you've improved. Often times you will get to a boss attempt on low health, die in one hit, and feel like you've learned nothing. Even on full health you'll die in two or three mistakes.

What's weird to me is that all of these feel like deliberate choices rather than mistakes. Choices that are justified because they make the game harder, and this is a FromSoft title, so any critiques can be covered with a reminder that the difficulty is part of the intended experience.

But at the end of the day, the process of getting good at Sekiro is simply frustrating and painful for me. And, at my age, I've got better things to do with my time. Or maybe it would be better to say that I've got more enjoyable things to do. So I'm calling it quits.

This game seems to be quite beloved by the community, but personally I would not recommend Sekiro, even on sale. If you want something similar but that is - in my opinion - actually fun to learn to play and get good at, I would instead recommend Lies of P or Stellar Blade.

Not every FromSoftware game is the same and this one isn't for me.

The biggest problem by far is how the fundamental idea of combat based engagement doesn't align well with the mechanics of the world.
Example A: 1v1 combat and stealth are the primary forms of engagement. Yet the environment makes it so most major 1v1 fights are accompanied by multiple enemies or the environments themselves are designed to only hinder the player, such as with General Kuranosuke Matsumoto and his four rifle men or Lone Shadow Swordsmen and a space so narrow my camera becomes the biggest threat.
Because of this, setting the stage for a 1v1 fight is a chore. Meanwhile stealth becomes an afterthought used to avoid combat or cheese 1 deathblow out of a mini-boss (which these opponents seriously do not need 2 of).

Example B: The tools and skills of which can be unlocked through exploration and killing mobs result in a lot of tools that have little to no pay out, specifically against bosses. This is because bosses tend to have the standard FromSoftware -High Mobility -One Hit Kills -Insane HP -Variable Moves, but now in addition to those they also gain -Insane Poise. This means you have to do a full boss fights worth of HP damage before any poise damage connects (which is how you deal deathblows), so moves like Mikiri Counter have zero effect while Tools like Loaded Spear do little to no damage. Which means Sekiro is more of an Intrinsic skill game and less of an Extrinsic skill game. Sen (money), Skills and Items are the only things a player can farm on mobs, and these will gain no favor over the battles that need to be fought. The only way to gain real Extrinsic power is by already having beat the enemies you originally needed the power for. Bosses give strength (damage) and Mini-bosses give Beads which become more HP.

So you have the usual FromSoftware cycle of live-die-repeat, except Hidetaka Miyazaki thought it would be really cute if the more you die the harder the game gets. Thanks to Dragon Aids, every time you die it gives way for NPC's to get sick. This causes any quests with an NPC to close, and it also causes Unseen Aid to lower. In Sekiro you can't recollect your dropped 'souls' on death, but you have a % chance to lose nothing on death with Unseen Aid, which is percentage based on current NPC's. The reasoning behind this Dragon Aid's spread is explained a bit in game but it doesn't make any sense why it would spread only to friendly NPC's but not any of the other endless enemies you engage with in the world. It's a very spiteful form of Aids I guess and an even ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ mechanic.

Ultimately, it feels like FromSoftware had an IDEA for a more technical based combat system in one of their worlds. Separate and far more linear than the mixed-pot form of their other games. However the cohesion between the standard FromSoftware world and the new combat system just isn't there.

Also, ♥♥♥♥ Snake Eyes Shirafuji. Infinite musket shots? wtf

A little hyped up by my love for other souls like games like Lies of P, Steelrising and Blasphemous, I bought Sekiro.

And now i stopped after around 8 hours of play time. Why?

Because the game doesn't respect my time. And that's not a problem with souls like games in general. It's specifically a problem with games from FromSoftware.

Example:

In an arena-like area, there is a mini-boss with two health bars. So you have to kill him twice to defeat him for good, which is difficult because you have very few healing options and the boss does a lot of damage (around 80% to 90% with some attacks).

But that's not my problem. With a bit of practice you can do it, and the game is even generous enough to let me take away the first health bar with a stealth attack from behind before the actual fight even begins. Challenging boss fights are one of the hallmarks of the genre after all, and I think that's okay.

My problem here is the large amount of other enemies in the area. They are not hard at all, but before i can attack the boss in a one-on-one duel, i have to kill those enemies first. That takes 3 to 5 minutes. And I have to do this EVERY TIME before I can fight this mini-boss one-on-one, because he is surrounded by a large number of these normal enemies, some of them with range weapons.

Eliminating these enemies is neither difficult nor does it really reward me. Sometimes i find money, but i lose it again when i get killed. And unlike other souls like games i cannot get it back by finding my corpse.

I think game mechanics like this are just rubbish!

It's not challenging at all, it's just pure harassment and a waste of my time. The enemies keep respawning and i have to kill them again and again before I can finally fight the mini boss individually after 3 to 5 minutes.

Unfortunately it's too late for a refund. To me this is just very poor game design, regardless of the high difficulty level of the boss fights. I can live with that, but not with repetitive sh*t between boss fight attempts.

Souls like games from other developers don't have this problem: Blasphemous, Lies of P and Steelrising are all challenging games with some really tough boss fights, but there challenging in a motivating way and don't just punish me mercilessly for every little mistake i make.

World design, atmosphere and the climbing and combat mechanics are good so far, but to me they're not worth the insane amount of wasted time where i have to do the same thing again and again until i "git gud!".

Stop me if you’ve heard this one before. I first played Sekiro a few years ago, fresh off Dark Souls 3, and didn’t like it much at all. I powered through the story, dodging and jumping my way through boss fights, grinding xp to unlock all the skills, and generally getting frustrated. After three hours of butting my head against the four-phase final boss, I put the controller down and decided it wasn’t for me.

Boy, oh boy how wrong I was. Through late 2024 and 2025 I’ve been slowly replaying a bunch of souls-likes, and after finishing Nioh 2 I decided this would be the next one on my list. I sat down, ready to be frustrated all over again … and fricking loved it.

As you’ve probably guessed, I finally understood just how different Sekiro is from most of From Software’s other efforts. Locking onto an enemy and circling around him is - apart from a few specific cases - going to get you killed. Dodging will get you killed. Running away will get you killed. As one of the NPCs tells you over and over again “hesitation is defeat”. To truly enjoy Sekiro you have to throw everything you’ve learnt in previous games out the window and charge at your opponent with your sword (or prosthetic hand) raised high, and a smile on your lips. If you manage to do that, you will be rewarded with not only one of the best souls-like that exists, but one of the very best video games that exists. Period.

Sekiro is exemplary in its simplicity. There are no weapons to upgrade. No armour. No stamina management. No magic. Less than a score of items to use, and most of those are for removing status effects or ephemeral stat boosts. You can increase your vitality, posture, and attack, but only by ten and twelve levels respectively. You can’t really grind here. You have to “git gud”

What’s wonderful about this, is that everything your learn from Sekiro’s combat will be useful right up until the final boss. Every enemy can be beaten with your sword. And there are only three different types of attacks : regular, thrust, and sweep. Within the first hour you learn how to deal with all of these : a thrust can be countered (once you purchase an early-game skill), a sweep can be jumped over, and regular attacks can either be blocked or deflected.

This is where my personal favourite thing about Sekiro comes into play. The posture bar. Every single enemy and boss has both a health bar and a posture bar. Any attack you deal with correctly (block, deflect, jump over, or counter) deals huge damage to their posture bar. As does every attack you hit them with (whether they are blocking or not). Fill it up completely and … bam! They’re either dead or onto the next phase.
The problem is the posture bar empties over time (just how rapidly it empties depends on their remaining health), so if you don’t press your attack, you’ll find yourself having to start all over again. I love this mechanic. It forces you to engage, much like Bloodborne’s health regen system, because if you don’t, you’ll lose all you’ve gained.

Once you’ve understood just how that works, boss encounters become a magnificent ballet of flashing swords. You attack, he parries, you deflect and attack again, he attempts a sweep attack, you jump, punish him, and attack again and again until he parries you once more. My two favourite fights in the game were against the same person (fought once in his prime and once at a later age), as he was constantly shaking up his moveset with lunges, jumps, and even shinobi tools like fireworks and shuriken.

Speaking of shinobi tools, your prosthetic arm allows you to find and upgrade tools of your own, such as the already-mentioned fireworks (incredibly useful against some of the harder human bosses) and shuriken (incredibly useful against wolves, monkeys and … butterflies), along with umbrellas, whistles, axes, and more besides. I’ll admit I tinkered around with these a bit but never over-relied on them. I found I didn’t need to.
You see, Sekiro is not a hard game. It’s challenging. It demands your attention. But it’s never unfair. Never punishing. And if you engage with its mechanics correctly, I’d argue it’s easier than both Nioh and Lies of P.
In fact, some of the bosses that gave me the most trouble were those who fought with tooth and claw rather than blade and spear. Their more unpredictable move sets and high posture bars meant I had to fall back into by Dark Souls muscle memory of dodging rather than parrying. Definitely not as enjoyable.

Not since Bloodborne have I had so much fun getting “in the zone”, where your hands become an reflexive extension of you mind and all you can focus on is getting that perfect parry. If you’re looking for a souls-like that rewards perseverance with some absolutely fantastic boss fights, this is the one to get.

As a bonus, here’s my list of bosses, from easiest to hardest (spoilers abound of course). I played through the game twice, once to get the “Shura” ending, then again for the “Dragon” ending. The endings are mutually exclusive, and the final encounters very different.

10. True Corrupted Monk
9. Genichiro Ashina
8. Great Shinobi Owl
7. Headless Ape
6. Lady Butterfly
5. Demon of Hatred
4. Guardian Ape
3. Isshin, The Sword Saint
2. Emma the Gentle Blade & Isshin Ashina

Posted: September 23
I am a 46 year old single father, probably one of the oldest people playing this game. My son, who is 15 now, begged me to buy Sekiro because he said it was “hard but fair.” I bought it for him, thinking it would be a good bonding activity.

For weeks, I watched him try and fail against the same boss. He would throw the controller down, swear under his breath, and look at me with frustration. I felt useless. I could not help him. I did not understand the game. I was just the dad in the background.

One night after he went to bed, I picked up the controller. The world felt heavy, quiet, lonely. I fought, and died, and fought again. Something inside me clicked. The rhythm, the patience, the precision. Hours turned into dawn. I finally defeated the boss that had beaten my son down for days.

When he woke up, I showed him. He did not say much. He just looked at me differently, not as the old man who does not get games, but as someone who fought alongside him. We sat together, and for the first time in a long time, we were not just father and son. We were warriors.

Thank you Sekiro for giving me back a connection I thought I had lost.

Fromsoft games have a pretty distinct formula that they've perfected. Sekiro is the alternative take on their formula, sort of like a remix.

The best part about this game is that there is basically only one answer for when you get stuck - you have to get good. It's not like you can go run off in another direction for a whole new area, the game is generally pretty linear and the answer is that you have to just figure it out. You can't summon help, you can't grind your stats up, you have one weapon which has a linear progression path dictated by how far you are, the only thing you can do is figure out how to beat the challenge in front of you.

Sekiro ended up being my favorite Fromsoft game and it stands to this day. I really loved my time with it, including all of the ragequits, because in the end I did end up beating the last boss and I did get that huge dopamine dump. It was good.

A single edge honed into rhythm

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is an action game carved to one purpose. Where others sprawl into builds and loot, it narrows into intent - timing, distance, nerve. The posture system makes rhythm the real health bar: deflections turn pressure into momentum, deathblows punctuate a phrase well spoken, and resurrection ties risk to restraint without cheapening failure. Difficulty is demanding yet legible; animations, audio cues, and clean hit logic show exactly how to improve, turning repetition into fluency rather than grind.

Traversal and structure
The grappling hook invites vertical routes and quick flanks; levels unfold like layered screens - focused, readable, and rich with purposeful options. Systems stay spare: no stat labyrinth to hide behind, only prosthetic tools and arts that widen expression without diluting clarity.

Art and sound
Art and sound seal it - steel, pine, winter sky; flutes sliding into drums - an atmosphere that braids folkloric hush with poised aggression.

Why I call it FromSoftware’s best since Kuon and Bloodborne
Because it unites their strengths into coherence - Kuon’s quiet, haunted texture and Bloodborne’s forward-leaning courage - refined into rhythm and form. Everything here - the mechanics, encounters, geometry, music, and story - pulls toward one idea: mastery as joy. In a medium crowded with checklists, Sekiro gives you one clear stroke to make with your sword.

it’s a brutal but rewarding test of patience, reflexes, and determination. FromSoftware took everything they’re known for—tight combat, atmospheric worldbuilding, and unforgiving difficulty—and crafted something unique that stands apart from the Souls and Bloodborne series.

The combat system is the heart of Sekiro. Instead of stamina management, it emphasizes precision, posture breaking, and perfectly timed deflections. Every duel feels like a high-stakes sword fight where a single mistake can cost you everything, but a flawless sequence makes you feel unstoppable. The boss fights are some of the most memorable and cinematic in gaming, equal parts terrifying and exhilarating.

The setting is breathtaking: a dark, reimagined Sengoku-era Japan. The vertical movement with the grappling hook adds freedom and fluidity to exploration, while stealth mechanics offer multiple ways to approach enemies. Every corner of the world feels alive, dangerous, and meticulously crafted.

The GOTY Edition includes bonus content like boss challenge modes and extra unlockables, making it an even better package for newcomers and veterans alike.

That said, Sekiro is not for everyone. The difficulty can be punishing, with no option to “grind” your way out. It demands mastery and persistence. But if you embrace the challenge, the sense of accomplishment is unlike any other game.

After finishing the base game of Lies of P, I decided to give Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice a try—mainly because of the parry mechanics. In Lies of P, I managed to break Laxasia’s sword using parries alone, which gave me the confidence to tackle Sekiro, a game often referred to as a "parry simulator."

I was curious to see if the skills I developed in Lies of P would transfer over. However, I quickly noticed that the parry mechanics in the two games feel quite different. Lies of P seems to demand more precise timing for a "perfect" parry, whereas Sekiro offers a slightly more forgiving window. That said, what makes Sekiro truly satisfying is how central parrying is to its combat—you’re encouraged to use it constantly, far more than in Lies of P.

In the end, Sekiro did not disappoint. It might just be my favorite FromSoftware title—and possibly my favorite game of all time.

FromSoftware didn't iterate on the action genre here; they rewrote its DNA. The parry system is revolutionary, demanding precision, rhythm, and nerve. Its influence is so profound, other studios have borrowed from it—not out of necessity, but out of reverence—cementing it as the blueprint for those who come after. This is the most exhilarating action game experience I've had. Every encounter is a duel; every boss a test of your resolve. And (without spoilers) just when you think you've conquered one of the game's most infamous foes, it gets back up—reminding you that in Sekiro, victory is never handed to you. It's earned.

The story is cryptic and deeply emotional. From the first cutscene, you're hooked—not by exposition but by the atmosphere and mystery surrounding the war-torn region of Ashina. Yes, it's tough, but if you can break through the soulslike difficulty curve, you'll find one of the most rewarding experiences gaming has to offer. And be sure to remember,

Sekiro is one of my favorite games that FromSoftware has made. I just want to say that the game is very difficult and requires quite a bit of time to acclimate to the timing of different enemy attacks and the counter system in general, since each enemy is approached differently. There is no easy mode for this game and no native accessibility options for those who are looking to play regardless of ability.

The combat and movement feel incredible all the way through. The story, as it unfolds, is interesting and completely enveloped in Japanese folklore. The fantasy aspect of the game is beautifully executed with the different environments, bosses, and enemies. The sound design is just as stellar, which is what you'd expect given FromSoftware's track record for soundtracks.

My favorite thing about this game is that what you learn from one fight carries over and remains relevant to other bosses and enemies. As the experience compounds, everything feels very rewarding when you counter or use techniques as you go from fight to fight. I don't believe anyone playing this for the first time will be able to find everything the game has to offer. There are a lot of things you can miss if you're not following a guide, and replaying the game on New Game+ gives you the ability to see the three different endings, additional lore, optional bosses, and more.

Hope you enjoy this game as much as I did! Cheers!

The combat system in Sekiro is the best combat system I've ever had the pleasure of engaging in. I enjoy the combat in FromSoftware's other titles, such as Dark Souls 3 and Elden Ring, but Sekiro's rhythmic parry-based combat stands above them all. As punishing as this game is, it is also rewarding.

The final boss of the game is my favorite final boss in all video games I've played — he is there to test literally everything you've learned along the way of playing the game. Despite the relatively short game, the vast majority of the bosses to be fought are crafted masterfully. The first boss will test your grasp of the parry timing, the second will test your ability to play aggressively, and the third will test you on both. There are only a few bosses in this game I don't like fighting.

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice – A Masterclass in Combat (With Minor Flaws) – 9.7/10

The Best Combat System FromSoft Ever Made
Sekiro’s combat is a masterpiece. It rewards aggression and precision, turning every fight into a rhythmic dance of clashing steel. The perfect deflect mechanic isn’t just satisfying, it adds realism, as enemies actively defend instead of mindlessly swinging. Posture management forces you to engage fiercely, making battles feel like tactical duels rather than stat checks. Most fights are cinematic without relying on spectacle (looking at you, Elden Ring AOEs and arena-wide acrobatics). Bosses want to fight you, pressing the offense rather than running away. Movesets are brilliantly designed, and while some assets are reused, it makes sense lore-wise (e.g. shared martial arts styles). The only missteps are Souls-style bosses (bull or giant), where dodging replaces deflection -> these clashes with Sekiro’s core design.

World Design: Small but Dense
Typical FromSoftware excellence. Levels are intricately crafted, with shortcuts and verticality reminiscent of Dark Souls 1. The world feels diverse despite its compact size, thanks to distinct biomes. Enemy placement is deliberate, and the pacing between mini-bosses and bosses is stellar. The only downside is linearity, while you can somewhat sequence-break, it’s minimal. Still, exploration is rewarding, even if loot often feels underwhelming ("Ceramic Shard x5").

Story & NPCs: Solid, Not Spectacular
The plot is serviceable (a revenge tale with mythical twists), but the NPCs shine. Their backstories and side quests are layered, uncovering deeper lore through dialogue. No "amazing" writing, but definitely a solid one.

Difficulty & Replayability: A Double-Edged Katana
The learning curve is punishing but fair, once the combat "clicks," the game becomes exhilaratingly smooth. That said, mastery cuts both ways: subsequent playthroughs can feel too short / are not challenging (~5 hours if you’re skilled). No RPG builds limit replay value, but it’s a worthy trade-off for Sekiro’s razor-sharp focus. A hardcore DLC would’ve been perfect to test endgame skills (please!).

Performance: OK
No framerate drops, no major bugs. FromSoft isn’t known for polish, but Sekiro runs like a dream.

Here's my review and take on Sekiro

Gameplay & Mechanics
The combat system in the game is like a lot of RPGs, you slash and deflect attacks. You can engage onto enemies by either sneaking up on them or going head on against them, although it is a bit difficult whenever there are multiple enemies coming from all directions. It is a matter of knowing attack patterns and expecting when they will land to time the deflect perfectly.

The difficulty is really great, the game starts off pretty difficult due the lack of prosthetics tools/buff items you have at you disposal which they can be beneficial or necessary to beat certain bosses/mini bosses. You can vary the difficulty of the game by giving Kuro his "Kuro's Charm" (Added chip damage for when you block attacks, forcing you to adapt into deflecting attacks more often, increased enemy vitality and posture, increased vitality and damage taken and status effects build up roughly 40% faster. But with an added XP boost for skill points and increased gain of sen, the in game currency) back to him and/or utilize the "Demon Bell" (Pretty much the same deal as giving Kuro's charm back with an added increase of loot drop chance) . Note that not having Kuro's Charm and having the Demon Bell, the disadvantages will stack. The difficulty cannot be changes unless you start a new playthrough with a new save or NG+ (Except for the Demon Bell).

The more you play, the stronger you get. Pretty much the spiel of majority of RPG games; you get more health, more posture, multiple resurrections, increased attack power, more prosthetic tools/items are your disposal, more skills/combat arts and more healing items.

The visuals and graphics of this game are . The environment and the combat arts VFX are so well made.

The UI is minimalistic, showing your health bar, how many resurrections you have, your current prosthetic tool, current quick item, how many Spirit Emblems you have, enemy vitality and posture, boss/mini boss vitality, posture and how many phases they have.

The game fairly long, taking 20-30 hours on their first playthrough or 50-100 hours just to 100% the game by unlocking every single achievement this game has to offer.

The story is amazing, the pacing is very well done. Nothing much more to say about it there.
There are 4 endings present in the game.

The side quests are available in the game, most of them are necessary to earn certain achievements and others are pretty much useless. They usually reward you some useful goodies or story related items. You don't need to do them all for 100% achievements. But if you want to do them just for the thrill of it, go for it!

For me it was kind of annoying needing to find all of the prayer beads and gourd seeds to max out vitality/posture and my healing gourd but oh well.

The combat is really fun and with the added combinations you can do with prosthetic tools really makes it more fun.

A frustrating experience

After hearing so much about Sekiro, I went in, expecting a challenging but rewarding experience. What I got instead was a frustrating, punishing game that feels more like an exercise in patience than actual fun.

The biggest issue for me is how unbalanced the game feels. Most bosses can kill you with just one hit(and that's not an exaggeration), and they hit you even when you are far. It doesn't matter how much you try to improve or upgrade, just one small mistake and you are back at the last checkpoint. There' no real sense of reward after beating a boss.

The gameplay revolves around parrying and if you don't get the timing right, well... go to checkpoint. There's little room for creativity or different playstyles

While the game does have stunning visuals an a nice story, they are overshadow by random camera movements(in a fight, specially in thigh spaces) and controls that often works against you! If you were expecting something like or you gonna be disappointed

I tried playing Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice when it first launched on PS4. After a few frustrating hours, I gave up and told myself, “Maybe these Soulslike games just aren’t for me anymore.” And to be fair, I’d already beaten Demon’s Souls, Dark Souls 1–3, Bloodborne, and a handful of imitators. But I bounced off Nioh and Sekiro hard. I figured I was done with the genre.

Fast forward a few years—I’m playing on the Steam Deck, and Sekiro runs beautifully. So I figured, “Welp, let’s see if I still got it.” And wow… this game is good.

Yes, it’s tough like any Soulslike, but it’s not really about your gear. It’s about mastering the mechanics, learning enemy patterns, and pulling off those parries like a boss. Once it clicks, it clicks and man, it’s satisfying. I swear every time I nailed a boss fight, I had to trim the new chest hair that grew from every successful parry. It’s that kind of game.

It’s not easy, but it’s fair. If you gave up on it like I did, maybe give it another shot. Especially on Steam Deck—it plays like a dream.

Sekiro Isn't your traditional souls game and that's okay. Its a new take on the Genre from the people who do the Genre best. While the aesthetic isn't for me its beautifully done. The sense of being confined and alone against the word is very well conveyed at any moment your on top of a mountain even though i jut jumped down to the bottom of a ravine. The world is masterfully crafted and amazing to be in. Combat is where i struggle to love this game. at many times i found myself fighting enemies that felt like they don't belong in this game and belong in a dark souls game or Elden ring. I at first despised the parry mechanic and opted to dodge more then parry like i would playing any other souls like. But when fighting certain enemies and bosses its easily the most rewarding combat system i have engaged with in any game. Genichiro Ashina, Gyoubu Mastaka Oniwa, Headless Ape, True corrupted monk, Great Shinobi Owl, and Divine Dragon all engage with the games combat really well and beating them felt genuinely rewarding. My gripes with the combat system come from its use of unblockable attacks and the Shinobi tool. Lots of times it feels very hard to predict weather or not an unblockable attack is a thrust or sweep attack. The Mikiri counter might be one of my favorite mechanics in a game and I'm really sad for a lot of the bosses and enemies even the ones i felt engaged with the combat system really well had attacks that made using it feel like a coin flip of a thrust or sweep attack or even an attack that was neither of these but felt like it should have been affected by Mikiri counter. The Shinobi tool might be the biggest let down. it felt like it could have been a tool that lead to diverse build crafting in the game but instead felt pidgin hold into picking Shinobi tools based on bosses weaknesses instead of to fit my play style. The Shinobi tool can only be used if you have spirit emblems witch is a resource that you have an extremely finite amount between bonfires and only drop from enemies or buy being purchased. Which leads to having to grind for Spirit Emblems instead of taking more attempts at a boss im stuck on. A fun side note the games combat is easily at its worse when being forced to fight multiple enemies which is my main grip with the Scholar of the first sin addition of Dark souls 2 which its main change from the base game is the increase of enemies in areas through out the game which takes away from my favorite part of the orginal game which was learning how to manipulate NPC enemies to make fighting through sections easier which is an area i feel Sekiro also falls short in. I decided to call my play through quits on the final boss. i had squeezed all the fun i could get at the game and the final boss was making me more and more frustrated. I'm willing to admit im defiantly below the average skill level for this game. I give this game an honest 8/10 i am not the target audience for this game but the parts i liked i really liked and would recommend this game to others. Maybe later on i could see myself doing a play through while following a walk through but for now I'm happy with what i got out of this game

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice isn’t just a game, it’s a full-blown test of character. You don’t play Sekiro, you survive it.
You’re a shinobi with one arm and a whole lot of unfinished business. The swordplay? Sharp as a razor. The timing? Tighter than a drum. Every fight feels like a showdown in a back alley—no shortcuts, no mercy. You mess up, you go down. You learn, you get back up. That’s the rhythm.
The bosses? Forget it. They’re not enemies—they’re life lessons wrapped in armor and bad intentions. You study them like you’re cramming for finals, and when you finally win, it feels like you just climbed Mount Everest in flip-flops.
Visually, it’s a masterpiece. Japan never looked so haunting and beautiful. But don’t get distracted—this world is out to teach you discipline, patience, and how to keep your cool when everything’s falling apart.
Bottom line: Sekiro is tough, but fair. It’ll break you down and build you back up. If you’re ready to earn every victory, this is the one.

There are many things I love about Sekiro, but that does not mean I don't think it has any flaws. My simple review is that it is a very good game that is sure to provide a challenge.

Now my complicated review, starting with the things I find good about the game.
I thoroughly enjoyed the combat because there are many aspects that you can improve in as you get better at the game making you feel like a better player rather than making it feel like you are just getting better equipment. Even after 100% the game there are still aspect of combat I could get better at, .
Most games will have a few bosses that are just not fun to fight, either because they have a stupid gimmick or they are just poorly designed. For the most part I like the bosses in Sekiro. Most of them felt fair and reasonable, and if that was not the case the boss could be avoided.
For those who care about dialog, I think the game kept the amount of dialog in check and it doesn't feel overwhelming on the first play through. It gives you the information you need and then sends you on your way, and for people who like more dialog to build out characters, the game allows players to get extra dialog .
My final compliment is that Sekiro has a fair amount of exploration because there will be side paths that can be taken that normally lead to dead ends. Exploring is encouraged by the game because, while it does hide easy to get consumables, it also will occasionally grant the player more valuable items on these side paths. That's what really encouraged me to explore because finding something important always felt rewarding.

Now for the bad.
I mentioned that I liked most bosses, well there are a few that I think make the game worse by just being in the game.
Other than that my only criticism only applies to those trying to 100% the game. If your don't mind grinding out xp that much, it won't be terrible, but trying to get all skills isn't very fun especially because after getting a few NG+ in, bosses stop giving enough xp to get you a level, meaning you have to kill enemies in mass to get levels.

My criticisms do not outmatch my praise and while I have enjoyed other games more, Sekior is truly one of the best single player games I will likely ever play. I will come back to challenge run the game and play more once I have 1,000 hours in Silksong.

Posted: August 23
After 70 hours of playtime, I have just rolled credits on this game with every boss and mini-boss defeated.

I've bounced off every single Souls game, Bloodborne, and Elden Ring. There was always something not quite right about the controls and the flow of combat. Everything felt a little too slippery, a little too spongey, and not quite as reactive as I would like.

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice suffers from none of these flaws. The player character feels amazing to control, and the combat is so tuned, and so reactive that I suspect it will difficult for me to enjoy another action game for quite a while.

Something I see frequently in the discussion surrounding this game is that you can't level up, and there isn't any loot that makes you more powerful. This simply is not true, and I'm not sure why it comes up so often. There are multiple skill trees. You can increase your vitality, posture, and attack power. There are a ton different secondary weapons—some of them with very powerful effects that can make a huge difference if they are used in the correct situation.

However, the sentiment behind this statement IS correct. There is no skill, no upgrade, and no amount of grinding for XP, that will on a basic level allow you to complete this game without understanding the combat system. The skills and upgrades can greatly increase your effectiveness in combat and your survivability, but even with everything upgraded to the maximum level, even non-boss enemies will easily overwhelm you unless you are using those upgrades skillfully.

Performance was excellent, although I would expect that from a 6 year old game running on a 3070 and 5900x.

I really would have liked to play this game in 21:9, but it looks great regardless at the non-ultrawide resolution. The HDR implementation is very good.

The score is good. It sets the tone well, and the audio mix doesn't prevent you from hearing important sounds in boss battles.

The story is substantially less obtuse that the standard FromSoftware lore soup that usually pervades their games. You won't have a to watch a 40 minute YouTube video to understand what happened. I didn't play this game for the story, but I thought it was effective for driving the gameplay.

The Japanese voice acting is great, bordering on exceptional.

Overall, if you like slick movement, fast combat, and the rush of mastering a difficult challenge, then I would absolutely recommend giving this game a try. It's very unlike FromSoftware's other games, and for me that made all the difference.

Posted: August 21
Like any FromSoft game, Sekiro takes aspects of past games and adapt them to fit the whole Japanese setting. It is clear what they did to the way the map is interconnected... it screams Dark Souls 1 and it works so well. It really hits the same way when you find a crazy shortcut and dives into exploration.
Few places in other FromSoft games are comparable to the stunning visuals seen here. For those games you need to think and pick specific places that you think are pretty, whereas this game is always pretty. FromSoftware snow levels, as seen in Painted World of Ariamis (DS1), Frozen Eleum Loyce (DS2), Cainhurst (BB) and Ashes of Ariendel (DS3). All of them greatly contributed to what we get to see here, which also helped shape what we later see on Consecrated Snowfield (ER). . You get the idea. Lots of inspirations thrown everywhere. The Demon Bell is literally the Company of Champions covenant from DS2. The Armored Warrior fight is kinda like Taurus Demon........ and it goes on and on.

The start of the game reminded me of my first time playing bloodborne, and how much agressively you had to play to succeed. This isn't the type of game to hit and back off until you get an opportunity to hit again. You'll get punished for doing that. Like Isshin himself says in the game: "Hesitation is Defeat". You need to forget the Dark Souls mentality and fight with no hesitation. It'll be frustrating as ♥♥♥♥ at first but when you learn everything you'll feel like a god. The combat and overall movement mechanics are amazing. Everything flows nicely.

After beating it once, I'm now going for the remaining 3 endings and still finding new stuff while I play through the same sections that I've played before. Replayability is key.

I do think there's some crazy ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ moments in this game but for the most part it's amazing. You'll need to read the wiki btw. Ain't no way you're doing it all by yourself.

I love dark souls, and I've logged over 500 hours on that game. This is not Dark Souls. Dark souls has very tight mechanics and gives you several ways to play the game to suit your style. This game has clunky mechanics and only allows you to play in one style which the game dictates to you. If you don't like that style, you're out of luck. No weapon upgrades, no different types of shields, no different types of weapons to suit play style. Also, unlike Dark Souls which has amazing fantasy environments, this game has mundane Japanese buildings. No sense of grandeur or mystery.

One important thing: whereas in Dark Souls I always felt I was getting better, here it doesn't seem to matter what I do, I don't seem to get any better. In the end, I wind up avoiding most enemies since even simple grunts take a huge amount of life away from you in order to kill. Coupled with almost no healing, this game just isn't fun.

Pretty big miss from From Software. I'm not sure how most people seem to like this, but maybe it's just me. Oh, well.

A Beautifully Crafted Challenge Worth Admiring

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is a polished and breath-taking experience. Even though I’m not the biggest fan of the genre, I can’t help but appreciate the passion and craftsmanship poured into this game. The environments feel alive, the enemies are immersive, and the overall design shows just how much care the developers put into every detail.

My confession: I couldn’t fully complete the game. I dropped it after my battle with the first real boss,, simply because I wasn’t willing to dedicate the time and effort required to master its demanding combat. That said, the hours I spent with Sekiro were still memorable, and I genuinely value the experience.

Just because I didn’t finish it (for now) doesn’t mean I can’t recommend it. This is a game that deserves admiration. If you’re up for the challenge, Sekiro will reward you with one of the most beautifully crafted adventures in gaming.

Updated: Post 100% achievement

Review - Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice
My 1st title, gave it a try after hearing it had fast combat rather than slow endurance fights. Didn't really care that much about character customization or plot at all; my expectation buying this game was pure gameplay.

Aside from a few unpleasant bosses, the core gameplay feels quite fun. Posture-based fight allows very fast combat if you manage to counter all attacks perfectly, much like a perfect combo in rhythm games.

For this reason, I don't think core gameplay will be enjoyable for people who don't like fast reaction & practice-based games. Like rhythm games, it has a relatively small input control but requires high timing accuracy and precision to pass through the game. Hard & requires practice to pull off perfect combos, very punishing on combo-break, but very rewarding on a successful attempt.

From non-gameplay standpoint, Sekiro graphics quite nice, suit thematically, and reasonably optimized. Runs at 60fps using 4800H + Mobile 1660ti. Aside from the stupid motion blur which can be disabled, no complaint at all about graphics & performance. Didn't find any bug during entire NG+5 playthrough. Probably outlier for AAA game which usually riddled with bugs and unnecessary bloat.




However, I find other aspects of the game to be quite lacking. I don't feel there's much thought put into the game design aspect. Maybe the devs decided to go with "standard AAA recipe", making the game "safely meet" the average AAA game expectation, but it falls flat if you expect more than that.











Anyway, I guess I can still tolerate the downside and think of it as a chorework need to be done. Played the entire game mostly through full-posture fight w/ demon bell, and it was a pretty fun experience (thank god the final boss doesn't leave bad taste on my mouth, unlike the final optional boss). Maybe a bit overrated and a bit overpriced only for the core gameplay. I expect a "GOTY-worthy title" and premium price tag to be way better than the average AAA cash grab in game design aspect, but oh well, maybe I'm barking up the wrong tree.

Ugh, what a pain "all skills" achievement. Had to do NG+5 w/ charmless bell to get it---better than rotting myself on antechamber xp farming.

There's no big changes about my review & gameplay experience, but here's some extra points:





Maybe NG+7 charmless bell (yeah I push NG+5 a bit because why not) is not enough to experience Sekiro at "GOTY" level. Oh well, at least getting 100% is not that bad.

What can I even say, that hasn't been said about this game already? It's a masterpiece, period.

From the absolutely perfect gameplay design and mechanics, to the astoundingly beautiful world and engaging story, this is an instant classic and a game everyone should play once in their life. Much has been said about its difficulty, and yeah, it's a hard game to master with no difficulty settings, but like all games in the From Software Souls-like family, that difficulty is actually rather dynamic. When you use all the mechanics on offer the way they are intended, you get a lot of help along the way. More damage given, less damage taken, and more items and experience to make you stronger faster. However, *unlike* all other Souls-like games, the core combat mechanic is indeed much less susceptible to "breaking". There's no magic system to fall back on in order to keep distance or abuse weaknesses, and no shield to hide behind to wait for the perfect opening. No, the true test of mastery in this game is your ability to learn the combat rhythm itself, to internalize the instinctive choice between deflecting incoming attacks and hammering back with attacks of your own.

Finishing this game was, absolutely, very difficult and took a lot of effort, but with it being so perfectly designed to teach you the fundamentals and train you in becoming better all the time, it doesn't *feel* hard. It just ends up feeling like something you must do, and will do, the only question is how long it will take. And as soon as you throw down the controller in frustration, vowing to abandon the game forever... that sneaking desire to have *just one more go* at the boss appears.

This is one of the best games of all time, easily.
