For a shinobi, the player character handles like a WW2 tank. Mechanics are obtuse and poorly explained, opting to tell you in text boxes instead of showing you. The multiple mechanics that punish failure on repeat attempts are unfathomably stupid and backwards considering that trial and error and persistence is a fundamental feature of every Fromsoft game, including this one. Core mechanics are entirely optional and missable. Plus little things like ledges can’t be dodged off of, loot climb and ledge hang are tied to the same button, run and dodge are tied to the same button, all of these things lead to little moments of jankey unintended movement that feel bad and cause frustration.

Genichiro, Owl, and Ishin were great fights, but those moments of properly balanced progression and execution were just too few and far between. There is an art to giving a player the experience of being on the razor’s edge of success and failure, and Sekiro is all over the place. Looking back, more than half of the experience sucked and was absolutely not enjoyable.

This game requires perfection and insults you after injury if you ever fumble. I finished it out of defiance.

Sekiro is one of my favorite Souls-like games. It plays a bit differently than Dark Souls or Elden Ring, but you can still clearly feel the quality we’ve come to expect from FromSoft. I can only recommend it to everyone. While the slightly different gameplay may feel unusual for Dark Souls fans at first, once it “clicks,” you simply can’t put it down. It’s no coincidence that the game became Game of the Year.

Achievements
Required time: 35-50 hours
Difficulty (in my opinion): 8 out of 10
No

As for the achievements, the biggest challenge is probably getting all the endings. You’ll need almost three full playthroughs – or even four, depending on how you approach it. There’s a lot to keep in mind, and it can be somewhat convoluted at times. For that, I once again recommend the excellent guides by Cynic 0055. Beyond that, all tools need to be fully upgraded and every skill acquired, so I’d also suggest using a guide to avoid missing anything.

If you thought Sekiro was punishing before, wait until you meet the Gauntlets of Strength.

These boss rush challenges don’t just test your mastery of the combat system — they demand perfection. Every deflect, every dodge, every moment of hesitation is magnified when you face back-to-back encounters with no safety net. Completing all of them felt like climbing a mountain made of steel and fire.

What I loved most is how each Gauntlet pushed me in a different way:

Gauntlet of Strength (Divine Heir) sharpened my fundamentals: posture management, aggression, and timing.

Gauntlet of Strength (Father) forced me to unlearn bad habits and embrace precision over panic.

Gauntlet of Strength (Shura) was pure endurance, an iron trial of consistency after fatigue sets in.

Finishing them all wasn’t just about beating bosses. It was about learning to flow with the combat system, to embrace the rhythm until I wasn’t reacting anymore, only executing. The sense of growth from my first failed attempts to that final, clean victory is unlike anything I’ve felt in an action game.

The game is already one of the greatest action titles ever made. The Gauntlets make it eternal.

If you complete all three, you’ll never look at boss fights in any other game the same way again.

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is pure, sharpened brilliance. The combat is fast, aggressive, and unforgiving—every fight feels like a deadly dance where precision matters more than raw power. Breaking an enemy’s posture after a tense exchange is exhilarating in a way few games ever achieve.

The story is subtle but powerful, exploring loyalty, sacrifice, and the weight of duty. Wolf’s bond with Kuro pulls you through a world that’s as beautiful as it is brutal, filled with memorable characters who feel like real parts of its history.

The boss fights? At first, I hated them. Then I learned them. Then I loved them. Each one is a masterclass in design, testing your skill and nerve while delivering unforgettable moments of triumph.

From the stunning Sengoku-era landscapes to the pulse-pounding soundtrack, every element works together to create something truly special. It’s not an easy game—but every victory feels earned.

If you’re willing to bleed for it, Sekiro will reward you with one of the most satisfying experiences in gaming.

Sekiro: Shadows Die (more than) Twice

Welcome to Sekiro, where you're not just a shinobi you're a one-armed legend with more parries than your average fencing tournament. you start the game thinking you're a silent, deadly warrior of the shadows. Ten minutes later, you're screaming at a giant rooster.

Combat? it's not hack-n-slash. It’s rhythm-based sword ballet of death. Parry like your life depends on it because it absolutely does. Miss one block? Boom Back to the Sculptor’s Idol with your dignity shattered and your posture gauge in ruins.

Then there's the bosses each one more terrifying than your CA Intermediate exams. Demon of Hatred? More like Demon of Existential Crisis. Isshin, the Sword Saint? He doesn’t fight you he teaches you pain in three parts.
By the time you're done, you've transcended. You're not just a ninja. You're a shinobi warrior monk god of parries and PTSD.

10/10 would die to a random sword guy again.

Posted: August 6
All in all, I enjoy Sekiro more than I dislike it, but there are certainly plenty of things to love and plenty to hate.

What I like the most is the slow-paced exploration typical of Souls games. Death lurks around every corner, but step by step, you inch your way through the world. This goes hand-in-hand with a rewarding sense of progression. Returning to previous areas feels like a walk in the park. In the starting areas, you move like a knife through butter—nothing can stop you. However, the downside is that there's not much palpable reward for this. Sekiro features just one weapon and a very limited number of prosthetic tools. As a result, exploration rewards are mostly expendable items—and optional bosses.

Many optional bosses are mini-bosses, typically easier than the main ones, but they might still give you a nice bonus, like prayer beads. If they don't, they feel more like a waste of time. One notable example is the Headless boss. I spent an hour or more fighting it before giving up and turning to online guides. How was I supposed to know that confetti was so effective against him? The worst part is that the reward for killing him is just experience. There are three more Headless bosses in the game, and I didn’t even bother trying to fight them, knowing full well how little you gain in return.

Another advantage of optional bosses is that they are, well... optional. You can skip them if they're too challenging at the moment. For me, the prime example was an optional boss in Hirata Estate that guards the Mist Raven prosthetic tool. I gave up after half an hour of unsuccessful attempts when I first encountered him. Later, after defeating another miniboss with a similar moveset, I returned and took him down on my first attempt.

This example could be attributed to learning the exact moveset before going back, but the same strategy can be fruitful even with unique bosses. Take Lady Butterfly, for example. I'd heard she was quite tough, so I attempted to fight her just once, failed miserably, and left the Hirata Estate. I returned right before facing Genichiro Ashina, as preparation for that fight, and surprisingly, I beat her on my first try. I'm not entirely sure of the reason for this. Maybe it was the number of healing gourds I acquired or a skill I'd learned. I'd like to think it was my own improvement as a player, but the most valuable combat lesson was yet to come.

I'm not a seasoned Souls player—I've only played Dark Souls 1 and Dark Souls 3—but even so, I was accustomed to the typical Souls approach: patience, waiting for openings, and dodging enemy attacks. Sekiro requires a different mindset altogether—you need to parry actively and be aggressive; otherwise, the enemy’s posture bar will quickly reset. Genichiro Ashina is a perfect example of how taking the initiative makes all the difference. Many of his attacks can be interrupted by attacking him first, making it particularly effective to corner him.

Another great example is the Guardian Ape. Seeing him for the first time, I was frozen in fear and immediately tried to run away, only to be swiftly killed. Switching to a more aggressive approach allowed me to defeat the boss relatively quickly. But there's another lesson Sekiro teaches, often unexpectedly—you can parry practically anything, not just weapon swings. I learned this the hard way when fighting the bull boss. Initially, I futilely tried dodging its attacks. Only after checking online did I realize deflection was possible, and then victory came swiftly on my very next attempt.

Another big difference compared to other Souls games is stealth. While stealth is possible in other games, it usually doesn't play a major role. Granted, stealth in Sekiro isn't especially versatile, but I'm also not a stealth enthusiast myself. I tend to use stealth whenever possible but have never really been drawn to games heavily focused on it. In Sekiro, stealth is highly effective for thinning enemy ranks and avoiding overwhelming encounters. As a side note, the game truly shines during one-on-one combat. Fighting groups of enemies can be rather frustrating. Stealth helps deal with powerful enemies you’d rather not face head-on. Even minibosses can fall victim to stealth—almost all minibosses can have one "life" bar shaved off stealthily, with exceptions for paired minibosses and those who have just a single life.

Speaking of multiple "lives," it makes me think about the concept of perfecting boss fights. Souls-like games often give the impression that you must perfect a boss's moveset to progress, but upon reflection, luck also plays a considerable role. Certain attacks might be tougher for you to parry or avoid, and sometimes, you simply have fewer of those attacks in a particular fight, increasing your odds. However, multiple "lives" change this dynamic entirely. You can't rely on luck several times in a row. When facing bosses with multiple health bars, you really must perfect your performance to stand a chance. Thus, stripping a miniboss of one life stealthily feels like a legitimate cheat—one that makes you feel clever, as if you've successfully tricked the game.

There are other notable differences between Sekiro and traditional Souls games. A negative example is how the game handles death. Usually, dying means losing all your souls, and to recover them, you need to return to the exact location where you perished. This makes death feel significant, though it also forces you to keep exploring in the same direction upon revival. Sekiro takes a different approach. Death strips you of half your experience points and money with no chance of retrieval. Occasionally, this penalty might be avoided by chance, but it's random, and you can't count on it. While this approach allows greater exploration freedom, the punishment is so severe that I can’t say I prefer it over the Souls games' method. Its severity is somewhat balanced by the resurrection mechanic—technically, you could flee a tough encounter after dying once. However, sometimes boss fights trigger unexpectedly, locking you in an arena. In those cases, you're guaranteed to lose resources, making it feel unfair and extremely frustrating.

Other common and frustrating traits from FromSoftware games include controller button prompts, even if you’re playing with keyboard and mouse; unavoidable deaths when you're cornered against a wall; and camera issues that render fights impossible. I'm unsure if the latter problem can ever be fully resolved, especially in tight spaces, but the former is simply evidence of a lazy console port.

Posted: August 4
Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice: A Blade's Edge Revolution
Combat Reimagined, Bushido Perfected

FromSoftware reinvented action games with Sekiro. Ditching stamina bars and rolls, its revolutionary "Posture System" transforms combat into a lethal rhythm dance—every parry, deflection, and deathblow feels like walking a razor's edge. The metallic clang of clashing swords and cinematic executions when enemies stagger blend visceral brutality with poetic artistry.

But innovation goes deeper. The seamless triad of stealth-assassination-duel gameplay, grapple-hook verticality, and mechanics like lightning reversals or combat arts shatter the "Soulslike" mold. Every boss fight in Ashina (especially Sword Saint Isshin) is a trial by fire, with victory delivering unmatched euphoria.

Hard? Absolutely. Yet precise hitboxes and unlimited revives make the learning curve fair. When you finally cut through bullets with a blade, you’ll realize: this isn’t masochism—it’s the awakening of a true shinobi.

Lemme tell y'all something: Sekiro is not just a game. It’s a spiritual journey. A pilgrimage of pain. A test of patience so severe that Buddhist monks use it to reach enlightenment.
(Source: trust me bro)

I tried Sekiro at a friend's place and thought, "Hey, this looks fun. The boss is called Lady Butterfly 🥹💖✨️ Des so cute! How hard could it be?" ☺️✨️ Spoiler: I couldn't beat her. She beat me. Physically, emotionally, and mentally. I died more than twice. I died so many times, I started thinking the title of the game was some kinda sick joke. A lie. A cruel marketing ploy. Shadows died many times. Many many times actually. Repeatedly. Violently. Sometimes in mid-air. Other times, if you're really lucky, while stuck in a chicken coop getting mobbed by big black chickens. ☺️ It was such a lovely experience that ofc I had to buy the game. 🥹 I was in a pretty dark place when I got Sekiro. Now I'm in a darker pla- /j
This purchase was the best decision I've ever made. 😌✨️

The first thing Sekiro teaches you is that blocking is for cowards, and dodging is for Elden Ring players. Here, you must deflect enemy attacks with the precision of a caffeinated shinobi god. Mistime it? Congrats, you're dead. Mistime it slightly? Still dead. Perfectly time it? Also probably dead, but at least it looked cool and you felt something for once. 🥹✨️ (It was despair.) Best combat system ever. 10/10

Trying out Charmless Ng+ at the moment and unless you perfectly deflect, you take chip damage. I really thought the game couldn't get any better, but I was very wrong.

"Suffering is what I desire"
Quote: Wolf
Words to live by 😌💅

Anyhoo

Story? Beautiful. Emotional. I wept. Not just because of the plot, but because I was on my 69th attempt at O'Rin of the Water, and she countered all my hits with the smug energy of a woman who knows she's about to ruin your entire afternoon, weekend, and possibly life. I tried Puppeteering some mobs to help me fight her, but it was to no avail. So I chose the logical approach. A tactical retreat. (with no intention of returning.) 😌💅
Spoiler: She chased me all the way to the Corrupted Monk, and then they beat me up together. 🥹✨️
I love this game

Every boss fight is a masterpiece. The game doesn’t just teach you how to fight. It teaches you how to fail. Repeatedly. With style. It teaches you not to fear death, but to embrace it. After all, you’ll be seeing that death screen more than your own family.
But when you finally beat that boss. When the music swells, the posture bar shatters, and your shinobi does that sexy little deathblow move (ngh 👁🫦👁), it’s like injecting pure dopamine into your soul. I screamed. I cried. I woke the neighbors. One of them clapped.

Would I recommend Sekiro? Absolutely. This isn’t just a game. It’s a character-building exercise disguised as a sword fight. It will humble you, break you, mold you into a parry god and leave you mentally and emotionally ruined in the best way.

Please play this game y'all. It's become my absolute favourite 🥹✨️
You won't regret it! Trust me on this one!
(Me when I lie)

To my surprise, Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice was not the hardest game I’ve ever played. As my catalogue of FromSoftware titles steadily increases, my experience with difficulty in these games has grown as I’ve overcome some of the hardest challenges that the genre has to offer. However, where you could get away with a somewhat sloppy run against a boss even in Elden Ring and Dark Souls III’s most intense battles, that margin of error does not exist in Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice. Above all else, this game demands perfection, and to my surprise, with a little bit of patience and skill, it is entirely possible to master this game like no other. When this game clicks, you feel it.

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is immediately set apart from its Dark Souls brethren by its gameplay. Anyone who’s played the game will immediately be able to explain the differences; the necessity of parrying, the importance of jumping, and knowledge of exploiting weaknesses through prosthetic abilities. Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice’s gameplay is that of a dance, one where the mesmerising showers of sparks from your clashing swords set the rhythm of a fight. Knowledge of your enemies is more vital here than in any other game I’ve played, and being able to perfectly perform a series of attacks or beat a boss without taking damage provides a feeling that will keep me coming back to this game for years to come.

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is also separated from the rest of FromSoftware’s library through the level design. The game places huge emphasis on vertically, with the game’s grappling hook providing the solution to many environmental challenges (you know the game’s peak when there’s a grappling hook involved). The game’s world is also incredibly interconnected, with few places that cannot be reached without fast travel. Finding secrets in all the hidden nooks and crannies of the world is always fun in these games, but here the abundance of upgrade materials and useful consumables make every bit of exploration worth it. Each area also puts on full display this game’s impeccable art direction. I remember literally having to take a moment to recover from first seeing the Fountainhead Palace, and there are many other locations that ooze atmosphere and the kind of beauty only the most tranquil of Japanese sites can evoke.

Undoubtedly, the best part of Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice are the boss fights, which like the rest of the game, are not as difficult as some other fights in other FromSoftware games, but will not fall on a lucky first try. So many fights in this game have cemented themselves firmly in my list of best bosses of all time, in particular; Gyoubu Oniwa, Genichiro Ashina, Great Shinobi Owl, the Divine Dragon, Isshin, the Sword Saint. Each one of these fights and many more took some time to beat, but there was no feeling more satisfying than finally overcoming them and seeing that classic samurai movie spray of blood.

The only regard I think Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice falls short of its FromSoftware companions in is the music. While I think the game’s voice acting, sound effects and design are all top notch, the music tends to be more of a background detail than a core part of the storytelling this time around, which I found to be a real shame. No boss theme really stood out to me during my first play through, however, I think this can mostly be attributed to the fact that in almost every instance, I was fighting for my life.

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice was a game I was terrified of playing. Watching gameplay and hearing about the challenges the game posed were almost enough for me to never pick up the game at all. However, I overcame my hesitation and did what I never thought was possible. I still can’t believe I managed to beat this game, and I cannot wait to do it all over again.

I was born in 1995, and on my third birthday in 1998, I got a bootleg Famicom console (third world country problems). That day changed my life. Since then, I've been playing video games regularly.

One of the games I want to mention is The Flintstones: The Rescue of Dino & Hoppy (1991). I played it endlessly as a kid. The game had a small overworld map, but in my childish mind, it felt enormous. In the top-left corner of the map was a castle. I managed to reach it a few times but never beat it. I always thought, “Well, this must be the endgame.” It wasn’t. One day, just before going shopping with my parents, I beat it, and the joy and satisfaction I felt was beyond words. I didn’t even turn off the console; I kept playing as soon as we got home.

Fast forward a few years, the second game is Taz: Wanted (2002). It had a city-themed hub world, and one of the museum levels had a part that was so obscure for me at the time that I couldn’t beat it for months. I kept replaying the earlier levels, never getting tired of them, but longing to move forward. One day, I finally beat that section with my dad. The joy was so intense I couldn’t sleep well that night. I even woke up at 7 AM the next day, excited to keep going.

I tell these stories because Sekiro somehow managed to bring back that exact same feeling after all these years. I've played countless games since then, many filled with great emotional moments, but Sekiro did the impossible: it recreated the pure magic I thought was lost to childhood.

Sekiro is a masterpiece. Its gameplay and design are beyond incredible. It's a work of art that everyone should experience at least once.

Fun fact: I actually lost weight beating the final boss.

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice – A Masterpiece of Aggression and Stealth

When I first started Sekiro, the experience felt tedious. The game overwhelmed me with mobs of enemies, and the parrying mechanics didn't quite click. Up until the encounter with Genichiro, I struggled to find the rhythm, and the game felt unnecessarily difficult. However, everything changed when I faced this boss. It was then that I truly understood the beauty of Sekiro.

Sekiro is not just another action game—it's a game of aggression, where applying pressure on your opponent is key. The feeling of overwhelming your enemies, bouncing between attacks, parries, dodges, and counters, is unlike anything I've experienced. The combat is intense, demanding precision and timing, and when it all comes together, it’s incredibly satisfying.

But Sekiro isn’t all about brute force. The stealth mechanics also play a significant role, and this was something I initially overlooked. Unlike the older Dark Souls games, where you’re expected to fight every enemy you encounter, Sekiro allows you to bypass many encounters. Exploring the world becomes more about strategy than simply engaging in combat, making it not only more logical but also more enjoyable.

Sekiro has its challenges, but once you understand its rhythm and embrace its aggressive nature, it turns into an exhilarating and rewarding experience. It’s a unique action game that blends combat, stealth, and exploration in a way that keeps you coming back for more.

The combat system is the main selling point of this game. There are also some RPG elements, a great story and some stunning visuals.

Unlike to the games in the dark souls trilogy, in Sekiro the camera is actually your friend. And the best example to illustrate that point is the guardian ape fight. You are confronted with a large enemy with an erratic beahviour that jumps a lot, however you can always see what the ape is doing and repond accoringly once you memorized all of his patterns.

Although, you can improve the character hp and attack power if you have not learned how to deflect or blocs the incomming attacks you will have a real hard time making any progress. Therefore, the focus is shifted from improving the character by leveling up to imprving yourself by mastering the combat mechanics. Your ability to memerize enemies pattern is what trully makes a difference. Moreover, you do not need to have some lightning speed reflexes as all the attacs are quite well telegraphed but you will still get hit a lot in your learning phase. This can be extremely frustrating, especially if you die more than twice. This game demands from players a considerable investment, but once it clicks you will be able to brutalize the boss on which you were stuck for several days. Finally, you can not create a broken build to overpower your enemies, meaning that the vast majority of the player base will have an almost identical experince.

After doing 100% on every Fromsoftware soulsborne game, Sekiro has by far been the hardest but also my favourite, in terms of gameplay and story. For gameplay the combat system feels 100x more rewarding than any dark souls game or elden ring, basically the joy of getting a parry in elden ring but in every single fight you do and having the whole system be based around getting them, as well as things such as the mikiri counter making the dopamine hits even greater during these boss fights. Boss fights in the game were also 10x harder than any souls fight, fast paced, low downtime attack cycles with curveball moves thrown in at every turn to punish even the slightest slip, Sekiro rewards players who are patient but also not afraid to be aggressive when the opportunity arises and who are willing to throw themselves into the fight over and over again until they have a strategy against every single attack a boss has, the sheer joy felt when you can complete a full deathblow without a single hit is orgasmic.

Additionally the stealth and movement in this game are also way more rewarding than the other fromsoftware games, being able to grapple across roofs, crawl along edges to get behind a mini-boss and sneak attack them at the start of the fight to knock off one of their death blows is so fun. The map of sekiro, while small feels way more interconnected than DS1, you have the ability to go in and unlock what are considered mid to late game areas straight away in NG+ because they are just always accessible once you first arrive at Ashina castle.

Unlike every other game by Fromsoftware, in sekiro there are only two stats, your vitality and attack power, and unlike the other games you do not upgrade these using something like Souls or Runes instead you are rewarded with more health for exploring and beating mini bosses to get prayer beads and rewarded with more attack power for defeating main bosses through the memories. Additionally theres no classes, no alternative weapons or spells or anything, the only real customisable things are the relatively limited amount of shinobi tools, the three ninjitsus and your combat skills. Despite having fairly limited options for combat it works well with the more direct story that sekiro has in relation to the souls games, and it means everyone gets the same experience and faces mostly the same challenges while still being open to some strategising, no running in with a big club after grinding out a million souls to max out your strength and just one shotting the boss, you need actual skill to succeed.

On the topic of the story, instead of the "tarnished" or "chosen undead" who never speaks, that you customise a ♥♥♥♥ ton and isn't really connected to the characters in any way before the game, sekiro has a named and voiced main character with a set look, interacts with characters has his own meaningful story and actual connection to the characters in the game. Sekiro sets itself apart by having the bulk of its story actually explained, while still retaining the classic souls method of hiding lore in item descriptions and props in the environment. Of the four endings only one seemed strange and not like something the character would do, that ending also not so coincidentally being the "bad" ending. All the endings have emotional impacts and explore themes of sacrifice, death, and corruption, and the overall story and world of sekiro incorporate tons of actual japanese mythology and folk lore, as well as historical accuracy surrounding the sengoku era that it is set in.

Sekiro was one of the greatest games I have had the pleasure to experience and one of the more rewarding to 100% especially due to the lack of grinding covenant items like in Dark Souls III. Will stand by this being the most enjoyable and unique fromsoftware title, unless they can manage to top it.

Sekiro is a game unlike the rest of Fromsoft's catalogue, some of the defining hallmarks of their Soulsborne series are mssing from this game like careful stamina management, stat-based leveling, stat requirements, scalings, weapon classes, and much more.

All of this is removed to make way for sekiro's unique approach, One sword, one moveset, a few extra customizables, and your left bumper. Sekiro's combat ditches the concepts of stamina management and dodge rolling in trade for an In-your-face deflect mechanic that lets you face your opponents head on, and with the right timing and skill, live out a samurai movie esque battle between you and some pretty stunning boss battles.

When i say the deflect in this game is good, i mean it's REALLY good. as someone said a long time ago that i repeat to this day, "Sekiro's deflect feels like pouring liquid chocolate into your dopamine receptors" and i stand by this every bit. from the sound design to the fact you perform them repeatedly at times to their gameplay use, the deflect mechanic in this game is executed with near perfection. the game also provides you with far greater movement than the other games you may be used to, including grapples, wall hanging, wall jumps, jumping off enemies, aerial finishing blows, and various other options. this game is meant to make you feel like a shinobi, quickly moving from one target to the next, and fighting with every trick you have to survive, and it achieves this perfectly.

If you've never played a game in fromsoft's catalogue, Sekiro is a fair starting point as it requires very little foundational knowledge to learn and start building up some skill in, and will take you for a challenging yet fair ride that always forces you to push yourself just a little bit further.

If you have played other fromsoft games then you probably already know what this game is and what it's about, so play it! wait for a sale if you want a steal, but this game is absolutely worth your time.

I think Sekiro is the most close-to perfect game out of every game I've ever played.

I won't lie, my first playthrough, I was extremely close to just refunding the game and forgetting about it. It is super difficult to learn. Every enemy is different, no boss fights the same, they all have a different and unique set of moves which makes it very enjoyable, yet frustrating at the beginning. When you first meet Genichiro and get absolutely obliterated by him, you can tell the game isn't going to be easy.

Start of the game, I do have a slight flaw, and it's that the bosses at the very start don't really teach you great mechanics in my opinion. Chained Ogre doesn't really teach you to parry, you CAN but I feel like everyone just dodges all his attacks, and Gyobou also has some awkward parries for your first playthrough, I don't think I actually focused on parries at all when I first fought him. Then finally Bull, you get punished for parrying which is a big design flaw for the start of the game as the game is focused around this one mechanic.

All of this aside, progressing after this gives you the perfect opportunities to learn. The Ashina Elite boss, teaches you quick parries. Then Genichiro, has a mix of fast attacks and some easier ones, perfect for teaching how the game is meant to be played.

The map also doesn't feel super confusing. Playing through Dark Souls 3 I always feel very lost and just no idea where to go, however I found it way easier to navigate my way around in Sekiro. The map layout is fantastic, it's a beautiful looking map.

Finally, the main mechanic: parrying. The single most satisfying thing I have ever done in any game. It takes time to learn it for each enemy, however hearing that sound of a perfect parry is the most rewarding sound ever, and the sounds for this game truly are a 10/10.

My one criticism with this game comes with how the story ends. That said, I still think the story was interesting, my first playthroughs I did want to learn more, it was enjoyable.

Overall, the game has very little to improve upon. I've done 10 New-Game+ playthroughs.
Mods make this game super replayable with the new attack patters and increased difficulty, Resurrection mod is some of the most fun I've had replaying the game and adds a new level of satisfaction when re-learning all the attacks and timings.

Genuinely a 9.9/10 game.

Sekiro Rewards Skill Like No Other Souls-Like Game

Carve your own clever path to vengeance in the award winning adventure from developer FromSoftware, creators of Bloodborne and the Dark Souls series. Take Revenge. Restore Your Honor. Kill Ingeniously.

Story
In Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice you are the 'one-armed wolf', a disgraced and disfigured warrior rescued from the brink of death. Bound to protect a young lord who is the descendant of an ancient bloodline, you become the target of many vicious enemies, including the dangerous Ashina clan. When the young lord is captured, nothing will stop you on a perilous quest to regain your honor, not even death itself.

Gameplay
Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is third-person action-adventure souls-like game developed by FromSoftware. Sekiro’s combat is fast-paced and rhythmic, focused on Posture rather than traditional health depletion. The game is very difficult, but rewarding and satisfying once you get a hold of it. It requires demanding reflexes, patience, and mastery of mechanics. You wield just one katana throughout the game, with no armor or appearance customization. A grappling hook lets you zip across rooftops and hidden spots, promoting exploration and stealth. Sekiro emphasizes parrying over dodging.


Unlike Dark Souls, you don’t level up your character in the traditional way and spend points on stats. As you defeat enemies you earn skill points. These points should be spent quickly, as dying can cause you to lose unspent ones. To use them, visit a Sculptor’s Idol (save point). After unlocking skill trees, you can spend points and gain useful abilities and attacks.

Increasing attack power isn’t straightforward. To do it, you must defeat main bosses, which grant a "Memory" item. Then go to a Sculptor’s Idol, select "Enhance Physical Attributes" and choose "Confront Memory." Despite the name, this won’t trigger another fight — it simply boosts your attack power/damage.

You can increase Wolf’s health by defeating mini-bosses, who often drop Prayer Beads. Collect four, then use them at an Idol to boost your max vitality. You can also improve healing by giving Gourd Seeds (also found from mini-bosses) to Emma at the Dilapidated Temple, which increases your Healing Gourd’s uses (like Estus Flasks in Dark Souls).


Combat is centered around breaking an enemy’s posture to deliver a deathblow - an instant kill. Posture is like balance, that can be broken by attacking, blocking and deflecting enemy attacks. Every enemy and boss has posture, and some require more than one deathblow to kill them. Low vitality slows posture recovery, making it easier to fill. The game also emphasizes stealth, where you can instantly perform a deathblow on an enemy if unseen. Aerial deathblows can be done from a ledge or mid-air. After unlocking a certain skill, you can kill two unaware enemies in one stealth move - a double deathblow


When you die, you can resurrect on the spot — once per life, with more uses unlocked later. If you die again or don’t have resurrection available, you respawn at the latest Idol. You then lose half your Sen (money) and unspent experience toward your next skill point. This loss is permanent — no way to retrieve what you lost (unlike Soulsborne games where you can recover dropped souls).

Resurrection comes at a cost: Dragonrot — dying too much can “infect” NPCs and block questlines. You’ll then need Dragon's Blood Droplets to heal the world.


Wolf has a mechanical ninja arm fitted with a variety of powerful attachments, each offering different abilities called Prosthetic Tools. They consume Spirit Emblems (ammo) when used. Later, tools can be upgraded with materials. Each tool is useful in specific scenarios and depends on the enemies weaknesses — no single tool is best for everything. A few examples:



Enemies and bosses in Sekiro are among the game’s greatest strengths. They offer great variety, each with distinct attack patterns you need to learn to master Perfect Deflects. Their unique behaviors, strengths, and weaknesses make every fight a true test of skill and strategy. There are also mini-bosses that are tougher than regular enemies and often have unique movesets.

The bosses are the highlight but also the hardest part of this game. Some are vulnerable to posture damage quickly, while others require you to chip away their vitality first to slow down their recovery to make it easier. Once you get better at a boss fight and know when to strike and when to deflect, it’s like a beautiful and satisfying dance.

Set in a fantastical version of late 1500s Japan during the Sengoku period. Similar to Dark Souls, the world is semi-open and interconnected, with shortcuts and hidden paths. Each area features mini-bosses, secrets, upgrades, and lore, making exploration richly rewarding. The environment is varied and atmospheric, ranging from dense forests and mountain temples to Ashina Castle and flooded valleys, each area has its own unique look, enemies, and challenges. Stealth spots like tall grass, rooftops, and bushes allow you to bypass or assassinate enemies. The level design is great.

The visual design is beautiful, containing architectural styles like castles, temples, villages, and shrines that are detailed and authentic. Character designs, armor, and weapons reflect period-accurate samurai and shinobi influences. Environments and enemies have eerie, supernatural touches—twisted forms, glowing eyes, and otherworldly effects. The landscapes are rich and varied, such as misty forests, snowy mountains, decaying temples, flooded ruins, and more.

The soundtrack blends traditional Japanese instruments (like shakuhachi flutes and taiko drums) with orchestral elements. Each weapon strike, block, and parry has crisp, satisfying audio cues. Enemy attack sounds give important feedback, helping you time deflections and dodges. Prosthetic tools have distinct sounds, aiding players in recognizing what’s being used in battle. The sound of swords clashing when you deflect attacks are so satisfying.

Only have one complaint: Demon of Hatred. This boss is more about getting behind it and attacking away than deflecting its attacks. It also has some very annoying attacks that are very difficult to dodge and the fight suffers from camera issues due to the boss’ being very large. The fight is somewhat badly designed.

Sekiro is definitely one of my favorite games and one of the best souls-like games ever—a true FromSoftware masterpiece. The combat rewards skill like no other souls-like game. FromSoftware delivers a rewarding experience where mastering posture-based combat and perfect deflections turns every fight into a satisfying dance of skill and timing. The enemies, bosses and the prosthetic arm adds variety to the combat, and the world is beautiful and immersive. The only notable drawback is the Demon of Hatred boss fight, which breaks from the game’s core combat rhythm and suffers from awkward camera angles, making it feel somewhat out of place and frustrating compared to the otherwise tight and refined gameplay. It’s a very challenging game and you will die many times—more than “shadows dying twice”, I promise you that. But I also promise you’ll have fun.

Собственно, мой отзыв по Секиро:
- Мне понравилась прогрессия. Не надо дрочить душу, не надо дрочить титанит, не надо дрочить мобов для оружия - все тебе дают по ходу прохождения. В этом плане она похожа на старые слешеры нулевых, это плюс.
- мне понравилось управление, хоть я на него и бомбил ♥♥♥♥♥♥. Но, после душ это просто отдушина, можно прыгать, бегать, плавать, цепляться за всякую поеботу (сделано хуево, но хотя бы есть).
- Мне понравилась боевка, но с нюансами. Тут нет особого разнообразия, но тут чуть побольше вариантов. По сути, каждый протез что-то дает, каждый активный скилл позволяет чуть по разному вести бой, ну и расходники тут довольно полезны или даже необходимы. В бою нужно юзать и дефлекты, и прижки, и увороты, а все вместе еще и сочетается так что микири каунтеры, прыжковые каунтеры, вот это все говно. А уж если добавить протезы так вообще, можно делать грязь.
- НО, у большинства (ну, некоторых) боссов есть одна-две атаки, которые если ты не понимаешь как законтрить - ♥♥♥♥ тебя в очко, а если понимаешь как законтрить - подставляют очко босса тебе. Как-бы не совсем понятно, это так задумано или так получилось, но вот, допустим, с геничиро я долго сидел, не мог понять че делать с луком. Ну просто тупой был, с кем не бывает. Оказывается - он просто дефлектится. Очевидно? Да. Но я не понял. И эта атака меня ректила, пока я не понял, что делать. И все, она после этого вообще ♥♥♥♥♥ стала. Но в целом боссы тут немного более уныле чем в Элден Ринге, даже несмотря на то, что их тут немного, и у игрока больше вариантов в бою. Я еще не высираюсь по поводу того, что половина боссов и мини боссов это тупо повторы. Я пьяницу убивал раза 3 или 4, наверное, как котов в Элдене.
- Мне не поравился главный герой, то, что его нельзя кастомизировать и то, что тут нет разнообразие билдов. Но, после прохождения, я изменил свое мнение. Тут все на своем месте, как надо. И ГГ стал нормальным, и билды какие никакие есть, да и суть игры не в этом.
- Прокачка - мне понравилась. Конечно, есть мастхевные скиллы, есть мусор ♥♥♥♥♥♥ и есть филлеры, которые вообще ♥♥♥ пойми зачем нужны. Но, наличие выбора лучше, чего его отсутствие. Поэтому тут плюсик.
- Сеттинг. Изначально мне он не зашел. Япония, одинаковые домики, одинаковые враги, ♥♥♥ пойми куда идти, ♥♥♥ пойми что делать. Я как-бы не фанат жапана, не виабушник и ко всему этому с прохладой отношусь, но вынужден признать, что по итогу мне понравилось как тут все показано. Тебя не загружают кучей разной ♥♥♥♥♥, как в ниохе том же, тут нет какой-то мифологии, верований, богов и прочего. Здесь все максимально просто, но, в то же время, со стилем и шармом. Плюс, мне понравился сюжет и как он обыгран. Плюс мне очень зашли расходники, тут в полной мере ощущается то, что игра не дженерик хуита, а имеет свой стиль и направление. В общем, лайк.
В целом - это охуенное развитие идей слешером/экшнов нулевых годов, фромы взяли костяк, который работал, прикрутил к нему свой геймплей и выпустили в продажу. Не считаю ее лучшей игрой фромов, как многие считают, но мне она понравилась.

Posted: July 18
The Song within the Blade

Sekiro was in my backlog for so long, and I was honestly scared to start it because of its reputation for difficulty. I’m not a Souls player — just a casual gamer who plays a bit of everything — but wow, this experience did not disappoint. It challenged me, pushed me, but never felt unfair. By the end, I wasn’t just surviving; I was flowing like a god with a sword.

To walk through Sekiro is to step into the beauty and mysticism of Feudal Japan and esoteric Buddhism. The landscapes are poems — haunting waterfalls, serene temples, crumbling castles — each echoing the decay and divinity of the land. From the quiet beasts to the somber humans, everything feels like it belongs, part of a myth slowly unraveling. It’s not just a game world — it’s a spiritual ruin, and an honor to traverse.

I like seeing all the main endings when I connect with a story, and here, that means replaying the whole game due to the route-locking system. Missing a specific boss fight I’d looked forward to hurt a little. But despite that, the combat is something truly special. It’s not just swordplay — it’s a rhythm game in disguise. Every boss has their own tempo, and the only way to win is to drown them out with your own. Once it clicked, it felt like I was playing music with my blade.
Sincerely the most finely-tuned combat I’ve ever experienced.

Actually annoying game. borderline cancerous and NOT FUN. The key component here is NOT FUN. It just looks good.

There are so many things to do gameplay-wise, and yet i end up using the same 2 buttons every time. The skill tree is useful only for the passive skills and rarely for active ones, which ends up being just cumbersome and useless for the major part.

There are so many routes, so many mechanics, so many words and paragraphs, so much talking about stuff that i wasn't introduced properly to, or i just don't care about. The main storyline is, so far, extremely boring. The confusing nature of the game doesn't help: sometimes, i end up in a place that has a dead end. There are so many enemies in those areas, that i've been conditioned into NOT FIGHTING. I just run through them, and it's much better than fighting for literally NO REWARD. I'm guessing that the items i picked up in those places are for quests... that are, in Fromsoft fashion, obscure and incompletable if i don't use a guide. What if i want to get into the game blind and play it myself? You either act coherent, like Dark Souls, and give me literally no hints and no random extra routes to confuse me, or just don't bother.

I haven't used the main currency once so far, i keep losing it. The unseen aid mechanic is probably the most useless thing i've ever experienced, and could have been scrapped easily during development for something actually useful/cool/functional. I just ignore it now.
On that note, I haven't used a single consumable. Why add them in the first place if their use is either limited, needless or straight up useless? It reminds me of the 45 cookbooks in elden ring's dlc that have been scattered in empty wastelands with nothing else in them.

It's incredible how your progress in a single, insignificant battle can just be thrown OUT THE FU***** WINDOW because an enemy stepped back. Yes, that's a thing. The posture mechanic is flimsy and inconsistent, absolutely NOT FUN TO PLAY WITH AND AGAINST. This game's base mechanics are the worst thing i've seen in years: at the base, they're really simple, but executed in such a poor way that it's laughable. i truly don't understand the "overwhelmingly positive" reviews, there's no way someone didn't find this game horrible to play through.
and oh people... wait for the most balanced duo fight ever. Posture meters endlessly recovering, spam, cancer, disease, all of it. in one beautiful fight. Extremely unbalanced, not fun and antithetical to the game's design; let me say it out loud: THIS GAME IS NOT FOR 2 ON 1 FIGHTS. The base mechanics themselves DON'T work for those scenarios.the fight wasn't difficult: it was terrible, not fun, and should not be in the game. It clearly displays Fromsoft's inability to understand their gameplay objectives. And yes, i'm tired of seeing this mediocre company having their jewels polished every time they release a game. THEY ALSO AREN'T PERFECT.

Most of the game can be beaten running through it and you wouldn't lose anything form the experience. Especially your mental composure and sanity. I have come to a conclusion: i don't care if the game looks pretty, if all you have to do is go on a straight line. Exploration is NOT WORTH IT. I am not bothering with it. It's not rewarding at all.

The movement mechanics could have been scrapped fully. The climbing is so janky and the platforming too. Just give up on that dream fromsoft, you won't be able to make good platforming mechanics EVER.

This game is an empty shell.

Terrible game. Money wasted, uninstalled. I won't finish it. Never trusting fromsoft ever again.

Posted: July 15
This is yet another perfect example of FromSoftwares unforgettable and difficult experiences. This game takes a step forward from your average Souls Like (Dark Souls, Bloodborne etc) and focuses mainly on tight, deliberate and rewarding sword play. Rather than hack and slash and roll spamming, the game requires you, the player to be as patient as can be. Relying on deflecting your opponent over "beating them to pulp" as one tarnished had written. The posture system they had implemented is simple, yet fair, extending fights long enough to be incredibly satisfying once you've defeated said enemy/boss, or disheartening when you were so close to finishing them off, yet slipped.

The games general world is based on a fictional version of Sengoku-era Japan, ranging from war-torn battlegrounds, to snow covered lands, to blooming landscape. Every location set in this game was meticulously crafted and well thought out, some even inspired by Japanese landmarks. and on top of that the soundtrack makes for some incredible ambience, sitting in place and becoming immersed with the surrounding environment. The soundtrack itself also uses traditional Japanese instrumentation, in an effort to show the essence found during the Sengoku time frame. Its noted as being both soft, yet methodical.

After giving up on this game 2 times due to difficulty, I finally decided I'm not letting it beat me and gave it my all, and I am really glad I did. It's now one of my all-time favorites.

The combat system is extremely smooth and rewarding. You may get more HP, tools, buffs and weapon damage as you progress, but the biggest thing you upgrade is yourself. Also, funnily enough, playing souls games before Sekiro actually makes you worse at it, because you'll want to dodge out of reflex, but you really shouldn't dodge in most cases (the exceptions being sweep, grab and some AoE attacks; and arguably you dodge as well to trigger mikiri counter by dodging into perilous spear/thrust attacks).

The narrative is compelling and the aesthetics are wonderful. The voice acting is great too, and the sound design stands out as well. You will have boss battles where you get stuck for a while. Eventually, as you practice, improve and focus more, you will lose yourself in the cling-clang of the swords as the rest of the world fades away - it's oddly tranquil.

My only complaints are about some janky fights where you have to fight extra mobs on top of the boss and it turns chaotic, and that you need to spend some time dedicated to grinding skill points to actually unlock everything if that's a goal you have, even with NG+es. But these are minor, it's a nearly perfect game. Try it, stick with it.

Posted: July 13
Sekiro is in my opinion the best Fromsoft game.
Please, give this game a chance. You will not regret it.
Let's start with combat. This game has the best and most satisfying combat of any game I have ever played, yet it is very very challenging. Once you master it and start perfectly parrying every boss attack, you just feel like a God (Godess). I really love how intuitive and reactive it is. In this game, you only got 1 weapon, which is a major shift from other Fromsoft games such as Elden Ring and Dark Souls. I prefer this, since the bossfights are much more about skill rather than finding the most op build (You can of course abuse weapon arts such as Mortal Blade and Prosthetic tools for some bosses if you prefer the game to be easier). I personally played all Souls games and Elden Ring and bosses took me like 2 attempts on average. In Sekiro, some bosses even took me over 30 tries (Saint Sword Isshin, Father Owl at Hirata Estate).
The Japanese setting is awesome. Locations are well desighned and moving between certain parts of them with grappling hook also feels really really good.
Bosses are also very well made, from giant monkey to shinobi master I enjoyed all of them. My favorite has to be Saint Sword Isshin, he took me very long to learn, but on my NG+1 playthrough I 1st tried him and on NG+2 I no-hit him, which made me feel really awesome <3.
This game has 4 endings, all of them are fairly easy to get and all of them offer an unique experience.
From achievement huntresses perspective, achievements in this game are also very easy, just play the game as intended and go for all endings and you will probably unlock all of them. Only a bit grindy achievement is to get every skill, but I was easily able to farm them in about 3 hours combined on NG+4.
Once again, please give this game a shot.
Easiest 10/10 of my life, without a doubt in my head.

Posted: July 13
Sekiro: Fun, but not a masterpiece

Most of Sekiro’s enjoyment comes from making the player a slave to fleeting dopamine hits. Hours after hours of trying to get one boss completed, just to have that 30 seconds of mmm yeah accomplishment, and it starts all over again when you get to the next boss. As GOTY 2019, this would be okay if there was something to gain from fighting each boss, but I look back after completing the game and realise there is little to gain— perhaps I’m good at Sekiro now, but where does that get me? A fun story? An interesting world? Unique class building? No, no and no. It’s all for its own sake.

Probably the only substance this game has is that its combat is seen as difficult, but even this difficulty can be artificial. Compared to other games, such as Expedition 33, the parrying window in Sekiro is huge, so much so that it was brought to Google Stadia (!!!). In reality, a large part of Sekiro’s difficulty comes from arguably unfair sources— the camera breaking and losing lock-on, combos stun locking and 1-2 shot killing, the environment fighting against you… all of these are problems that would have ruined any other game, but it’s been widely ignored or even glorified by gamers because it’s made by Fromsoft, and that’s a point I really want to drive home.

Sekiro has an incredibly predictable story; one that could be completely ignored and would make little difference to the game’s enjoyment. Typical hurr burr ‘break the cycle’ BS that we’ve got from Fromsoft in pretty much every game since DS1, but yet again, it’s okay or even cool because it’s Fromsoft. Adding onto this, the story is told in incredibly conservative parts, so nobody really knows what’s going on, even if they read all the item descriptions and eavesdropped conversations. Black Myth Wukong got criticised for skipping over some minor details and being implicit, but Sekiro gets unanimous praise. Make it make sense.

Sekiro’s progression is also underwhelming. The story makes a huge deal about the prosthetic arm, and how it’s a metaphor for the repeating cycle of the shinobi, but when in practice it is of laughable consequence. You could max out every single prosthetic in the skill tree and it would make a flaccid difference in the difficulty of the game or its enjoyment. The same goes for latent skills and combat arts. The only form of ‘progression’ that actually does something are the occasional increases in damage, vitality and posture gained after defeating various bosses. In other words,

Additionally, Sekiro’s exploration and open world are confusing, and often had me searching guides to help complete the story. In games such as Elden Ring, BG3 and the modern Zelda games, I can forgive this, as each corner of their worlds have something interesting to discover, but Sekiro doesn’t. You spend countless hours trying to get to a new part of the world, fighting overpowered mini bosses, using remnants for guidance, only to find… Maybe a rare consumable, or a new (useless) prosthetic if you’re lucky. Again, most of the enjoyment comes from little dopamine hits of defeating enemies over and over without gaining anything of substance.

I think I’ve said enough now. You may have noticed, though, that despite all these criticisms, I still have Sekiro rated fairly highly, so you may be wondering why I’ve talked so badly of it. This comes down to whether a game being fun and therefore whether we should recommend it.

Sekiro’s only gameplay loop is its constant dopamine drawl and withdrawal, but this doesn’t mean it’s In reality, despite all these drawbacks, I still had a fairly enjoyable time with Sekiro and do believe it is a fun game. The dopamine loop works well when it’s supposed to, but that doesn’t mean it’s a good game Especially when we consider all the praise Sekiro has received.

Now, I know what you’re thinking. “What do you mean a game being fun doesn’t make it good? Isn’t the entire point of a game is that it’s fun?” Yes and no. Games should be fun, yes, but games are also artwork. If the sole point of gaming is to have fun, then how is gaming different to alcohol or cocaine?

The reality is, most people will enjoy Sekiro in the same way they enjoy rhythm games, Doom-likes, or even something like Fortnite. Its sole purpose is to be fun, and nothing more. The problem arises when Sekiro receives unanimous glazing, wins GOTY 2019 and is hailed as a masterpiece. Or even worse, when Miyazaki fanboys put down other soulslikes such as Black Myth Wukong; games that should be appreciated in their own right.

And this highlights something that’s so wrong in the gaming community. We form tribes, pledge blind allegiance to corporations, and mindlessly give away our money — all for these companies to stagnate and make the same product over and over. Would EA still be around if not for FIFA? Would Ubisoft still be around if not for Assassins’ Creed? No, but these are the obvious examples. Miyazaki fanboys do the same thing with Fromsoft, but we don’t realise it because FromSoft games are of a higher quality, and release less often.

It’s high time we look at Souls games more critically, and appreciate new entries to the industry with respect, even if they are derivative. Sekiro is not a masterpiece, and is not deserving of its GOTY award. It has many flaws, from its poor story to its confusing open world, but overall it is still a fun game. The combat works very well when it’s supposed to, meaning I would still recommend it to anyone who can knowingly appreciate it in that right. 6-7/10.

I've finish Sekiro years ago on my Xbox. Then last couple of years, I bought this game on Steam and completed it. Only now I felt like writing a review on this game.

In my opinion, Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice has the best gameplay mechanics of all time. You can't get the parry mechanics to work in other games. Sure Lies of P and First Berserker Khazan (and even Clair Obscur: Expedition 33) does come close to this, but they are not as good as Sekiro.

Definitely the most unforgiving souls-like. The 50% deficit of your experience on death, the rot essence, no other primary weapons to choose from (youre just stuck with your katana), no ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ spells. You literally need pure gaming skills to beat this game. And once you do, that satisfaction feeling is unmatched. Pure adrenaline.

Definitely has some of the toughest and best designed bosses in the genre. Just like the gameplay mechanics, the bosses are also unforgiving. You won't beat the boss (especially endgame bosses) unless you get good. Beating and mastering this game, is definitely one of my personal proudest achievement in gaming.

And last but not least, Hesitation is Defeat.

Posted: July 13
I died a lot more than twice

After dropping Dark Souls 2 and 3 out of boredom I became concerned. Maybe I simply wasn't that guy, the one who foams at the mouth and gets an instant erection the second FromSoftware is mentioned. I had given up hope. While I did really enjoy the first Dark Souls it didn't really "click" in the same way that my favourite games have, after finishing it I felt no desire to play it again for the foreseeable future. After my experience with the second and third games, I started to question my value as a human being, "What's wrong with me? Is there something I simply am not seeing? Am I the problem?"

The idea of FromSoft making a game that "clicked" for me seemed like an out of reach pipe dream, the kind of thing that simply does not happen in real life but something that you'll always desire in the back of your mind, like a 10/10 Latina wanting me, I had to just accept that it was not something would see in my lifetime. However, as a last Hurrah, I thought I may as well give Sekiro a go, I mean I did have it installed after all, let's just try it otherwise it will sit in my library staring at me accusingly until I either off myself or stop playing video games and instead live a meaningful life with fulfilling relationships. Neither of these things are happening, so why not click play?

This decision turned out to be it, I finally felt that "click" that I never thought I would feel. The locations, the bosses, the story, don't even get me started on the combat, it's just all there. After playing this for the first time I proceeded to play it 3 more times in a row afterwards and finally got that beautiful 100% badge, this really has become one of my all time favourite video games, and while I understand it may not be for everyone, I can't recommend it enough, because for me it was one of those rare games where I felt that click.

A truly incredible experience, while I regret many of my actions throughout my life I'll never regret buying this game on sale, and I highly suggest you do the same.

Fantastic, rewarding experience. Yes, it took me over 7 1/2 hours to best the final boss but wow, what a feeling once you see that deathblow circle.

If you're looking for some entertainment, go read the 1-star comments, they're hilarious. Most people just don't want to dedicate the time for this demanding game. I don't think it's fair to bash it on difficulty alone, I mean, you're playing a souls-like genre; do your research before you buy.


Gameplay: 4/5 - when you get the timing down, you feel like a master bladesman. Controls can be wonky and you will input a dodge or deflect and it doesn't register right away. Another issue is the camera lock when it cycles to a different target and you wanted to stay locked on (sensitivity seems off)

Graphics: 5/5 - absolutely gorgeous, especially Fountainhead Palace and Senpou Temple areas (jaw drops). FromSoftware has a specific art style, so you're going to get a similar feel here

Music: 5/5 - also stunning and one of my favorite elements of the game - you'll likely be so focused on the fight at hand to appreciate it, so I recommend pulling up the album on YouTube and listening to it without the gameplay distraction

Value: 5/5 - this is extremely biased because it is highly dependent on your level of skill and/or dedication to learning the game - there is a lot of content in this game for the price and it's well worth it if you take the time to master it - I believe it goes up to NG+ 7 (new game +) and there are gauntlets where you can fight a series of bosses back to back - you can re-live (re-fight) all of the bosses too - I feel that this gives the game A LOT of content, but if the base difficulty level was Mt. Everest to you, I can see why you wouldn't see this of any value


All-in-all this game is going to stretch your patience and skills to the limit, but it does so while giving you obvious clues (boss weaknesses, certain items/skills right before a fight/area) as to how to keep pushing on. You also lose xp and gold upon death, but this can also be mitigated by playing safe right before your next level up (you cant drop below your current level), and buying coin pouches to protect your gold from being lost (store it to consume these later when you find a merchant). I think the biggest learning curve, is the un-learning curve of previous games like Bloodborne and Dark Souls. You really need to learn the block/deflect system in this game. You wont use the dodge mechanic nearly as much. Killing bosses by wearing down their posture and then deathblowing them is quite literally the most efficient way in a lot of cases. It takes some getting used to, but if you're aggressive and good at timing your deflects, you will beat this game.

To anyone severely struggling, put this game aside and go purchase Ghost of Tsushima. The game is an absolute masterpiece and will perfectly transition you into Sekiro's demanding combat style

After 100 hours of playing this game, I think it's time to say goodbye to it for a while.

Even though I had some pretty bad moments with this game, there were also some really legendary moments. I can't say I regret playing this game, I loved every moment I spent playing it.

To be honest, it's a special game for me, one of the few games that really impressed me. The story is human. Any ending you complete, it feels like it's the one that should really happen, whether it's the worst or the best, it humanizes the aspect of the game in some way, and you somehow find yourself in their story, at least that's how it was for me.

I feel like Dark Souls, although it was an extraordinary franchise, couldn't compare to this game, for me Sekiro embodies the peak of From Software games, not only as a mechanism, but also as a story and conclusion overall.

Should there be a sequel? It would be great, but they should finish the story completely in the next game, and they should think of such a story, so well structured, that it would not be said that "We didn't need that" and that it would ruin everything that the first game was. 2/4 endings to this game draw a conclusion to the story, and the other 2 could be a potential next game, if the developers chose to follow the narrative thread of those 2, or one of them.

This game is the only one I've played and know that forces you to learn it, in such a way that you don't run away from enemies, or avoid them, the only way is to confront them.

In any case, it was an unforgettable experience, with things that, if interpreted more deeply, can also help you in real life.

This game is brilliant however, despite the fact that I have given a thumbs up (recommended), I do not recommend this game to everyone; only a specific target audience.
Basically this game has to break your back in before you can really start enjoying yourself. Don't get me wrong, the combat is enjoyable from the beginning but it takes time to click and you really have to earn the win in Sekiro. Rarely will you find a way to cheese a boss unless you just look up walkthroughs.

If you're someone who really wants a challenge and you want to feel like a badass samurai while you're at it, then this game is for you otherwise give it a miss. Even if you're the type of person to select the highest difficult in games, this still might be too hard for you. If you're a casual gamer then FORGET IT.

Things that need improving: some of the explanations for learned skills are too vague; for example the skill "breath of life" states that you can restore vitality on a successful deathblow but the catch is that they don't tell you HOW MUCH vitality you get... as a result one would assume you just restore all your vitality but NO, you only get a very small amount back, maybe 1 point (this skill is a high ranking skill)? The same issue also exists with another skill that increases the number of spirit emblems you can equip: the skill states that it will increases your spirit emblem count but not by how much... it turns out that it was only by 1 too and this was one of the higher skills it takes time to get to in the skill hierarchy -- what a joke -- sometimes the skill upgrades can be disappointing/underwhelming and sometimes they can be amazing like the skill where you can step on thrust attacks (which the game totally hides btw) and is vital for progressing.

Another thing, you have the ability to scorn or praise a remnant which is a message recorded by the player -- a pretty cool mechanic -- however, the ratings shown on the messages do not tell you which ones are scorns and which ones are praises which makes it difficult to tell if a message with a high rating has been scored negatively or positively -- maybe this vagueness is intentional by the developers to create "mystery" but I find it annoying when a player leaves a message to trick you and you can't tell if he's been rated down or up? Maybe ratings should decrement every time someone scorns and increment every time someone praises?

Reviewing (mostly) every game (or DLC) in my library, part 204:

⭐️⭐️⭐️⭐️⭐️⭐️⭐️☆☆☆ (7/10)

Sekiro is one of those games I respect more than I enjoy. It's beautifully crafted, deeply original, and fiercely committed to its own identity. But at the same time, it’s punishingly rigid and often feels like it's testing your reflexes and muscle memory more than your strategy or creativity. Coming from I expected a challenge, but this was a whole different beast. It's also a game that draws a hard line in the sand. If you’re not ready to master parrying under pressure, you’re out. For some, that’s exhilarating. For others, myself included—it’s exhausting. Fun? Sometimes. Impressive? Absolutely. But built for me? Sadly, no.

100% completion finally!
Took 43.5 hours on a single file, but i already have 30-isch hours in other files as well so had already gotten most achievements. I don't think I would recommend 100%ing this game unless you want to replay the game way more times than it takes to get all the endings or grinding a bit. The all skills achievement is not hard but it does take a lot of time to get, this either means something like 5 or 6 playthroughs, or it means grinding for an hour or two in NG+3.

Beyond 100% this is an incredible game that I think is a must play. It's absolutely one of my favorite games of all times with only one real flaw in my mind which is certain boss arenas. This is par for the course for fromsoft, but certain arenas are just incredibly uncomfortable to play in, especially with how the camera works with lock on but I think it's the worst in this game. A lot of enemies have very small arenas yet when parrying you take a lot of knockback so it's very common to be pushed up against the wall and for the camera to freak out. Either learn to play without lock on or learn to position yourself properly.

Okay okay I'm done complaining. The bosses in this game are incredibly consistent in quality, with only one boss and one mini-boss that I strictly dislike. And saying consistent makes it sound like they're all good but that's just not the case, they're all incredible. Yea there are moves I don't like, but you learn to play around them. This game is also incredibly satisfying to play through when you've learned it and the replay value is absolutely there. The learning curve is steep, but when you're past it? Nothing beats the feeling of playing this. 9.5/10 game, please play it.

1. Intro & Recommendation
Overall Verdict: ★★★★★ – Strongly Recommended
Key Takeaway: A masterful blend of precision combat, vertical exploration, and grim, atmospheric storytelling—designed for players ready for an uncompromising challenge.
Context: Action‑adventure; ~30 hrs to complete main story. Tested on high‑end PC (RTX 4050 / 16 GB RAM).

2. Gameplay & Mechanics
Sekiro departs from Souls‑style attrition mechanics and focuses instead on posture-based parries and aggressive counters. Deflecting enemy strikes builds posture damage; Once broken, enemies are vulnerable to lethal deathblows. A unique feature is the resurrection mechanic, allowing players to revive immediately upon death, adding a strategic layer to combat by offering a second chance to turn the tide in difficult encounters. Additionally, most attacks in Sekiro deal damage based on a percentage of your total health rather than fixed numbers, meaning that it only takes one or two hits to result in instant death. This design choice truly requires precision and avoidance over tanking hits.

Boss Encounters: feel like rhythmic duels requiring both mental acuity and muscle memory.
Traversal: Grappling, stealth, verticality, and mobility options add layered strategy.

MDA Breakdown:
Mechanics: Tight parry/deflection system; Prosthetic tools (shuriken, flame vent, grappling hook).
High-tempo, punishing yet fair with every mistake being very costly.
You know you can never go wrong with a Samurai theme.

Snow-draped castles, moss-covered temples, shadowy valleys—all intricately designed with Japanese artistic influence. Environments are vertically layered, tactically rich, and visually arresting.

The soundtrack isn’t memorable in a traditional sense, but every sound cue enhances tension, especially during stealth or boss fights, enhancing the experience.

You play as Wolf, a shinobi on a mission to rescue his kidnapped lord. Unlike other Souls games, Sekiro offers a voiced protagonist and clearer dialogue-based storytelling.

Deeper layers are discovered through optional exploration, item descriptions, and cryptic NPC dialogue—ideal for players who enjoy piecing together subtle world-building.

Runs smoothly on PC with consistent 60 FPS on a modern hardware.
Some minor lag-spikes moments if you're running the game for hours.
Overall solid, with only a few minor edge-case bugs.

Sekiro is undoubtedly punishing during both the early and mid-game phases. The game’s combat system is one of the most demanding in the genre, requiring strict timing, precision, and discipline. However, once the rhythm of combat becomes familiar, the experience shifts noticeably — encounters feel more manageable, and the game’s difficulty begins to feel deliberate rather than overwhelming.

maintains a relentless pace, driven by its challenging combat and structured progression. The game's design incorporates vertical exploration and stealth mechanics, offering occasional pacing shifts that provide brief respites without detracting from the overall intensity. These elements are seamlessly integrated into the game's world, allowing players to approach encounters from various angles

~30 hrs for a single playthrough; multiple endings and NG+ encourage replays.

– Players who love challenging, precise combat
– Fans of tight level design and vertical exploration
– Story-driven gamers who enjoy indirect world-building


– Casual players or those who dislike high-difficulty learning curves
– Players seeking RPG customization or multiplayer features


– Best-in-class combat mechanics: posture, parry, and timing | Precision Combat Mechanics: Sekiro's combat system emphasizes posture-based parries and aggressive counters, requiring players to master timing and rhythm for success.
– Deep, multi-layered world design | The game features interconnected environments with hidden paths, encouraging strategic navigation and discovery.
– Boss fights that are intriguing, emotional (mostly in a bad way) and mechanically satisfying.


– High difficulty may alienate casual or time-constrained players
– Occasional repetitive stealth loops due to AI resets
– Minor technical quirks: grapple bugs, animation glitches...

Sekiro is a technical masterpiece that redefines the action-adventures genre with an intense precise combat. It can be very demanding in aspects like skills, timing, and strategy, rewarding players who master its unique posture-breaking mechanics. The world is immersive and atmospheric, filled with rich lore, stunning environments, and memorable boss battles that test your patience till the limit.

Posted: July 4
Sekiro: A category of its own
When I first started this game, I really wasn't feeling it. Having played many other games with heavy parry mechanics, I just didn't understand why every enemy kept blocking my attacks, and waiting to parry every enemy for counterattacks felt very tedious. It also didn't help that just about every section of the map was packed with enemies that would swarm me as soon as one got alerted to my presence. I didn't find the game "hard" per se; I beat every one of the early bosses I encountered on the first try, but I found the overall gameplay very tedious.

I was about to call it quits, until I beat a certain boss, that I finally understood the game and its core combat mechanic: posture.

See, what got me confused is that Sekiro kept getting described as a "parry game". And while parrying is somewhat of an important mechanic in this game, Sekiro should be more accurately described as an "aggression game". This is because that unlike other parry focused games out there, Sekiro isn't really about the patient dance of parrying and counterattacking.
Really, it's more about hacking away at enemies and consistently applying pressure due to the posture mechanic. Because guess what, it doesn't matter if your enemies block your attacks; your goal for 95% of encounters is to overwhelm your enemies with enough attacks to allow you to land that insta-kill deathblow.

And it is AMAZING how much you can get away with by simply tapping the attack button. Oh no, you opponent is about to hit you with an unblockable attack? Well, just thwack them in the face with your sword and they get interrupted (disclaimer: does not work every time). It was pretty funny realising that instead of waiting for enemies to attack, I could simply continuously attack, parry counterattacks every 2-3 hits, and then just keep repeating the same process.

Also, dodging was often times a lot more efficient than parrying, because several bosses would straight up not finish their combo if you dodged to get behind them. This is most obvious with the much hated boss, which is trivialised by merely getting behind him, and jumping every now and then.

The second most important thing that I learned the hard way, was just how much is important in this game. Sekiro absolutely SUCKS at dealing with more than one enemy at a time; it doesn't even matter what sort of grunt you're dealing with- face more than one enemy at a time and you end up dying very quickly.
So once I actually started playing like a ninja- crouching and luring away enemies for backstabs, exploring the world became a lot more tolerable and even enjoyable.

Can take awhile to get used to, but once the posture mechanic "clicks", along with adopting a stealth approach to exploration, Sekiro can become a very satisfying experience. ()

The bad:

* The Dragonrot mechanic is half-baked

* The visuals are excellent
