I suck at this game. Let's get that out of the way first. I have 11 hours in this game and I have not once made it past that grabby golem. I find the game to be incredibly boring when you have to fill up the enemy parry bar so you can actually win the fight. This game has changed the video game industry for the worse.

Every game that has come out, based on this game's popularity, has put parrying into their games. Dodging is not an option anymore in Soulslike games. It's just rampant parrying. And parrying for little to no reward. Look at Lies of P Overture. You parry like a mad man for 10-20 hit combos only to get in one, single R1 attack then go right back to parrying. Parrying has even jumped the shark now, as it's in JRPGs. Clair Obscur Expedition 33 has parrying in it. That's crazy.

This game has irrevocably changed the landscape of the gaming industry. Parrying games is the new World War 2 shooter. Because video game developers are parrots, not independent thinkers. They just want to copy what is popular instead of creating something new. Parrying has always been around in games like this since Demon's Souls, but now the focus is on parrying. As an aging gamer, that takes me out of the game, as it were.

I keep using Lies of P in a Sekiro review, but before the DLC, there were many options for combat in that game: dodging, throwables, the Aegis shield. With the release of the Overture DLC, all that is gone now. Throwables are nerfed and do no damage now (unless you find the ring that "increases damage of throwables" READ: Puts them back to pre-nerf levels), the Aegis shield can only block 3 hits before your stamina is gone, and forget attacking from behind the shield as there is no stamina to do that. Dodging is gone too, because of HUGE enemies in TINY arenas that LITERALLY pin P against the wall and force parrying.

Why am I bringing up Lies of P so much in a Sekiro review? Because the director of Lies of P wanted to be a Sekiro speedrunner. This game has single handedly ruined this industry and the Souls/Soulslike genre. At least Black Myth Wukong is out there doing the lord's work and allows the player to dodge instead of parry (even though the game does have a single parry skill, it's not completely dependent on it). And the parry in BMW actually REWARDS the player.

Hopefully, this silly parry fad runs its course and the next thing that comes out is better than that, but I doubt it will be. *Sigh* Back to Dark Souls 1 challenge runs, I guess...

Posted: June 29
Like all From Software games, I recommend you play this with a second screen, so you can use checklists and search for stuff online.

Sekiro has the best combat system in From Software games, only rivaled by Bloodborne. With that being said, it also has some of the most unbalanced 'mini-bosses' in any of the games, with the hardest difficulty curves. The bosses require cheese strategies to beat, like luring Snake-eye Shirahagi into the poison pool. Furthermore, there are occasional fights that have absurd problems, such as not registering counters, bosses that poise through attacks, and terrible camera angles that prevent you from playing the game (like the Lone Shadow fight at Ashina Reservoir).

It is also very easy to get lost in the game, because there are a lot of secret paths, that require knowledge of where and when to grapple. Undoubtedly, a lot of players made the decision to quit before beating the game, because it is too challenging. The Dragonrot mechanic does not help motivate players either, who feel like every death has the possibility of ruining their playthrough. In reality, it is barely an inconvenience, as you can cure all NPCs with a single droplet, and there are many droplets in the game. All you need to do is kill a bunch of bosses to open up new areas, and then use a droplet to progress through multiple quests.

Many players agree that the game gets much better in new-game plus, as knowing where to go, and internalizing enemy attack patterns makes the game more fun and satisfying, this is your 'real' playthrough. With that being said, Sekiro is not for everybody. I picked the game up years after I 'quit' it, because I got lost, and after having beaten it, I can conclude that there's nothing else like this game. The combat is extraordinarily satisfying, and I like it more than Elden Ring for this reason.

Posted: June 28
By all accounts, I should dislike this game. I don't really like Souls games; I don't really enjoy parrying as a mechanic; I don't particularly seek out difficulty in my games. That all being said.. I absolutely LOVED Sekiro.

The core strength of the game is its combat system, which features one of the best fundamental designs of any game on the market. Rather than simply whittle an enemy's health to zero, you want to break their posture, which functions sort of like an auxiliary healthbar. Breaking an enemy's posture makes them vulnerable to a 'deathblow' attack, which will instantly kill them regardless of what their current health is. Similarly, enemies can break YOUR posture, leaving you wide open to attack. At the center of posture is, as Sekiro is known for, deflection—you can block enemy attacks, just as they'll block yours. But time a block carefully and you'll instead parry, which completely negates any posture damage you take and actually damages enemy posture instead. This means that carefully-timed attacks and parries alike play a role in your offense so you can break your enemy and land that sweet deathblow.

It's really a genius system, and the tempo of attacking and parrying consistently hits a hypnotic flow. I think it works because, in large part, it's actually quite simple. Sekiro doesn't really have any RPG features—you have one weapon and a modest array of supplemental abilities. This means you can really focus on getting the timing right to hit that flowstate and conquer enemies, which is especially satisfying because the more difficult enemies will absolutely trounce you when you first encounter them. Sekiro takes a "fundamentals first" approach to game design, and it consistently pays off during enemy and boss encounters.

That all being said, the game isn't perfect. As I had touched upon, this isn't an RPG, and, while you have a few skill trees and secondary abilities, the progression for those isn't particularly satisfying. Shinobi Prosthetics, which function as subweapons of sorts, often fail to be useful outside of their dedicated niche; as a result, acquiring and upgrading (more like side-grading) them isn't particularly interesting, nor does it really change your playstyle. Similarly, a lot of the Combat Arts and Ninjutsu abilities are undercooked, with the latter category only have three options in the ENTIRE game. This all amounts to a gameplay experience that doesn't change much as you work your way through the game. Thankfully, the core combat is so good that you'll still be engaged. Nonetheless, it's a little disappointing, considering that FromSoft are typically masters of character customization in their games, be it an RPG warrior or a giant mech.

A final note about difficulty: if you're interested in Sekiro, but turned off by the difficulty, don't be. Yes, the game is very hard. I'm also not a particularly good gamer—I probably spent 6-8 hours on the final boss alone, which was no doubt the most difficult enemy I've ever defeated in any game (not to mention plenty of other death streaks throughout my overall playtime). That being said, as I mentioned earlier in this review, the core gameplay of Sekiro is relatively simple. The game isn't actually asking you to do a lot: really, you just need to attack and use 1-3 different defensive options when prompted. Rather than leaning on complexity, the game demands consistency; as a result, merely practicing difficult fights to hone your sense of timing WILL lead you to success eventually. Sekiro is not a mechanically demanding game, nor does it require particularly fast reflexes; all you have to do is commit to trying again, and you will ultimately see victory. The vast majority of fights feel very fair, with well-telegraphed attacks; you just need to memorize those attacks and act accordingly.

This all being said, I would HIGHLY recommend Sekiro. It offers a definitive gameplay experience than any and all action fans should try. There's a reason every game seems to have parrying in it nowadays—Sekiro is that reason, and I've yet to encounter a game that handles parry-focused combat as well as Sekiro does. With an amazing first-time experience and plenty of NG+ options, Sekiro is well-worth the full asking price.

Farm to get skills that are necessary to beat bosses. The levels are just not that big and you don't get enough 'stuff' the first time through that your forced to look up the best farming spot, turn on some music, and farm for a couple hours at at time. I'm glad I powered through the final boss which is no doubt the hardest in FromSoftware games. But i'm happy to say i'll never play this game again.

I don't even know where to begin—this game is good. Like, GOOD GOOD. It might take a while to get used to the parry system, but once it clicks, it’s one of the most satisfying mechanics in any game I’ve played. You’ll die. A lot. You’ll die to bosses, minibosses, maybe even the very first NPC you meet. But every death is a lesson, and slowly, steadily—you get better.

There’s no leveling system here. The only thing that improves is you. Your timing, your strategy, your patience. It's tough, it's punishing, but it’s fair. And once you overcome each challenge, the payoff is exhilarating.

It’s fun. It’s challenging. It demands your full attention and rewards your growth. Just remember the Shinobi code:

"As long as your intentions are honorable, fight as dishonorably as you can."

Easily the best Fromsoft game thus far, albeit shorter than its predecessors (but that necessarily isn't a bad thing in this specific case).

This game has everything: insanely good combat (although limited to one "main weapon" and no "leveling stats through money/EXP", you can equip other subweapons to adjust to your enemy and learn special skills), compelling and interesting story, and an ambiance that Fromsoft almost never fails to deliver on. The environment is also a small, yet complex interwoven web which helps reinforce the links across Ashina.

After the miserable experience that was Elden Ring, I delved again into this game on Steam and replayed its entirety for a few friends on stream (which was gifted to me even though I already had a PS4 version, thank you very much!). This was as good as I had remembered it to be, and reminded me of the time when Fromsoft understood how to make good boss designs:

1. Enemies and bosses have clear tells, flashes, and reads you can pick up and trust you won't get murdered for, for no reason (No button reading BS like in Elden Ring. Bosses will punish your bad decisions on the spot, or through your own mistakes on reading/inputting your commands.)
2. The encounters are fast paced, well thought of and every moment can be an opportunity, depending on how you tackle them.
3. Enemies and bosses are beholden to the same rules as the player: if you get your posture destroyed, you take massive punishment... But so does the enemy.
4. Using the same tactic all the time does not help you defeat bosses, nor does spamming a guard without learning proper timings. You WILL get punished if you rely on the same crutches, and the game varies a lot on the strategies to take each boss on, and it also gives a lot of powerful hints on how to tackle them at times.

The only, single blemish in the entire game has to be the , as it has quite a few problematic elements which lead to really unfair deaths, with next to no recourse other than tanking the hit and healing after. Namely the .

But otherwise, this is easily the best Soulsborne Fromsoft has to offer to this day, and it's not even close. It is to the point, fast paced yet meticulous, simple yet deep, and more importantly, extremely fun to play.

Get it at all costs if you enjoy any good action game or Soulsborne. You won't be disappointed.

Posted: June 26
A Masterpiece of Combat — Intense, Rewarding, and Unforgettable

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is the kind of game that grabs you and doesn’t let go. It’s been a long time since a game had me gripping my controller so tightly that my fingers literally went numb—and I loved every second of it.

The boss fights are absolutely fantastic. Each one is challenging, intense, and beautifully designed. Every victory feels truly earned, and the sense of accomplishment when you finally bring a boss down is unmatched.

I also love how the game world is handled. It’s not a massive open world, but a focused map filled with secrets, treasures, and hidden paths. Exploration is always rewarding, and you’re constantly uncovering something new.

The story is another strong point. It’s straightforward, clear, and easy to follow—without losing the depth and emotional weight that FromSoftware is known for.

What really stood out to me is how progression works. Every boss you beat makes you stronger, without needing to constantly change gear or manage endless equipment. The skill system is great, too—choosing different abilities can significantly change how you approach combat, adding variety and depth.

Sekiro filled a void I didn’t even realize I had after finishing other games. I’m already looking forward to chasing down all four endings—it’s that good.

This is a game that’s worth every penny. If you’re looking for a tight, focused, and deeply satisfying action game, Sekiro is one you absolutely shouldn’t miss.

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is a masterfully crafted action-adventure game from FromSoftware that blends punishing combat, a gripping narrative, and a beautifully atmospheric world inspired by Sengoku-era Japan. Unlike the Souls series, Sekiro abandons traditional RPG elements like character builds and stats, opting instead for precise swordplay, stealth mechanics, and a more defined protagonist – the Wolf.

The combat is fast, fluid, and deeply satisfying, requiring players to learn enemy patterns and perfect their timing. It’s a true test of patience and skill. Every boss fight feels like a duel, and every victory is earned, not handed. The posture system and parry-heavy mechanics make each encounter tense and rewarding.

The world design is stunning. From snow-capped mountains to burning temples, each area is rich with lore, hidden paths, and haunting beauty. The story, while less cryptic than previous FromSoftware titles, delivers emotional weight and moral complexity, especially through the choices that affect the game's multiple endings.

Sekiro isn’t for everyone – it’s brutally difficult and offers little hand-holding. But for those willing to rise to the challenge, it’s a truly unforgettable journey.

FromSoftware had something unique in mind when they decided to make this narrative driven game. In contrast to previous and later titles, Sekiro does not involve a custom character with no voice lines. Instead, it is a FromSoft. style game that follows a voiced protagonist through a detailed story. While there are still familiar FromSoft. ways of lore building through item descriptions, there is so much more to gain from character interactions than your usual title by the same developers. This also means that Sekiro, the protagonist, only specializes in one primary weapon with secondary tools used to tackle different challenges or provide more player freedom.

What differs this game from the rest, in terms of gameplay, would be its rhyme style combat. Although other titles include parrying as an "important" mechanic, parrying is vital to the progress of this game. Failing to master timing parries--and yes, the game rewards or punishes you based on how well you time your parries--can result in a far greater challenge on the more fast paced bosses. In some instances, you may even run into progression barriers. This can be especially frustrating when the controls can interrupt your flow. For instance, the parry button is also the same button that--when pressed simultaneously or even in close succession with the attack button--can trigger special attacks that cannot be canceled out of, therefore leaving you open to attack. For those who master the perfect parry, however, it may well be the easier FromSoft title to date! Take that as you will.

I should also mention the "dragon rot" mechanic, but without spoiling too much. You can skip this paragraph if you wish to keep that part a complete surprise. To put it simply, it is the way FromSoftware tries to increase the "difficulty" or perhaps the stress that their games are typically known for. It does so by punishing you for dying, or rather it punishes NPCs who then become "inert" when it comes to their side quests. This is completely unnecessary and can and has turned many people off from continuing the game. If they create a difficult game expecting players to die, then punishing them for playing the game just isn't good business. While there are remedies to this, they are few and far between. Since Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice doesn't have a soul/echo mechanic like the other games, where you drop all of your experience and have to pick them back up or slay the enemy carrying them, this clearly must be FromSoftware's substitution. Honestly, they should have just stuck to what they usually do.

When the game works as intended, and it recognizes the inputs you provide, it can be one of the best FromSoftware titles. At times that the game seemingly ignores you and does as it pleases, it can be perhaps the worst, and proof that FromSoftware should instead stick to what they know best. I, for one, am glad they tried something so ambitious, but I would be a liar if I didn't admit to be frustrated with the game at times. Still, despite its shortcomings, it is a very rewarding game to play, especially every time you accomplish a deathblow on a powerful adversary. With that being said, purchase at your own risk. You'll know well before the refund window closes if it's something you're willing to invest your valuable money and especially time on.

Truly a masterpiece. I had always heard FromSoft fans talk about this game, and it is typically considered the most difficult title FS has released. I got frustrated with it the first time around because I had just beaten Elden Ring and expected it to play similarly, and I refunded it. After shaking the salt off, I came back to it and really focused on learning this completely new style of gameplay, and I fell in love. Yes, there is the trademark FS ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ in some parts (looking at you, headless ape fight), but in general Sekiro is a very fair game with a parry system that is perfection. Many people describe it as a rhythm game, and that when the combat finally clicks with you, it becomes amazing. I can honestly agree to this, as once I finally clicked with the game, each boss fight became like a dance. Some dances are harder than others, but a dance nonetheless. I usually only write reviews for games I dislike, but every so often I like a game so much I’ll review it positively. I’ve had Sekiro for a while, so why not write a positive review earlier? Simply put: I didn’t know how much I liked Sekiro until I played something similar that showed me just how good Sekiro was. This game in question is Lies of P. In short, I really dislike LoP, for many reasons, chief amongst them is the mechanics. LoP tries too hard to mix the systems and mechanics of ER, Sekiro, Dark Souls, and Bloodborne together while also maintaining it’s own identity. This results in a frustrating hodgepodge of enemies, mechanics, and other jank that really only exist to piss you off. LoP tried to implement a parry system similar to Sekiro’s, and it is just straight up boiled dog ass. The timing is too tight, the animations are not clear enough on when exactly to parry, there are no interesting countering mechanics to a lot of the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ attacks, etc. There are other cheap imitations in LoP that show just how much better Sekiro is, such as the Legion Arm compared against the Shinobi Prosthetic, and more. Playing Sekiro after that dumpster fire is such a breath of fresh air, and is exactly what I needed to cleanse my palate of LoP. Nothing substitutes testing your mettle against the Sword Saint, who is probably the perfect boss fight; tough, but fair. I say all of this to say that Sekiro is the gold standard for a game with these types of mechanics, and that if you are on the fence of whether you should buy it or not, I say yes, because it will be money well spent.

FromSoftware does it again—but this time, with a Katana in hand and no multiplayer safety net in sight. Sekiro is unforgiving, unrelenting, and utterly brilliant. The parry-focused combat system demands precision and timing, but when you hit that perfect deflection rhythm, it's pure bliss. The stealth mechanics are fluid, the boss fights are breathtaking (and sometimes controller-breaking), and the world building is as rich as any Miyazaki creation.
What sets Sekiro apart is its insistence that you adapt. There's no grinding your way through; it's about mastering your tools and learning every boss’s tell. The lack of traditional RPG elements throws some people off, but if you're looking for tight action, meaningful progression, and a true test of patience and skill—it doesn’t get much better.
10/10 would get annihilated by Genichiro again.

Just like anything else by fromsoftware its just another beat your head into the wall simulator but thats not a bad thing at all
Locations you visit are beautiful, creepy, whatever depending where you go but the vibe is always on point for the locale. Combat is definitely difficult as youd expect from anything made by fromsoftware but never impossible so long as you have the patience itll drag you in and make you wanna master it. The story was decent and interactive so it really can go wherever you wanna go! Weather it be the way of a hero or a demon...Its a decently fun time. Only issues I saw were: 1.) This is not a soulslike, yes its made by the soul creators of the soulslike genre but this is just an overly difficult action RPG. DO NOT try this if youre going to come into it thinking it is darksouls, elden ring, bloodborne, yada yada. 2.) Weird input lag, not sure if its my rig (32gb DDR4, 4TB of m.2 SSD storage NO HDDs, 5800x processor, and 3090 GPU) but PLENTY of times id be spamming jump when getting near a ledge because singular presses would not go through and I'd fall to my death which i found to be SUPER annoying and even then it would still come through late at times (not to be confused with it coming in after beginning to run, whenever you begin to run you dash first which will put your jump input in AFTER the initial dash) and no amount of lower graphics, closing other programs would help the issue so be warned if you have a lower end rig or if you already preemptively know this will drive you insane. It possibly also affects blocking for parrys but ngl i didnt notice an issue with that and i 100%'d (shoutout cynic 0055 for the baller guide that helped me efficiently get all achievements in a minimal amount of time, if i did it all my own with how much stuff you gotta do for each ending i wouldve easily doubled my play time and play throughs go check it out if you want to efficiently 100 the game) it so even if it does you can still complete the game with that issue persisting if you just spam the block button. Overall I personally think this games a 7/10 because its 6 years old so the input lag being the way it is just shouldnt be an issue, esp for the price theyre asking you pay to play it still in 2025. So that knocks it down from the 9/10 i would have given it for the story and combat being as good as they are.

Posted: June 18
Easily one of the greatest games of all time. Here From Software blend their fine tuned signature gameplay and difficulty into a new revamped ideal; setting Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice on its own pedestal as the crowning champion.

One key feature or change to the norm is both player and opponent have a posture bar. When the posture bar is exhausted you and the opponent are open to attack or deathblow. This system rewards aggressive sword play and perfectly timed parries, which in turn makes the game play more like a rhythm game than a traditional Dark Souls experience. Master the dance of sword play, however, and you'll be unmatched, unequalled and truly satisfied and fulfilled.

The game's story really shines here, ditching creatable characters for one protagonist and a set of intriguing main characters, each with their own tales, secrets and backstory. Themes of duty and love, honour, friendship and family are the foreground to a bustling feudal Japanese backdrop with fantastical elements.

The lands of Ashina and beyond are truly beautiful and breathtaking to behold. From Software are not known for incredible graphical fidelity, but what they lack there, they more than make up for in visual style. Several areas of the game you will be turning the HUD off and scanning your keyboard for the screenshot key.

This game truly is Miyazaki and From Soft in their prime. All in all, the gameplay style and learning curve can be a complete turn off, but if you're patient and persistent the pay off is worth the struggle, twenty times over. A solid 9/10 from me, as close to perfect as it gets and a must play for any fan of RPG, Stealth and action games.

A very unique souls-like title that introduces a parry based combat system which may throw a lot of people off. Overall the games story is a dark masterpiece similar to the rest of fromsoftware titles. The challenge is there for those that enjoy them! Considered to be one of the more controversial titles that fromsoftware made, due to its difficulty and different combat system it offers a rewarding experience after an intense fight.


Feel free to check out an overview of all on a spreadsheet which is updated regularly.

Posted: June 17
One of from soft best off beat experimental title. That shows even when straying far from their usual formula that they can produce a really uniquely challenging and satisfying gameplay.

So while a prior title blood borne done away with shield and mocked player for its induced passivity. Sekiro also does away with the shield but tells you actually blocking is very satisfying and is just another tool for aggression.d borne done away with shield and mocked player for its induced passivity.

The deflecting mechanic is at its most satisfying against bosses. The sound of successfully timed block is just music to the ear when you deflect a barrage of strike. Its like a symphony of clashing sword.

You must answer with aggression after successfully deflecting or bosses will regain stamina when not pressured. This result in intense sword dance with bosses that may induce IRL fatigue. I certainly had my finger gotten sore from constant deflecting and even ended up wearing out the L1 bumper. That aside, the dance itself is fun and the rush of winning is just the bonus.

To ensure the game isn't just deflecting some enemy and bosses will throw a curve ball with low sweep, poke and grab and you need to act appropriately. Mikiri Counter for thrust is always satisfying, I find myself thinking about Mikiri on thrust in other games.

This game has been the inspiration for many deflect based games. But the original is a must to experience.

Posted: June 17
Sekiro is that one game that doesn't try to pull a trick mid fight just to make the game more challenging. The game rewards you for mastering the combat, and fights become easier once you know which moves to punish.

Dark Souls is known for stat checking you constantly, which is why the first game got its reputation for being extremely difficult when in reality new players reached a late game area too early (it doesn't help that you can access said areas at the beginning of the game). Once you find a weapon that's really busted, then the difficulty gets trivialized because you out-damage enemies and do enough damage to bosses to where you don't have to constantly dodge their attacks all the time. Sekiro isn't like that. It's all a skill check, so fights become more rewarding because of it AND Remember HESITATE AND YOU LOSE.

Posted: June 16
I played through the game and while the bosses were (some very much so) enjoyable and fun, the rest of the game just isn't there. The areas look stunning, but traversing through them with this games janky movement just makes the exploration -at least to me- unenjoyable. Adding to that, fighting basically any of the enemies in the areas has very negligible upsides, leveling and farming just isn't much of a thing in this game (which I actually enjoy, you actually have to get better). This, though, has the effect that the easiest way to get to fun and meaningful parts of the game is to simply skip overwhelming majority of the fighting in the areas, which makes every single enemy other than bosses and minibosses feel like complete filler. Not to mention that the design of these fodder enemies is often leaning very heavily towards causing the maximum amount of frustration, instead of providing interesting and difficult fights.

TLDR; If you really want to experience some really good boss fights, get this game, but expect to have to slog through endlessly obtuse and janky game to get to the actually great parts. For most people though, I just cannot recommend it.

It's really only marred by some unintuitive and annoying level design, which is fairly minor in comparison to its strengths. Absolutely would highly recommend to anyone even slightly interested. The story is both less interesting (in my personal opinion) and more straightforwardly told than From usually is known for, but it's by no means bad or poorly executed. That said, the primary, secondary, and tertiary focus of this game are the combat, which is fantastic, and the bosses, which are likewise excellent.

Its on of my favorite from fromsoftware, i know people probably like bloodborn more, and i do love it too, but im not a playstation person, i played it offline and while i don't mind 30fps after a while it can be jarring, i did like my secret ending i got i think with the doll (redacted) .

Anyway, Sekiro for me is the game where combat clicked, it is a rythm game at its core, and it is by far the hardest, i don't beleive there is anyway to actually one shot bosses in this one meanwhile there was a way in other fromsoft game (thats how i judge difficulty, is using all the tools in the game allow you to one shot the boss while using all tools in this game doesn't then this one is harder) you can't cheese as much or atleast in the same way, tho tools still give a huge advantage so you should use them to your advantage, i don't mind cheesing , im just saying, this game is harder it play differently and you have to meet it on its therms before you can break it down, if ever there was an actual git gud fromsoft game , then its probably this one.

However i dislike that git gud mentality, i find it both toxic and reductive of what fromsoftware create, their game are a lot deeper than boss hard me defeat it me gloat internet...

Its a must play, but go into it knowing that it will be probably more challenging than u might expect, that is until it clicks, and u do a second playthrough and realise how much of a breeze it can be, its so fun...

I think a negative for this one, the ogre at the start, the fight is quite different than most of what you encounter troughtout the game and it incentivise dodging instead of deflecting (i suppose you are supposed to use fire, or firecrackers), but and this was confirmed to me by having friends ragequit and drop this game because of this guy...
It being mandatory to access the whole rest of the game i feel maybe it wasn't a good call, its a barrier that isn't intuitive to most new player and doesn't incentivise the main mechanic of deflection, the game do a good job at teaching mikiri for exemple actually too good of a job i would say it rly hammer it home but it still so satisfying to pull off everytime.. but this guy i don't think he acheive something beneficial, and even on my own playthrough while i did not rage quit, it felt odd, like the i was finally allowed to play the game after i defeat an enemy that didn't belong, you never meet anything like this again for the rest of the game. so ya outside of that wierd choice its easly one of my favorite game, the vista are pretty too , i kinda love jungles, and snow and asian architecture, i love most older periods regardless of location tbh... maybe outside of deserts.

oh i guess also, random nitpick, i kinda missed a grappling spot to the main tower didn't see it, so i did like all the game side stuff before noticing and being confused where is it going next, because i had missed the actual path forward and first fight with geni (not counting tutorial, as a result i basicly did the ape before geni, and the haunted forest area and all the harder enemy, then arrived to him wich is funny but also kinda wierd that the main path you have to look up and notice a grapple icon to enter a window,i honestly would have never found it on my own because i felt like i checked this area until a friend pointed it out. felt dumb a bit of me haha.

10/10 Masterpiece!

This game is amazing, but also the most frustrating game I've ever played (it's my first Souls-like game).
The story mode is interesting, the soundtrack is fenominal, the gameplay is fantastic but very hard to master (I played this game with keyboard + mouse, btw.).

The boss fights are VERY HARD, even the mini-bosses. I've given up many times, but something in my heart tells me I have to finish the game. Not to get all the achievements, but just to finish the game.

So I tried, tried and tried. I wasted many hours on the same boss. When I reached the final boss (even though I had skipped Owl (Father)), I had a difficult time.
The final boss reads my inputs and kills me every single time.

So I did what they call a pro-gamer move: I just installed the "Sekiro The Easy" mod.
AND BOY OH BOY! THIS MOD IS TOO POWERFUL!!! It only took me two minutes to beat the boss. XD (I wish I had found out about this mod earlier. T_T)
I would still recommend playing the game without mods. I didn't use any mods before the final boss fight and I had one boss fight that took me 3 hours (I'm looking at you, Great Shinobi Owl).
If you just want to experience the story mode, you should use the mod I mentioned.

You have to treat this game like an open-world fighting game! Except that your health bar is gone after two hits. You have to parry like in Street Fighter 3 Third Strike, you have to dodge like in Soul Calibur, and you have to attack like in Killer Instinct, where the boss recovers after a few hits and gives you hell with COMBO BREAKERs or rushes at you like a tank.

(I wish FromSoftware had included an easy or story difficulty mode so blockheads like me could enjoy the game without getting stuck on the same boss for several hours.)

I don't know if I ever want to play a Souls-like game again, since I'm so bad at that genre. It's also very time consuming and I still have a lot of games in my backlog that I need to finish.

Overall:
Anyway, I'm glad this game is available on PC. It would be great if more FromSoftware games were available on GOG. Just for the sake of game preservation.

Im going to make a very bold claim here: The gameplay is objectively bad.

Its fine if you like it, its fine you "got good", i get the appeal. Its still objectively bad.

Discplaimer: Im very early on in the game (and Im waiting on a refund), so Im literally just at the 2nd boss - the chained big guy. From what I can read - but not evaluate in the context of this review - my point about parrying not even being neccessary below would change later on, but I dont know that.

So let me describe the actual gameplay, vs what you are actually supposed to do with parrying:

What you are supposed to do:
Parry attacks from enemies... because otherwise you get ♥♥♥♥♥♥.
Let me make it more clear: Parrying isnt satisfying or per se good (let me explain below), its just that doing something else gets you ♥♥♥♥♥♥ because of hitboxes.

How the actual gameplay makes you want to play (again, before and including the chained Ogre Boss):
Normal enemies: Spam attack while isolating them one by one.
Bosses: Either dont need to parry (ill explain) or you cant (I dont really see what attacks I can parry with the Ogre boss)

Why am I saying this? And what exactly am I saying:
1) Parrying is stupid
2) Parrying isnt needed,
3) Except you are kind of forced to...

1) With what I have seen so far, every time in terms of actual gameplay is literally punishing you for parrying:
If I parry an attack from an enemy, my bar fills up, which is bad. Im am literally punished for parrying. So I am literally incentivised to dodge, walk back or to the side or jump up/away. I cannot see (before the chained Ogre boss) any circumstance where parrying would actually help me.
In fact im confuse entirely about why its even in the game: I can literally just hold the parry button nd dont take any damage, and I never need to release it, because I dont have stamina that stops me from doing so. In fact - from readng discussions on it - doing this, literally recovers your own bar faster.

Yet somehow parrying is supposed to be the entire "thing" of the game? "You are dancing the with the enemies, not fighting them enymore once you master it" or "Its like music, you have to get the rythm down"
I dont see it. Like at all.
- Im punished for parrying by having my own bar fill up
- And i literally can just hold the parry button pressed and Im invinceible until I see a red sign and jump away, which I do anyway, because parrying is literally bad for me in terms of gameplay

Now this applies definitely - and I would argue objectively - to normal enemies, and mostly to bosses.
Hence point 2) Parrying is quite literally not needed. Not at all for normal enemies and only slightly (so far) for bosses.

I literally tried this out before the ogre boss. There are the 2 Samurai at the fire. So i killed the first/weak one and then death after death at the ogre boss i just spammed attacks on him without enything else. 100% of the time (like 10 times) he just died and had no counter at all. Even the times when he hit a perfect parry himself (spraky red parry - I assume thats a perfect parry?) literally nothing happened to me and I just kept on spaming attack... because there is literally nothing stopping me.

Some other enemies sometimes have the red attacks that you need to dodge... except you can more often than not just keep spammming attack to interrupt them from doing it. There is third point here (4) but Ill explain that later.
This even works on bosses sometimes and if timed right ( = mostly by accident).

Above I wrote:
3) (Parrying isnt needed,) Except you are kind of forced to...

why do I write this: because before the Ogre boss, there is a small boss that y+ou have to slice up 2 times. With that boss, you at least CAN parry - which I cant say about the ogre. And since it was the first real boss i experimented with it a bit. So yeah you can parry... but with his one you still did absoluetly not need to. But you can.

Except sometimes you are forced to. Its not just an option, but you actually need to. (Im sure if you are a dark Souls speedrunner you ca get over rthis, I wasnt in my 2 hours)
The reason is because the hit boxes often are insane, so dodging is impossible.

This is also tied to point 4, so lts go over it:
4) What the hell do you do with those red attacks?
In the tutorial it says that you can - and thats my understanding of it - either jump or dash away from the attack thats not able to be parried. Except both for those "red" attacks but also normal attacks, it often does not work because of the hitbox and a mechanic the bosses have.
A) Hitboxes: I dont know how many times I for sure dodged an attack or saw the attack go over my head yet I still got hit. Sure Dark Souls (3) isnt perfect here either, but comapred to this its a holy grail of hitbox accuracy
B) From what I can tell, bosses can and do use the same mechanic as a player with focusing on the player with that dot you can focus on the boss too. I came to that conclusion when the ogre boss attacked me by jumping so that he could double kick me in face for a massive hit, and I dodged most of the time, for him to just rotate to me when I dodged to the side. So he literally rotated in the Air to give it to me.

Now after looking into some discussions I realized that apprently there are swing attack, that you need to JUMP over, but there are also thrust attacks the you need to dodge to the side (like speer pokes). Guess what: They both use the same red symbol and you also cant really tell them apart perfectly.

This was so bad for me, that at the ogre I tried a few rounds (thats when I also tested the stronger samurai) of just dodging by jumping and just dodging by dashing. There I realized how f'd the htboxes are with me jumping up or to the side for his grab attack (when he jumps, not just grabs in front of him) yet even though I was outside of it, I clipped back into his loving arms.


5) Last main point for the review: I hate when games queue up attacks as if you cant react to it. Its such a stupid thing. It does not feel good (Not in Dark Souls and not here - havent tried Elden Ring yet) and its so ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ annoying. I mean try this: Throw a ball, exceptin the middle of throwing it stop. "OMG THATS ANYMOATION CANCELIING AN BLABLABLABLABLA". B*itch shut up. Why the hell do develepers literally have to stop me from reacting to make a game harded. I reacted, let me control my character.

I hate this and I cant understand why on earth its so har for even From Soft to let go of this relic of who knows when what and where. If I react, let me react MF'er, and its more realisitc. It jsut sad if you need to do this to make it harded. Am i so strong as a player taht you need to articially stop me from dodging/parrying/blocking compared to your bosses?

Just stop with this trope, please.

----
I can still get why people may like the game, but I objctively think the gameplay is contradictory in its nature and within the gameplay sets the wrong incentives compared for what you actually are supposed to do. From what I read parrying actually becomes neccessary and fun ("Dacne" and "rythm") later on, but I decided to get my money back before I get there, because I have so many other problems than even listed above.

For highlights of the rest of my complaints:
- Why am I on the ground even if my parry bar isnt filled up?
- Why do I have to watch some stupid animation everytime I die and want to reawaken? ( Is literally any moment in the entire game where you dont want to use it?)
- I never had it once my parry bar was full that I actually got attacked and lost one of my red points. I just spammed jump and jmped away before the enemy as able to do anything.... so again: What the hell is the parry bar there for?
- Why are the bosses so much bigger than me? Even If I parry im s far away from being able to attack, that I need to first run back to them, espcaially once they step back a bit themselves after I parry.
Cant write mo

I would consider Sekiro as one of the best FromSoftware games ever made.
Overall, at its core, it’s a rhythm game.
This game just hits different than anything you’ve probably ever played.
The combat, the scenery—everything about it just feels so incredibly good.

Spoiler Warning from here on – you’ve been warned.

As an example: the Divine Dragon.
It’s like the gimmick fight of Sekiro—basically like Rykard, Lord of Blasphemy from Elden Ring.
The fight overall isn’t hard compared to anything you’ve really fought before him.
It’s just the amazing atmosphere that makes the fight memorable for me. Everything about his attacks makes you feel something—it feels straight out of an anime.

Or take Isshin, the Sword Saint, the final boss for any ending except the Shura ending.
Yes, the fight is hard—incredibly hard—but there’s just something about this fight that feels so right to lose.
You don’t fight against some ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ god-creature like in Elden Ring.
You fight against Genichiro’s grandfather, whose last wish was to fight for Ashina.

And how he fights...
He makes you regret every hit you take.
He makes you scream in rage.
He makes you mad.
But it’s not some stupid Waterfowl Dance move he does—he just tests everything you’ve been taught so far in the game.
And yes—he’s just the perfect final boss, in my opinion.

It took me around eight hours to see his fourth phase for the first time.
When he just starts catching lightning with his sword and makes you shoot it back at him...
And when you see his poise bar slowly getting fuller—that’s when you really feel your heart pumping like crazy.

For me, it was the first time ever experiencing something like that:
The music gets quieter, your vision gets fixed on the game,
you forget about everything around you,
and suddenly you don’t even hear your stupid friends on Discord anymore.
It’s just you and the game.

And then, after eleven painful hours, you’re finally able to finish him.
I almost cried out of pure joy—of finally being able to finish my playthrough.
Isshin even motivates you to finally end it.

Just such a unique experience.
10/10 – would recommend for anyone searching for a hard-as-f*ck game.

Posted: June 10
Sekiro might have the most engaging combat in any From Software title.
I genuinely think it is fantastic from a gameplay perspective and if that's all you care about?
Then ignore the rest of this review and buy it.

But it is a very substandard PC port.
60FPS locked.
No Borderless Windowed mode.
No non-standard resolutions.
And no FOV or camera settings.
But it DOES have HDR.

I'm pretty tolerant when it comes to this stuff, I don't really care about graphics or FPS or anything.
But the V-sync input lag is huge and there's no kind of adaptive sync or nvidia reflex here to help offset it. If you are on a high end PC, you WILL need to unlock the FPS to get it playable.
It also does the Elden Ring thing of cutting off the sides of the screen if you have a non-standard aspect ratio. But it doesn't change the FOV of the camera. So when I got to the 1st boss, I couldn't even see the top half of his body to parry his attacks.

That's when I spent about 3 hours trying to mod the game to get 144FPS, borderless windowed, ultrawide support (black bar removal, UI fix and vertical FOV fix) and I just couldn't get all of them to work at the same time.
I just settled for 144FPS, FOV and resolution.
The game runs and plays great now.

But after beating Lies of P which just has all that stuff natively, spending so much time messing around with .dlls and .exes and patchers when all I wanted to do was play the game was frustrating and a huge let down.

I really hope From Software steps up their game and starts making actual PC ports one day.

Game title: Serkiro: Shadows Die Twice
Overall Rating: ★★★★★

1. Gameplay
• Precise, smooth and, rewarding
• Challenging gameplay that rewards the player for being attentive to the enemy
• FromSoft decided to give the player no choice in character building, and very limited options in character leveling which some might not enjoy if they are fans of the previous entires. Although I found this off-putting at first I can’t deny that it just makes sense. You can't just over level your way through this entry you got to learn and improve, if you want to get through this game.

2. Graphics & Art Style
• The game’s visuals beautifully capture the atmosphere of Sengoku-era Japan, with lush environments and striking character designs.
• Graphics hold up very well, although I could have appreciated if the game wasn't locked in at 60fps

3. Sound & Music
• You will be addicted to listening to the sound the deflect makes when you hit it perfectly
• The soundtrack enhances the experience when entering a boss fight or just navigating through areas

4. Story & Writing
• First play through I didn't get much of the story as it took the backseat while I focused on not dying around every corner
• Second play through I got to pay a bit more attention and really enjoy the story that at times can feel hidden but still interesting.
• I love that there are several endings kept me coming back

5. Performance & Stability
• Minimal to no bugs, with stable 60fps throughout my entire playtime

6. Replayability & Content
• As mentioned before there are several endings which kept me coming back but recently the devs have added new modes. You can now replay boss fights as I recall if you beat the challenging gauntlet modes you unlock cosmetics

7. Value for Money
• The game to me is worth the $60 but frequently goes on sale making it super worth the money
• NO MICROTRANSACTIONS

Positives
• Great challenge, great atmosphere
Negatives
• locked in at 60fps
• Not personally a negative for me but it might be for some, there is no multiplayer, and it's so challenging that it can feel frustrating at times. (get gud)

Would you recommend this game?
• Yes

Posted: June 10
I had to FORCE myself to complete this game.

Longtime FromSoft fan, played armored core since I was a child, beat all the souls games, blah blah blah. Beat Sword Saint. Beat DoH. Required less attempts on either of them than several ER/DS bosses. I got gud. The game just isn't fun. The combat everyone praises is boring and repetitive.

The story is whatever/boring. The environments are few and recycled, same with the enemies. None of the characters are compelling or interesting. The warning system with the red kanji symbol has got to be one of the biggest sins in gaming. Could mean 1 of 4 things, each requiring a specific answer/timing to handle. It only serves to put you in a state of panic in which you're more likely to fumble and make a mistake. Sometimes it can even obstruct your vision of the enemy and the move you're supposed to be looking out for.

Most of the gameplay boils down to spam R1 or parry when appropriate. Most of the prosthetic devices you find have one specific niche use and the rest of the time (99% of the time) they're useless. People will refute this and praise Sekiro's combat but ultimately this is what it is. Almost every boss in the game has one if not several moves that feel dishonest to the player. Wolf builds up posture at a snails pace whereas a boss will 2 tap Wolf if he makes a single mistake. Bosses require the player to play essentially perfectly, it often feels unfair and unfun. Beating the 'hard' bosses gave me no feeling of satisfaction or gratification, just a sigh of relief that I will never have to do this again. Which is another point in itself - I have replayed every From game I own multiple times over. Several playthroughs. I will NEVER play this game again. I have zero interest in another playthrough because it will be exactly the same.

What blows my mind is the endless praise this game gets, yet only 57% of players defeated Gyoubu (first boss). Less than half for Genichiro. It's clear people give up early, so who is praising it? How did this get Game of the Year when half of its players didn't even witness or experience like 80% of the game??? It makes no sense.

Overall I wouldn't recommend this game to anyone unless you're a masochist. Or you really like to learn enemy attack patterns. Cause those are the only satisfactions you'll get from this game: self-inflicted pain or the 'satisfaction' of learning how to perfect parry every move a boss throws at you.

The best Fromsoft game.

- Less obtuse
- A functional pause
- Tighter controls that feel responsive (deflect/attack correspond to button press vs roll being your main button on other games, which happens on button release)
- Clearer skill progression. I feel like as I became more skilled my performance became way more consistent than it ever did in any of their other titles. I would improve in those, sure. But there's way less tilt/running in and immediately dying stupidly in Sekiro for whatever reason. It seems more like once you're good, you're good. But people also no-hit Bloodborne and Elden Ring, so it's obviously user related. Either way Sekiro feels way more natural and intuitive to me, especially with practice.

PS:Sekiro — O Cântico Eterno do Lobo Imortal
Sekiro não começa.
Ele desperta.

Não como um simples guerreiro,
Mas como uma força primordial,
Uma alma forjada nas chamas do sacrifício,
Na forja ardente da perda e da redenção.

Ao nascer, não há mundo —
Só o vazio frio da solidão,
E um juramento gravado não em pedra, mas no próprio tecido do ser:
Proteger o Príncipe Divino, mesmo que o custo seja a própria eternidade.

Nas sombras das montanhas sagradas,
O Lobo caminha sozinho,
Cada passo ecoa como um tambor ancestral,
Chamando a atenção dos deuses, dos demônios e dos fantasmas.

Seu olhar é profundo, carregado de histórias que nunca foram contadas,
Histórias de amores roubados pela guerra,
De amizades dissolvidas no sangue e na traição,
De sonhos desfeitos no frio da noite.

O Lobo não apenas luta — ele vive a dor de cada batalha,
Cada golpe desferido é uma oração,
Cada esquiva, um poema de sobrevivência,
Cada morte, uma lágrima invisível derramada no abismo do tempo.

Seu braço prostético, metálico e vivo,
Não é só uma ferramenta —
É a extensão da sua alma ferida,
A manifestação tangível do que ele perdeu,
E do que ainda luta para manter.

Em seu caminho, monstros surgem — não apenas de carne,
Mas de lendas esquecidas, de pesadelos ancestrais,
Seres que desafiam a sanidade e o próprio destino.

Radicalmente solitário, o Lobo enfrenta cada um deles com a coragem dos que não têm escolha,
Pois a morte, para ele, não é um fim, mas uma promessa quebrada,
Um destino que deve ser desafiado a cada momento, a cada respiração.

Mas, no fundo desse coração imortal, arde uma chama tênue,
Uma luz chamada Kuro, o Príncipe Divino,
Ligado ao Lobo por um vínculo que transcende sangue e carne,
Um elo sagrado que os une na esperança de um amanhã que pode nunca vir.

A jornada do Lobo é uma dança macabra entre luz e escuridão,
Entre a honra e a perdição,
Entre a humanidade que ele luta para preservar
E a fera que ameaça dominá-lo a cada instante.

E, no entanto, mesmo quando o mundo parece ruir,
Mesmo quando a dor parece esmagá-lo,
O Lobo levanta sua lâmina —
A lâmina que não só corta o inimigo, mas corta as amarras do destino.

Nas noites silenciosas, quando a lua cheia ilumina as ruínas,
O Lobo ouve os sussurros do passado —
Vozes que o chamam para desistir, para descansar,
Mas ele não pode, não quer.

Pois sabe que sua luta é a luta de todos nós:
Contra a perda, contra o esquecimento, contra a escuridão que habita dentro e fora.

E quando o mundo desaba ao seu redor,
Quando as lágrimas se misturam ao sangue,
O Lobo ainda tem força para erguer a cabeça,
Para olhar nos olhos da morte e dizer: “Não hoje.”

Sekiro não é apenas um jogo.
É a essência da resistência humana,
A expressão máxima daquilo que significa lutar com o coração partido,
E ainda assim encontrar beleza na luta.

Cada confronto é um sacrifício,
Cada derrota uma lição gravada na alma,
Cada vitória uma centelha de esperança que brilha no infinito.

A história não é apenas contada —
Ela é sentida, vivida, sofrida,
Como uma melodia triste que ecoa pelos vales do tempo,
Um cântico sagrado que nos lembra que, mesmo na dor,
Existe uma luz que nunca se apaga.

E no final, quando a última batalha se desenrolar,
Quando a lâmina do Lobo for finalmente repousada,
Seu nome será lembrado não apenas como um guerreiro,
Mas como um símbolo eterno de coragem e amor.

Pois Sekiro é isso:
Uma ode àqueles que carregam cicatrizes invisíveis,
Uma celebração dos corações que batem apesar de tudo,
Um testemunho da imortalidade da alma diante da efemeridade da vida.

Nas terras devastadas por guerras e magia,
O Lobo não apenas caminha — ele carrega o peso do mundo em seus ombros.
Cada pedaço de terra, cada folha ao vento, parece chorar sua ausência,
Enquanto a natureza lamenta a dor da humanidade.

A solidão que o envolve é como um manto pesado,
Feito das sombras de todos que ele amou e perdeu,
De todas as promessas quebradas
Que agora são ecos distantes, sussurrados no vento frio.

O frio não está só no ar — está dentro dele,
No âmago de sua alma que arde em febre e tristeza,
Um fogo que consome tudo, menos a vontade inquebrável de seguir em frente.

Ele já conheceu o desespero mais profundo,
Viu o mundo despedaçado diante de seus olhos cansados,
Sentiu o aço cortar sua carne e seu espírito,
E mesmo assim, levantou-se — sempre, inabalável.

Os fantasmas do passado o assombram —
Memórias de um mestre caído, de amigos traídos,
De um príncipe que confia nele como em mais nada no universo.
Eles não são apenas lembranças — são feridas abertas,
Que sangram uma dor quase divina,
Pois o amor e a perda nunca estiveram tão entrelaçados.

O Lobo entende que a imortalidade não é um presente —
É uma maldição que arrasta a alma para além do tempo,
Um tormento que torna eterno o sofrimento,
Mas também a esperança.

E naqueles momentos em que tudo parece perdido,
Quando a escuridão ameaça engolir tudo,
Ele encontra forças não nas lâminas ou técnicas,
Mas em sua própria humanidade —
Na chama persistente do coração que ainda bate,
Mesmo quando o mundo inteiro parece pedir silêncio.

As batalhas se tornam rituais sagrados,
Onde cada golpe é uma palavra de um antigo cântico,
Cada esquiva, um passo na dança da sobrevivência,
Cada suspiro, uma prece para os deuses do destino.

O Lobo não teme a morte, pois ele a conhece intimamente,
Mas teme o esquecimento —
Ser apagado da história, do tempo, do próprio universo.
E por isso luta, sempre, contra o inevitável.

E quando enfrenta seus inimigos —
Sejam homens, monstros ou sombras da sua própria alma —
Ele não os vê apenas como adversários,
Mas como reflexos distorcidos de sua própria jornada,
Lembranças do que ele poderia ter sido,
Do que ele ainda pode ser, se sobreviver a mais um dia.

O mundo que ele protege não é só um lugar —
É um símbolo daquilo que acreditamos ser sagrado,
A esperança que nunca morre,
Mesmo nos momentos mais sombrios da existência.

E quando o vento sopra através das árvores antigas,
Quando o sol se põe em tons de sangue e ouro,
O Lobo sente o chamado da eternidade,
O sussurro da redenção que está além da dor e da luta.

Sekiro é mais que um jogo —
É uma experiência espiritual, uma meditação sobre a dor e o amor,
Sobre a coragem diante do inevitável,
Sobre o poder do espírito humano para resistir, amar e transcender.

Porque no final, quando as lâminas se cruzam pela última vez,
Quando o silêncio envolve o campo de batalha,
O que resta não é a morte,
Mas a luz eterna do Lobo Imortal,
Que brilha para sempre nos corações daqueles que ousam lutar.

Desperte, Lobo.
Caminhe pela eternidade com a tristeza como seu manto,
E a coragem como sua espada.
Pois mesmo na escuridão mais profunda,
Você é a luz que nunca se apaga.

I mean there's a reason everyone talks about the boss fights and strong enemies, because that's when the combat works. It works in one on one fights.
But the rest of the game just becomes trying to figure out the most effective way of killing enemies without getting too many of them to agro onto you, which isn't fun at all. This game doesn't make me *feel like a shinobi*, it makes me feel like a ♥♥♥♥♥.

I'll make a separate paragraph for this. No, fromsoft fans, it is not too hard for me. Thank you for the steam points from the awards though. And I did beat a lot of stuff. When I beat a boss I thought to myself that was awesome this game might be fun, but then I went back to killing normal enemies and the game became a chore again.

I think the reason souls games aren't a chore is because I have freedom, they have rpg mechanics, officially known as fun mechanics. But Sekiro has no freedom in comparison, you will parry, you will use a grappling hook, you will do stealth kills. No you can't make certain moves stronger or switch the power around between your attacks and parries, best thing you can have is a skill tree, prosthetics and an upgradable flask.
I didn't expect this game to have all the good mechanics from the souls games, I knew it was going to be different, and that is exactly why it ended up not being good.

Sekiro just isn't a souls game, and that's fine.

1) dark protagonists in dark environments fighting dark enemies on dark backgrounds looks cool until you're playing it
2) why is running so clunky
3) what I feel is artificial difficulty

The real issue I take with this game is that you go out of your way to create such a robust, skill-expressive combat system built for exciting 1v1 fights, and then you constantly jam into large groups of enemies that you either run from to reset aggro or try to hack through. Even using stealth to trim down the group only goes so far until eventually you're in a smaller group fight.

I really wanted to like this game, but there's too many hangups that I have to recommend it. The prosthetic tools are a cool addition, grappling adds some fun movement to help scout areas, and the stealth is impressively well done. But the awe and fun of the game as a whole is limited by moment-to-moment gameplay

Posted: June 6
Elden Ring was my first FromSoft experience and I loved it. When I finally beat Elden Ring, I wanted more so I thought, “I’ll give Sekiro a try, why not? I’m pretty decent at Elden Ring, how hard can it be?” Expecting the combat to be similar to Elden Ring completely threw me off. Couldn’t block or parry. Gave all my NPC friends dragon rot, built up some money and xp, just to lose it all. Got frustrated, wanted to snap my Steam Deck in half, gave up. I went back to my comfort zone and did Elden Ring NG+, but Sekiro was always the back of my mind. I came back to Sekiro about 6 months after my initial try, watched some YouTube videos and just told myself this is gonna be brutal but I’m gonna figure this out. And I did. I finally beat Sekiro and have since gone back to do NG+ for my third time now, trying to get all the trophies. Sekiro not only tops Elden Ring, but I believe it’s my favorite game of all time. The game is completely punishing and rewarding at the same time. When it clicks, it’s so satisfying. Sekiro is hard, hardest game I’ve played to date but completely worth it. Some of the greatest boss battles I’ve ever experienced and the final boss is an unbelievable challenge but such an interesting experience in failure again and again, until it clicks. The story is also fantastic and emotional. Before I ever played Sekiro, I would read comments or reviews saying, “hesitation is defeat” and now I understand. If you’re on the fence or gave up like I did my first attempt, get back after it, don’t hesitate, Young Wolf.

How this game became GOTY is beyond me.

I feel FromSoft should have patched the combat to make more aspects available to people, working on your own playstyle - instead you are left with rather slow sword swings and and RIP yourself over and over again to learn how enemies fight to MAYBE be able to parry their attacks.

You go through the same scripted areas with the same enemies over and over again to earn gold to buy some stuff only to loose half of it if you die. That's fair - just don't die - in a souls game.

Some people are stuck on some of the bosses for years as your barely have any real option to practice against the threats. sure you have a training area but that won't help you one bit if the enemy's moveset/weapon is different from the trainers.

Look, I get it. We love overcoming hard challenges - and that's the drive in any souls game, but Sekiro's flawed combat makes this more frustrating than rewarding. Maybe it has something to do with me coming from Elden Ring, but I feel a adjusting combat-patch could fix all the problems the game has.

Posted: June 5
This is a game that feels like a really fast turn-based combat where it either clicks for you or it doesn't. They attack you parry, you attack then they parry and repeat. I've played this for many hours and it still hasn't clicked enough for me to kill any boss in less than 20 attempts. it's really hard and I've given it enough time that it should have "clicked" for me by now. Maybe I'm too old, maybe I just need to keep working on getting good.

All that said, it's not for everyone and demands you play by the games rules. No choice of weapon, no classes, no specs, no gear. Just you and the katana and you either learn to parry well, or you die. Can't do anything about it. When you fight your first mob to the last boss the combat and playstyle is essentially the same, so if you don't like the build the game forces on you, there's no changing it and must just accept it. The game has a huge learning curve and it's definitely not for everyone. Despite this I rate it a thumbs up, but only barely. It's not worthy of a GOTY from me, but playable to some and loved by some others. 7/10.

I think others could enjoy it, but I'm failing to enjoy it. Don't get me wrong, I'm getting through bosses just fine, but almost all of the time I'm just glad the fight is over rather than excited over a fun fight. Which sucks, as someone who really does enjoy Soulsborne games.
I think the difficulty isn't entirely the issue, but its the surrounding punishment for deaths locking you out of questlines and draining all money that you have that are the issue. I want to love the game, and it feels so cool to get into a 1v1 with a boss, but with weird hitboxes, miniscule player damage output, and with such little margin for error, I'm just not having the same fun that I would in other Fromsoft games.

I want to love it, I just don't think it's for me, and that's alright.

Posted: June 4
Unless you fail your deflects, at times a boss fight can feel like a continuous stream of strikes and deflects, with no pause in between. There is no stamina bar, no limit to how many attacks and deflects you can do in a row if you don't fail. You can run and grapple through areas seamlessly, no stops. No other game feels this way.

Some devs have tried to copy the deflect mechanic, but their games still have stamina or some other way that doesn't make the fights feel fluid. They also forgot to make the games good, too.

Most of the bosses are great, minibosses not so much. The areas are not that great either. They expect you to stealth through them, but it's not actually a fun stealth game. It's a fun dueling combat game, and fighting multiple mobs at the same time isn't fun.

The game is also great for replays, as experienced players can just run through almost every area and skip the mobs. Also great for challenge runs and speedruns.

Nope Sekiro is absolutely not my game.
Although it has some good things like the different parry options, the arm prostetic and the option to one hit enemies, it just doesn't feel enjoyable.
I am fine when some games gives you a hard challenge, but then you should design it in the corret way. That is what Sekiro is lacking:
It feels like they designed it like a normal action adventure but then turned the difficulty high up. For example there is one boss that is actually fun trying to beat him but everytime you die, you not only need to run to him since the last checkpoint/bonfire but also need to skip the cutscene every time on the arrival. Like it feels that the developers didn't know that you die a lot in this area. This is just a bad checkpoint implementation, no matter if it's made by FromSoftware or not.
Also while the overworld is kinda easy to fight through, the bosses are extremely hard... so hard you get stuck a lot because you struggle to beat that one boss for multiple hours. So what do I do? Instead of trying to parry every attack of the boss, I ran away all the time until the boss made one attack that was easily to counterattack. So instead to die one hour more, I made long 20min fight. That was the part where I made a rage quit, not because I couldn't beat him but because I beat him.
So for the upcoming bosses I could either continue with this boring startegy or I keep dying... Yeah no thanks this is not fun.

Posted: June 3
Sekiro... is a strange game, especially if you are not already a big fan of Soulslike game.
This game knows how to piss you off... but also knows how to make you get better at fighting against it.
It's like spicy food... when you first have it, you hate yourself, curse why you ever had this, but then find yourself strangely addicted and growing more immune to it.

When I first played Sekiro, I got very frustrated with the deflect timings, seemingly endless boss attack patterns, and unfair amount of damage boss does, and "not too obvious" tell for boss special attacks. At this point, I was thinking "man, why is this game so brutal; everything I do feels slow, and I get 2 shot by everything! When do I get strong??" I quit the game after 4 hours (this was probably after beating 2 mini bosses).

Several months later, I resume this game once again, because I wanted some challenge. With some extra motivation, I got quite far, until I got stuck on this one boss, Lady Butterfly. I probably spent 2 ~ 3 hours straight bashing my head against this boss. And here, I rage quit once again.

Few days later, I am back again, but this time, I had a question in mind: there is no way the early game boss is this hard. There must be some another way that I am missing. And boom, I completely forgot that the path I took was a side path, and I was fighting a boss that was extra difficult to fight at this point. I continued down the main path, got stronger, then came back to this boss, and I beat it on 3rd try. I then proceeded through the game, and beat the whole thing in about 4 days.

Until I got to the half way of the game, I rated this game as 6/10. It was too difficult, and I couldn't react in time for enemy attacks. However, as the game went on, I found myself actually getting better, finding ways of dealing with different enemies. I then started becoming more adventurous, trying different tactics at the cost of my life, and I was getting stronger. Not necessarily the character getting stronger, but I as a player was getting really good. When I was fighting the last 3 bosses of the game, the feeling of achievement after the fight definitely made this game at least 9/10.

And I think that's the core strength of this game. This game trains the player to endure hardship, and constantly gives the player a challenging fight. The character and the player grows together, and by the end of it, you find yourself so good at this game and the same boss that you struggled at the beginning feels like a complete joke.

The only downside of this game is the navigation. For side quests and navigation, I highly recommend people to look up the guide if you ever get stuck. Some of them are not obvious, and I definitely spent at least 5 hours going to random places to see if anything new has unlocked.

TL;DR: Game is spicy, but see if you can endure until you beat the first real boss (which allows you to upgrade your attack power). The game really starts to pick up from there on.

I usually take my time with Fromsoft games, simply because I find taking my time and letting the game grow onto me more fun and meaningful than the alternative. This game, however, I bulldozed through in about 1.5 months and 60 hours of playtime to obtain the Sekiro achievement. I found the base gameplay loop so gripping, that I ended up spending a lot more of my time on this game than I had initially planned.
(For context, I pre-ordered ELDEN RING, started my playthrough on release day and have since garnered an average playtime of 40 seconds per day over the last 3 years and that playthrough is still not even close to finished; My first Dark Souls 2 playthrough took 2 years; And my Demon's Souls playthrough also took well over a year to finish; That's why I say 1.5 months is really fast)

In any case, this game is all about observation. Pay attention to the enemy, read their actions, predict their attacks and dodge, deflect and counter accordingly. The game has a great combat system. Enemies aren't too dumb and going up against more than one will put you at a great disadvantage. For every difficult situation you can find yourself in, there is a tactical or strategic solution to and more than that, trying to brute force an area will not yield good results.
To get a bit more concrete, combat is basically a reaction based game of rock-paper-scissors that requires you to observe your opponent. Enemies will block, your aim is to break their posture and you do so by attacking them, damaging them, deflecting their attacks and using your tools for various different ways to accomplish the aforementioned with each tool having its ups and downs. If an enemy's posture is hard to break, you use more movement to get behind them and damage them directly, thereby weakening their posture recovery and making it easier to break down.

This system is ridiculously fun and that's because whether or not you beat the enemy depends entirely on how well you can read what they're about to do. And it's that reading, that observation, that knowing the enemy that makes Sekiro so replayable and enjoyable. And this is the same for all enemies in the game. Not to mention, there is always multiple ways you can approach an encounter and I don't mean this tactically/strategically. Rather than that, the fact that you can deplete both the health and posture of enemies in order to defeat them and doing one also feeds into helping do the other, which is just great.

I've heard many people call Sekiro "effectively a rhythm game" and whilst I don't fully agree with the statement, I can see where they're coming from. On that note, rhythm is yet another highly satisfying part of combat, as in either trying to match your own rhythm to the enemy or attempting to dictate it yourself. When all goes right and when you finally get the combat down, running through the game and fighting the marvelously designed bosses feels like an electrifying dance of blades, wherein every movement, every action, every strike naturally fits into the melody of clashes that is combat. It's a relaxing trance and one, that I, for the last 60 hours, have been unable to get enough of.

Aside from that, the music is quite nice, the story is really quite solid and the game looks gorgeous and detailed, which is quite impressive, considering it took up about 14 GB on my PC.

Having played every soulslike that Fromsoft has made from Demon's Souls up to ELDEN RING, I'd say that this game feels like one of the highest quality games they've produced. It's a one of a kind game, that puts it's whole focus one an aspect of combat I had no idea I could ever enjoy so much.

9.7 / 10

Finally finished the game, finally able to give a fair review.
This game sucks
It's where fromsoftware fight design has started going bad.
The setting, the music, the story the vibes are all fine, nice even.
It's the combat system that sucks, and it's funny that it gets so much praise.
While the posture system is a nice idea on paper, the execution here basically means that you literally cannot deal damage if the mob doesn't feel like taking damage.
Dealing damage isn't earned, it's actually the mob lowering their guard and letting you get hits.

Enemy's Deflections are incompatible with the posture system
If you haven't played the game, enemies basically have 2 separate healthbars.
1 Classic health bar
2 Posture health bar
To kill a boss, you have to either empty the health bar, by doing damage, or fill the posture bar, by attacking the enemy while he blocks.
So where's the problem? Deflections.
A Deflection is a perfect block. When mobs do this, they won't take either health or posture damage.
A small stupid random mob can take ages to kill if he so chooses. You can attack as much as you want, and he'll keep deflecting.
You are forced to deflect yourself, because your deflections hit his posture bar.
Problem solved, right? Not yet.
Because while health doesn't recover, posture does.
.
This especially sucks on bosses, because the is tied to their amount of health.
This means that .

All of this summed up means that .
You can try, and then it's up to them to decide if they'll take damage or not.
With some bosses this becomes astoundingly stupid and pointless.
I still remember vividly the Owl boss fight at Hirata estate, and how he would deflect everything i threw at him, it was like hitting a wall.
Then, for no reason at all, he would neither deflect or block, just stand around and let me smack him in the face.

This makes no sense and it sucks. It's inconsistent.
While i'm familiar with the "learning the boss" process in Fromsoftware games, what happens here is that the boss will deflect everything and then let you win for no reason.
You haven't changed the attack pattern, they just decided not to block nor deflect.
This is also exacerbated by the fact that this is where Fromsoft has started their trash .
Enemies know perfectly when they can hit you for free, they know perfectly how to deflect your attack.
Everything you do needs to be done inside the .
You'll have a designated window to heal, a designated window to hit.
Do anything outside the designated window? Get hit, get killed.

To win you have to do everything the intended way. You can't attack how you want, you have to "react" to what it wants.
. Except the button to press isn't shown.
Enemy attack? PRESS L1
Enemy thrust attack? PRESS O
Enemy sweep attack? PRESS X
You want to attack? Wait the designated window and pray to Miyazaki that he won't deflect.

If i can't do anything my way, if i need to play the , then i might as well watch a movie.
