⭐️⭐️⭐️⭐️⭐️ (Not Soulsborne — something sharper, harder, and unforgettable)

Sekiro isn’t just a FromSoftware game with a samurai skin — it’s a whole different beast. There’s no stamina bar, no RPG builds, no grinding for levels. It’s just you, your sword, and your skill. And it’s absolutely brilliant.

Combat in Sekiro is the heart of the game — and it’s some of the tightest, most demanding melee combat ever put in a game. It’s not about dodging and waiting for an opening — it’s about clashing blades, breaking posture, and deflecting at the exact right moment. Every boss is a duel. Every regular enemy is a threat if you get cocky. And when it all clicks? You feel like an unstoppable shinobi god.

It’s hard. Brutally hard. But not unfair. There’s no “cheesing” your way through — you either learn the fight, or you die trying. And that learning curve? It makes every victory feel insanely earned. You’ll struggle, rage, and probably consider uninstalling... then come back the next day and win in a clean run. That feeling never gets old.

The world design is peak FromSoftware — vertical, interconnected, mysterious. But unlike Souls games, you’re fast and agile. You have a grappling hook, stealth options, tools, and actual mobility, which completely changes how you explore and fight.

The story is more focused than usual too. You’re not a nameless undead — you’re Wolf, a shinobi on a mission, and your journey is surprisingly emotional, with some excellent lore and character moments baked into every encounter. It’s grim, quiet, and steeped in myth, but it never drags.

Boss fights? Top-tier. Genichiro. Isshin. Owl. Lady Butterfly. Guardian Ape. You’ll remember every one of them — mostly because they’ll crush you ten times before you win. But when you do? You’ll want to go back and do it again.

If there’s a downside, it’s that there’s no build variety. No swords to switch, no stats to tweak. You will play the way Sekiro plays. That’ll turn some Souls fans off — but for those who embrace it, this might be FromSoftware’s most pure and focused experience yet.

Sekiro isn’t an RPG. It’s a test. A beautifully brutal sword dance that demands patience, timing, and guts — and rewards you with one of the most satisfying combat systems in gaming.

I know fromsoft has a big catalog but genuinely this is the best title of their's in my view. It's definitely smaller in scope compared to something Elden Ring, but that trade-off was important because it meant the experience could be tighter and more focused. The combat loop is so consistently satisfying and the main experience is fantastic.

The downsides are mainly in the side content, which is still worth critiquing in my opinion as it is part of the game you're paying money for. The headless fights are awkward in my opinion, and while I get they were going for event fights that are different from the rest of the game, the utilization of movement restriction and an overly punishing instant death meter (fear) was I think misplaced. I'd much rather something akin to a Black Knight Fight: optional, rewarding, makes use of mechanics you're already familiar with, no needless mechanics to add spectacle. Furthermore, the treasure carps are cute the first time but make next to no sense as a side quest on subsequent playthroughs, and it feels like the items you get from treasure carp trading would have been better put in their own separate relevant side quests instead of just almagamized into one shop with special currency. It's tedious and has no replayability on ng+ cycles. Also the mask that lets you spend xp on attack power just, sucks. The amount you need is so high and because of how AP works in this game (diminishing returns), it can have a near negligible impact on your progression, making its inclusion near pointless. Lastly, I think the added forms of difficulty are just, not fun. Chip damage through blocks kinda makes sense, but you even take chip blocks through certain deflections. Furthermore, the damage curve is already pretty intense in the base game and the higher difficulty makes it borderline unplayable for most skill levels in my opinion. Especially if you combine charmless with the demon bell. Add on a couple of new game plus cycles and it's just absurd. Some people enjoy it, but those tend not to be the same people who are playing this game in between 8-10 hour shifts of a full time job. So unless you have *a lot* of free time or play games for a living, I really don't think you're gonna find much joy in the base game's form of difficulty increase. But that segues perfectly into...

THE RESURRECTION MOD. This mod single handedly raises this game from a potential buy to an ungodly must. Play the base game first, have your fun, then for ♥♥♥♥'s sake get this ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ mod. I don't want to spoil it too much but it takes an already fantastic base game experience and elevates it to near perfection. Get the game, play it, get this mod. You will have one of the most memorable gaming experiences of your life.

All in all Sekiro is a beautiful experimental title for fromsoft and I hope is the marker for a separate but important genre of fromsoft design that breaks from the grand looser feel of something like Elden Ring in exchange for overall shorter campaigns with far more intention behind each encounter. If you love yourself, you'll play this game.

Why I fu**** hate this game?
It hurts my heart to give a game from FromSoftware a bad review, BUT...

As a FromSoftware fan who has played every souls game (except for Demon Souls), I went into Sekiro expecting another masterpiece. Instead, I got a rigid, repetitive parry simulator that punishes creativity and drags out every fight with artificial difficulty. I’ve beaten most of the game in about 20 hours, and I can confidently say, this isn’t fun. It’s just exhausting and frustrating.

The biggest problem is that Sekiro forces you into one playstyle: deflect or die. There’s no real build variety, no meaningful upgrades, and no way to make fights easier if you struggle. Even with multiple health upgrades, bosses can still one-shot you, making progression feel pointless. The posture system is a nightmare, enemies recover way too fast, turning fights into endless slogs where a single mistake resets minutes of progress.

Bosses and mini-bosses are the worst offenders. Many rely on cheap tricks like hyper-armor attacks, delayed swings, and absurdly long combos that demand frame-perfect parries. Mini-bosses are even worse, just recycled enemies with bloated health bars, often surrounded by mobs to make them "harder" in the laziest way possible. The game doesn’t reward skill; it rewards memorization and patience, which isn’t the same thing.

What’s worse is that Sekiro has no replay value. Other Souls games let you experiment with builds, weapons, and strategies. Here, you do the same thing every playthrough, parry, attack, repeat. There’s no depth, just repetition.


I didn’t hate every moment of Sekiro, but the frustration far outweighs the satisfaction. While Sekiro’s bosses and combat mechanics often feel punishing and restrictive, the game does have one redeeming quality—its world. I like one boss fight tho, the Mist Noble is a peak! Anyways, exploration is actually enjoyable, with lush environments, intricate level design, and a sense of verticality that makes traversal engaging. The art direction is stunning, blending feudal Japan with dark fantasy in a way that feels fresh yet authentic.

Fighting normal enemies is where Sekiro shines in small doses. Stealth is viable, letting you thin out groups before engaging, and the movement system makes encounters fluid. The prosthetic tools add some variety when used against regular foes, allowing for a small glimpse of creative approaches.


At the moment (about 23 hours), I'm only finishing Sekiro out of sheer stubbornness, not because I enjoy it, but because I refuse to be beaten. I want to be able to say that I beat it, that it wasn't a "skill issue", but it's a nerve-wracking, teeth-gritting torture that feels more like an endurance test than a rewarding challenge. The game doesn’t make me feel accomplished,it just makes me feel tired. And when I finally beat it, I doubt I’ll remember it favourably. I’ll just be relieved it’s finally over.

If you love parrying nonstop, maybe you’ll enjoy it. But if you prefer fair challenge, build freedom and variety or just some sense of progress, go play something else, and thank me later. Sekiro isn’t fun. It’s work. And no amount of victory screens will change that.

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is a difficult but incredibly rewarding game. It doesn’t forgive mistakes and doesn’t offer an easy way out—every encounter feels like a mini boss fight, and every actual boss is a test of patience, timing, and precision. There are no RPG builds or magic—just a sword, a prosthetic arm, and your own skill.

The game pushes you to learn from your mistakes, memorize enemy patterns, and stay calm even when things seem hopeless. The feeling of finally defeating a tough enemy after dozens of attempts is something few games can deliver.

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice asks a bold question - What if a Souls game was actually fun?

In all seriousness, I don't have (much) of a problem with soulsbourne games, but I also never really clicked with them either. Finished a couple, but didn't particularly enjoy the experience.

Sekiro though?
In nearly every aspect, Sekiro felt like the experience every soulsbourne fan promised me the other games were supposed to deliver. The combat is fast, fluid and incredibly unforgiving - right up until the moment it clicks, and then it felt as easy as breathing. The feeling of going from panic spamming blocks at the start of game to flawlessly dancing through bosses at the end is so damn satisfying. The game really does show how Fromsoft's design skills can shine when they aren't shackled to the framework of a 15 year old series.

The design changes in Sekiro feel tailored to reduce the annoyances that plagued dark souls. Movement and traversal make make getting around feel like less of slog and there are much fewer over the top instant kill moves from enemies. While bosses can be difficult, they almost always have means of countering/avoiding their attacks and the one-time revive takes away a lot of the sting of dying from a single critical slip up.

Overall, Sekiro is far and away the best Fromsoft game I've ever played, and quite possibly the best action game they've ever made (can't confirm this until they port Bloodborne to PC, so I guess never lol)

If you played Dark Souls or Elden Ring but never really felt the hype, then definitely give Sekiro a go.

Posted: May 14
This is actually one of my favorite Fromsoft games and my experience with this game has been amazing. I think the devs did a really good job at making Sekiro a parry game by encouraging players to parry more due to the nature of the game and how the fights usually go. Obviously you could dodge every move & not parry and still kill the bosses/NPCs but that would take longer as it would be more so a battle of attrition. Hence, parrying is usually the preferred method of battle & it forces players to conform to the game which is a learning curve new players have to adjust to.

The combat aspect of this game such as swordplay is TOP TIER. I think part of it is because of how you kind of create rhythm when you are constantly exchanging blows with the enemy by parrying/attacking. For example, the fight with is one of my favorite bosses in the game because of how you have to constantly engage in the fight and pressure him by parrying every move he has to offer. Sekiro is definitely quintessential when it comes to fast-paced combat & swordplay and it is what makes the game so good.

The addition of grappling hooks in the game creates a new level of dynamism into movement, allowing players to avoid getting ganked by enemies & traverse through the map. It also creates more opportunities for players to initiate a fight and to escape from it. This kind of movement is very befitting for a shinobi-themed game like Sekiro.

Personally i think the story in this game is rather straightforward and not as complex as other as Fromsoft titles like Elden Ring. However, the story is not boring or bad as it still hooks you in especially when they first talked about and the whole heritage thing.

The only negative thing I have for this game is how most of the bosses have very similar movesets. I understand that Sekiro is a game of just exchanging blows with your katana and stuff like that which i really enjoyed. However, I was hoping for new boss mechanics to be introduced into the game and I was very pleased when i first learned about the mechanic that some of the bosses have. That was pretty much it. I was hoping for more but it seems like Fromsoft decided to stick with that and there was not any crazy moves unique to a boss unlike Elden Ring where Scarlet Rot is unique to Malenia etc.

In Dark Souls and other FromSoftware titles, often you can progress past a boss by brute forcing it in some way. Whether it's an overpowered player you summon in Souls, or a mimic tear in Elden Ring, there's a back-up plan you can rely on in case you get stuck. This game allows you no such luxury.

It's true, the game is tough. Yet, it's the most balanced and refined game I've played by FromSoftware. I completed 95% of it years ago and made it to the final boss, but just couldn't beat him. Finally gave in and looked at strategies online to beat him, including "cheesing" the boss. Still was getting my ass whooped. Then I put the game down for a long time considering it "effectively beaten". Cope.

Started playing Elden Ring again and had the idea to try and beat this boss once and for all. Had about 20 attempts on yesterday and couldn't get it done, but felt much more composed throughout the fight. Finally got it in about 5-10 attempts today. No cheesing either, just slowly learning the boss patterns.

Dying in this game is a consequence. But dying is also part of the core gameplay. Sometimes I'd get to the last phase and die, just to lose to the first "easy" phase on the next attempt. It's frustrating, but also a reminder that no part of the fight is guaranteed.

Amazing theme, visuals, and gameplay. Absolutely recommend.

Posted: May 11
Probably my favorite souls-game.

The combat flow is amazing. Your timing of the parries improves with each enemy encounter, which promotes a more aggresive playstyle, leading to more risk-taking and mastery of the systems the game has put in place. Once you realize, that almost everything can be parried, the game becomes so beautifully rhythmic. This is even further emphasized by the really satisfying sound of a successful parry, which almost in and of itself feels like a reward. The skills you unlock by spending your skill points are meaningful and provide a lot of utility and change the combat quite a bit (best example for this is the mikiri counter, which lets you parry thrust attacks).

The locations are all beautifully crafted together. The Japanese vibe really shines through with the level design and never feels forced in any way. With the addition of the grappling hook, the areas gain a certain verticality, which hasn't been previously achieved in other souls-games. It feels unique, intuitive and makes you feel like a true shinobi swinging through the battlefield, gaining new angles on enemy encounters.

Stealth is also an underappreciated aspect of this title. Backstabs feel satisfying and rewarding. Certain skills even improve your capability to sneak around enemies, making this playstyle feel even better. You can even skip a healthbar of certain minibosses using this mechanic (for example, snake eyes shirahagi), which is especially useful.

The bosses in this game feel meaningful and introduce new mechanics with every new encounter. Best example of this, is your fight with Genichiro, where you need to use Lightning Reversal to deflect his lightning attacks. The main bosses of this game feel like insurmountable obstacles, which really challenge your skills and if you have honed them enough across your playtime. This is especially apparent with the last boss of the game (the ending where you are loyal to Kuro) Isshin Ashina, who utilizes every mechanic the player has come across during his playtime (thrusts, gunfire, lightning attacks and much more). He truly deserves the title of the final boss of this game, as the player is forced to interact with every system the game has thrown at them and must prove his mastery of them.

The artstyle of this game, the cinematics and overall artistic design is absolutely beautiful. The Divine Dragon boss reinforces this. This boss is more thematically beautiful rather than difficult. The arena in which you fight is absolutely stunning and the boss fight feels more like a dance than a true battle. Its theme, purpose in the story and design are perfect.

The story of Sekiro is beautifully written. I have to admit, that to progress some questlines/endings, you have to do some things which aren't clear from dialogue or any other clues. Aside from that, the Endings are absolutely stunning and it's 100% worth getting all of them. Sekiro's character development is really well-written. Especially the fact, that his hand doesn't grab his sword instantly at the beginning and even trembles. In contrast to the ending of the game, where it is apparent that he has complete mastery of his sword and focus.
The main plot of the game is very strong and provides a purpose to not only Sekiro, but also the person playing him. You sympathize with Kuro and his wish to sever his immortality. This drives you forward to complete your goal at any cost.
The side characters offer a lot of insight into the world and don't feel meaningless. You can give booze to the elderly Isshin Ashina in his tower, which nets you more dialogue from him. You can also do this with various different characters with various drinks you find on your adventure. This seemingly simple feature makes the interaction with other characters feel more lively and builds upon the vast world.

In total, this game is one of the greatest games ever made. If you like souls-games, Japanese culture/folklore/style, an immersive combat system, story-rich environments and bosses, this game is most definitely for you. Once you get into the flow of combat and parrying, this game becomes a lot less difficult than it seems at first glance.

Remember, "Hesitation is defeat" - Isshin, the Sword Saint

Sekiro™: Shadows Die Twice – A Masterpiece of Precision and Perseverance

FromSoftware has once again delivered a breathtaking experience with Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice, a game that pushes the boundaries of the action-adventure genre and challenges players like never before.



The combat system in Sekiro is nothing short of revolutionary. Unlike traditional RPGs, Sekiro emphasizes precision, timing, and strategy over brute force or grinding. The posture-based combat requires players to read enemy moves carefully and respond with well-timed deflections and counters. This creates an intense, rhythmic dance of death that is both satisfying and deeply rewarding.

encouraging players to learn from each mistake and improve their skills. Every victory feels earned, and every defeat teaches valuable lessons. It’s a true test of patience and perseverance that keeps you coming back for more.



The beautifully crafted world draws inspiration from Sengoku-era Japan, combining historical elements with fantastical touches. The environments are diverse and meticulously detailed, from serene temples to eerie forests and imposing castles, all brought to life with atmospheric lighting and dynamic weather effects.

focusing on themes of honor, loyalty, and redemption. While not as overtly story-driven as some other titles, the lore is rich and rewarding for those who take the time to explore and piece together its mysteries.



allowing players to approach encounters creatively. Sneaking past enemies or executing silent takedowns can make difficult sections more manageable and provide a refreshing change of pace from intense combat sequences.

enabling diverse playstyles and tactical approaches. Whether you prefer aggressive swordplay, ranged attacks, or clever gadget use, Sekiro accommodates your preferred method of overcoming challenges.



It offers a uniquely rewarding experience for those willing to embrace its steep learning curve. With its impeccable design, stunning visuals, and deeply satisfying gameplay, Sekiro stands as one of the finest action games of its generation.

If you’re ready to test your mettle and immerse yourself in a captivating world of skill and honor, Sekiro awaits.

The Best Combat System I’ve Ever Played

Sekiro is known for two things: Its difficulty and its combat system. And boy do they ever deliver.
I loved this game. It has some distinct flaws, but the combat is so good, that it turned this game into an instant classic that I’ll remember for years to come.

Positives:

- The entire combat system is a gem. It’s rarely ever cheap, rather, it exemplifies ‘git good’. It’s also nice having the dual system of posture for death blows, and a health bar, with different enemies being weaker to one or the other. It adds a nice degree of depth without feeling too complex or confusing. I enjoyed it even more than the combat in Dark Souls.
- The level design is quite good. It’s not nearly as varied as I would have liked, but it does have a sense of interconnectedness, which makes the world feel ‘real’. Being able to look across giant chasms to see areas you’ve previously visited is a very cool experience. It’s not on the level of Dark Souls, but it’s still very good.
- The game is artistically beautiful, with my favourite area being Senpou Temple, followed by Fountainhead.
- The grappling hook is so much fun and never got old.
- The frequency of Sculptor’s Idols (save spots) is a godsend. That almost eliminates one of the biggest frustrations I had with Dark Souls – runbacks.

Negatives:

- The resurrection system needs work. A full circle should return when finishing a boss phase. If one death blow only returns part of a circle, then what’s the point of having 3?
- NPC quests are still confusing and non-obvious. And you can miss about 30% of the game if you just answer wrong. You never know whether a dialogue is key to entire sections of the game, or whether it’s just for some throw-away line, like with handing alcohol to NPCs. For example, who on Earth would figure out the Senpou kite jump without having been told about it? And it’s absolutely necessary for one of the game endings.
- The whole Dragonrot system was pointless, much like the Humanity system in Dark Souls.
- The game isn’t too impressive technically. Character models and textures are nothing special.
- I like that it’s streamlined to be less RPG-like as a break from Souls game, but all the building materials, prosthetic arts, and skills seem excessive, with so many that you never use.
- Sekiro actually has a story (unlike Dark Souls), but it wasn’t a very good one. I honestly didn’t care about the Dragon Tears/Frozen Tears/Everblossom nonsense. And the underlying lore was a lot LESS interesting than in DS.



- Sekiro’s music is solid but nothing spectacular. Voice acting is surprisingly good in many cases. The game defaults to Japanese, but I tried that and I preferred the English. Sekiro and Isshin were both excellent, and Gyoubu was a classic. Kuro and Divine Child were a bit corny, though.
- I think a chunk of content could have been removed – namely, the Sunken and Depths stuff, which slowed down the game between the great Ashina Castle segments. But overall, the game was more consistent than Dark Souls. The ending was excellent, seeing areas you’ve already been to, but now in flames.
- Folding Monkeys was clever conceptually, but didn’t really work for me. As far as gimmick bosses go, it was still much better than Bed of Chaos, though.
- In terms of difficulty, the Sword Saint has a reputation for being one of the hardest bosses in gaming, and while he was fantastic fun, I didn’t think he was even in the top 5 most difficult in Sekiro.
















Isshin Ashina took my 7 hours to beat, Owl (Father) took me nearly 4, and most of the rest took 1-2. DoH was a lot easier than I anticipated. I finished him in under 10 tries.

From the outside, the initial experience of Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice can seem brutal. I, for one, can attest to the fact that the first 50-60 hours were a grueling test of patience. The combat, a complex dance of parries, deflections, and calculated aggression, felt less like a fluid ballet and more like a series of painful stumbles. There were moments, I admit, when the controller nearly met its demise against a wall. The difficulty, renowned even among FromSoftware's notoriously challenging titles, felt less like a rewarding obstacle and more like a relentless gatekeeper.

However, there was something that kept pulling me back. The world, steeped in a fantastical version of Sengoku-era Japan, was undeniably captivating. The environments, from the serene beauty of the Ashina Outskirts to the haunting depths of the Sunken Valley, were meticulously crafted. The art direction, a blend of stark realism and ethereal beauty, created an atmosphere that was both oppressive and alluring. I found myself driven to push forward, not just out of a sense of obligation, but because I genuinely wanted to see what lay beyond the next seemingly insurmountable boss.

Recently, I decided to revisit Sekiro. Armed with the knowledge of the game's mechanics, the patterns of the enemies, and a newfound appreciation for the rhythm of combat, everything clicked. The frustration that had plagued me for so long evaporated, replaced by a sense of exhilarating mastery. Each successful parry, each perfectly timed deathblow, became a testament to my growing skill. I began to understand why this game is so beloved. It's not just about overcoming difficulty; it's about the journey of becoming a skilled warrior. It's about the satisfaction of learning, adapting, and ultimately, conquering. Sekiro is a masterpiece, and now, I finally get it.

FromSoftware’s Sekiro™: Shadows Die Twice is not just a game—it’s a trial by fire. Set in a beautifully corrupted version of Sengoku-era Japan, it strips away the crutches of traditional RPG progression in favor of pure, punishing precision. This is not Dark Souls in a ninja mask; this is a game with its own brutal rhythm, demanding the player learn to parry, adapt, and persist.

You play as Wolf, a shinobi bound to protect a young lord, thrust into a web of betrayal, myth, and mortality. Unlike the studio’s previous titles, Sekiro focuses on stealth, posture-based swordplay, and vertical traversal. The grapple hook changes everything, letting you attack from unexpected angles and explore dense, interconnected levels with surprising agility.

Combat is where Sekiro shines with merciless clarity. Each enemy encounter is a duel, and each boss fight feels like a clash of fates. Timing is everything—every swing, dodge, and deflection feeds into a fluid, high-stakes dance. You don’t level up strength to overpower foes; you sharpen your mind and reflexes.

The game’s resurrection mechanic adds a clever twist. You can rise after death—but at a cost. It plays thematically with the ideas of loyalty and consequence, subtly influencing your decisions in the moment.

Visually, the game is stunning. From moonlit castles to corrupted valleys, every location is dripping with atmosphere. The storytelling is more direct than Dark Souls, but still leaves much in shadows, rewarding those who dig deeper.

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is not for the faint of heart. It doesn’t care how many Souls games you’ve beaten—it wants to break you, then rebuild you into something sharper. It’s a masterclass in tension, patience, and design. If you can endure its trials, the victory is among the most satisfying in gaming.

Rating: 9/10

Alright, so this game, Sekiro. They say it's from Japan. Good people, the Japanese, very smart. They make tough things. And this game? It's tough. Maybe too tough, some people are saying. Crooked difficulty, folks. You die, you die a lot. Shadows Die Twice, they call it. More like Shadows Die A Million Times, believe me.

You're this ninja, a one-armed ninja. They call him Wolf. Strong name. A winner's name. And he's got this young lord, a kid, very important kid, with the dragon's blood, or something. Everyone wants this kid. Bad people. Very bad. And Wolf, he's gotta protect him. It's all about loyalty, which, you know, I know a lot about loyalty. The best loyalty.

The fighting in this game, it's all about swords. Clang, clang, clang. You gotta be sharp. You gotta have the best reflexes. My reflexes? The best. Everyone agrees. You can't just swing wildly, like some of these other games, these failing games. No, you gotta be precise. You gotta break their posture. It's a whole thing. Very complicated. But when you beat a boss, and some of these bosses are just nasty, really nasty creatures, it's a tremendous feeling. A total victory. Like winning an election, a big one.

You're jumping around, rooftops, grappling hook – very fancy. The world, it's this old Japan, very beautiful, but also very dangerous. Lots of enemies. They come out of nowhere. Not fair! Many times, I said, "This is rigged!" But you gotta be tough. You gotta be a winner. You can't be a low-energy shinobi. No sleepy little ninjas are gonna win this game, I can tell you that.

They say it's hard. And it is. It's probably the hardest game. Maybe ever. People get frustrated. They quit. Sad! But if you stick with it, if you're strong, like me, you figure it out. You become the best. The One-Armed Wolf. He's a winner. He doesn't give up.

So, Sekiro. It's a tough game. A very, very tough game. But it's a quality game. High quality. Not like some of these other loser games. If you like a challenge, a real challenge, and you want to feel like a winner, a big-league winner, then maybe you try Sekiro. But don't come crying to me when it's tough. I told you. It's a tough one. But the best ones often are. Believe me.

"A brutal, beautiful journey through feudal Japan that rewards patience, skill, and perseverance."

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is not your typical FromSoftware game, even though it shares the studio's signature difficulty and atmospheric storytelling. Unlike the Soulsborne titles, Sekiro leans heavily into action and precise combat rather than RPG elements. There are no stats to grind—just your own skill to sharpen.

The swordplay is incredibly tight and satisfying. Every encounter feels like a deadly duel where posture, timing, and reflexes matter more than raw damage. Boss fights are intense and memorable, and the learning curve is steep but fair. Death is part of the process—each failure teaches something new.

The game world is stunningly crafted, with gorgeous, varied environments inspired by late 1500s Japan. The level design encourages exploration, and the verticality introduced by the grappling hook adds a fresh dynamic to movement and combat.

Narratively, Sekiro tells a more direct story than previous FromSoftware games, but still leaves room for interpretation and mystery. The voice acting and music are excellent, further immersing you in its unique world.

It’s not a game for everyone. The lack of difficulty options, limited character customization, and punishing learning curve can be off-putting. But for players looking for a challenging and rewarding experience, Sekiro stands as one of the finest action games of its generation.

Very great game, it took awhile to actually get to me but after one full playthrough I had a blast doing NG+ with all the skills unlocked and I realized just how deep and enthralling this game is. It is obviously very different to most Souls experiences as I quickly noticed being a big fan of the Dark Souls trilogy and Elden Ring but it's like a breath of fresh air after having played through them all and it is nice to see Fromsoft attempt to diversify a bit. I would love to see some sort of Sekiro 2 or even just a spiritual successor as From tends to do set in a different location in Japan with a different Shinobi. The story was really really engaging, probably the most out of any From game and probably also due in part to how small and concentrated the game feels. Again, coming from Elden Ring this game is comparatively tiny but makes up for it with just how dense it is. I prefer this vastly over Elden Ring, it reminds me a lot of the Dark Souls 1 map and how intimate it felt. You feel like you can piece together where everything is relative to everything else which is really cool, the game even has a primitive map function which is neet. The bosses, while there aren't many and they repeat a lot, are also very concentrated and well designed. The three main bosses who I won't spoil but any one whose played the game should know who I mean are very well designed and if I was to refight anyone 2-3 times I would want it to be them. But again that depends on your playthrough, there are 4 different endings you can get and each are interesting in their own way. I'd say the main ending is the most boring as there are no extra fights for it but that is to be expected. Overall this game is worth your time and fully understanding its mechanics and design is a treat that you should actively work towards, you won't regret it.

Sekiro at its core is like the long lost cousin twice removed bastard child of the Soulsborne style of games, it shares many similarities on the surface but underneath has so much depth not quite like the other games by FromSoftware. Sekiro has one of the most in depth skill ceilings of any game I have ever seen, after finally playing it myself and having beat every boss I finally get why people have always said Sekiro has the best combat system. Sekiro's combat is an incredible back and forth between you and your enemy, for each move you play well is another opportunity to gain advantage and turn the tides in your favor, and every play you fumble is another moment lost where you could keep building posture damage to get that perfect kill in 30 seconds flat. Sekiro's parrying, skills, and prosthetic tools are one of the best chained together systems I've ever had the pleasure of using. I seriously think this game is a model of how battling from the most simple opponents to the most terrifying bosses can be engaging at any level and difficulty. In other words this game was amazing and Id recommend to anyone who loves a good challenge.

This game delivers a deeply immersive experience, featuring innovative and satisfying combat mechanics that keep players engaged. The stunning visuals and atmosphere create a rich, captivating world that complements the solid storytelling and character development, ensuring an emotional connection with the journey. Boss battles are well-designed, offering exhilarating challenges that demand strategy and skill, making every victory feel earned.

Additionally, the rewarding learning curve fosters a strong sense of achievement, encouraging players to refine their abilities and discover new tactics as they progress. Its exceptional quality was recognized with the Game of the Year award, solidifying its place among the best titles in gaming history.

However, the game’s high difficulty level may lead to frustration, particularly for those new to the genre. While the combat is engaging, its linear design limits variation in playstyles, which might feel restrictive to some players. The shorter length compared to other titles could leave players wanting more, while the steep learning curve may deter newcomers from fully enjoying the experience. Furthermore, limited character customization options mean that personalization is minimal, potentially reducing the depth of player-driven storytelling.

Despite these drawbacks, the game remains a compelling experience for those who appreciate challenging, well-crafted gameplay, and its GOTY recognition proves its excellence in delivering a memorable adventure.

Sekiro’s world is breathtaking in every sense. From the snow-covered rooftops of Ashina Castle to the haunting serenity of Senpou Temple, every corner oozes atmosphere. The art direction is less about spectacle and more about evoking feeling: a crumbling dynasty, fading honor, and the ghosts of ambition whispering through the wind. Even the grotesque—from rotting monks to centipede-infested bodies—feels meaningful, never gratuitous. Decay has purpose here.

At the heart of Sekiro is a tale of devotion and identity. Wolf, a shinobi bound by an oath, must navigate loyalty, loss, and the burden of immortality. Yet, unlike FromSoft’s more cryptic entries, Sekiro’s narrative is remarkably intimate. The bond between Wolf and Kuro is the emotional axis around which the game turns. Their quiet interactions say more than most games manage in pages of dialogue. This is a story not just about saving a lord, but about what it means to let go.


Every landscape is in decline. Every character clings to something vanishing—glory, power, revenge. The world of Sekiro is dying, and only those who accept change move forward.
Immortality is not a gift here—it is a curse. Those who seek to escape suffering (like the monks or Genichiro) deepen it. Only through embracing pain and confronting attachment does Wolf find peace.
Sekiro, the character, struggles with identity—shinobi, tool, protector, killer. His journey mirrors the Buddhist path of shedding ego and purpose to reach inner stillness.
The different endings reflect this principle clearly. True peace comes not from power, but from releasing what we most cling to—life itself, even love.
Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice isn’t just hard—it’s honest. It challenges not just your reflexes, but your philosophy. It asks you to die, again and again, until you understand why you fight at all. With stunning visuals, masterful storytelling, and rich philosophical depth, Sekiro is a quiet, brutal masterpiece. It doesn’t scream its truths—it whispers them beneath falling cherry blossoms and blood-soaked blades.

I would certainly give Sekiro a solid 9/10 in all categories.

The only reason I ding it one point is due the the design that forces you to have a complete play through (Save Stepping the three endings) and an NG+ play through to get the Shura ending. It will also require some grinding for EXP and Mats in order to get it done in the two cycles. Yuck. I personally feel this sort of forced game play design is cheap and not worth doing just for Achievements. -1 for that nonsense.

On the subject of Deflect (parry) in combat. If you have read some of the negative reviews, you may have encountered statements regarding the use of the Deflect mechanic in combat. Most of these statements are flat out wrong. While the Deflect mechanic is indeed important, there are so many more additional skills, tools, moves, combos, etc. to work into the combat. Depending only on Deflect is not 'getting gud', it is actually not the best, or intended way, to play. FromSoftware puts all the tools and skills in the game for a reason. They make the game so much more fulfilling and allow for many different strategies and tactics against all the enemies in the game. These other tools and skills can sometimes make a Boss that is extremely difficult to defeat with just Deflect, an easy win. Of course, there are some times when Deflect is the way to go. And times when Deflect will not work at all.

I can highly recommend this game for all levels of player. And if it is to hard, for whatever reason, there are Mods available to make it easier. There are also guides and even step-by-step walk through videos to illustrate how it is done. The Environment, NPCs, Bosses, are all top-of-the-line. Don't pass on this one.

Like most games with a "its way too hard" identity I was sceptical with Sekiro at first. A few hours in and I understood why people said it. The combat is a learning curve but genuinely the best combat in any game I have played. The visuals and the sound cues of the combat is what makes you want to keep playing. You really do feel like you're improving at the game even within the same boss fight. The combat becomes a rhythm game which is just so satisfying. And I don't think any of the bosses are "want to smash your head into a wall" difficult either. They're all very unique, have their own character and are well designed (not you Demon of Hatred).

The story was surprisingly shorter than I expected but it was a fantastic story nonetheless. Very replayable with a stunning semi-open world too. And a NG+ option.

As of writing this review I have just started a NG+ to go achievement hunting and try achieve one of the various other endings to the game (and also just cos the game is super fun). Even though enemies are stronger on NG+, because you have all of your experience from your first playthrough you feel unstoppable this time round!

10/10 for me. 100% would recommend to someone who is looking for a challenging yet fun, unique and satisfying combat game!

Posted: April 27
Sekiro é, sem dúvidas, o melhor jogo de combate e ação que já joguei. É tudo tão simples e perfeito que mal consigo descrever com palavras a sensação que é terminar um dos finais desse jogo, fazendo muitas side quests e derrotando quase todos os bosses e mini-bosses. Sekiro consegue transformar uma mecânica tão simples (o parry) em algo tão incrível e complexo, em certos momentos, que vai ser difícil algum jogo conseguir replicar isso de alguma forma ou até chegar perto.

Só joguei dois Souls na minha vida: Sekiro e Elden Ring. E, por incrível que pareça, achei Elden Ring mais difícil e estressante que Sekiro. Ouso dizer que isso vai se repetir com qualquer outro Souls que eu jogar, pois Sekiro é o jogo mais justo que já joguei, justamente por ser VOCÊ (o jogador) o único responsável por sua derrota ou vitória.

Claro que isso se aplica a qualquer Souls, porém, em Elden Ring, por exemplo, uma build ou estilo de jogo pode te beneficiar ou prejudicar em determinados pontos do jogo. Já em Sekiro, a essência da gameplay é a mesma do começo ao fim, em qualquer rota que você escolha. Isso me proporcionou um aproveitamento e uma facilidade absurdamente maiores em Sekiro. Claro que ainda admito que o jogo é difícil, mas, como disse, é o mais justo possível.

Fora que toda a atmosfera, ambientação, atuação de voz e sound design transformam esse jogo em uma das maiores obras de arte dos videogames.

Dito isso: apenas jogue Sekiro e consiga superar as primeiras 3–5 horas, em que você vai apanhar bastante — e, caso não apanhe, apenas aproveite essa experiência incrível.

Posted: April 27
FromSoftware has crafted something truly special with Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice. While it carries the studio’s signature challenge and intensity, Sekiro feels like a bold and refreshing evolution. Sekiro demands absolute mastery of a fixed, beautifully honed combat system and the result is exhilarating. Where the other games lean into a deep heavy mysterious atmosphere and player customisation, in Sekiro lies a deeply emotional tale about loyalty, loss, and personal redemption with an actual protagonist - not a blank slate.

The swordplay is nothing short of breathtaking. Every clash feels deliberate, with parries, counters, and perfectly timed attacks creating a rhythmic dance of steel. Victory isn’t won by brute force or grinding stats; it’s earned through learning, adapting, and, above all, perseverance. This makes every boss fight — every triumph — incredibly rewarding.

The world of Sekiro is equally stunning. Set against a mythical, war-torn vision of feudal Japan, the game is a painterly blend of beauty and brutality. From the crumbling castles to the misty forests and sacred temples, each area tells a silent story, inviting exploration and rewarding curiosity with interesting side-quests.


In short, Sekiro is a masterpiece.

Whilst I didn't like the start of the game, I enjoyed it a lot more after picking it up again, to the point where it is probably one of my most favorite souls game. The bosses, the traversing, the equipment, the skill tree and various other mechanics added to the fun of my playthrough. Fighting bosses was incredible with the parry mechanic. In typical souls fashion, you have to bring the whole bar of a boss to 0 to defeat them, in this game - you can break their posture and completely ignore the health bar. When you execute these deflects perfectly, it feels incredibly rewarding. Although people say that the bosses in this game are the hardest, I do not think that is the case. Every boss is easily learnable through repetition and it pales in comparison to the speed of the Elden Ring bosses. There wasn't a single minute in my playthrough where I didn't like traversing the various parts of Ashina. It didn't feel exhausting, boring or depressing like in the dark souls games. The grappling system and the speed of sprinting adds satisfaction to exploration, not to mention that every item in the game has some good use, so if you find some loot around the map, without a doubt it is useful, which makes those hikes throughout the valley's and temple's that much more enjoyable. My only downside to this game is that it is short. This is not a bad thing because the game doesn't have that big of a story to tell for it to be big, and you do gain over 30 hours of gameplay from one playthrough which is incredible, but I think what it lacked was more challlenging bosses like Isshin and Genichiro. Overall, if you are sleeping on this game, don't, it is truly a masterpiece that would be incredibly hard to rank with the other souls games because of how good it is. It's up there with fan-favorite's like Dark Souls 3.

"Sekiro: Died More Than Twice"
Along the way, you will find that in "Sekiro", the bosses you face are not just enemies; they are harsh mentors. Every battle is like a lesson of self-cultivation, challenging your limits and tempering your skills.

Before playing "Sekiro", I had already completed "Black Myth: Wukong" and "Elden Ring", both of which I breezed through in 15 to 20 days, although I more or less looked up the guide. But "Sekiro" is different - although I also looked up the guide, but I still struggled with this game for a full six months. Every time I was defeated by the boss, the frustration almost made me collapse. So, I often gave up and turned to other games until I calmed down and came back to face the challenge again. Repeatedly, until I finally broke through and finally cleared the game.

Although I was beaten to a pulp by the boss countless times along the way, the sense of achievement that "Sekiro" brought me far exceeded the previous two games. Because here, the growth I deeply felt was not the improvement of the protagonist Sekiro's strength, but that as a player, I gradually became more resilient and sophisticated through repeated training.

Do not hesitate to pick this game up; there is simply no other ninja/samurai games that play like Sekiro. FromSoft has always been innovative in their projects (e.g. Bloodborne) and Sekiro pushed action genre combat mechanics to another level. The game does not demand long grinding and upgrades; all you need is your own skills and a controller. This game is your to-go if you want a playable, hard, rewarding anime fight scenes like those from Naruto or Demon Slayer. This game is made for anyone who will try.

Awesome game, I only recommend it but if you want a more contructed opinion, here's one:

Good points:

-It's in Japan (Japanese person here lol)

-It is a really challenging game, good for tryharders like me but not casual friendly, it's a dark souls like

-The story is pretty good for an action game (the map changes as the story goes on)

-The fights are really intense and unforgiving but makes you feel really good after beating a boss or a hard ennemy.

The things I didn't like:

-There's no map so you can be lost a bit easily but it is also the point, it makes you explore the whole map and discover new horizons (and get destroyed by overleveled ennemies)

-Like I said before it's not casual friendly, I was a casual at first so I got a bit demolised, I didn't care since it was fun and all but someone with less patience than me can get frustrated

-When you die you lose half your exp (to the next level, not your total exp) and half your money. You will get used to it but can be annoying sometimes but it makes you think about if you should fight the ennemy (or not) and not going in blind because you have "nothing to lose exept dying and go to the last checkpoint".

All I said before are just little things that annoyed me at first but you get used to it pretty quickly, I realise it's just part of the game and it's really fun at the end.
So even with those tiny details I rate it a 10/10, go for it you won't regret buying it :)

Sekiro is one of the only Japanese games that doesn't make me like a disgusting pervert with its story or setting. Fromsoft's approach to quest design, story telling, dialogue and plot is still as horribly clumsy as ever and I've always felt that's a shame. It's such a well thought-out world and an excruciating amount of effort has gone into realising it so beautifully. However, they never let it shine and keep everything only as so much set dressing. I’ve never liked how Fromsoft keep their lore so secondary as when a game is as good as this and is as so well realised it’s a failing in my eyes to keep everything so cryptic.

You’re here for the combat and Sekiro has the some of the best combat in any action game and to this day there is nothing as satisfying as memorising and mastering its mechanics. The game resembles a rhythm game with the performance of trading hits and countering your opponent to land your strikes. Everything is masterfully put together and it's a game that slowly gathers momentum until it clicks and then you're right with it up until the end. The game tragically comes apart for the crowd fights and this also falls victim to Fromsoftware's design of you never feel like you're fighting thinking opponents but rather animatronics that slash and cut at thin air. Your mileage may vary with this but I think the only improvement that could be made to on-the-ground gameplay is having a much more reactive enemy AI and the dream of a sequel will never die as I know I’m not the only one clamoring of more of this.

Sekiro is so stunningly refreshing and is the one game I recommend everyone try out of their catalogue as it has the most to offer and is the most consistent. Its performance was perfect and is so well polished it just seals the deal for one of the best action games on the market. I completed the game in around 19 hours but I know I'll be coming back time and time again. It worked perfectly with my X1X controller and had zero performance issues.

The game is incredibly linear in how it is meant to be played, so either you get it or you don't. FromSoft continues its trend of providing a larger set of gameplay mechanics than in previous titles without adequately explaining them (maybe this is an EN localization issue). Example: the poor explanation of dragonrot (both which deaths trigger dragonrot and the actual consequence of dragonrot).
Boss designs seem to go against gameplay lessons too: assuming the game wants you to parry a lot, two of the first three bosses are best beaten (by beginners at least) by avoiding hits; the Ogre has lots of grabs that cannot be parried and the bull damages you regardless of whether you parry or not, so dodging it is preferable if you are a beginner and cannot reliably parry. Gyoubu is mounted and spends a lot of time outside of your attack range, so this fight teaches you that playing passively is better than playing aggressively (which is not how this game is meant to be played).

Game is very average. Game play boils down to memorization and timing blocks/dodges, but you must have memorized the enemies entire move set before you can do the blocks/dodges. Otherwise you're trading blows at a considerable penalty to the player.

The game does however introduce an interesting system for these games with the danger symbol. Unfortunately the symbol system is mostly worthless because A you still need to memorize the entire enemy move set and B there is only 1 symbol, but there are 3 different types of "danger" moves. So the game tells you a special move you need to take a special action is coming, but not which one and the problem is the danger moves can happen so fast that if you don't already know which one it is before it happens you're getting hit. This makes the whole danger symbol system is largely pointless. There should have been a different symbol for each of the danger types.

The game also fails because it's constantly changing the rules on the player. When attacking enemies they will block and after X amounts of blocks you will get a red flash. This is supposed to indicate the player needs to stop attacking and prepare for a counter except when the developers decided that nope it doesn't work like that anymore. Some enemies will do counters before the red flash and some enemies will counter so fast that the red flash comes too late. Again this all boils down to the game being more of a memorization game more than a skill based action game.

When I finally beat the final boss I was so bored of having to deal with the constant cheap garbage design that I was more than happy to put the game down.

Posted: April 18
Phenomenal (9.5/10)

Best rhythm game I've played, even if it's not a typical one at that, nor is it a typical Soulsborne Game; this is it's own thing. The combat's mechanics are complex, unique, dynamic, precise, and fun, and the setting is equally well done. FromSoftware already nailed German-French medieval settings with Dark Souls, so it's no surprise that --since they're a Japanese studio-- they'd nail a Japanese atmosphere and setting. The music is incredible; the character designs are incredible; the aesthetic is incredible; and the story, while partially hidden and subtly horror-themed, is completely unique from what they've done before.

The combat's primary actions are attacking, deflecting, and perilous countering. There's also the optional use of prosthetics, combat arts, and quickstepping. Healing is an aspect as usual, though it's often pushed down as the bosses often have powerful gap closers and quick attacks that either prevent you from healing, or do a sizable chunk of your health, thus effectively wasting your heal. The shift away from dodging in favor of deflecting allows for a much faster, dynamic, and rhythmic experience than the other Soulsborne games have achieved. This of course is highly unconventional to those who've played the other Soulsborne games, so it can be quite an adjustment. When you do learn to deflect instead of quickstepping, the combat becomes a rhythmic dance of satisfying exchanges and sword clashes. It really is something special. The perilous attacks --chiefly grapples, sweeps, and thrusts-- act as small breaks and resets on the combat, allowing you to take a quick peak at the enemy's Posture before continuing. It's a brilliant addition to an already amazing combat system.

Along with it's three chief actions, the combat also has two battles that intertwine: Posture and Vitality. Vitality is the enemy's health bar, while Posture is most similar to Poise from the other Soulsborne games. Posture recovers on its own while no actions are being takes, though this happens at a slower rate the more Vitality damage one has taken. Unlike in the Soulsborne games, this mechanic is even more important than Vitality--as generally speaking, attacking the enemy's Posture is more effective than attacking their Vitality. Posture is damaged either by attacking, deflecting, or performing a Mikiri or Head Stomp in response to their Perilous Attacks. The more aggressive you are, the faster their Posture will build up, even if their Vitality barely takes any damage. As a result, you are never not rewarded for playing more aggressively, provided you have the speed and precision to do so without going down. The game really does tell you to "Get good" instead of "Go do something else and level up."

And of course, there's the game's aesthetics, design, and music. Between Sekiro and Bloodborne, I really can't pick a favorite. Sekiro's beautiful architecture, geography, and color schemes are something to behold, even when they shift as you explore different areas of the game. It nails the feeling of a feudal, horror-instilled land where people will do everything in their power to survive. The whole game looks and feels like it was made by someone with intimate knowledge of the culture, folklore, and mythology present in Japan in the Sengoku period where the game takes place, even taking small liberties that nonetheless feel authentic. The music does wonders to enhance the setting as well. Traditionally, FromSoftware has preferred a calming silence during the exploration of their games, where all you heard were your own footsteps on worked stone, the chilling winds atop a mythic castle, and the creaking, mechanical devices used for various purposes. In Sekiro, they took a step away from this therapeutic sound design, and instead gave every area its own sound design. Our protagonist, being a shinobi, doesn't make much sound with his light steps, so they can fill in the rest with sound tracks that express grandiose, beauty, mystique, and moments of safety. Entering combat changes these sound tracks to those of battle, hardship, and fury to further drive home the premise that you're in the Sengoku period. Despite the diversity of these combat sound tracks, they all appear to have the same DNA, while also expressing their difference, it's almost magical how well they work for what they do.

Sekiro might very well be my all-time favorite. Its combat and setting are some of the best I've experienced in any game with none to match.

Just a phenomenal game. I had originally tried to play two years ago and got about 5 hours in before I gave up after I couldn't beat a boss. I'm so glad that I gave the game a second chance and pushed through. It's challenging but if you go in knowing that it will be a challenge you'll be much better off.

The combat is next-level. When I played for the first time one of my issues was that I felt the game was pigeonholing the player into one specific way to play. I learned in my second playthrough that this is not the case at all and you can really get creative with different bosses/enemies/situations. The combat is fluid, intense, engaging, and never gets stale. Each checkpoint you reach and boss you beat gives you that very rewarding feeling that I think is what makes games like this so appealing - it can be a rush.

If you're a fan of other Souls-like games then this is a must-play. If the souls-like tag turns you off but you like idea of a high-intensity action game, then I would still highly recommend it - I don't feel as though the souls-like elements are as 'hardcore' as Dark Souls/Bloodborne/Elden Ring.

I may be late to the party but I'm very glad to have finally played this game; I hope I can convince others to play too. I really wish they had made DLC.

Would I recommend Sekiro? Yes, absolutely… but not to just anyone.
I first discovered FromSoftware through Elden Ring, and after pouring hours into that game, I received Sekiro as a gift. I figured, "Hey, I beat Elden Ring, I should be able to handle this too." Oh boy... how wrong I was.
Sekiro is a beautiful game with beautiful environments, incredible music, impressive graphics, and a compelling story in Japan. But it's also brutally difficult x.x And yes, Elden Ring is tough, too, but Sekiro sometimes felt even more punishing. I won't pretend I was enjoying every second of it. There were long stretches where I genuinely wasn't having fun and just felt stuck, trying to figure out how to play the game properly.
That said, the satisfaction of finally beating a boss—especially the optional ones—was something else. Those moments made it worth it. Finishing the game, and especially defeating Isshin, didn't leave me feeling like some hardcore gamer but more like someone who just went through a very intense test of patience (mostly with myself).
Ultimately, it was a challenging, MANY TIMES frustrating, but ultimately rewarding experience. So yes, I recommend Sekiro, but again, just know what you're getting into.

Posted: April 13
I have beaten Sekiro, here's my review:
Positives
1) Rewarding Combat:
The combat feels crisp, with the posture and parrying system driving good gameplay.
Nailing each deflect makes every fight intense and satisfying—a standout mechanic that carries you through the grind.
That's just ONE side of the story. Read negatives for more on gameplay.



Most bosses are recycled throughout the game—lazy design. You fight the same archetypes with slight variation. Repetitive.
Different bosses share identical moves. Owl, Genichiro, and Tomoe share some identical moves, and Isshin’s lightning attack is just Genichiro’s with a new coat of paint. Lazy and uninspired.
Some areas are repeated late-game with minimal changes. A zone might turn into a “combat area” with fire or purple warriors, but it’s the same old map—pure laziness.
Most combat moves in the skill tree are pointless. They’re flashy animations with no practical use, offering zero advantage in fights. Wasted potential.
The game halves your currency every time you die, and since death is constant, it forces tedious farming. Prosthetics (extra weapons) require emblems bought with this currency—bad design. A cooldown system would’ve avoided this grind.
Side quests are nearly impossible without an online guide. NPCs spout boring, cryptic dialogue, and you meet them later in different levels with new hints. Miss a step, and the quest stalls. The carp side quest? Utterly ridiculous.
Alternate endings are a nightmare to unlock blind. Eavesdropping on specific characters and collecting certain items feels random, demanding extreme attention to bloated story twists. Without a guide, it’s a lost cause—major design flaw.
The camera’s on you to control, and it’s terrible. It loses focus even against one enemy, leaving you staring at the sky or ground while a boss pummels you from behind. Frustrating and sloppy.
The game’s biggest flaw—and why I don’t recommend it—is its so-called “difficulty.” Sekiro is hyped as FromSoftware’s toughest, but I’m quoting “difficulty” because it’s fake. Bosses feel hard because the game doesn’t teach mechanics through intuitive gameplay—it forces you to memorize moves like you’re reverse-engineering code. Delayed animations demand perfect timing, turning combat into a bizarre Guitar Hero rhythm game. Worse, animations lock you into actions, ignoring inputs until they finish. Start an attack and see the enemy counter? Too bad—you can’t cancel, even early on, and 99% of the time, their move interrupts yours, dealing damage. Memorizing patterns isn’t skill; it’s just tedious. Beating Sekiro doesn’t make you a hardcore gamer—it just means you endured frustration from flawed mechanics. After slogging through the final boss, the game had the audacity to ask, “Want a second playthrough?” Yeah, right—more rage for a different ending? No thanks, Sekiro. I beat your sorry ass and never want to touch you again. Glad I got it on sale for peanuts. If you crave testing your patience, go for it. Everyone else, steer clear—this game’s overhyped.

Whilst I have no doubts that I would strongly recommend this game to any who are up to the challenge/are intrigued by the setting/concept of the game, I need to stress that it is not without its flaws.

I have a lot of praise to give this game, so I will start off with the negatives to get them out of the way:

- Many of the mini-bosses, and a few main-bosses, are repeated in this game, which to me is relatively lazy in a game that is the shortest in length out of any of the Fromsoftware (souls-like) games. Moreover, whilst some reoccurring bosses such as have real reason to appear multiple times and feel satisfying to fight every time, others, such as being reused felt lazy and meaningless.

- Whilst it is subjective, as someone who has beaten many of the Fromsoft games now, I felt as though this game easily had the weakest world building, atmosphere and characters, despite being a much more narrative driven game than previous titles. To me, the ambiguity of the worlds Fromsoft has made has such a uniquely engaging feel that hooks you in and makes you want to know more. Whereas here, whilst passable, the story/world feels somewhat lacklustre to me.

However, with this said, the high points of this game are so strong that I can forgive some of the frustrations I have with the game. The back-end of the game, especially when going for the purification ending to fight , on top of the other few stellar late game bosses is an exhilarating gameplay experience. I wouldn't say many of the bosses in this game other than particularly captivated me in terms of their designs, but the actual deflecting-based gameplay in Sekiro is amazing when it's at its best (in 1v1, sword on sword duels).

Thus overall, despite a few issues I do have with the game, I would still highly recommend this game, especially to those who crave the challenge of Fromsoft games, as this is perhaps THE most challenging of them all. Consequently, it's also one of the most rewarding to master, and the deflection based gameplay is something I'd happily see return one day to another mainline game.

9/10.

I learned to stop staring at posture and health bars and just focus on the fight.
Reacting to patterns, punishing openings, staying sharp — that’s where Sekiro shines.

Don’t be afraid to look up a counter or two. The tools are there for a reason.
The combination of prosthetics and combat arts gives you just enough versatility to handle anything, as long as you stay present and adapt.

This game taught me to read movement, not numbers.

The flow of deflecting, dodging, and countering in Sekiro felt more natural to me than anything in Dark Souls or Elden Ring.
There’s no builds or stats to hide behind — just timing, rhythm, and growth.

If you need help, check out Sekiro Guru on YouTube. Great guides, no fluff.

Posted: April 11
One of my favorite souls-like games out there.

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is a masterpiece in both gameplay and storytelling. FromSoftware has crafted an intense and rewarding experience that feels incredibly unique, even among other souls-like titles. The combat is fast, precise, and brutally satisfying. Every enemy encounter demands focus and mastery, and the feeling of finally overcoming a tough boss is unforgettable.

I especially loved the posture-based combat system—it pushes you to be aggressive and strategic rather than overly cautious. The setting is beautiful and atmospheric, with stunning environments and a haunting soundtrack that perfectly fits the tone. The story is deeper than it first appears, with emotional beats and hidden lore that kept me engaged from start to finish.

That said, this game isn't for everyone. The difficulty can be punishing, especially for players new to FromSoftware games. But if you're up for a challenge and enjoy tight, skill-based combat, Sekiro is absolutely worth your time.

Highly recommend it to anyone looking for a unique and memorable action-adventure game.

From a gameplay standpoint i personally feel this blows everything else fromsoftware has produced out of the water. Combat in this game makes you feel like a crack addict in withdrawals after its all over. Love the lack of a stamina bar, and the boss posture bar system makes it feel as though your on the verge of losing your lead in mario kart, simply meaning that disengaging an enemy actively punishes you, almost setting you back to where you began in the fight. Overall the game is definitely the best one ive experienced out of the fromsoft catalogue so far, easy 10/10.

Posted: April 9
Sekiro Shadows Die Twice is probably FromSoftware's best work, as I haven't played Bloodborne yet.

Everything about it is just beautifully designed. It has a simple yet compelling story to keep the player motivated with interesting plot points. The level design is good but quite linear, however, that is actually a positive. You'll reach a point in the game where you can do some things in whichever order you prefer but the levels themselves are quite straightforward. Instead of souls/runes, you get skill points to unlock skills. Every time you kill a boss or mini-boss you get a reward that increases your health and attack power, giving you a reason to fight hard optional mini bosses. It's honestly quite refreshing not having to worry about what build you're going to go for.

As for the combat, it's one of the best in gaming history. The combat in this game is extremely rewarding with a steep learning curve. The enemies are all well designed for this type of combat, not to mention the bosses. This game has some of the most fun and well designed bosses. Fighting a boss is quite intuitive and instinctive as you can easily read their movements and parry accordingly, the skill relies on being able to pull off the parries perfectly. Beating a boss always gave me that sense of accomplishment and perseverance. The final boss in this game gave me a hard time and I got quite frustrated but never thought the it was unfair. Each death was a learning experience. It was such a good and balanced fight, it felt immensely satisfying when I was able to finally beat it on a “just one more time” try.

I played this game after Elden Ring with its ridiculous bosses and I firmly believe that game would have benefited from using some sort of parry like mechanic. This isn't an ER review, but playing Sekiro made me realize how poorly designed Elden Ring and it's DLC are. It's a shame because Sekiro predates ER and yet the only lesson they took from this game to put in ER was faster more aggressive bosses.

To finish off this review I just want to say that Sekiro is a masterpiece of a game. It was amazing fun from start to finish.

One of my favorite games of all time, and definitely the one I think has the best combat.

Most games are tied around a philosophy of "easy to learn, hard to master", which in itself is very good, but Sekiro leans more into "hard to learn, easy to master". Once you get good at Sekiro, any boss is really just a test of your capacities, but this also means that it's not that easy to get into.

The game has a grappling hook, by the way. Pretty convincing if you ask me.

The game's combat is heavily centered around parries, as you probably already know, but through the game you are also given a bunch of shinobi tools as well as combat arts, that can all be used in creative and fun ways (deflecting lightning using the feathers tool is definitely in the top 3 of my coolest moments in games I've played).

Overall, an awesome experience that makes me not only look cool, but feel cool, everytime I revisit it to beat some bosses' butts.

Posted: April 8
This type of sword-clashing game seems to be right up my alley and is enhanced by how brilliant the soulslike formula is. You, however, don't want to approach this game like a typical soulslike game, because this is an entirely different beast on its own. This game has a lot more focus on parrying, and every combat quickly turns into a rhythmic battle where the goal is to bring down each other's posture so either of you can deliver the final death blows. There are also some attacks (indicated by the red kanji symbol) that simply can't be parried, and you have to leverage techniques like the Mikiri Counter, double jump over the enemy's head, or just regular step dodges to evade those attacks. The SFX when you execute a perfect parry is oddly satisfying, and you can also experiment with prosthetics and combat arts. Some of these actually work particularly well against certain bosses and overall add a lot of variety to your combat approach, despite playing with only one singular build throughout the entire game.

My only complaint is that the game was surprisingly too short, but this justifies a new game cycle. The engaging combat and amazing bosses make replaying it enjoyable rather than tiresome.

Clangkino/100. If they make Sekiro 2 or something similar, I'd buy it in a heartbeat.

I use a 100 point grading scale to rate games and i don NOT look at reviews or before you buy videos before hand so that my opinion isnt biased!! there are 5 primary categories each with 4 sub categories. game play graphics monetization content quality and fun vs difficulty, with a bonus category that can add or subtract points. the main one being new player experience! if a game is trash within the first 2 hours then the other qualities wont have the same impact if they arent smoothed out until after the point that someone can get a refund! so only the new player experience has a plus or minus point system of more than 5 points!

that being said i rated this game at 68. then took 10 for the new player experience and gave 3 for the plot quality!
giving it a total of 61 !
for me to recommend a game it must score at least a 70.

here are my general thoughts.
i took control of sekiro and was immediately excited to carry on his journey after watching an absolutely killer opening cut scene!! great plot set up!
after the "here's what buttons do what and how to navigate the game world" intro wherein you loose your arm in an unwinnable even if you win fight the game really starts..
and thats where it falls off fast!
i wouldnt have a problem if they had more basic combat mechanics available right at the start that made fights more interesting and fluid! but they just arent there! you spend the first hour of the game parrying. and dealing with large or heavily armored enemies that dont have consistent dps output hit boxes. its not unbearable its just boring and frustrating!!
until you fight the red eyes tied to the stockade looking wooden structure.
i got hit by that piece of sh*t when he obviously missed me by a mile so many times that i didnt even bother to play anymore!
i love souls like games! ive played all of the Dark souls games and elden ring. granted elden ring is the poster child for games that try to use artificial difficulty to pretend to be hard and still are laughably easy! and i have cleared them all many many times!
Sekiro is in the realm of souls like but it tries so hard to be "hard" thats its "hard" for the wrong reasons! handicapping a new player just doesnt feel good and the only people that enjoy that are the die hards! and im not one of them!
realistically i wont say the game is bad! but i also would NOT recommend it to any one that doesnt play every game they ever play on Hard mode from the first time they launch it!
its no a game for casual gamers! and it is NOT new player friendly!

TLDR: if you like obscenely hard games then pick this up! if not then dont waste your money!

Posted: April 6
TLDR; Recommended only if you are a From Software fan, not so much for casual players.

The From Software engine is deeply felt in this game where reaction time is paramount. The camera goes crazy when the boss jumps around the arena, gets up in your face or simply is larger than you. Your commands get buffered in. You are forced to watch and skip the same cutscene as you retry certain bosses. However, you cannot respec to have an easier time here, you have to master the system. You die in 2-3 hits most of the time. And you cannot call or summon help for hard fights either.

I had a lot of trouble, not knowing why things seem like half a second off most of the time and I never quite figure it out even at the end. The frustration was so great I was almost in tears multiple times as it felt even more unfair than the other games.

But when it works, it felt great. Unlike other games where the bosses just fade away, here you get to execute them at least. It felt like closure and it is animated brilliantly for a game this old. The battle mechanics for certain encounters are incredible, you cannot find it anywhere else. Even for the longest story path, including all the optional encounters, after getting stuck in fights, it is still at a relatively standard 30-ish hours.

Would I be interested in a sequel? Maybe. If only a similar game, with such innovation is created on a different engine. Do they deserve GOTY? Yes. If I did not struggle with the system, this is a brilliant game. So take the plunge if you are familiar with their game engine, otherwise you have to be very patient.

This game actually ruined all souls games and most other souls-likes with how fantastic the gameplay is. I play DS1 and see the attack timings of an enemy and my brain defaults to trying to deflect it.

Posted: April 5
Game looks great, has nice animations and is totally unfun and not designed for you to enjoy. It has taken the extreme end of the punishing side of souls like games and cranked it up and stripped it dry of choices and options.

Combat is a slog, every fight, every boss is a slog. Bosses don't have HP in the normal sense, HP reduces the rate they regen stagger. Stagger is how you kill them by parrying *certain* attacks. Some of the major bosses can be totally unkillable if they spam repeat a few moves when they are low on stagger as you can't do anything but block or parry them and it doesn't build up stagger and all the while they are regening.

The parrying can feel good but perfectly fighting a boss for 2 mins straight, attacking on every open, parrying every attack, but getting hit once can mean you are either dead or near dead and all that stagger you built up? Gone. You would be better off starting over again.


The armaments you get via your prosthetic hand are 99% useless. Some can have a niche scenario but honestly could be removed from the game without much change.

There are better games out there and while there is quality in Sekiro it's wasted because of how overly punishing every single boss fight is. Even Demon Souls 2 is less of a time waster than Sekiro.

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice (2019) was a significant departure from the usual formula, focusing more on pure action and precision rather than RPG elements. Set in a fantastical version of feudal Japan, players took on the role of Wolf, a shinobi seeking vengeance. The gameplay centered on perfectly timed parries and breaking enemy posture rather than whittling down health bars. This shift from traditional Souls mechanics polarized fans, but the intense focus on mastering combat made Sekiro a standout title. Its storytelling was also more direct, following a defined protagonist rather than a nameless, hollow character.
