This game isn’t just about becoming a sword god—it’s about proving you have the patience of a Buddhist monk and the reflexes of a caffeinated raccoon. It's a masterpiece of blade-on-blade action, where every fight is a brutal ballet of parries, precision, and pain.

The bosses? At first, they feel like divine warriors sent to humble your soul, each one more awe-inspiring than the last. But then you actually learn how to play, and suddenly they’re just glorified sword-wielding piñatas waiting to get posture-broken. But even when they get easier, they remain incredible—every design, every encounter, every perfectly choreographed duel is a love letter to samurai cinema.

The story? Peak. The visuals? Exquisite. The satisfaction of deflecting an entire boss combo with perfect timing? Better than finding an extra fry at the bottom of the bag. The only real downside? The game is over way too fast, and now you’re just some guy with no more swords to clash.

I came in expecting to get absolutely bodied, but once I got the mechanics down, I was slapping bosses around like I was their disappointed dad. Still an amazing game, but by the end, I was parrying everything like a damn rhythm game champion. 10/10, I wish it would’ve humbled me a little more.

15+ hours to beat Genichiro + Isshin. Yeah maybe I still need to get gud. But that fight, among the several others that took me 5+ hours, highlight the biggest problem I have with Sekiro and other Souls games: you're not necessarily getting "gud" in the sense that your skill must increase to beat the bosses and enemies. Skill does increase and is important for beating the bosses, but memorizing arbitrary movesets for each specific boss is actually the key to winning. Like you could train someone for 1000 hours in Sekiro, then introduce them to a new boss and they still wouldn't beat it the first time. Other Souls games have this problem, but Sekiro is probably worse in the sense that it's more than just "dodge everything." You have to deflect, jump, dodge, and ideally counter moves, and the timing of all of it needs to be perfect. And why did the last boss need to be 4 phases (where 3 of them are each pretty unique, the last one just builds off the previous)?? Whose idea was it to make the boss "harder" by just increasing the number of phases??

With that gripe aside, I think FromSoftware probably achieved exactly what they set out to, and executed on that idea perfectly. The fighting system is nearly flawless (except for the occasional counter attempt that turns into an unexpected dodge for some reason). The music is amazing, the story is pretty cool, bosses are incredible. The levels are interesting and interconnected. I personally didn't ever look forward to logging into this game, but maybe it's just not my cup of tea, so I can't fault the game for that.

Incredible game. But don't play unless you want to understand true pain.

After a few years with this game, I finally decided to share my opinion. I completed the game and tackled the optional boss towards the end.

Gameplay
The gameplay experience is certainly hardcore, with mechanics that must be learned to progress. However, the difficulty stems from the wrong reasons. It's not about mastering the combat system but about memorizing enemy and boss attack patterns.

At first, the game gives you the illusion that mastering the mechanics it provides is enough to excel. In reality, it demands that you know every detail of enemy attacks, forcing you to predict their moves to defend effectively. This system penalizes players who haven't already studied enemy behavior, making the challenge feel artificial and less intuitive. In essence, battles become a matter of rhythm to follow, but only after you’ve previously learned it.

Story
The story is flat and uninspired, without significant twists. While narrative depth isn't expected in a game of this genre, in this case, it's entirely negligible.

Graphics and Art Design
The graphics are average but uninspired and, at times, downright dull. They fail to immerse the player in the Japanese fantasy world that the game aims to present, especially when compared to other titles with far superior artistic design.

Audio
The soundtrack is decent but falls short compared to other titles by the same developer. The same tracks often repeat too frequently, becoming annoying after just a few hours of gameplay.

Characters
The characters are bland and lack depth, including the protagonist and his master. The only ones that stand out slightly are Genichiro and Owl, but even they are tied to banal and unimaginative narrative motivations.

Conclusion
The game proves to be a significant disappointment, filled with false promises, and backed by a community of staunch defenders. After defeating the final boss, one of the most difficult in the game, I found the experience more akin to a repetitive task than genuine entertainment. The victory, achieved after numerous attempts, didn’t leave me feeling skilled but merely aware that I had memorized the battle.

Each encounter requires strict adherence to predetermined patterns, leaving little room for player creativity. The limitation in object usage and the obligation to follow precise sequences make the gameplay monotonous and rigid.

Final Rating: 5/10. Not recommended. Finishing the game doesn’t make you skilled; it simply means you’ve memorized every single battle. If you're looking for a truly hardcore experience, consider playing a game like Elden Ring or another Souls title with a no-damage challenge. It would definitely be more fun and genuinely challenging.

Posted: March 29
After beating my charmless, beadless, demon bell run, I believe I have the ability to formulate a potent review on this game. "Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice" is (in my opinion) one of those games that checks every box, and then some; each aspect of this game is near to perfection. Of course, there are no 'perfect' games, but Sekiro is one that would hold its own quite strongly compared to other games in a similar genre and playstyle. The combat is the most fluid I have ever seen, I have played many Action/RPG games and I can very easily state that Sekiro got the deathblow on their combat, it is INSANELY slick. Spectacle is something that makes games like Sekiro really rise above all, and FromSoftware made sure not to skimp out on this. Not only is the combat fluid, it is also beautiful in a myriad of ways: the opponents you face and how the arena design effortlessly intertwines with said opponent, the visual clanging of blades, the sense of mystique as your adversary pulls a seemingly otherworldy attack on you, and of course the sound design. My god, the sound design. The deflect sound alone is one of my favourite sounds to ever come from media (alongside the trick gun from Django Unchained to name an example.)

Now, for the most potent part of this review: how this game makes you feel and what it teaches you. Sekiro will pit you against seemingly insurmountable mainstory bosses nearer toward the end (this is only really the case for less seasoned players of FromSoftware's titles) and each time you face this opponent and lose, you're gaining skill. Little by little this game will teach you without speaking a word. They hand you the buttons, give you a few tips, and then that's it. People who give up after the first few bosses or even the first boss are only letting themselves down; Sekiro has a personality check, if you are one that has your determined spirit waver in light of a decent challenge then you will fail. Truly difficult enemies will not only train you mechanically in your skillset, but will also teach you patience, resolve, critical thinking, this list could go on for miles. During my hardest run yet, that had the endgame bosses two hitting me on imperfect deflect and one hitting me when my guard was down, I felt such a rush of adrenaline when I killed truly difficult bosses, some taking 30-60 tries, with the final boss easily clocking in at around the 80 tries mark. But that adrenaline is genuinely something I cannot attribute to anything else, it truly felt like this game (compared to others) had brought me to the heights of my emotion when it comes to triumph. If you have beaten this game a few times, give this run a try, it will change how you view this game and you will have another humongous win under your belt, because I know you're a winner.

Thank you FromSoftware for creating this masterpiece, for giving me something to always come back to and enjoy. and for once again pushing the boat even further in terms of your creative advancements. I really do hope to see more of the Sekiro universe in the future but alas that is a dim future, which is truly, truly a shame.

That being said, if you're on the fence about playing Sekiro and need the top Pro/Con: When you win a fight, you will feel like a divine wind carried you to victory; not always by virtue of your own prowess because sometimes you feel like surviving a difficult encounter was simply fate or chance (it will feel like a blessing from any and all gods). You should get the game if you are ready to suffer a bit. I'd definitely recommend a guide, but remember to practice parries on the blue robed guys and the red hat guys, they're pretty quick, but they'll help you get better. Also, get Mikiri counter as fast as you can, and try to be relaxed when you're checking out a boss's attack patterns, it's easier to learn them.

Remember, Hesitation is death.

Smallish rant here:
To anyone who feels offended that it doesn't support 240 fps, 8K ultra-wide, this game came out in 2019, SIX YEARS AGO, and listen, It hasn't been updated since 2019. Again, that's SIX YEARS AGO! That means that no, the game cannot fully use every aspect of your rig, especially if you bought a 2,500 dollar monitor.
The game is in Japanese, and no, that's not a personal attack on English people either. The game is fairly difficult, it won't be fun for some of the people out there.
When fighting bosses that flinch, BE AGGRESSIVE.
Remember, Gachin's sugars are an item that boosts stealthiness majorly, and, you can farm them right after the first main boss.
If you are having trouble with directions, open up Fightincowboy's guide on Sekiro.
I am going to CRASH OUT if someone leaves another review like "trash" or "it's bad."


If my rant about the negative reviews was too long for you, here's the TLDR: The game is worth a purchase, if you have some impulse control, can keep calm, and are willing to memorize boss patterns.


Have a good day/night, and please stop complaining about things that have obvious answers to them. I'm sorry if you got stuck with this game after playing it for longer than two hours and ya didn't like it.

I used to be hesitant about playing games like this, but Sekiro has become one of the best games I have ever played. Although it feels extremely difficult at first, once you get used to it, the experience becomes truly enjoyable. The urge to quit is strong, but I highly recommend not giving up bcs once you grasp the mechanics, the sense of satisfaction is incredible.

Sekiro was my first souls-like game. At first I was exhausted from fighting bosses, getting stuck on some of them, and struggling to progress. But over time I realized that the real battle wasnt against the bosses. It was against myself. The game forces you to truly master its mechanics, and once you do, the reward is immense. This realization made the game far more meaningful to me because Sekiro is not just an action game it is, in a way, a rhythm game. Every boss has a unique combat rhythm and style. You cant just mash buttons and expect to win, you have to move in sync with the games tempo. This is why every successful deflect feels like a small victory in itself.

Boss fights are truly adrenaline pumping. You can physically feel your heart rate increasing while fighting bosses. But at the same time, it teaches you to stay calm, train yourself, and think strategically. The game forces you to rely not just on your reflexes but also on your mental discipline.

The world of Sekiro is captivating the atmosphere and design are incredibly well crafted. Shinobi Prosthetic offers various gameplay options, but the game ultimately encourages you to stay loyal to your sword, which enhances its overall philosophy. Unlike rpgs that allow you to switch builds and create overpowered characters, Sekiro demands mastery of a single combat style. If it allowed cheesy builds to trivialize boss fights, it would undermine its core philosophy. The way Shinobi Prosthetic adds variety without being overpowered is a remarkable design choice.

That being said, I cant ignore the games flaws. The camera controls are really frustrating. Sometimes, even when I have locked onto a boss, the lock randomly breaks, and by the time I find the boss on screen again, I am already dead. This becomes especially problem in fast paced fights against highly aggressive bosses.

Additionally the AI occasionally breaks immersion. For example, I can kill an enemy right next to another npc, and they wont even react. Similarly, if a mini boss spots me, I can simply run away, wait for about 20 seconds, and they completely forget I was ever there allowing me to sneak up and take one of their health bars with ease. A more advanced AI system would have made the game not only harder but also more immersive and rewarding.

Btw I highly recommend playing Sekiro with a controller. The sheer satisfaction of performing a deflect, followed by an execution... It’s hard to put into words how something purely visual and auditory can feel so rewarding, but you will understand once you experience it.

I have only played for 25 hours so far. I hope I can finish it.

Score: 9.1/10

It is still a Fromsoft game and therefore cant be bad, but in my opinion, it is definitely the weakest available for PC aside from DS2 for the following reasons.

There is not a single boss in this game that isnt suffering from at least one of the following:
- being a glorified mob (f.e.: the assassins in Ishina Castle)
- the moveset being designed around a tool of the gimmick-prosthesis (f.e.: Guardian Ape scream, Old Isshin fire)
- hyperarmor (pretty much everyone)
- input read (f.e.: every Isshin)
- stun locks (f.e.: Owl)
- bad telegraphing (f.e.: Inner Isshin, Monk)
- unintuitive dodging/parrying of grab-, sweep- or stab-attacks (f.e.: Guardian Ape roll grab)
- bad hitboxes (every grab attack)
- status effect spam (f.e.: Owl poison, Headless Ones)
- nonsensically delayed attacks (mostly bosses with long weapons)
- bad camera (f.e.: cavalry)
- bad arena (almost all of them - every boss sooner or later ends up in a wall or corner)
- reduced parry-frames making it impossible to dodge long combos (f.e.: Genichiro)

Many of these issues exist in every Fromsoft game, but this one is the first without a single boss, that isnt overshadowed by bad game design. The final boss, no matter the ending, is particularly bad, because he punishes you for doing what every other boss has been teaching you.

The rest of Sekiro isnt top tier either.
The game is very short and areas are not nearly as visually distinct as in other Fromsoft games, which usually manages to overshadow bad graphical quality.
Exploration might get you upgrade materials and such to enhance the gimmick tool, but doesnt feel very rewarding without either armor or weapons to change the gameplay significantly. This also hurts the combat, because boss issues aside, the satisfying combat system gets old incredibly fast. It doesnt help that you keep getting pushed into group fights, when the game is clearly designed around 1 on 1 fights. Movement also feels very clunky and jump inputs always appear to be delayed.
The level design is as convoluted as usual, but unlike in the other games, where exploration usually keeps you going into the right direction, in this one you keep ending up in dead ends you cant progress before doing something elsewhere, artificially stretching a first playthrough.
This just personal preference, but I also hate any Samurai setting.

Posted: March 26
This game suffers because its main mechanic is poorly implemented. Parrying should feel satisfying to accomplish, but the parry window is so large that it makes you feel disconnected from your character. You can deflect an attack by pressing the button for what seems like en entire half-second before the attack even comes at you; it is simply too lenient. I also found the solution to every flurry attack is not to learn the timings, it is to spam deflect because that works nearly every time.

Additionally, the movement is clunky and unresponsive. Jumping has an insane delay on it, as does changing directions since your character must decelerate before it can accelerate in another direction. Approaching a ledge only sometimes blocks you from falling off it, but its inconsistency makes it feel even worse. I found myself falling into the void more than I thought I should due to this.

Finally, the boss fights are amazing, but the content in-between is uninspired and repetitive. After each boss I dreaded the multi-hour journey to the next one with generously copy/pasted enemies, grappling across the same Japanese houses forever, and repeating entire stealth missions over and over again in some areas if you die to the mini-boss and want to fight it uninterrupted again.

TLDR: The parry window is too forgiving, the movement feels awfully clunky, and fighting anything but bosses is repetitive.

Posted: March 26
Really baffling design choices in it and I don't see how this was Game of the Year it came out.

1. When you die, there's no way to earn back your experience or gold, though it comes easy enough. It's just tedious. You could buy gold pouches to stash gold for later but that just adds boring trips to merchants for little reason.

2. The game wants you to engage the enemy and use the posture mechanic, which is fine, but it doesn't do enough to help you understand it. Additionally there are unblockable moves denoted by red kanji and a musical note, but it's not clear which enemies do what and how best to block them. There also appears to be some delay between when the note/kanji appears and when you are supposed to hit? I don't know it's weird.

3. Most of the game is the same killing trash uninteresting enemies until a miniboss (seemingly copy pasted though there is some difference between them) and rarely an actual boss.

4. They added a jump and grappling hook, but all that did with the level design was add over 9000 tree branches to hook onto and Japanese pagoda roofs to jump on.

5. The story is boring and dragonrot is just an easily solved nuisance.

6. The battle music is basically the same ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ track every time.

7. There is stealth (climbing roofs/trees, hiding in plants), but again, pretty basic.

I will list some brief pros though: grappling hook is neat idea (just doesn't really go anywhere), the prosthetic is like Bloodborne's weapon transformations but offers more permutations for the player to use them with each other.

I genuinely think this is once of FromSoftware's worst games in the modern era. It's a boring, tedious, glumly designed rip-off of Metal Gear Rising.

Posted: March 22
For a long time this game left a bitter taste in my mouth. People kept saying "it'll just click" and I would get more and more frustrated because it just wouldn't...

And then it just clicked.


It took me much longer to get it then it did others, too many hours on dark souls 3 seared some nasty habits into my brain. So f*ck it, forget it all, throw it all away and burn it down... kinda? but also don't, there's some useful stuff in that dark souls experience. And to be fair the first area of the game bad, both as a tutorial and in general.

This game requires patience, but not 'dark souls patience'. it requires ®. Rather than backing off and closing in, waiting for attack openings, baiting out attacks, etc, i.e: the normal dark souls gaming loop, Sekiro is much more about "Tempo". While there are times when you'll be weaving in and out or even baiting patterns, the gameplay is more so defined by constant, measured pressure. You should be trying to close the distance and consistently apply pressure to the boss: constantly attacking and parrying, anything to raise the posture bar. But not frantically. Rather at a consistent, measured pace, almost like a metronome. All while watching the boss for those certain attacks that break the pattern.

But to be honest this stuff is easy, its the bosses that upend this play pattern that'll really test your patience, but those are the most fun >:)

My first fromsoftware game. I love the combat system of deflecting enemy attacks. I have not seen a game incorporate this rhythm of deflecting attacks and attacking back. I have played a few soulslike and I have never seen enemies do such a thing as they just tank your attack or doesn't react to your attacks at all. The red kanji perilous attacks requires you to know what moveset (Makiri,jump, run away) is needed to counter. I wish that there was a different kanji implication for all the perilous attacks though because i can get confused on which move to use ie. jumping when it is a thrust attack rather than a sweep. I'm in love with the reflection system with how I can continue to beat bosses without the hassle of going through a new play through just to fight the bosses again. Honestly I feel this feature should be used more for souls and soulike games. Overall a 10/10 experience with sekiro.

An Incredible Journey

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is one of the greatest games I've ever played, and I mean that as sincerely as possible. After hearing all the praise it received, I dove in head first, fully aware of the immense butt kicking that I was in for. Well 25 hours and hundreds of deaths later, I don't know how to do this game justice with a simple Steam review. All I can say is that if Sekiro looks even vaguely interesting to you, give it a try. The combat is probably the best I've ever had the joy of experiencing, the world is beautiful, the story is intriguing, and the bosses are probably the best line up in any game ever. Is it hard? Yes. Will you die countless times? Probably. But the feeling of pure joy when you not just overcome, but master a boss fight that once seemed absolutely impossible, can only be described as orgasmic. It's unlike anything else I've experienced in a video game before.


Without a doubt the star of the show is the combat system. At it's core it's extremely simple. You can block, parry, jump, sidestep, attack and counter some attacks. While this may seem simple enough it's pushed to its absolute limits by the bosses. You also get a prosthetic arm that provides you with some special attacks but I eventually found myself not even using it because of how engaging combat is. Bosses will come at you with a flurry of attacks and learning the timings, and proper methods of either parrying, countering, or dodging them to open up an opening to attack is like completing a puzzle. Attacks that seem near impossible to deal with become a walk in the park once you learn them. People say Sekiro is like a rhythm game and they're not wrong. Once you properly understand a bosses moveset you fall into a perfect rhythm of parrying and attacking. You fall into a trance almost and it can only be described as magical. While I can understand some people will be put off by the lack of weapon variety or build variety you have since the only weapon you have is your sword, I think it works to the game's strength since these encounters were able to be built around a singular moveset that the user has, meaning every fight is as finely tuned as possible. You can unlock some skills as well but outside of a couple essential ones that should probably be in the base moveset like the mikiri counter and grapple attack, they don't make a big difference at all and once again I rarely found myself using them. For a game so focused on 1v1 combat, there is a surprising amount of gank fights in this game. While some of these can be countered by stealthily taking out some of the crowd, others can become very frustrating but all in all Sekiro has one of, if not the best combat systems in all of gaming.


Sekiro isn't anything crazy special graphically but the world looks beautiful with great set pieces and some beautiful areas to explore. There's a good variety of locations like a war torn castle, mountainous valleys, and flooded palaces. War torn Japan is truly a stunning place to explore. The sound design however is masterful. The clanging of sounds and satisfying pings of landing a perfect parry followed by a clang of sparks is as addicting as drugs. I was blown away by how incredible combat not only looked but sounded as well. The soundtrack shouldn't be slept on either and I appreciated that there was Japanese as well as English audio, and both of them sound great. The bosses all have such great movement and flow to them, only adding to the rhythm of combat.


In conclusion Sekiro is an incredible achievement in combat and game design. It has some of the most memorable and challenging boss fights in the medium of video games and I can't recommend it enough. In order to beat this game you truly have to master its combat system, and while the learning curve can be steep, the reward for doing so is having the privilege of experiencing and hopefully overcoming some of the most difficult and well designed boss fights ever. It's an experience that's worth dying for, maybe even twice. For more reviews, follow my

This game is simply wonderful. A few years ago, I played Sekiro for the first time without much experience in this kind of game, and it was so difficult that I couldn’t even finish it. But now, coming back to it with more patience and experience… what a game!

The gameplay is incredibly addictive—challenging, fast, and razor-sharp. Every fight feels like a deadly dance where timing and posture mastery are everything. The difficulty is demanding but fair, and every defeat teaches you something. Every victory feels genuinely earned.

The story is more straightforward than other FromSoftware titles, but still rich and immersive. The character design is outstanding, and the world is full of detail, with a setting that perfectly captures the dark beauty of a twisted, feudal Japan.

After finally completing Sekiro, I can confidently say I’m a FromSoftware fan for life. This game is a true work of art—unique, intense, and absolutely recommended.

🌟 10/10 – Shadows Die Twice, but You’ll Keep Coming Back 🌟

FromSoftware has crafted some of the greatest action RPGs of all time, but Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice stands above the rest. While it diverges from the traditional Soulsborne formula, it refines the essence of the genre into its purest form—tight, skill-based combat, an unforgettable world, and a deeply rewarding challenge.

🔥 Why Sekiro is the Best Souls Game:

🗡 The Combat is Perfection – No other Souls game offers a system as refined as Sekiro’s. It’s not just about rolling and waiting—it’s about facing your enemy head-on, deflecting every strike, breaking their posture, and finishing them with a single, brutal execution. Every duel feels like a battle of wits and skill, and once you master it, no other game’s combat will feel as satisfying.

🌏 A World That Breathes Feudal Japan – Ashina is one of the most beautifully crafted settings in gaming. From serene temples to war-torn battlefields, every location is dripping with atmosphere and storytelling. Unlike the cryptic lore of Dark Souls, Sekiro’s narrative is more direct but still filled with mystery and depth.

🐺 A Protagonist With Purpose – Unlike the silent protagonists of other Souls games, Wolf is a fully realized character with motivations, relationships, and a personal journey. His bond with Lord Kuro gives the story emotional weight, making every battle feel like it matters.

🎵 A Soundtrack That Strikes the Soul – The music of Sekiro perfectly captures the tension and beauty of its world. From the ominous drums of the Guardian Ape to the sorrowful melodies of Fountainhead Palace, the soundtrack is unforgettable.

👹 Boss Fights That Define the Genre – Every boss in Sekiro is a test of mastery. Whether it’s the relentless Genichiro, the unpredictable Owl, or the legendary Sword Saint Isshin, each fight is an exhilarating challenge that demands perfection. When you finally land that final deathblow, it’s one of the best feelings in gaming.

💀 Hard? Yes. Impossible? No. – Sekiro is brutal, but fair. There’s no grinding for stats, no summoning phantoms—just you, your sword, and your skill. It forces you to get better, and when you do, the sense of accomplishment is unmatched.

🔄 The More You Play, The Better It Gets – New Game+ is exhilarating, and the different endings, optional bosses, and hidden secrets make replaying Sekiro an absolute joy.

If you love the Souls games for their challenge, combat, and world-building, Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is the peak of what FromSoftware has achieved. It’s a game that demands everything from you—and gives back tenfold.

💀 Git Gud? No. Git Great. 💀

Final Verdict: 🏆 Best Souls Game Ever Made 🏆

Became my favourite Soulslike, no wonder it was GOTY 2019! In addition to the beautiful story, which is explained better than any FromSoftware game and the way they explain the lore/story; Sekiro is a higher risk higher reward Soulslike in which you are basicaly a glass cannon all the time. If you play well and aggressiveyou oneshot enemies but if not you are the one in the same position. Compared to other FromSoftware Soulslikes comes with a more indepth approach about evading,dodging and parrying; instead of iframes dodging needs better spacing, timed blocks parry and a new bar called posture is added: an alternative way to go for kills instead of damaging an enemies health bar. All this new infrastructure comes in the price of build variation. Instead of countless weapons and buffs-spells you are left with your katana alone and you improve your attacks and defenses by earning new mechanics to do with a versatile "tool" you get early game. A must try if you played FromSoftware Sousllikes and enjoyed it but as mentioned be vary that the leveling and attack mechanics are much different so difficulty is highly noticable.

Posted: March 19
This game does a lot of things right and a lot of things wrong.

- The health and damage upgrade system is useless. There is no reason for me to get a health increase or damage increase after defeating a boss, because future enemies are going to also have increased stats. You are just upgrading your character so he can take more hits from tougher enemies, but why not just bypass the entire mechanism by just having flat health and damage throughout the entire game. You're introducing systems into the game that make it more complicated unnecessarily. Also if get an upgrade, why do I have to click on a menu to activate it ? Just give it to me automatically, are you telling me there's a reason to not want a certain upgrade ?

-Knock downs. When you are knocked down you have to press the jump or dash button to roll, so you can get up. This happens quite often. This is retarded and upsets the flow of combat. If my character gets knocked down, I have to press a button to make him get back up or he will just stay there and get beaten to death. Pressing the attack or block button will not make him get up, it has to be jump or dodge. The reason this is bad is because you expect your character to just get up on his own, because there is no advantage to him being on the ground. You are vulnerable, and will die very easily. You have to force yourself to remember to press either "a" or "b", but if that's not what's on your mind, you might be thinking of attacking or blocking and you'll just stay there like dead fish. There is absolutely no advantage to just laying there, so the character should just get up.

-Unfairness. The game is difficult, which is fine, but is also unfair. It's not enough to be good enough to beat your enemies, you have to do it in a specific way. For example, bait all the enemies around a mini boss and then take him on 1 vs 1. Each time you want a chance to take on a boss, you have to be willing to complete a series of tasks in a specific order just to get the opportunity to do so. The problem is you have to do this every time you want to face the boss; this forces the player to jump through hoops just to get the chance to learn the boss' patterns. To fix this most enemies shouldn't respawn.
Another problem is that bosses will kill you in maybe 2 hits (max 3). If I need to kill 10 enemies in a very specific way to get to the boss, and die within 2 hits, I am going to adopt an overly safe and passive play style because risky moves are massively punished. The game is geared towards aggression to an extent, but massively punishes players for wanting to increase speed of combat. I learned very quickly that the game is all about patience and precision, but also wants you to be aggressive when the time is right, but what if I feel like playing aggressively just because ? I can't because I'll be killed in 2 hits and have to backtrack. I'd be a lot better at the game if I had more health, because I could spend more time experimenting and less time being a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ ♥♥♥♥♥, using my sword as if it were a spear. These problems are aggravated by kamikazes, unclear animations(anything that isn't a straight human is just confusing to fight), enemies appearing before you have the tools to dispose of them; and just general ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ that accumulates over the course of a few hours.

-Tool tips. I didn't like the fact that the red symbol that appears above the player that tells you the next attack is unblockable, doesn't distinguish between sweep attacks and trust attacks. They are not the same and require different responses. The fact that they have the exact same symbol, made me waste a few dozen deaths jumping when I should have been countering and countering when I should have been jumping...

+++ I love the way the world is designed, leads you straight into the action, without wasting ANY time, brilliant. I love that the world is linear without appearing so. I love the verticality of the level design, I love that the world appears much larger than it actually is, I love the easy stealth that isn't too punishing, I love the simple combat, difficult but rewarding. I love the themes in the story, I love the extra events that have you avoid hazards to break up the pace, I love the fact that it's not hyper realistic and leans towards dark fantasy like nioh, and I ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ love the grappling hook that makes level traversal quick and easy. If it weren't for the grappling hook this game could literally not exist. It's the only thing that makes it bearable to continue trudging along this slow maze of death because your tiny health pool and risk averse playing style are going to make every step risky, frustrating and at times, rewarding (although not enough for me).

+++General presentation is top tier, the graphics aren't overly demanding, and runs pretty well. Games really don't have a reason to look better than this, I hate it when games demand 100GB of your SSD, this game asks for only 13GB <3 Ran pretty good on a gtx 1070, 60 fps, 1080p mix of high and low settings.

+++ Level design is probably the best of any RPG I have ever played.

7/10 too frustrating for me, juice is NOT worth the squeeze. I'm surprised of all the positive reviews, it seems like everybody is automatically just glazing the game. The only reason I thumbs down is because the overwhelmingly positive reviews do not accurately represent this game to non-masochistic gamers. Just look at the achievements. Almost nobody is actually playing/beating the game. Almost nobody (including me as of writing this review) even bothered to get to the FIRST actual boss, yet by reading the reviews, you'd think everyone beat the game and thought it was a jolly good time, with not much wrong with it...


-Also the ammo for your prosthetic weapons should refill when you die(just decrease the capacity). They are very inconsistent with ammo, you never know when you'll have a chance to use ranged attacks like shuriken, so you'll opt to never use them. You also never know when a successful attempt is going to be, so you'll never know if you should use your consumables or not. Because if you do use them, maybe you'll still die and respawn with 0 consumables....straight bad game design...discouraging the utility of your own arsenal...

Hesitation is Defeat

I have always been skeptical about FromSoft games since my past uncultured friends only knew and talked about how they difficult are. Making statements that led me to believe that difficulty is all they are about.
Then I slowly decided to dip my toes in a few titles. First Elden Ring, followed by Dark Souls Remastered, and now Sekiro. What I've come to realise is that the lore that intertwines between each character and item descriptions, tells a tale that brought me closer to each of these games. Sekiro wasn't an exception.

Having done a 100% playthrough (and two additional new games for Plat) where not a single item was left behind, I've had an experience like no other. The story of Wolf, the powers residing in Dragon's Blood, characters like Owl, Genichiro, the Sculptor, and every single aspect of Sekiro kept me gripped to the game. Every night that I finished playing, I went to sleep eager to play more the following day.
The Shinobi who takes you through this journey is a man of remarkable aura. The opponents that he faces (I'm looking at you, Isshin and Owl) are even more remarkable. The side quests, visual fidelity, combat flow (the best I've ever experienced), and audio scores make this game a must-buy. Especially if it is available on sale.

One of the, if not the most curated game qualitatively speaking made by From
The combat is fluid and responsive and still has the need of committing to you actions, something i'm very sour about is the lack of AoE attack to deal with multiple enemies which is very needed some times, being a VERY fast paced game means that at any moment you can miss click and throw away the fight, sort of. The ressurrection mechanic is there for those moments, having a semi-reset button is a saving grace when the fight is close to being finished.
The story, as far as i could understand due to silly Fromsoft and it being extremelly rooted in japanese mithology an such, is good. The character are likeble enough to not make you want them dead.
The bad parts for me are: some bosses are not very fun to fight, mainly the ghosts, spirits and fight with adds (Sekiro has an incredible problem with add bossfights were the boss isn't even a threat without them), the scaling of enemy damage during the very end of the game (which has a lot of "1vtoomany" fights), the unfortunate lack of replay value past a second playthrough if you don't count secret story paths, the limited amount of items (such as the anti-ghost buff box) and something similar to Bloodborne vials' problem, the spirit emblems: it's obviously not as much of a problem but they are in a finite number and this makes me worry about how many i use and if i'll be able to recover them during exploration.

There is a lot more i forgot to say but it's generally a great experience, and the updates added post launch made it even better: the ability to replay bosses is something that somehow didn't make it to Elden Ring.

Oh, and the DLC: there isn't one... Damn you Activision! Tomoe is now a broken boss in Elden Ring.

Worthy game that embraces very engaging combat (making it difficult for beginners at the same time). The player will have to deflect attacks at the right timing (the window for deflecting is not too small), dodge certain 'dangerous' attacks by jumping or using abilities, and learning the enemy's move-set to achieve victory. When compared to other titles by FromSoftware, such as Dark Souls, whose combat system is slower and simpler, involving dodging, attacking (and abilities, depending on the game), and sometimes parrying, Sekiro takes on a very interactive approach to its combat, involving almost every mechanic in every second of every fight. The combat will come naturally assuming you continue playing, and practice can also be implemented if needed. There are many guides out there for this game, do not be afraid to seek these out and gain assistance.

A miserable experience

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is a third-person action-adventure game made by FromSoftware Inc. and published by Activision. It's considered the hardest game in the franchise, and it's not difficult to see why.

The plot revolves around the main character, Wolf—who is tasked with protecting Kuro, who is the last of Dragon's Heritage line. The game takes place during the Sengoku period; the land is ravaged by war, and the leader of the Ashina Clan, Genichiro, wants Kuro's blood to create an immortal army. We are tasked with a mission to get the young lord back.

The game has some things going for it. The combat is great; it rewards strategy and patience to defeat your enemies. Executing parries is so satisfying. Trying to take down multiple opponents is hard but not impossible. You can either go by stealth or full fighting mode. The former is low risk but slow, and the latter is high risk but faster. Unfortunately, the biggest gripe that I have with this game is the backtracking to the boss fights. The spawn points are far away from the bosses; therefore, you have to go back and try to fight them again. While the walks aren't long, it would add up to hours because you spend most of your time walking or running instead of actually fighting. Not to mention, the enemies respawn every time you die, so you have to engage in combat with them again if you want to go back—which is frustrating. The reason why I dislike the backtracking is that it's actively preventing you from trying practice against the bosses. Since most of them have patterns, the only way you can win against them is by trying again, yet, like I said, the game doesn't allow you to do that because it spawns you far away from them.

Nevertheless, the detail in this game is amazing. The castles, the environment, the culture, etc., make you feel like you're really inside feudal Japan. Admiring the scenery while the snow falls upon you makes you feel like you're part of the world—trying to explore it. The fights between the samurais feel like classic battles between two equally skilled opponents that want to test their physical prowess.

Would I recommend this game? If you want to purchase it after my critique, then you're free to do so. However, if you don't like backtracking, then you should skip this one.

It's different from Souls games. You're forced to parry to be effective in combat. This game succees in making you feel like an actual samurai, as you exchange sword clashes and counter at the perfect moments. This game has two of the best and most memorable boss fights from any FromSoft game.

At first, the game feels slow. You see things in the background that look like generic decorations. At some point the game blows your mind and you realize those decorations are actual places in the world that you can reach and it's actually an open world. At the same time, the fights get cooler and cooler.

But in the late game it starts to lose its magic. You find can instantly kill enemies from behind, but if they notice you then you engage in a long and difficult combat with them, so it encourages you to play a very boring sneak around style. Eventually you have to fight ghosts and you basically can't kill them without farming items first, and it's annoying to keep applying the items.

The journey this game takes you on is like boring to amazing, then boring again. It's definitely worth playing for the amazing part.

Posted: March 14
Another masterpiece by Fromsoft - 10/10. I have previously played Elden Ring only, and was worried this game would be too difficult. But it wasn't - instead it was an unforgettable rewarding challenge, followed with extraordinary level, sound and visual artistic design.

Fromsoft somehow managed to find a very simple formula at it's core (3 buttons: deflect, attack, dodge), yet made it so diverse and interesting to learn with all additional combat arts, prostetic arm upgrades, stealth and enemies variety. The cinematic design for each deathblow, each enemy dying is so satisfying, that I was never bored of completing the same level again and again.

Bosses of course are also another great thing in this game. Each of them giving you a completely different skill check, a different mechanic to learn, sometimes combining multiple of them. Some bosses are like "gateways" or "examiners", testing your absorbed knowledge about discovered combat mechanics so far and preventing you from progressing further if you haven't mastered them yet.

Music, sound design and as mentioned before, visual design are also top-level and immersive.

The only problems with the game I found are:
- Although it's common for Fromsoft games to have a confusing and hidden plot, it was okay for Sekiro, but there are too many opportunities to miss a lot of content in the game, and I had to consult guides in order to enjoy all of it.
- After completing the game, I'd expected a change in the core characters dialogues to somehow react to the ending I have chosen, but this didn't happen. Probably I am spoiled by Baldur's Gate 3 interactiveness.
- Some minor bugs and glitches, usually related to my character getting stuck between enemies and some obstacle, or camera being totally unplayable.
- In my opinion, a bit abuse of using the same bosses multiple times, without specific plot reasoning, which looks like a cut corner from game design perspective.

Totally recommended.

A Masterpiece of Precision and Artistry - Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice

From the moment you step into the world of Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice, it's clear that you're about to experience something truly extraordinary. Developed by FromSoftware, this game not only redefines the action-adventure genre but elevates it to new heights, combining brutal combat, captivating storytelling, and an atmosphere that is nothing short of breathtaking.

The first thing that stands out is the combat. Unlike other games, Sekiro rewards precision, and timing. The combat system is fluid, fast, and deliberate, offering a level of depth that is unmatched. The posture-based combat and the emphasis on parrying create a high-stakes experience where every move counts. It’s a true test of skill, but when you master the mechanics, the satisfaction is immense. The boss fights in particular are nothing short of legendary – each one feels like a battle of wits as you learn their moves, adapt, and finally overcome their incredible difficulty.

The world of Sekiro is a masterpiece in itself. The landscapes, whether it’s the serene beauty of the Ashina Castle or the haunting remnants of the battlefield, are meticulously crafted to create an atmosphere that is as awe-inspiring as it is terrifying. Every area feels alive with history and purpose, drawing you deeper into the world with each new discovery. The design of the world encourages exploration, rewarding those who dare to venture off the beaten path with hidden secrets, upgrades, and lore that enrich the story.

Speaking of the story, Sekiro delivers an emotionally engaging narrative that blends Japanese mythology, samurai honor, and personal sacrifice. The story of Sekiro, the “one-armed wolf,” and his mission to protect his young lord is poignant and compelling. It’s a tale of redemption, loyalty, and the cost of duty, told through both cutscenes and environmental storytelling. The writing is nuanced and impactful, with twists that keep you invested from start to finish.

The music and sound design are the finishing touches that complete the experience. The score complements the game perfectly, with stirring compositions that enhance the intensity of battles and the beauty of exploration. Every sound, from the clash of swords to the rustle of the wind, adds depth to the atmosphere, drawing you into the world even further.

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is not just a game—it’s a work of art. The level of craftsmanship, attention to detail, and sheer challenge it offers make it one of the most memorable gaming experiences of this generation. It’s a true test of patience, skill, and perseverance, but for those who rise to the challenge, the rewards are more than worth it. This is a game that will stay with you long after the credits roll.

Posted: March 14
So, as you might see, I have a few hours on the game lol. To put it in short, I LOVE this game. If you do not like a challenge, if you do not like dying 50 times just to finally learn how to perfectly deflect that ONE COMBO on the thirst phase of the final boss, if you do not like having limited options and not being able to cheese enemies with meta strats, then this is not a game for you. Whether or not the game is good, you will not enjoy it, it is as simple as that. HOWEVER, this game was everything I signed up for. Souls games are notorious for killing you a bunch of times, and this game is no different. IT IS DIFFERENT in the fact that you cannot go level up and come back stronger. If you want to beat this guy whooping your tail, you genuinely have to get good. My first play through was a struggle because I was solely trying to scrape by each enemy. This game is about learning your opponent, each combo, their habits, what works and what doesn't, etc. I think what makes this game shine is that you are not required to do anything. That combo keeps killing you? You can just avoid it, BUT THEN YOURE A WUSS. It is the idea that "I could avoid that attack and get a free hit, OR I could parry it perfectly just to flex" that has gotten me as many hours as I do. It is addicting to learn every enemy perfectly. You feel like the protagonist of an anime. I mean seriously, when you just stand there and deflect everything so easily you just feel so cool. But that cool-ness comes at a cost, and it is HOURS AND I MEAN MANY HOURS to learn every enemy perfectly. Other than that, I think what makes this game shine is that it makes you learn nothing. You can run past almost every goon in the game, like I said you can avoid attacks that you struggle with, and you do not need to level up at all to reach any area of the game. The world of Sekiro is genuinely in your hands. If this sounds compelling to you, then this game is worth every dime. I also LOVE the story. It is very sad once you fully understand it, and it makes me only wish there was more to the game.

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is like a funny clown hitting you on the head with a hammer while simultaneously telling a joke about your mother. You will laugh, you will curse, you will want to smash your controller, and you will simultaneously wonder if you are even still healthy.

The graphics are beautiful, the music is epic, and the story is captivating. But then comes the difficulty - an evil spirit that haunts you and constantly drives you into the abyss of despair. You will die. A lot. And each time you will wonder if the developers have found a secret recipe for nerve poison that simultaneously activates your laugh muscles. But hey, every time you defeat a boss, you will feel like a real ninja. A ninja who just ran a marathon, won a fight, had a sip of coffee, and is simultaneously telling a joke about an elephant. So if you are a player who likes to laugh, curse, cry, and at the same time wants to throw their controller against the wall, then Sekiro is just right for you. But if you prefer to play a game that doesn't constantly push you to the brink of a nervous breakdown, then you might want to choose another title.

Conclusion: Average Souls experience. Top 👍

I enjoy running my face into a wall (boss) until I get good enough to beat them. I like skill checks, but each boss in Sekiro is a patience check.

To retry a boss in Sekiro, run past 20+ enemies- no reason to fight them, they are just time wasting fodder. Crouch in a bush 'til they all forget about you. Then "stealth" kill the 3-10 grunts around a boss or else you will get swarmed as you fight the boss. Mind you, stealth in this game is just gimmicking the braindead AI. Go hide behind a wall for a minute and they forget about you, "stealth" kill 1 maybe 2, hide in a bush right in front of them til they forget about you, "stealth" kill another. Rinse and repeat. THEN you can finally 1v1 the boss, like the game is designed for.

1v1 combat is solid, satisfying, and well designed. It's a shame you have to waste so much time running, hiding, climbing, waiting, and "stealth" killing hordes of grunts to get there, 80% of the gameplay is just that. A waste of time. Delete everything except bosses and make it a boss rush.

I believe there's fun to be had in the game cause, when you play correctly, it's actually very fun.
But the amount of ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ I saw in only 19 hours is incredible.
Enemies can see you behind some objects even if you've been hidden all the time.
Certain enemy attacks hit you even if dodged properly and are far away from the weapon itself.
Some bosses get hyperarmor and items that are supposed to be a direct counter of the boss do not work during that window.
Idols are, usually (not always), too far away from the bossfight, making you run for at least a couple of minutes to get there again.
Game is extremely punitive in AoE scenarios (that sometimes you can't avoid), with enemies potentially stun locking you.
The camera and lock system are straight up awful: some bosses are either gonna jump around or over you, either making your camera "slide" against the wall, trees or wherever you are or making you lose sight of the enemy.
For a game that demands perfection from you, I at least expect the base mechanics to work properly.
I actually have no idea how the game is so popular and how it won GOTY.

It's hard to describe the feeling once you've mastered an enemies move timings and the speed at which you can defeat these enemies once you do. Instead of your basic dodge and riposte, here you will vault, counter, deflect and use opportunitys. Once you get the hang of it, you will understand the game.

Story and lore is easily also in the top 3 of FromSoft games. Music and mood is perfect too. Many camera angles give Ghost of Tsushima/Kurosawa vibes. Visuals are gorgeous and there's 4K textures available on Nexus if you want more eye candy and some nice visual mods for Sekiro.

You will die btw, not twice but many times and it will be fun. Trust me, it's a good game once you get the hang of it like all the other FromSoft games.

I have been playing sekiro for about 76 hrs and i want to rant about it a bit. So if you are a hardcore sekiro fan, you shouldnt read this. It is very subjective but here we go.

I have played Elden Ring and DS3 before and i think Sekiro is the worst of these three. Its probably cause i just like the medieval looking castles and fantasy stuff more than the samurai and temples. For me sekiro was really difficult cause it tries smth very differnent with the combat system. Dont get me wrong, it was amazing but hard to adjust. The difficulty wasnt the problem after a while cause you are used to it, right? It was not easy for sure but the hardest thing for me were the mini bosses. (chained ogre with hitboxes like your mama and the snake eye guys)

pros:
-the game looks very good with all the regions. Really enjoyed how the themes changed from snowy mountains to the rainy hirata estate. also the blood is sick. Fire too, especially in the end before you fight the demon.
-movement is cool with the grapple. but also running and jumping and the samurai arts pretty impressive stuff
-the combat system is rlly something new and i enjoy it now

and now the cons:
-bosses dont have this epic feeling, idk how to describe it, the illusive monk or the guardian ape, these are two main bosses but they have no cutscene and not much else (why spawns an extra boss if you clap the ape before the monk and it can fck you cause it is one of the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ duo fights i have ever seen.)
-the gameplay feels the same in every region, you have not much changed apart of the looks but always grapple on platforms, walk a bit on the edge and clap some noobos. If you wanted you could rush the game in such a short time.
-i hated it that you couldnt dive from the beginning, its just dumb backtracking to do.
-Almost forgot, like you have to retake the ashina castle. No its just not once its twice. And sry but i wont search the same area three times... was just annoying gameplay for me and didnt have the omg effect
-the thing about the combat system is that if you fight bosses and you already know how to fight them you can punish them with blocking and damaging and playing aggressive. BUT if you dont know the boss its twice as hard: you cant block right, you will take damage and you have to run and thats the problem. The bosses have rlly mean attacks to catch up with you. Doesnt matter if its genichiro or the ape and you dont have the mean attacks if you are always close. So this is very subjective but i accidentaly got the shura ending on my first run. And if you know what i mean its ♥♥♥♥ for the first playthrough. "Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice" was an amazing journey across the land of Japan, presenting the Sengoku period alongside the original characters of the game. FromSoftware pushed their limits to another level, creating something different than any other Soulsborne. Highly satisfying quick combat makes the exploration more enjoyable, giving you broader options than just a dodge roll. I've experienced this game twice on my PS4 and felt kinda refreshing revisiting it with some extra mods and improved visuals. Such a shame they didn't make any follow-up DLC, but still... a great game!

Posted: March 11
What's good:

Second to second combat. Each fight and action feels meaningful while you're in combat. It's probably what makes it distinct to any other souls-borne game. You're not mindlessly running around one-shotting everything you come across like you would in Elden Ring and Dark Souls (unless you're a stealthy boy).

Art style. The art style is by far the best in any of FromSoft's titles. I did not appreciate the desaturated environment in Bloodborne, and this game is a nice contrast to it. The closest FromSoft has come to the art in Sekiro is Elden Ring.

Most boss fights. Most of the boss fights work quite good. I like that the mini bosses are designed to teach a certain mechanic so you're prepared to use the mechanics in real boss fights. More on the bad boss fights later.

relatively linear progression + compact map design. I missed the linear progression in Souls-Borne games after playing Elden Ring. Sekrio has a clear sense of progression. I also appreciate the compact map design. I like to explore the entire map before moving on, and when I played Elden Ring this would take me 100 hours each playthrough, mainly because the map was so damn big I spent most of the time going from one location to another. I spent at most 2 hours in each area in Sekiro thanks to the compact map. It appears to respect my time more than Elden Ring.

What's bad:

The real boss fight; the camera. Dark Souls 1 had a better camera layout than Sekiro, and that came out years before. There's no settings to free the camera movement so it doesn't move while the player moves. Fights done in buildings are especially miserable because of how the camera interacts with walls. You cannot appreciate boss fights larger than a human because of how close the game is to the player (looking at Demon of Hatred in particular). The camera single-handedly holds Sekiro back from being a masterpiece.

Blazing Bull and Demon of Hatred boss fights. The Blazing Bull fight was simply annoying. I had to fight it without knowing about the firecrackers Shinobi tool, and I grew frustrated enough that I just looked up a guide on how to beat it, and I was really annoyed when I learned you're meant to parry its charging attack. A skinny Shinobi with a sword is supposed to parry a megaton bull charging towards you. How does that make any sense? For the Demon of Hatred fight, I found it boring. All you do is run up to its left leg and then behind it and start swinging. In its second phase it gains an attack that I found impossible to dodge, unless I'm supposed to run further away from the boss, which in that case makes the fight even more boring. The boss spams the classic butt slam attack that the asylum demons in Dark Souls 1 do all the time. The only fun I had with the fight was cheesing it to commit suicide and it thanking me for aiding in the process.

Bait and switch final boss fight. Sekiro set up a clear three-parter to the Genichiro Ashina boss. You fight him in the beginning of the game and lose, you fight him about 33% through the game and win, but for the final boss fight you kill him with ease and then fight some old dude. I wouldn't mind it as much if the old dude had similar attack patterns to Genichiro, but it's completely different except for a single jump attack. Genichiro Ashina was easily the best boss in the game and I would've liked to fight him one last time where he has 3-4 phases and each phase he gains new attack patterns.

The Shinobi: Owl boss fight could have been much more. This boss fight was way too easy for where you're at in the game. I thought it interesting how the boss used smoke bombs and firecrackers on you, but everything the boss did is easily avoidable and he hardly makes an effort to profit on your blunders. The boss was too passive.

Semi-clunky movement. This is a nitpick but was annoying to deal with in my playthrough. If you're close to a ledge, the game will sometimes, but not all the time, stop you from falling off. So if you're running away from a mob of enemies and you want to fall down, you can't, and you're stuck with the enemies unable to move. Another movement problem is the input lag when performing a running jump. There's a good half second delay between you pressing the button and the player actually jumping. If you jump without running it's instant, so I see no reason for it to be different while running. It only serves to annoy the player.


To conclude:

Despite its problems, the game is a 9/10. The majority of the time you'd spend playing the game will be enjoyable, and you do grow used to the camera and movement after a while. The game respects your time and intelligence with its map design and its combat mechanics. Other than a select few bosses, the boss fights were engaging and meaningful, and by far some of the best that FromSoftware has ever made. If you like saturated environments, you'll like exploring the many beautiful areas this game has to offer.

The combat and bosses are fantastic, probably the best out of any ARPG I've played (including Elden Ring) but this game is still so much more than that. My favourite part would be the world itself, it's beautiful and immersive, and the journey through it - sneaking around, assassinating patrols, climbing and exploring were some of the most fun I've had in gaming.

The learning curve is very steep (lack of support for some controller configurations doesn't help), but thankfully timewasting is minimal due to well positioned enemies and checkpoints, and your character being able to move very quickly and get back into action almost instantly. Moreover, once you beat each major boss, you also unlock the ability to replay them at will. Progression is also great, with very interesting skill trees and powerful items that replace permanent stat boosts once you acquire all of them. All these make this game very replayable, and guarantee many hours of entertainment should you click with the combat. Do not be disheartened if this doesn't happen instantly though, as for many it takes until the second or even third major boss, but the wait is worth it.

The game also has an abundance of varied high quality enemies and bosses, but admittedly some of its locations like the Poison Pool, Hidden Forest, Abandoned Dungeon and Fountainhead peak (Sanctuary) feel much smaller than they should. Also, not every boss is perfect - one of the earlier ones has an annoying grab attack that isn't well matched to your hit box, another is just a poorly designed duo fight that requires an abundance of patience, and lastly a certain endgame boss can have camera issues due to his size and erratic moveset. Thankfully these issues do not extend outside of these mentioned cases, and in general the game is polished to an impressive degree. What's even crazier is that there are several gimmick encounters, which I usually find boring in games, but loved all here, even when replaying them!

The only major complaint I have is how they handle NPC dialogue, quest tracking and progression. The story itself and characters are great, and much less vague than some other games of this type. But for certain quests you will literally need to spam talk, leave and re-enter the entire area and talk again multiple times just to unlock further dialogue, without any dialogue choice or indication. Some are even worse and include very specific places you need to stand to be able to overhear them talking, which can be tedious to discern without online guides. On top of that, there isn't even a quest log to track progression.

All in all, despite some of the above annoyances this is one of the most impressive games I've ever played and can strongly recommend it to any fans of this genre.

Gave the game 100 hours. Completed NG+ without Charm and with Demon Bell. The combat is absolutely atrocious. I don't see how anyone can praise it. You're not asked to be creative; you're asked to do exactly what the game expects from you. Which in turn makes the game extremely hard when you try to be creative, and extremely easy (yes, actually easy) when you play as the game expects.

Everything else about the game is near perfect. Great japanese voice acting, great atmosphere, great world building, great graphical design, and even a nice variety of enemies (tho the combat is completely puke worthy). If I could, I would just let an AI play the combat, so I could enjoy the rest of the game.

For a game that has alot of its focus combat mechanics, I can not give this game a thumbs up. The combat is made for people that enjoy playing on other people's exact rules. If you have a robot, AI or a half a brain friend that can play the combat for you, then I absolutely can recommend the game. Otherwise play the DS series or ER.

Nevertheless, if you can forgive and get past these parts of the game, you will be rewarded with moments so great they will have you forget any frustrations you might have had leading up to them. Sekiro Shadows Die Twice may not be From's best world. Or it's best bosses. But it is my favourite of the bunch. The combat system is the best in any of their games. Unless you wanna cheese everything like a ♥♥♥♥♥ with firecrackers.

I'm of two minds of the prosthetic tools. I believe that every Boss which isn't a human with a sword are the only time you actually need to use them. But I don't like using them. I feel this game works best when you perfectly time a block and go in for the attack after parrying the enemy. When the rhythm of the game clicks, it's so satisfying. Once you understand this philosophy, you can beat the entire game. I'm not gonna say it will be easy. Because it won't be. But it will be thoroughly enjoyable.

The final Boss is my favourite Boss in the entire From catalogue. I finished it and I was exhausted yeah. But I loved every single minute of it. Because it's incredibly fair and the combat is super satisfying to pull off. I'm gonna go back and do all the Bosses I couldn't do through decisions I made in the game. But yeah, this is an absolute masterpiece. From's best looking game by far. This game is gorgeous to look at. My draw dropped entering Fountainhead Palace. My only negatives on this game is that I think the score is fine. But nothing special and of course there's only one style of play. Meaning multiple playthroughs will basically be the same. Posted: March 9
Sekiro is a perfect action game and my favorite game of all time. I have something like 400 hours on PlayStation and was so happy to buy it again for Steam Deck.

It's the best parry game, with adaptive animations and a genius posture system. It's the best ninja game, with satisfying stealth and stylish attacks. It has the best boss fights, incredibly variable and thrilling to master. It has a compelling story, subtle and tragic and beautiful in all the right places. It pays homage to great Japanese films, from Kurosawa to Princess Mononoke. It digs into themes about Shinto, Buddhism, greed, hatred, and parenthood, often through gameplay itself. It looks gorgeous thanks to a strong art style, striking composition, an evolving color palette, and meaningful particle effects.

I can't say enough about the combat... It is a masterpiece of mastery, an immensely satisfying network of tight systems that shine brighter and brighter the more time and attention you give them. I'm so glad this game has inspired many others since 2019, but honestly nothing comes close to how tight, purposeful, and razor sharp Sekiro feels to play.

Nice to know it takes me 11 hours to do everything I like in one playthrough, which is absolutely everything except the Headless. Hate those guys.

Owl Father is my favorite boss fight ever, and Sword Saint Isshin is a perfect final boss. Sekiro is a pinnacle that I hope continues to find its loving audience for decades to come.

Posted: March 7
Sekiro differs massively from other Fromsoft games. This difference comes from several factors.

The first and maybe the most outstanding one is combat. In sekiro your combat mechanic depends mostly on deflecting/guarding enemy attacks or attacking enemy attacks to make its attack be deflected/guarded and eventually breaking its posture so that you can get a deathblow. However, there are some powerful enemy attacks like thrust, grab, sweep and lightning which cant be guarded and some of them even cant be deflected so you should dodge/jump/mikiri counter for some not all. Thus, the game at first glance feels a bit hard maybe frustrating as well but the main thing is knowing the every enemy’s attack set and imply your strategy according to it. To elaborate it, you can’t try mikiri counter to a grab attack but a thrust attack. Once you know attack set of the enemy and what you should do to counter, the game becomes so much easier.

Let’s get into building the character. The second and for me the most unenjoyable difference is building your character. The game has lack of variety compared to other Fromsoft games. You wield a katana and have a prosthetic tool. There is no weapon choice other than katana and no armor sets in the game. This result in lack of variety. The only variety comes from prosthetic tools and skills you find/learn through the journey.

There are 10 prosthetics tools and you can upgrade them to change the usage they have. You can use them for a defensive or offensive purpose according to the boss or enemy you encounter. There is a currency for the prosthetic tools and every prosthetic tools have a cost per usage. This currency is called as “spirit emblem” which can also be required to use advanced combat arts like mortal draw, ninjutsu techniques or spiritfall buffs. You can buy spirit emblems in any sculptor’s idol (bonfire or site of grace) inexchange for sen which is the gold currency of the game, but there is a limit you can carry with you (15 at the beginning, maxed at 20). In addition, you can use ceremonial tanto to refill your spirit emblembs to a max of +5.

There are 5 skill trees focusing on stuffs like combat, stealth, prosthetic tools etc., and 3 ninjutsu technique skills which can only be used after a backstab. You can use them depending on the playstyle you wanna go for but there is no respec option which let you reallocate those spent skills. This is a con for me because it limits you in a way, ofcourse you can collect all the skills (i learnt all of them in ng+2 for the achievement) but it takes lots of levels which ending you up in grinding. If you wanna have all the skills you better go for ng cycles which is totally okay for me because i like going ng cycles in games but maybe not for people who don’t wanna go for it. So, that means you already got limited variety in the game and making it harder to vary by not giving players a choice to respec.

It is simple, your vitality and posture increases as you collect prayer beads and using per 4 of them. There are total of 40, so you can increase your vitality and posture 10 times.

This one is simple too. When you beat a boss, you get its memory and use it in any sculptor’s idol for permanently gaining attack power. There is also another way to increase your attack power but it comes with a cost and i don’t recommend it if you aim to get all skills in your run. By dragon mask item, you can transmute 5 level of your character to 1 attack power. However, as you go higher in level your exp required to level up go higher as well and you can suffer leveling up to get all the skills in each of 5 skill trees.

I read in somewhere something like “every enemy is a boss in this game”. The monks carrying twinblade suit this phrase nicely. In addition, some of the miniboss are quite a challenge too. Isshin, the Sword Saint, Owl Father in Hirata Estate are my favourites and worth fighting again and again. Genichiro is the first real challenge and i guess many players learnt the combat mechanic with him. Demon of hatred is the only boss i can believe if you say that is coming from Elden Ring or Dark Souls universe. In a nutshell, we can say bosses are well designed and qualified and i had quite a fun and satisfaction during fighting.

In conclusion, it is a must play if you like Fromsoft games by acknowledging it is a different one. Maybe you can like it more or less, it all depends on preferences of the person and there is no need for arguing. Only con for me is lack of variety in building the character. This and lack of multiplayer experience may lead a decrease in total play hours compared to the other Fromsoft games. The combat is just fine, satisfying and feels so smooth after you master it.

My recommendation rate is 9/10.

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is the only game I have ever played that is genuinely a completely perfect video game; I say this without the slightest hint of exaggeration.

Sekiro is as close to being completely flawless in every aspect that a video game can possibly be, to the point where, to credit it as anything less than being genuinely perfect would do the game an actual injustice.

FromSoftware developed and refined this masterpiece to the point of true perfection. Exactly everything they set out to do with this game they did to the point where it literally could not be more perfect than this.

Unlike their other action RPGs, where you have an uncountable number of drastically different options to approach each combat encounter, Sekiro narrowed it down to where you basically only have one option and some flexibility with your shinobi tools. And in return, the moment to moment gameplay have never felt more satisfying.

Everything, and I genuinely mean EVERYTHING in this game, was beyond expertly tailor-made to play and fit perfectly with every single ability you are given. I have never experienced gameplay in any other video game more tight and thought through than this.

Sekiro may not be as grandiose in sheer scale as FromSoftware's other Souls games, but in return for that, if you're up to the grueling challenge to master it, it is the most perfectly refined and optimized combat experience you can have in gaming. With no shortage of an absolutely incredible story with set pieces, characters, and bosses that you will never forget.

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is truly refined to absolute perfection.

Comparing Fromsoft games - Personally I found this game to be somewhat significantly easier than Elden Ring, while still maintaining a very fun difficulty. Unlike Elden Ring the story is actually comprehensible and very good, I was invested.

Sekiro is as close as you can get to playing a flawless game, I'd recommend it to any gamer looking for a bit of a challenge.

So much potential. But completely ruined by a camera system, and enemy behaviour, which completely negates the core mechanics of the game itself. By that I mean a target locking system necessary in order to play most of the combat as intended, and execute key moves, actively works against the player from virtually the first encounter onwards. When added to a camera that refuses to allow the player even the most basic level of control, and forces you into enemy combat situations where you literally can't see what's attacking you, and you have an awful game ahead of you. Given the crux of player controlled combat is supposed to be your ability to time blocks, and dodge attacks, having the game itself work against that doesn't add to the difficulty - just to frustration with shoddy development. When you also have enemies whose attacks you've dodged, and who have begun an attack along one angle, suddenly pivot mid attack and execute down the angle you've just dodged towards (purely because of the auto camera adjust), the net effect is a complete waste of time and the very opposite of a skill based reward. So much potential ruined by shoddy development.

**Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice - A Masterpiece of Precision and Artistry**

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is an absolute triumph in gaming, blending challenging combat, breathtaking visuals, and a deeply immersive world into one unforgettable experience. FromSoftware has once again proven their mastery of game design, delivering a title that is as rewarding as it is demanding.

The combat system is nothing short of perfection. Every clash of swords feels deliberate and impactful, requiring precision, timing, and strategy. The posture mechanic adds a layer of depth that keeps every encounter fresh and exhilarating. Defeating a tough boss after countless attempts is an unparalleled feeling of accomplishment, and Sekiro delivers this in spades.

The world of Ashina is a visual and auditory masterpiece. From lush forests to crumbling castles, every environment is meticulously crafted, oozing with atmosphere and detail. The sound design, from the clashing of blades to the haunting soundtrack, further immerses you in this feudal Japan-inspired setting.

The story, while more straightforward than other FromSoftware titles, is compelling and filled with intriguing characters. Wolf's journey of vengeance and redemption is one that will stay with you long after the credits roll.

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is not just a game; it's an experience. It challenges you, rewards you, and leaves you in awe of its artistry. If you're looking for a game that respects your intelligence and dedication, Sekiro is an absolute must-play. A true 10/10 masterpiece.
