A great game with excellent combat system!

I havnt played any FromSoftware games before and initially thought Sekiro would be another simple fighting game with minimal story and repetitive fights that quickly gets boring. I am happy I was wrong!
The games combat mechanics are brilliant. Instead of overwhelming players with a complex control scheme, Sekiro keeps the number of buttons and actions relatively low. However, mastering the combat requires precise timing and keenly observing the enemy to anticipate and execute a counter, making every fight a challenging that feels rewarding. I would say the fighting mechanic is easy to grasp but a challenge to master.

Beyond its gameplay, Sekiro is a stunning experience in both visuals and sound. The world is beautifully crafted and the soundtrack perfectly complements the game. Its a well made game and I hope we see more titles like this in the future.

Posted: February 28
spent most of this (probably the hardest game ive ever personally beaten fwiw) exhilarated but slightly resigned strangely enough....as ive said in several places before i do not have much interest in souls games as pure action exercises, i prefer when their priorities are stacked in other directions (for those unfamiliar with my taste, my favorites are still demons souls and ds1 if that gives u any indication). but sekiro just wore me down in the best possible ways....for one, even if i remained unmoved, its hard to turn down what is very arguably the best combat in any 3d video game. this game ofc represents a massive increase in mobility for a fromsoft souls game, but it plays into the one on one encounters surprisingly little, to the point where my biggest roadblock (the guardian ape) is one of the only bosses to fully ask u to make use of it. everything else is genuinely as simple as an extremely twitchy game of rock paper scissors, just about every attack having an obvious Correct answer. it often aims to eliminate the inherent ambiguity of dark souls style combat and tbh 3d games in general...it asks for appropriate execution of call and response rather than something as flawed and compromised as Movement In A 3D Space...while still greatly indulging in said movement for traversal, especially the stealth that often precedes encounters where u thin the herds to make the encounter play nicer with the extreme lack of options for crowd control. somehow i think sekiro more often than not has its cake and eats it too....as involving a digital space to traverse as any fromsoft game, yet collapsing into total precision whenever it is viable to stand your ground and just answer every input. there are certain combos to learn, and u will learn them just thru repetition even if ur not trying, but a shocking amount of sekiro rewards pure reactivity...mindfulness and trusting your immediate perception of the animations in front of you. the more decision based wider encounters where u navigate an arena with stealth and agility and find your own way to unravel them, climaxing in these long bosses where all the choices are effectively made for u and giving u access to a flowing nearly thoughtless state, cover just about every bit of ground i could personally want from 3d combat and a couple things i never thought were even possible to this extent

but more than its action mastery in a vacuum, sekiro is simply, like all the other fromsoft souls games, a great and thoughtful and moving piece of art. its a great one on its own, but it might be even better in relation to their other games. for one, while keeping their impressively unified thematic voice and fixations, it also contextualizes them within actual historical and spiritual ideas that just make everything around it feel richer and more storied, more tied into truths (or at least ideas of the truth) that have been around for centuries. the inherently dehumanizing pursuit of material power that destroys first everyone around u and then U is a constant in these games, and it feels especially cosmic here as its cursed immortality is framed thru a buddhist framework...the endless accumulation of scars of life in the physical world, never to be forgotten or cleansed or reset, a temporary rush bought at an infinite cost to the world and urself. this recklessness is physically and philosophically contrasted with austerity, clearness, mindfulness, and discipline which are ofc all folded into the precision and inflexibility of the combat. playing as a specifically realized character, u are to inhabit him wholly or die, an extremely interesting design space thats totally new to fromsoft...the narrative focus in general is as worthy an experiment as anything else here. more than anything sekiro is the product of a team that despite being some of the premiere blockbuster creators in this medium, have absolutely no complacency...base elements of these games are still in the air to be thought through and reconfigured into a new mode of expression and a wholly new play experience.

i suppose i could quibble a little. obviously the main objection many will have is the complete lack of builds and flexibility. i tbh have something like an opposite hangup, from a perspective i must admit is so niche as to be just about useless. because i am terminally scared of using finite resources in games, i did not use the prosthetic tools literally At All and only used consumables on a few occasions when a bit of extra healing or damage was all that stood between me and victory. as such, for my experience, these were actually the closest thing the game has to Clutter...a vague gesture towards customization that seems too stilted to feel as vital as any other part of the game. still, theres a difference between Theoretical flaws and flaws that actually made a dent in my experience. i tbh dont think ive rly done this game justice, i probably need to live with it longer and absorb more of its artistic accomplishments under my skin to do a writeup thats a little less book report-y than this one (not that theres anything super wrong with that but its not generally my style). but this is a rly special game, one that illustrates the rabid creativity and flexibility of miyazaki and his team and shows just how wide the expressive expanse is for the soulslike genre...other people have now had their chance to come in and do their own takes on it, and i love those ive played, but tbh no one outdoes the master in terms of taking this style into new and interesting places. and considering the setup of AAA games is to do exactly the opposite, it feels like an absolute miracle this run of games exists at all. what a time to be alive.

If, like me, you love soul games and wanted to see more genius from the same developer BEWARE... this game is not for you.

-The stealth doesn't work well (Seriously, game made me miss the broken mess Assassin's Creed is now)
-Bosses aren't well designed
-Controls are bad
-Camera is bad
-Invisible walls everywhere

Now I know I'm gonna hear "Git Gud" but the thing is I wasn't even stuck when I decided to quick. I just realized over the course of this game not once was I having fun and have been thinking "wish I was playing Elden RIng" the entire time

Posted: February 24
Despite my complaints, the one pro outweighs all of them, The game is great with the boss fights but, there is a lot of little things that drive me up the wall while playing it.

Pros:
- The game is at its best when you encounter a boss and are able to go back almost immediately after death.

Cons:
- Terror is a dumb game mechanic, Burn and Poison are fine but a status effect that just kills you is dumb. I am fine with some enemies that use it when it is telegraphed like the Monk, saying "heads up there is a terror attack get out of the way". However, there is no reasonable way to kill the Shichimen Warriors without Divine Confetti or the Purple Umbrella. Having to rely on a finite consumable for a game designed to be difficult, where you will need to do the fight more than once, is dumb. I do not want to stop fighting the boss to farm gold to purchase more divine confetti. Even with the umbrella, the fight is extremely boring. With other bosses it's a matter of staying out of their way for some moves but for the warrior, it is almost all the time. Hitting them once or twice and waiting for an extended period of time is boring.

- Dragonrot, the loss of experience, loss of gold after death is a dumb mechanic. Not that it is punishing or harmful, it is just pointless. If I really needed gold or experience, i could just farm it. There is no real reason to cure dragonrot unless I wanted to do a side quest for fun, but it does not effect the game that much at all.

- The Camera is awful. There are enough times to be annoying where I'm near a wall or a big enemy and the camera just decides to freak out and stop locking onto the target.

- Some mini-bosses have enemies around them that require you to kill them every time you respawn. I would have preferred it if they died only once because it is not difficult to kill them, it just takes up time when I just want to fight the main boss.

- At times the game is not good with telling you where to go. I must have the worst sense of direction because there were many times I had to look up a guide just to find out where to go next.

- The "true" ending requirements are so incredibly convoluted, there is no reasonable way to find out how to get it without a guide.

For context, I've 100%-ed the entire Dark Souls Trilogy & Elden Ring. I may change this review as I get further into the game, but I can't sit down for a full hour on Sekiro because it's not enjoyable for me, and I may drop it entirely.

Other FromSoftware games give you more choice in your fighting styles, but fighting bosses in this game feels too linear, as if the game plays you, instead of the other way around. Early bosses seem to demand spamming the same ability until you win, and unless you're sneaking, fighting standard enemies feels wrong for so many reasons. It simply does not make sense to wield a sword and deal 5-10% per hit, while watching an enemy's kick or shield bash deal more than 50% of your health. A wooden shield or a bamboo hat blocking a Japanese Katana is disrespectful to entire generations of Japan's master swordsmiths. (Watch a Tatara documentary & tell me the bamboo blocked my attack again. I'll wait.) Respawning right where you died, in a MELEE GAME, makes zero sense, especially deriving from Souls games, where your souls are moved back from where you died. Adding a drawback to the game for dying in the same place is another factor that will make potentially interested players quit; too many negative or unrealistic elements prevent me from enjoying the movement improvements implemented in a Souls game.

+20hrs: Nope, not changing this review. Mid-game bosses also demand spamming the same ability again & again, you need an axe to cut through rotting wood & bamboo because your sword is too dull, homeward idol takes so long that you're guaranteed to die while using it in a battle, gathering items to increase your HP bar makes this game feel more like a chintzy scavenger hunt than an RPG, the fact that you can't change your appearance [even clothing] makes this a really drab experience, enemies just stop attacking you or magically miss during your executions, and to be honest, I could go on & on about this. The failed elements of realism significantly cut Miyazaki's claim that this is a "more fluid" game than other FromSoftware titles.

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice – A 60-Hour Pain Simulator

So, I decided to play Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice, a game that’s basically Dark Souls on crack but with more parkour, fewer RPG elements, and a combat system so tight it could cut diamonds. And after 60 hours of slamming my controller, questioning my life choices, and considering meditation as a coping mechanism, here’s my review.

Story – “Where’s my damn sword?”

You play as Wolf, a shinobi with the emotional range of a brick but the combat skills of a literal god—if that god was constantly getting his ass kicked by samurai, corrupted monks, and a giant rooster (seriously, why are the chickens stronger than some bosses?). Your goal? Save your kidnapped lord, Kuro, from immortality-related nonsense, and along the way, kill anything that breathes.

The story is actually pretty good, but let’s be real—you’ll be too busy dying to read half the dialogue.

Gameplay – “Why is everything parry-based?!”

Sekiro doesn’t care about your Dark Souls muscle memory. You can’t roll spam, you can’t outlevel your problems, and shields? HA! Instead, the game forces you to parry everything like you're in a never-ending rhythm game, except the consequence for missing a beat is death. If you don’t get good at deflecting, the game will personally escort you to hell.

Every fight is a 1v1 anime duel where you either achieve absolute mastery or get absolutely embarrassed. And then there’s the prosthetic tools—grappling hooks, firecrackers, and a giant axe to absolutely disrespect enemy guards. They’re cool, but let’s be honest, 80% of the time, you’ll forget to use them because you’re too busy panicking.

Bosses – “I fear no man, but that thing… it scares me”

If Sekiro was a horror game, its monsters would be the bosses. Every major fight is a skill check designed to humiliate you. Some highlights from my personal suffering:

Genichiro Ashina – First time you fight him, you’re thinking, “Okay, I got this.” Then he enters his second phase, pulls out lightning, and suddenly, you’re questioning every decision that led you here.

Guardian Ape – It’s a giant monkey that throws poop at you. Disgusting. But wait! You kill it and celebrate… only for it to stand up headless and come back with a sword. Absolute nightmare fuel.

Owl (Father) – Imagine an old man who raised you, trained you, and then decides to ruin your life in a boss fight. He dodges, counterattacks, and bodies you like he’s in a fighting game tournament. Father’s Day? Cancelled.

Demon of Hatred – You remember how the game was all about sword duels? Yeah, forget that. This thing turns Sekiro into Dark Souls again, and it’s just three phases of suffering.
And then there’s

Isshin, Sword Saint. If you get to him, congratulations, you’ve made it to Sekiro’s final form of psychological torment. I have nothing funny to say about him. He’s just pure pain.

Verdict – 10/10, would cry again

I went into Sekiro expecting a tough but fun experience. I came out of it feeling like I’d gone through military training. This game will break you, but once you finally master it, the feeling is like no other. The combat is perfection, the world is stunning, and beating a tough boss after hours of failure is pure euphoria.

Would I recommend it? Absolutely. Would I play it again? …Give me a few months to recover.

Edit after beating the game.

I don't think I've had more mixed emotions on a game. Never in my life have I seen such tremendous heights alongside such horrendous lows. I'm the billionth person to say this, but having a mixed rating option would be nice. I settled on a negative review because I'd rather not play through it again.

The highs: Human enemies. All of them are such fun to face in 1v1 combat, however if you find yourself against more than one it's clear this game was NOT designed around that. The way parrying works is truly only suitable for duels, in which this game shines.

The lows: Non-human/Ghost enemies. These guys are ABYSMAL to fight. Like the least fun battles From has ever made. I'd rather fight Bed of Chaos over these annoying ghosts. See, to deal significant damage to ghost enemies, you need to use an item which allows you to hurt them. Otherwise, you're better off swatting at them with a piece of paper. The ghosts are just tedious for no reason. As for the beasts and other creatures, they just don't feel fun. Fighting with weapons ends up being a wonderful dance, while the beasts... You just run in circles and slap their legs a bit. Very lame, doesn't look nice and CERTAINLY doesn't feel nice. Solid 40% of the enemies are the unfun ones.

All the items you come upon in the world (besides the ghost-hurty buffs) feel totally useless. Run through the first area, bam. That's all the item variety. That's what you get. I didn't find a lot of use with the prosthetics. outside of a shuriken to keep the stance bar up on a boss or maybe a firecracker on an animal boss. They don't feel super integral, despite being just about the only form of a "build" you can get.

That brings me to my next point- variety. One of the things I love about Souls games are the crazy and varied builds. There's none of that in Sekiro. You get the sword, and that's it. For that reason, I don't REALLY consider this as part of the Souls family. That alongside the more obvious storytelling, of course. The characters are good, liked em lots.

Overall, a very mixed bag. The best parts were perfect, the other was the least fun I've had with a game. No replay value. 5/10.

Sekiro Rating: 10/10

There is beauty in simplicity, and this is what makes this game great. A very elegant approach that aligns with shinobi theme. Sekiro is a must-have title for all Souls-like fans. For others, this game might be quite challenging.


While Sekiro takes a slightly different and more direct approach to storytelling compared to other FromSoftware titles, its storyline is simple yet great. Even five years later, it remains a very atmospheric and visually pleasing game. The English voice acting is well-suited in its own way.

When it comes to gameplay, combat, and progression, Sekiro simplifies things as well. The game forces you into a very aggressive playstyle—the only playstyle there is. Character progression leaves no room for customization or builds. Previously, I said that Sekiro is the hardest game I’ve ever played. I need to roll back on that. In my opinion, what strongly differentiates Sekiro from other Souls-like games is its steep learning curve. Once you get the hang of it, the game becomes actually more fun and not as difficult. For example, I struggled with the Genichiro fight for a couple of days, over 20 tries. On NG+, it took me only 2 tries.
However, you can’t become overpowered in Sekiro; your strength comes mainly from your skill.

In other words, Sekiro keeps it simple to make it hard.

Going onwards, you must adjust hands as needed, do not keep the blade close to your body, keep your breathing steady. This is the life cut. You must watch your footwork. Your feet must be controlled whether planted on fire, air, water, or earth in equal measure.
Breathing is very important! Is the violent breath of life in you not hot? Exhale! Exult!
You must strive for attachment-non-attachment when cutting. Your cut must be sticky and resolute. A weak, listless cut is a despicable thing. But you must also not cling to your action, or its result. Clinging is the great error of men. A man who strikes without thought of his action can cut God.
To cut properly, you must continually self-annihilate when cutting. Your hand must become a hand that is cutting, your body a body that is cutting, your mind, a mind that is cutting. You must instantaneously destroy your fake pre-present self. It is a useless hanger on.
A brain is useful only up until the point when you are faced with your enemy. Then it is useless. The only truly useful thing in this cursed world is will. You must suffuse your worthless body with its terrible heat. You must be so hot that even if your enemy should strike your head off, you shall continue to decapitate ten more men. Your boiling blood must spring forth from your neck and mutilate the survivors!











Reach heaven by violence.

It is with a heavy heart that I write this review. Let me preface this by making it abundantly clear how much I adore From Software games. I played Otogi: Myth of Demons back on Xbox front and back. I have Dark Souls 1, 2 and 3 on at least 4 different consoles, and I am absolutely obsessed with Bloodborne and Elden ring. I love the souls model with my whole being. I even ventured out and beat games like Remnant, Surge, and the fantastic Lies of P.

So believe me when I say that I don't mean any disrespect to the franchise or the creators, but I cannot stand Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice one bit. Its NOT a souls game.

The problem for me lies in the basic design differences that make it such a departure from the other titles.
No real customization option for your character
Cant change weapons(sure you can change the prosthetic)
It costs money to use your prosthetic for some reason
Your life is incredibly low and you are given very few "estus flasks" in the beginning
There are an unreasonable amount of unblockable attacks
The level designs are a bit boring, albeit beautiful
There are archers hidden in the tall grass everywhere and theres no way to tell when they are firing at you from behind
Enemies are designed to throw you off cliffs

I wish I could come at different bosses in different ways. I wish I could change up my tactics with a new weapon or ability that doesn't force me to back peddle and grind just so I can fire 15 more shurkins and die.

The level of swordplay looks so amazing, it makes me want to replay Metal Gear Rising. I love the Parry mechanics in Lies of P and even in Nine Sols but somehow even if you parry like a god in Sekiro you still get grabbed 33% of the time and die.

Reading the reviews makes me feel like a crazy person. While I may be biased, and I did judge this game with the outlook of a souls player, I still feel like this game is just a bit over hyped. It's closer to a rhythm game.

Edit after 100% complete the game:
I've spent over 60 hours playing Sekiro and earned every achievement in the game. After completing 8 playthroughs, I can confidently say it's one of my all-time favorite games. It was also my first experience with a FromSoftware (Souls-like) title, and yes—it was extremely difficult at the beginning. My first playthrough took me 31 hours, and learning boss patterns and mastering the combat system was a real challenge.

But once it all clicked—wow. The combat system in Sekiro is second to none. It's precise, rewarding, and incredibly satisfying. Every fight feels like a lesson, and you genuinely feel yourself improving with each encounter.

The story and lore are also beautifully done—simple yet powerful—with four different endings to explore. If you're into skill-based combat, samurai/shinobi aesthetics, and stylish Japanese themes, this game is an absolute must-play.

Posted: February 16
This feels weird but, I recommend everyone to try this game but can't really recommend actually finishing it.
Let's set the record straight here, the game has a lot of good things going for it, but there is definitely a reason why they made Elden ring so different from this game and other games before that, that is one of the reason why I dropped it after few hours, which weren't really the thing for any other FromSoft game.
Let's go piece by piece, story is good, music is top notch, graphics are too, optimization is actually present and gameplay is good. For me the problem is with the new approach to gameplay, developers give you so many tools to use, much more than any other game from the studio but the problem is that they are not really optional anymore. In Souls games and in Elden Ring tools were kind of difficulty sliders of a sort, you can find a specific use for them and make the specific aspect of the game easier but here....it a necessity not an option. There are multiple types of enemies that require you to use specific tool/prosthetic and whether you like it or not you will have to find combination that works for specific enemies and not just ones that you like to use. And while it grants you a lot of flexibility, you no longer have a leveling system or any other equipment that is more straightforward in it's use, there are no builds to try. Summing it up, in a lot of cases the difficulty of the boss lies in the fact whether or not you found a correct tool/prosthetic combo, not so much your personal skill or your build.
And here lies another problem, map is very funky. You have no specific road to follow, which makes finding equipment all the more challenging, added verticality and necessity to go for stealth (because mobs are genueinly more pain in the ass than most of bosses) makes navigation even more confusing. So searching for the secrets/bosses will lead you to...demise in most cases, whether you will fall into abyss while trying to reach for specific item or will mess up with stealth, which by the way sucks...and this one is objective, it's badly made, sometimes you can walk up in front of an enemy and he won't notice but in most cases enemies will see you from other side of the map when you crouch in the grass.
Next aspect is reward/punishment. The game punishes you for death much more than any other Souls game, gameplay and storywise, which in turn goes back to combat. You are forced to play the game certain way, which for me doesn't make any sense, as it genuinely takes away from me trying different stuff on my own volition. This combination creates a gameplay loop of trying stuff, dying in process, suffering consequences and trying different stuff untill you find what works. That is all too gimicky for me, but that is subjective so decide for yourself, it's just a shame there is no demo for the game.
Oh...and hitboxes on grab attacks absolutely suck.....

The combat in this game is the best of any other Fromsoft game I've played, though I would have liked to see my katana do a little more damage to bosses. is a masterful action-adventure game that combines challenging combat, intricate level design, and a rich, atmospheric world. Set in feudal Japan, you play as the "one-armed wolf," a shinobi on a quest for revenge and redemption.

The combat system is the game’s crown jewel, focusing on precision, timing, and strategy. Deflecting attacks, breaking enemy posture, and executing deadly counters create a thrilling and rewarding experience. The stealth mechanics add another layer of depth, allowing you to approach encounters creatively.

The world is beautifully crafted, with interconnected areas full of secrets, shortcuts, and challenging enemies. The story is engaging, blending historical elements with supernatural twists. The GOTY Edition includes all updates and additional content, making it the definitive version of the game.

Pros:

Challenging Combat: A deep and rewarding combat system.

Beautiful World: Stunning environments and intricate level design.

Engaging Story: A rich narrative with historical and supernatural elements.

Cons:

High Difficulty: May be too challenging for casual players.

Limited Customization: Fewer RPG elements compared to other FromSoftware games.

Steep Learning Curve: Requires patience and practice to master.

Rating: 9.8/10 - A brutal and beautiful masterpiece that’s a must-play for action-adventure fans.

If you are coming from the Souls saga and you think you are ready to tackle this --- you are not.

The muscle memory that you've trained so far will probably only hinder you, and there is a high probability that in the beginning you'll hate this game. When you start, a lot of the animations from normal enemies are basically invisible, parries make no sense, and you can't even evade for saving yourselves with i-frames.

With that said:
This game is freaking great. Definitely not better than the Souls saga like I've heard so many times IMO, but it's ♥♥♥♥♥♥ good.

When your new muscle memory starts to take shape, when you learn to spam the parry button in front of new enemies to learn their patterns, when you start clashing and deflecting swords rather than just attacking, that's when the game starts to get seriously fun.

The world is gorgeous, the story rich, the bosses challenging without being totally unfair, the movement so fluid. This is definitely one mandatory step in the journey of any FromSoft enjoyer.
Just remember: this is not a souls game... It's a rhythm game. You have been warned.

In conclusion - do I recommend this game?

An incredible Action/Ninja/Samurai Game, where the difficulty is ruthless, and your enemies don't play around.

You'll be Jumping,Parrying,Mikiri Countering, and attacking your whole game. Miazaki has done it again by re-inventing the wheel by adding a posture system, that allows you to instantly killing enemies (or removing one health bar from them if they have multiple) if you break their posture. The game also has some stealth elements, where you can get an upper hand in a few combats by stealth, and not Fighting every enemy you see, or simply executing an enemy that didn't see you, or at least removing one of their lives.

Some enemies its better to break their posture by perfect parrying and doing actions that do a lot of posture damage(like mikiri counter) while some you have to burn through their HP.

And the main mechanic of this game that is on the name, you can revive once and sometimes twice in the same fight, while

Let's talk Asthetics and presentation: The game, visuals and asthetics are incredible, the sound design is slick, and the enemies are incredibly varied. And so are the bosses and mini-bosses. The soundtrack while very fitting for the game, i wouldn't really hear it outside of it.

Overall: a masterpiece, i give it a 10/10 - .

I pretty much only vibe with souls games, and even after dark souls and elden ring, this one is still my favourite. The limited weapon choice makes for extremely tight combat, making defence offensive makes it sand out from dark souls where you are now encouraged to be aggressive and get in the fight vs avoiding combos and waiting for an opening, and there is still good variety between skills and prosthetic arts to keep it from getting boring.

Mostly though, improving is the most satisfying with this game. Bosses that took 20-50 tries the first time around are all do-able in under 5 tries 2nd playthough. When the combat clicks, it *clicks*, and nothing is as satisfying as the *ping* *ping* of deflecting perfectly.

Highly recommend. Fromsoft makes good games that are complete on their own, no bugs, no microtransactions, no ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥.

When I first started playing this game on my friends recommendation, I couldn't get past the blazing bull, so I quit and wrote it off as not for me. For some reason, I decided to pick it back up about half a year later. Then I got stuck at Genichiro. But with my friends watching in the discord call, I couldn't give up in front of them because that would just be embarrassing. After hundreds of deaths and probably 4 hours, the combat clicked, and I killed him. That's when I knew this was a good game.

After that I was free to appreciate every detail all of these artists put into this. So many beautiful rotten locations, so much terror, so much artistry in the bosses. After Genichiro I knew I could take whatever this game threw at me and overcome it. Beating a boss wasn't just worth it for the adrenaline and the pride though, but for the story as well. This world is far too fascinating for it to just have one game. The final boss is definitely one of the most brutal things I've had to do in a videogame, and it was all worth it, and I bet I'll fight them 20 more times. What a special game. It's not objectively perfect, but no other game has been able to do what Sekiro can.

After finishing it once, I thought it was one of the best games ever made.

After playing it twice, I can confirm that is the best game ever made for me.

Never before has a game's story and gameplay affected me so profoundly.

I didn't think this game was for me, I'm glad they proved me wrong.

Peak gameplay, it's as simple as parry everytime the enemy attacks and yet it's cranked up to a notch with other various features. It has a steep learning curve very difficult to master and yet when you do, you're immersed in an actual intense fight with your mind and body focused on it, and succeeding in each challenge leaves a big feeling of accomplishment not only upgrading your character, but also yourself, although some bosses don't focus on that system.
All that, accompanied with a dark but beautiful worldbuilding with its compelling story and lore, is what makes Sekiro : Shadows Die Twice a game you'll stick with for a long time, not just because of its difficulty but also because of its beauty in its gameplay.

I will start by saying it took me like 4 years to actually play this game. This game has very few points where I think you get this feeling it definitely starts really rough with two early bosses being chained ogre and blazing bull which teach you the wrong lessons as the majority of enemies are supposed to be parried and the ogre cant be and the bull hurts you when you do. But once you get past those few hiccups at the start the game takes off and keeps the momentum all the way through to the end. You will start to get a feel for the combat and very rarely does a death feel like it was unfair. Going through my first full playthrough was awesome I loved learning all the movesets of the bosses and enemies and beating them with my new found skills. This game doesn't make you ever feel overpowered you get past bosses by learning them and it feels great to do it. Once I went to go for all achievements it was really satisfying playing through again and seeing how much I improved when I once took 20 tries on some of the bosses and can now consistently first try them. I 100% recommend this game and promise if you push through the rough first hour or two you'll come to love this game too!

Posted: February 4
"The One Game That Gets Better The More You Understand It!"


Sekiro is an incredible game that though definitely not for everyone is one of the most satisfying games to learn (Especially Boss Fights)


When I started my playthrough I was expecting the game to be more of a stealth/ hacknslash action game and less of a souls game, but it definitely shows its strong Dark Souls DNA, especially until around the midpoint of the game when you start unlocking and upgrading your character to have the necessary tools to stealth around most enemy packs and deal with most seemingly "cheesy" situations.

Let me be clear that I am still not the biggest fan of how the open world and normal mobs work like they do in other Souls games. The game often puts you in positions where you are overcrowded with multiple enemies (I was getting DS2 PTSD at times) and if you try to stealth every mob like you would in a game like Rise of the Ronin or Tenchu, you will quickly alert everyone in the nearby 20 meters once you execute your first enemy.

Many of the mini boss fights in this game are also super corny and at times not of the best design in my opinion, however the moment you start fighting pretty much any one of the tight and polished selection of proper mainline bosses, you will experience some of the best combat and boss design. I almost wish the game took the approach of a boss rush game similar to Furi because some of these fights are truly peak gaming.

Overall, even if I am not the biggest fan of this game like many other people out there, I really do think this game is worth experiencing, and honestly now after I have mastered all the systems and beaten it, after I TRULY UNDERSTAND the game, I kinda want to start a NG+ playthrough. The game unlocks super fun gauntlet style boss fights once you beat it and I managed to quickly beat the first one, but I think they are a really fun way to hone your skills even further!

Absolutely amazing combat system, that is simple yet really deep, and unique, also rewards skill, it's really a "get good" game.
Genuienely one of the most well crafted combat system in any game I've ever played.

However, I don't believe a game is as good as the sum of all its individual element, but it's the experience that matters, and to me, it was such a frustrating and unfun game that I just pushed through not for the enjoyement, but just for the sake of finishing it, always waiting for a fun that would never come.

I could list all of the things I hated, but that's most of it, and at the end of the day, it's all sybjective, and most people seem to enjoy it so does it actually maters?
The main thing I hate are as follow.

I love souls like level design, I believe they are the best 3d level design ever, but I was getting lost ALL the time playing this, and their was no, wow moment for me, no ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ look at this scenery (except for the white flower field), no opening the door which leads to the area you've been at 10 hours ago, no figuring out all the 15 different paths that all interconnect: if you were to map out the world map, it's more of a tree structure than a web structure.

I can't believe this world they've crafted. To me personally, it is impossible to get immersed in this universe. There's just so much that make no sense, and I don't mean the fantastic elements, those are fine, but the realistic elements, just don't work for me. For example, Ii think I've seen like 1000 man, 5 women and 3 kid. The roads make no sense whatsoever, if you take 5 min to think, wait how are the soldiers getting their foods, you'll realise it makes very little sense, same thing with weapons I just don't see a world like this exist, and I don't mean that I can't imagine a world exist with these supernatural elements, but just not this one

I hate how it teaches you the mecanic of the game. Souls game have some of the best tutorial ever, they teach you the base mecanics than give you increasingly difficult tasks that test your skills so you incrementaly increase your skill, because it rewards good skills, by that, I mean the way to suceed at a task, or the most intuitive way to beat a task is usually the right one. To go further in detail, imagine the asylum demon, the first skill check in dark souls, you see this huge threat in front of you, depending on your skill, the first thing that'll come to you is either to fight it or flee. If you think about fighting it, it will use slow attacks, so instinctively you'll dodge, then when you'll find oportunity, you'll strike back, very simple and intuitive. If you decide to flee, you can come back later to strike the demon from a higher point one shoting it (if I remember correctly), teaching the player this time that positioning and making the best use of the level design is a thing in this game. The easiest options are the most intuitive ones. That is not the case for the vast majority of Sekiro. For example, one of the first fight worth mentioning is against the chained ogre, now this game tries to teach you to play in a very specific way, block enemy attacks, dodge if not possible, mikkiri if available, then strike between the enemmies attack to build up posture then execute once their posture is broken. So what is the easiest and most intuitive way to beat the ogre: dodge then strike back, the same way as Dark Souls. A bit later on, you'll fight the raging bull, easiest and most intuitive way of winning: run to one side of the arena, this will force it to run at you, parry once, then strike back, rince repeat, it will always do that one attack. Why is this a problem, because, while other games can be cheesed, they aren't convenient or intuitive ways to cheese, like for example finding a spot where you can hit the boss outside the arena or making the perfect int build, but these methods of winning are complex enough that unless you specifically search for them, you won't accidentely discover them by accident, like in sekiro. Also, by easy, I don't mean fastest, I mean the one that requires the least amount of skill, the one most suited for a beginner. NExt example, the first monk fight. He has an attack where he stike 6 times in a row, if you parry all 6, you are flung backward. However, the ai is dumb as ♥♥♥♥, just run as soon as he start swinging, and he will continue to swing blindly into nothing, just wait for him to be done, then attack him. I tried hard to parry everything that is parryable, the intended way to play the game, but I just found so much more success in not doing that. The game actively punishes me for playing it the way it wants me to play it, if I am still trying to learn how to play. The one good tutorial of the game, which is imo, one of the greatest tutorial of all time, is genichiro, perfectly cool and fair fight, super fun (kind of the only fun I had in this game), that actually required me to learn how to parry. But this fight was like 8-10 hours in for me, 8 hours before that were just plain boring, even if the rest of the game was good, 8/10 boring hours is just not worth it, that's enough time to play so many indie gems. Anyway, after genichiro, still so many fight that are just so much more easy to cheese than to actually fight, like the ape for example: just run and turn in circle, wait for him to do that one attack on the ground, strike once, repeat, it's boring, but super intuitive and easy. Sekiro has the potential to be so fun, but my god it's like it's actively trying to teach me how to not have fun playing it.

And even when a fight works, like the second monk fight, where I did it really well, not taking any or close to any damage phase one and 2, just demolishing the dude in few minutes, it's just not fun, like the audio is good, the feedback, the attacks, it almost creates a dance between me and the monk, and yet, I just hated almost every second of it. When I finished it, what I said was closer to a "F******* Finally, HAAAA" than a "Let's GOOOOO!".

At the end of the day, this is just a subjective review. I've recommended many games that I didn't like but I can see why others would, I don't recommend this game, because enough reviews explain why it's a game you might enjoy, so I've decided to explain why I didn't.

Also, this game often goes to 50% off, so if you plan on buying it, wait for a sale.

Posted: February 1
Sekiro: Perfecting Combat

I happened to buy Sekiro without knowing it was a FromSoftware game, and by then I never had any interest in the souls series and the souls-like genre, so this is the first time I played something of the sorts and I didn’t know what to expect. It instantly became one of my favorite games: it was so good that after I finished I immediately started a new playthrough, which is something I never do. I kept learning the fun combat system until the end, and still wanted to learn and engage more with it. After playing Elden Ring a couple years after, it made me appreciate this game much more because of how much it has done right and that’s what I want to share in this review.

First of all, I was impressed about how Sekiro is such a “complete” game, for lack of a better word. It has a good story, cool characters, interesting lore and an amazing world to navigate with its own mythical creatures and awesome mobility tools. It has cinematic action sequences, it can be a stealth game at times, if you want to, and the boss fights are the best I’ve ever seen by far. The pacing is on point while also giving the player freedom, with many optional paths and enemies. The game is challenging, but always fair. That’s what I mean about this feeling like a complete game, because even though it has the , it doesn’t use it as a crutch and still delivers some amazing experiences outside of that. Just like the combat system itself, the whole game is extremely well designed.

I’ll mainly discuss the combat system: why do I think it’s the best? There are a multitude of factors that combine with each other very well, and they basically are:

: It starts with the posture system, which is similar to a second, independent health bar. When the actual health bar reaches 0 the enemy posture maxes out, you’ll get to deal a finishing blow to the enemy. This system brilliantly rewards constant pressure and not allowing your opponent to regain their posture, helped by the fact there is no stamina system, but will still work if you have a slower and more defensive playstyle. The replacement of the cheap hidden stagger mechanic is welcome and results in a more transparent system in which the player has both enough information and control of the situation at any given time.

A complement to this system is the refined implementation of deflections (or parries), which greatly impact the enemy posture while creating openings for the player to be offensive at the same time. For that reason, deflections are the bread and butter of Sekiro’s combat and is the first skill you’ll want to master since it opens so many choices and fundamentally changes how you approach combat.

: The responsiveness of controlling your character that comes from forgiving timing for button presses and cancels is a major part of what makes the intense combat sequences possible. This means you are not punished for the tiny input mistakes that are bound to happen and a lot of the time you can recover from mistakes if you correct your actions in time. You can almost always cancel a move soon after starting it, and what is even better about all of this is that the enemies will react to the attacks starting just as well as the cancels. Sometimes they might cancel their attacks to block a swing you made even if it wouldn’t connect. Such a simple detail goes a long way into making a better and more fun fighting sequence, giving the opponents more options and different reactions to the player’s moves.

To prevent the player from spamming attacks, bosses will usually deflect one of the attacks after some time, which buys time for them to make their own move. Some of these moves are called perilous attacks and each one has their own strengths and weaknesses that require different countermeasures, but these are basic mechanics that the player has access to, including even basic movement, which is enough to deal with some of them. This creates a back and forth that will go on until the player or the enemy make a mistake, and these situations can get really intense - the best fast paced and combat there is. You must think about when to attack and how to attack, and how the enemy can defend and what defense they choose to do to react accordingly and plan ahead. Doing this all in one big sequence, without leaving room for Sekiro and his enemy to recover their posture is what I strive for while playing, because it’s a really unique feeling, pure action packed fun with non-stop decision making.

: By balance I mean two distinct aspects of combat: the player must use most of their tools to be the most effective and how well all these tools work.

While you don’t have to actually engage with most of the tools and combat mechanics the game offers you, it highly rewards you for doing so. Some will reward a certain playstyle more than others, but with very few exceptions, each one of them are great for a specific situation. Aside from basic mechanics like the satisfying Mikiri Counter to counter thrust attacks, the player has access to prosthetic tools and combat arts.

Prosthetic tools are more specialized, but extremely powerful in the right occasion. These are weapons and utility tools and while some counter specific types of enemies, others can help you with defense. They might seem simple and even weak at first but integrating them into your game plan is very exciting, since they can complement your strategy or fill some gaps. I’ve been surprised multiple times how fun it is learning how to use a new tool effectively.

There are also combat arts, which are basically a selection of unique fighting moves from different martial art styles. Some of them are versions of moves of bosses and specialist enemies, like ninjas and monks and they can be focused around utility, mobility or their damaging capabilities. They are a form of progression, functioning like a skill tree, and there are quite a few of them. Just like prosthetic tools, combat arts are also incredibly fun to integrate into your gameplay, learn and eventually master.

: Enemies in this game are amazing as well, mostly because they are designed specifically for Sekiro’s toolkit. This might sound silly as a statement but it’s the opposite approach of regular souls-like bosses which have exaggerated movement, tracking and large area of effect attacks to account for every player build. This results in bosses being more interactable while still allowing different playstyles, since there are multiple good solutions for a huge majority of their moves because of precise hitboxes and good telegraphing done via animation.

Since the game knows you have the tools at your disposal, it test how you use them. There are specialist enemies that have defined strengths and weaknesses and will usually teach you a lesson about a new tool you might not have thought to use before. The variety of enemies is pretty surprising too, the game has cinematic bosses, strong warriors, big beasts, specialists and even has a puzzle boss.

I feel like learning and mastering the combat system is plenty of fun by itself. The combat might be daunting at first, but there’s a common saying within the community that once the combat clicks for you, it’ll be a breeze. It means after understanding it and experiencing it a bit, you’ll have plenty of options. At the end of the day, all these mechanics give you freedom to play your preferred way, and that’s one consequence I really treasure this game for.

Every once in a while I revisit this game to challenge a random boss and I have already done three playthroughs to see different endings. I intend to keep returning to it to scratch the otherwise unscratchable itch of the best combat system ever made and that's why I highly recommend playing this game.

This game is pretty good, aside from some of the boss fights.

Most of the game is a brilliant stealth-action where every encounter requires careful planning and execution. This is achieved by making combat really difficult and death really punishing, so you are forced to rely on stealth and your various shinobi tools in order to survive. Unfortunately, there are a few points in the game where you have to fight a mandatory boss, and the above concept just flies out the window - you are not allowed to use stealth, tools are mostly ineffective, and you're not even allowed to run away from the fight to try and regroup. Instead, you are supposed to do about a hundred perfectly timed deflects in a row, and if you miss one you get staggered, which spells certain death. This feels extremely cheap, considering how punishing death is, and how you're supposed to use strategic planning to avoid it during the rest of the game.

Don’t get me wrong: this is a good game. Possibly even your favorite game. Your game. But that all depends on what you want out of your Souls-like, or more accurately your stealth-action game. It shares a lot more with the likes of Metal Gear Solid than it does with Dark Souls.

There are no substantive RPG mechanics, no multiplayer, no PVP. 99% of the time it’s you, your sword, and your enemy (after a cheeky stealth kill, of course). If you love the bosses, the parries, the environmental storytelling, and especially the difficulty, Sekiro might be your favorite game. If you wanted an RPG or wanted to steamroll the game with a magic build, you’ll probably be sorely disappointed.

Must play!

Posted: January 31
Recommended, if you can get the hang of it
Recommending Sekiro is no easy task, since it's a mechanically obtuse game which rewards nothing else but a very aggressive gameplay, without delving into recklessness territory, against a rather short but intense game centered around boss fights.

Indeed, The major difficuly in Sekiro comes from being extra squishy to any forms of incoming damage. Pretty much everything can destroy the player with an average of 2 well-placed hits.

This however gets eased a bit since the Blocking and, furthermore, Perfect Parry are very strong and the your only true defensive mechanisms, if not your secondary damage source.

"Perfect parrying" (a high pitched sound of clashing blades) -along with some special moves - causes a lot of effects to happen:
Your posture will never get broken, even if your posture damage bar is at 99%
Will cause posture damage to enemies
Under some conditions, negate late-game enemies chip damage

Speaking of "Posture Damage", both Sekiro and all enemies have a "posture", which acts as some sort of secondary life bar.
The player, however will die only if the "life bar" gets depleted. Getting "posture broken" will trigger nothing but a vulnerable state where all incoming damage gets maximized

All Bosses demands a special fight consisting of "death blows", where each Death Blow may happen if either his "life bar" or "posture bar" gets depleted. (a fully depleted boss "life bar" will instantly trigger a "posture bar" death blow).

Upon Death, the Resurrection mechanic allows Sekiro to return into battle... except resurrections are limited to 2-3, and each resurrection can't be used one after the another (eg: you can't die over and over).

The catch of all of this, is some sort of half-baked "dragon rot" plague. Consecutive "true deaths" (eg: loading screen to a bonfire) will cause the "dragon rot" to plague random NPCs. This conditions will cause said NPCs to stop progressing their questlines and requires a special limited item to be consumed to heal them back (1 to cure ALL npcs)

Sekiro plays out very well only during a NG0 run, where you don't have access to all the flashy stuff. Once you hit NG+ the replay value falls down to near zero, except for a chance to unlock a different ending, at the cost of beating the same enemies over and over.

Expect, without tricks, to endure 4 NG+ cycles to 100% the game.

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is so insanely ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ fun. its easily my favorite action game that i have ever played and if youve heard of the game im sure you at least know this much about it. its so fun that it hurts my heart to rate this game four stars but deep down i really just cant go further than that. every single fight in this game is a challenge, even the little fights against random little pawns can be deadly if you are not absolutely locked in and focused on this game. with the weight of each battle, its just so satisfying everytime you make it through certain stages of the game. the deflect mechanic is pure euphoria, especially done in succession, and the sound design on everything makes the weight of every deflect feel huge. from the first to the last deflect i performed in this game, they both felt grander than ever.

the level design in this game for the most part is pretty genius. pretty much everything except for some memories is connected through one big world, and not very much is locked behind closed doors. in fact id probably anticipate a speed runner could probably just run past almost everything and beat the game in like under 30 minutes. yet everything draws you to it. every little minion is begging to be fought and everything is begging to be shredded to pieces by sekiros sword. each new area captivates with its beauty and tearing through is a sublime and satisfying experience. all the fighting and fighting in gorgeous landscapes all the way until the end and you look back and you say, what even happened?

just a little more backstory i guess about my playthrough. i played through the whole thing without using any sugars or consumables and using only the gourd, and every prosthetic i picked up ended up feeling like a gimmick to me that didnt feel entirely useful. the skill tree gives you a few extra moves, but only a few of them really being absolutely necessary to use. so in essence by the time i beat the final boss, i was relying on pure swordsmanship and collected poise. and i simply dont think i wouldve had it any other way because everything else is kind of a mess. the consumables are simply a difficulty lowerer that feels cheap to use, prosthetics never feel worth learning because they only work in niche scenarios that dont feel worth devoting time to, and the skill tree just adds some new moves which you have to equip each time you want to use them anyways. i forgot to mention stealth too which pretty much just exists and is something that you use once in a while but isnt anything impressive. im not gonna spend too much time hating on features that i never really used, but my extremely avoidant nature towards using anything besides my sword and gourd feels like a testament to how tedious it feels to engage with a lot of the mechanics that exist in the game.

my biggest problem with the game is the story. somewhere within this game there is a really really compelling story about loyalty and immortality, but its never really delved on very much. the plot is definitely there somewhere, but the way its told is so lost. i really wanted to engage with the characters and learn more about them but unfortunately a lot of the story asks you to infer each persons personality through their actions, which works in a minimalist storytelling fashion but at the end of the day the story isnt very poignant. the aura of the level design and art direction is so affecting, and the story feels attached to it at all points of the game but never really examined or broken down. i mean the game is no baldurs gate or anything but i still wish it gave us more for as grand of a story this couldve been.

overall, i dont see another game this ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ tight and insanely fun coming out anytime soon. the rigor it requires of the player is immense, but rarely painful. in this world of gaming where all of the imagery is usually dark and gothic and gloomy, the FromSoftware design team really knocked it out of the park with this world of violence painted on a blossoming fantastical replication of sengoku japan. it has its shortcomings when it comes to minor issues, but this is first and foremost a game of duels and combat, and it does it wondrously. also one of the greatest quotes in gaming of course.

Sekiro is phenomenal. It's focused, precise, and intentional in its design, and a welcome diversion from the souls formula the studio is known for.

My experience was entirely defined by a single moment that happened during one of the early boss fights against Lady Butterfly. The swordplay mechanics are introduced with two fundamental mechanics: a slash, and a deflect. When you are the aggressor, you slash, and when the opponent slashes, you deflect. Successful deflections will result in stagger (analogously stun) damage to the opponent, and a completely stunned enemy will keel over for a critical blow, which will either kill them or deplete a heath bar. The beginning of the game is tough! The defensive timing is tight, you don't have much health to work with, and the effective range of Sekiro is fairly close, so you'll have to expose yourself to danger in order to score meaningful damage. That first section of the game is difficult because you don't speak Sekiro's language yet, the ebb and flow of offense and defense, and you'll find your offense being interrupted and not knowing why. The Lady Butterfly fight was defining for me because her strike and parry animations look identical. In the middle of the fight, I naturally asked the question "If I can't visually distinguish between her turn and mine during combat, how am I supposed to go back on offense?" Almost immediately my next slash was parried, and for the first time I paid attention and noticed the accompanying audio cue. "Oh, this is what the game is about!" Within that single moment, the game unfolded to me, and I spoke its language. Not through a tutorial, dialog, hints or explicit communication, but entirely through exploring mechanics and asking questions did the game teach me its most valuable lesson. This is what it means to interact with the game and its systems. That moment is evidence alone of the thoughtfulness and intentionality of the game's design, and ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ does it build on it.

To that end, the swordplay is perfect. All of the enemies are fun to fight against, the bosses engaging, and the feedback loop (once you are attuned to it) is beautifully progressive. You don't need buffs, grinding, weapon upgrades, builds or guides, your improvement in this game will come from interacting with its systems.

The acoustics of the game are gorgeous, and appropriate for the natural environments of Sengoku period Japan. Play with headphones on. Not just for the music, birds chirping and running water, but because the audio and gameplay are interwoven, as stated earlier. The visuals similarly have a consistent, grounded language and even the magical elements fit appropriately within the visual style of the world and culture that is portrayed. If you are familiar with the consistent and precise direction of the souls titles, then this is unsurprising.

The story and lore are on par for the course with most FromSoftware titles, as in they exist directly to the extent that they support the gameplay and more can be found in the subtext of the item descriptions, or more realistically when you hear about them in a lore description by VaatiVidya. I personally have nothing more to comment on here, it's extremely high quality and you should watch that youtube channel if you want to get a sense of how good the world building is without doing the work of piecing it together yourself.

This is my favorite FromSoftware game. That isn't to say it's without its blemishes: while the swordplay is perfect, the combat mechanics that stray farther from that fundamental strength of the game's design are weaker and more niche. There is an array of ranged weapons and alternative weapon arts that can be unlocked and upgraded, but honestly most of them are only situationally useful, and consume limited resources to discourage their overuse. If these were removed from the game, it would play more or less the same. That isn't to say they are bad, it's just that in a game with such a flawless core system, these other mechanics seem unnecessary in direct comparison. For a game that is as stripped down and purified as it is, it's surprising that they left this stuff in.

This game is enormously fun, intentionally constructed, mechanically and aesthetically cohesive, and in my opinion the best media this studio has put out. 10/10, obviously. Please play Sekiro before you die. After all, hesitation is defeat.

Posted: January 28
My review of "Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice."

Deflecting to Break Enemy Posture

The deflect mechanic in "Sekiro" is pure genius. Instead of the usual hack-and-slash, you're encouraged to time your blocks perfectly to break an enemy's posture. Nail the timing, and you'll stagger your foe, opening them up for a devastating deathblow. It's like a deadly dance, rewarding precision and patience. But miss that beat, and you'll find yourself on the receiving end, which keeps the tension high and the battles intense.

Dying More Than Twice

Despite the game's title, "Shadows Die Twice," I feel like I've died more times than I can count! It's all part of the journey, though. Each death is a lesson, pushing you to refine your strategy and "git gud."

Tenchu Vibes

If you're a fan of "Tenchu," you'll feel right at home. The stealth elements, grappling hook, and overall ninja vibe are reminiscent of those classic games. It's like "Tenchu" got a modern makeover with a dash of "Dark Souls" difficulty.

Overall Experience

All in all, I'd give "Sekiro" a solid 9 out of 10. The mechanics are tight, the story is engaging, and the challenge is real. That missing point? Probably because I need to sharpen my skills a bit more.

If you're looking for a game that blends intense combat with stealth and strategy, "Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice" is definitely worth a playthrough.

Posted: January 28
If you like Elden Ring, Dark Souls, Bloodborne, or any other Soulslike, this game is really awesome. The combat is tougher than any of the Souls games, at least until you become a master. Even then, the fighting is very fast paced and relies significantly on parrying more so than chipping away at an enemy's health, which is the biggest distinction between Sekiro's combat and the Souls games. In addition, you can return to life up to 3 times (although this is limited to the boss stage you're in, or if you use a consumable item to restore a resurrection node).

In terms of the environment, there are significant similarities between this and the Souls games, including in how the map is almost entirely interconnected (although more like Dark Souls 2 than 1). First, the Sculptor's Idols are this game's Bonfires. The Gourd Seeds are Estus Shards, and function the same. The upgrade system works differently, though. You can purchase items in the environment with Sen, like souls, but they purchase items - not upgrades, although you can buy upgrades for your many available tools. You need to find Prayer Beads for upgrades health, and for every 4 that you find (through mini bosses or environmental exploration, and sometimes in stores), you gain a bigger health bar and more posture (more blocking stamina). You gain more attack power from boss fights. Overall, I like how this game did its upgrade system even more so than how it is done in Dark Souls.

The story is really awesome and nicely voice-acted, the music is excellent, the graphics and environments are beautiful and overall it is a really dope game. While I wouldn't say that it is always fun (I was at the final boss for 6 hours!), if you like the other Souls games, you will definitely like this one.

This game stood out to me because it reminded of a game way back called Tenchu; where you also play as a ninja and primarily use stealth to navigate the levels until you reach a boss at the end of said level. This game follows that formula. Combat is tough, requiring you to read attacks to respond accordingly with either a parry, jump, dodge or a certain counter you learn as a "skill" in the game. I have a decent amount of experience with souls like games, having played Elden RIng, Dark Souls 3, and Lies of P, accumulating about 500 or so hours collectively. Those games have their difficulty but combat is more simple in those games where you primarily roll to take advantage of the invincibility frames given your timing is down. (Excluding Lies of P where similarly you parry for your main source of defense.) This game forces you to abide by its intended mechanics. However, combat, once you have mastered it, which i have yet to do but am improving, is extremely satisfying and rewarding once you can pull it off. Game has an emphasis on stealth and boss fights. Some fights are annoying but can be dealt with if you are patient and learn their patterns and use the games defensive mechanics as intended. If you are looking for a challenge yet a rewarding experience i could not recommend this game more.

Posted: January 26
Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is nothing short of a masterpiece that redefines the action-adventure genre. FromSoftware, the creators of Dark Souls and Bloodborn, have outdone themselves with this bold, exhilarating title that blends breathtaking combat, stunning visuals and a gripping narrative.

The game's combat system is a revelation. Ditching the stamina-based mechanics of its predecessors, Sekiros introduces a posture-based system that encourages aggressive play and precision. Each clash of swords feels like an intricate dance of life and death, demanding mastery, timing and nerves of steel. Parrying a blow at the perfect moment is endlessly satisfying, and defeating a boss after countless attempts feels like a personal triumph.

The encounters are unforgettable. From hulking beasts to lighting fast swordsmen, each fight is a unique challenge that pushes you to adapt and refine your strategies. These battles are more than tests of skill - they're cinematic spectacles that will leave your palms sweaty and your heart racing.

The game world is equally remarkable. Set in a re-imagined Sengoku-ere Japan, Serkiro transports you to a hauntingly beautiful landscape of ancient temples, misty forests and worn torn castles. The level design is intricate, with vertical exploration made possible by Wolf's grappling hook, allowing for new levels of traversal and discovery.

What sets Sekiro apart is its narrative depth. You play as Wolf, a shinobi bound by duty to protect his young lord. The story is deeply personal, laced with themes of loyalty, sacrifice and redemption. The sparse but evocative storytelling, coupled with rich world building, makes every discovery feel meaningful.

The game's difficulty has been a point of contention, but its on of its greatest strengths. Serkiro demands patience and persistence, rewarding players with an unparalleled sense of accomplishment. It respect your intelligence and determination, pushing you to grow adapt in way few games dare.

This game is a complete triumph. It's challenging yet fair, visually stunning and deeply immersive. Whether you're a veteran of FromSoftware's titles or new to their work, Sekiro is an experience that will test you, thrill you, and stay with you long after the credits roll. Its not just a game - its an art form.

11/10

Sekiro is a masterclass example of a game that asks something of you. It asks you to learn its mechanics and overcome its challenge. Your options are limited and you cant change your weapon, forcing you to "get gud" with a specific play style. Your patience and aggression are rewarded in this game, and its so satisfying.

My first play through was very intimidating, sweaty hands and a lingering temptation to not pick up the controller. The game was very hard at first. It wasn't until I was roadblocked by the midway boss atop a tower that I realized I was playing this game wrong. I was dodging and running away from the boss just like dark souls. You CANT play this game like that.

This game is all about riding that razor sharp edge of life or death. The combat is in your face and you have to be able to match it or die until you get it. In my opinion the combat perfectly captures that feeling of "one mess up and your dead". Of course it can be difficult, but that's only because its your first play through, you don't know the bosses attacks yet, and you're still adjusting to the game. Certain bosses are even placed at points like checkpoints to make sure you understand certain fundamentals before you can continue.

Once you learn the core of the game, it really does click. It sounds silly to say it was hard until it wasn't but it really do be like that. I can easily say this is an amazing game with so much detail and effort poured into it. Right up there with Bloodborne and some of From's personal bests. Don't be intimidated and let this game teach you what you need to succeed as a shinobi.

If Hesitation is defeat, then your will is the best weapon you have.

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is a stunning game that I really want to love, but it left me with mixed feelings. It’s undeniably a technical and artistic achievement, with gorgeous visuals, fluid combat, and a deeply immersive setting, but it’s not without its frustrations.

The good? The combat is a masterpiece of precision. The posture system rewards skill and timing, and when you finally master a boss, it feels incredible. The verticality of the environments adds a fresh layer of exploration, and the world is dripping with atmosphere, blending FromSoftware's signature dark tones with feudal Japanese elegance.

The problem? The game feels punishing in ways that don’t always feel fair. It demands perfection, often at the cost of experimentation or fun. Unlike other FromSoftware games, there’s little room for creativity in playstyle—you’re locked into mastering the parry-heavy swordplay. If that doesn’t click with you, the game quickly turns into an exercise in frustration.

The difficulty isn’t just high; it’s rigid. Where other Soulsborne games allow you to grind, adjust your build, or summon help, Sekiro offers no such safety nets. While this purist approach is appealing to some, it can alienate players who thrive on variety and choice.

There’s also a repetitive nature to the experience. Many minibosses are reused, and some mechanics feel unnecessarily punishing (looking at you, resurrection system). The story, while intriguing, is told in FromSoftware's trademark cryptic style, which might leave some players more confused than invested.

I appreciate what Sekiro tries to do, and I understand why so many people adore it. It’s beautifully crafted and offers an unmatched sense of accomplishment—if you have the patience to meet its demands. But for me, the lack of flexibility and the sheer intensity made it hard to enjoy consistently.

Rating: 6/10 – A breathtaking but unforgiving journey that isn’t for everyone.

It took me 3 tries scattered over the course of 4 years to really give this game an honest try; I always stopped around Ashina Castle. I give all credit to Nine Sols for finally putting me in the right frame of mind.

Unlike Elden Ring, Sekiro is not really accessible for a wider audience. It demands a single playstyle, and it demands that you perform it perfectly. Prosthetic tools exist as a sort of magic equivalent that may allow for beating tough fights, but I tried never to lean on them, as I have always felt that a solid grasp on the base game mechanics will take you further when your magic (or Spirit Emblems) inevitably run out.

In that respect, it's the most uncompromising of From's games, and if you can't vibe with that then it will likely be a frustrating experience. However, if you love games that have a technical combat sandbox and reward focus and reaction speed, Sekiro will be an unparalleled way to spend around 30-40 HRs.

It's clear they gave up at this point due to boredom, budget constraints, and/or the limitations of Sekiro's combat system.

Edit 2: turns into a memorization game and loses reflex-based fighting in end game due to input delay and too-punishing mechanics. I still recommend it for the first 60% of the game, especially all versions of Guardian Ape (which were my favorite fights). However, this is my least favorite FromSoft title, well below DS2 SotFS. With that said, even the worst FromSoft title is leagues above the vast majority of other modern games.

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is a breathtakingly immersive journey through a dark and reimagined Sengoku-era Japan, brought to life by FromSoftware’s signature brilliance.
From its intricate level design to its deeply atmospheric world, the game stands as a masterpiece that redefines what an action-adventure game can be!
The story follows Wolf, a shinobi bound by duty, as he embarks on a harrowing quest to protect his young lord and reclaim his honor.
The characters you encounter along the way, whether allies or foes, are rich with personality and steeped in mystery.
The atmosphere is unparalleled, with hauntingly beautiful landscapes, from serene bamboo forests to crumbling temples, each teeming with a sense of history and danger.
Combat is where Sekiro truly shines, offering a brutal yet rewarding experience that demands precision, timing, and strategy.
The game’s unique focus on posture and parrying creates a rhythm-based combat system unlike anything else.
However, if there’s one flaw, it’s that the parry mechanics can feel overly punishing at times, particularly for players new to the genre.
While mastering it is satisfying, the steep learning curve might deter some from fully embracing the game’s brilliance!
That said, everything else is executed to near perfection.
The boss fights are some of the best in gaming, with each encounter feeling like a high-stakes duel that tests your skills and patience.
The exploration rewards curiosity, with hidden paths and secrets that add depth to an already rich world.
Add to that an exceptional soundtrack that heightens the tension and drama, and you have an experience that lingers long after the credits roll.
Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice isn’t just a game; it’s a work of art!
Despite its slightly unforgiving parry system, the game is a testament to FromSoftware’s ability to craft a challenging yet deeply rewarding experience.
For those willing to rise to the challenge, Sekiro offers an unforgettable adventure filled with beauty, heartbreak, and triumph!

Posted: January 18
Judging from the 20-30% completion rate of some late game achievements, I'd say a lot of these Steam players have quit early on and for good reason.

Pros:
-Responsive attacks and movement.
-Various move-sets and gadgets to choose from against different enemies
-No stamina bar. There is a stagger bar if you hold block against attacks instead of parrying.
-Some bosses are challenging but fun.

Cons:

-Some boss fights are plain bullsh*t, with sometimes large AOE attacks that you can barely avoid, or attacks that don't leave much room for reaction time (ie. single frame attacks)
-Single hits from bosses can take half your health, therefore trivializing Vitality upgrades. THis makes learning boss fights a pain
-Checkpoints to boss-fights can sometimes be far away, making it a drag to learn move-sets
-Difficult to come back if you take a break from the game. No reminder on where to go or who to speak to or what to get.
-Lots of backtracking as a result
-Last quarter of the game is pure BS in map design, mob design, and boss design, but it's do-able.
-Death rot mechanic if you die too much. Look it up.

Not recommended for players who are not used to Souls-like. Better off starting with Elden Ring.

Definitely recommend this game for players looking for a challenge. It does a lot of things right, but when it doesn't, you will yell "what a bunch of bullsh*t" for 30 hours
