TL; DR: Don't play it like a Ninja-skinned Souls Game, it is not that. This game is designed to specifically punish that playstyle. Souls games are built around shields, dodging and parrying. Bloodborne was hyperfocussed on dodging with some ability to parry and no shields. Sekiro is hyperfocussed on parrying with some ability to dodge and no shields.

I was going to give this game a negative Review at first, having played a few souls games before (DS3, Bloodborne, Elden Ring) I thought this would just be another souls game and I liked the feudal Japan / ninja theme. I really tried to get acclimated for this game the way I acclimated to the other souls games. By this I mean trying to dodge through scary looking attacks, attacking from the back, and mashing R1 whenever I saw an opening. I kept dying over and over again to low-level enemies. I tried to switch my playstyle mainly be stealth-based (after all, you're a ninja!), but quickly found myself frustrated due to the game's quite lackluster stealth mechanics (stealth is basically unuseable as long as you don't have the "Suppress Presence" skill unlocked). I was more than ready to give this game a negative review and just be done with it, but I desperately wanted to see wherefrom all those reviews that shower the game's combat system with glory came from. I tried to switch my playstyle one last time, and that one worked.

For anyone struggling to get into the game the same way that I was, I started thinking about the game less like a souls game (where you dodge through attacks), or like a stealth game (like a ninja would operate), and took more of a (knowingly) clichee-based Samurai approach. Staying calm in the flurry of the enemy's attacks, parrying away, like a rock in the waves, waiting for the perfect opportunity to strike. Parry, parry, parry, mikiri counter, attack twice, repeat. The curtain fell, and I discovered how the game was supposed to be played. This happened after 8 hours into my first playthrough. This is how the game's combat is meant to be played, and it feels so, so satisfying when you finally get it. Where parrying was something many players only do when forced to in Dark Souls, this game makes it an essential that is more than required to survive combat.

As mentioned above, this game is specifically designed to punish anyone trying to play it too "Dark Souls"-y. This specifically means attempting to dodge through attacks or away from them. The dodge in this game offers way less i-Frames than the Souls games do, and dodging into the swing of an attack ensures you will get hit, no matter how perfect your timing. Sekiro really tries to throw a wrench in your system to make you "un-learn" your Dark Souls experience. This game really tries to make you perceive enemies and bosses outside the box, instead of them just being R1-sponges with some different skins and attacks. Every enemy really has a unique fighting style that requires you to be on the edge of your seat on every moment of the fight, ready to deflect an enemy blow whenever their blade is just about to strike you. This game's combat is amazing - but it will take some getting used to and it is not for everyone.

Just for reference, the achievement for the boss that marks the one-third mark for this game's story is owned by 45.6% of the playerbase, while the boss that determines roughly a similar mark in Dark Souls 3 is owned by 54.3% of the playerbase - despite DS3 having way more bosses than Sekiro (25 vs. 14) and being the longer game in general. This just shows that this game is meant to be punishing, it is meant to be hard - even for people used to playing souls games. It is not for everyone, and you probably have to have some ounce of masochism in you if you've played this all the way through.

It is painful, but it is worth it. Brilliant game.

P.S.: There are a few mechanics this game doesn't directly tell you about - two I found particularly important:
1. The game quietly reduces your chance at a perfect parry / deflection if you've L1 spammed too much before the parry, even if your timing is perfect (I believe this is a loading screen tip that noone sees nowadays due to faster drives). Holding L1 to block beforehand is fine, just "parry-dancing" seems to be punished.
2. You regain posture much more quickly while holding L1 to block. I don't think the game ever tells you this, but it made some bosses that hug your posture bar much easier for me.

Posted: January 17
Sekiro is an amazing game. It is stunningly beautiful in terms of graphical fidelity, writing, and it's uncompromising game design. If you somehow have not heard of the game, it is also brutally difficult. While this review is positive, the earliest stages of the game (for me the first 8 hours) were possibly the most frustrating and difficult hours I've spent in a game. It's a game that is almost literally an acquired taste, but once you have the taste for it? It is addicting

The combat is possibly the most important aspect of Sekiro. If you've ever played a FromSoft game, you know how difficult they can be. Sekiro though, it stands above all the rest in terms of the sheer brutal learning curve. This game has very few ways for you to dodge attacks, and even fewer ways to directly harm enemies. Everything therefor relies on your and your enemies posture. You can block almost any attack, but if you block it it damages your posture. Run out of posture and you get stunned leaving you extremely vulnerable. Your enemies can be stunned the exact same way though, and when you stun them you get a deathblow that either instantly kills them or removes on of multiple health bars. Posture also goes down if the character is not being attacked. You can avoid posture damage by either dodging with a dash that has almost no I-frames, or by deflecting an attack.

Those deflects? They're like a Dark Souls game's parry on steroids. You need near perfect timing to press the block button right before an attack lands. If you do, you damage the enemies posture instead and can usually start attacking back. Because of deflections being the most common method to avoid taking damage, and because avoiding the enemy to get your own posture back let's them get their posture back to, the game is subtly pushing you to be hyper aggressive. Every fight is like a race to see who slips up first, who runs out of that precious posture gauge. Getting to the point where you're comfortable being that aggressive is the difficult part of the learning curve. Once you do though, almost no other game comes close to being that satisfying.

The audio and visual design really helps make you feel that exhilaration as well. The sound deflections make is simply euphoric, the animations of enemy attacks are beautiful and diverse, and everything else just slots together so dang well. Not to mention the actual environments of the game. This game is unironically one of the most visually stunning games I've played from the 2010s. There are a visually diverse amount of areas to explore and some of the boss arenas, in my opinion, outrank even Elden Ring and Dark Souls in terms of sheer aesthetic.

Despite the graphical fidelity being so high, I've also had very few performance issues with the game. Two boss arenas in particular had framerate issues. Other than those two areas though I've had no crashes, no frame drops, nothing. This game runs like a dream.

Before I close off, I do want to go over one issue I had with the game is terms of design. It's the camera. Sekiro's camera system is the single worst camera system out of any FromSoft game. If you get thrown against a wall, if you run too fast, if you clip a tiny object in the scenery? Your camera will unlock from the enemy, and good freaking luck getting it to lock back onto them. Genuinely, this is my only gripe with the game, the camera is wonky and unreliable at best. At worst, it's unusable.

Overall though, Sekiro is one of those games that I didn't understand how it got GOTY 2019 until I Now that I have played it through multiple times, I 100% agree with it receiving that award. and I won't hesitate on buying a sequel if one ever releases. Here's hoping that one day we get one.

this truly is one of the best soulsborne games, it relies solely on focus, reaction times, and learning from your mistakes. if you die in this game however, you perma lose 50% of your cash, 30% of your xp. (unless you're lucky enough to get "Unseen Aid" which prevents it from happening sometimes)

unlike the soulsborne games, you can't go look for a better weapon to deal more damage. instead you're forced to learn from your mistakes, adapt to your enemies' attacks. beating bosses is the only way to increase damage dealt.

after 100% completing the game at 56 hrs in, I still felt like replaying the game a 5th time because I couldn't get enough of the game. the combat loop is just.. perfect. it's so satisfying to perfectly parry everything from a boss or enemy that attacks consecutively. but rather than just replaying the game, I decided to challenge myself with the "Resurrection" mod, and charmless. (where you start taking damage even on blocks/guards, or bad deflections)

I highly recommend the mod for those looking for an even greater challenge. anyhow, I defo recommend this game for souls-like fanatics. or for those who'd like to play a genuinely challenging and difficult game.

IMO Sekiro is the pinnacle of FromSoft games, and given the high quality of every other Souls game they've made for me that says something.

The game is a win on just about every level. The combat is tight, satisfying, and challenging in all the right ways. The lack of alternative movesets and weapons means that all of the moves you can do are perfectly implemented, and lower emphasis on stats and levelling up means that when you have to learn to use all of the mechanics effectively to progress and improve. In a way unlike all the other Soulsborne games, you progressing really is you actually mastering the mechanics more than you optimising your build.

This is combined with a gorgeous, interconnected world map, excellent enemy and visual design and an engrossing setting created by a team that is intimately familiar with the inspirations that lead to it. For me, Sekiro is one of the best games ever made.

Posted: January 14
Sekiro is an amazing experience with an extremely enjoyable combat. It's definitely the most unique FromSoft title, but it still stays true to the souls-like formula. While some parts of the game can be challenging (mainly Demon of Hatred and Sword Saint Isshin) you never feel lost. It's always extremely clear what you are supposed to do to win the fight unlike in other similar titles. The game's combat revolves around parrying, but it's actually "optional". I've went through the whole game doing minimal parries and just dodging or blocking attacks. Was it optimal? Definitely not. Was it fun? Hell yeah.

When it comes to graphics Sekiro holds up great. In 4K HDR the game looks gorgeous (especially areas with a lot of flames) and it runs very well as well. Performance-wise it might be the best FromSoft game to date.

One thing I would recommend is reading . Item descriptions, NPC lines and eavesdrops are crucial if you want to see all of the bosses, because in a true FromSoft fashion quests are very cryptic and not exactly easy to follow.

Overall Sekiro is amazing, the combat, the graphics, the exploration, the atmosphere and the interconnected world all blend perfectly to create the perfect game and there is not a single flaw I could point out.

Posted: January 14
Sekiro is a stealth game masquerading as an action/soulslike. It is very much different from the games that it inspired. In these open - yet - linear worlds, you face a barrage of enemies to make it to the end. The game is fun if you know what to expect from it.

PROS:
1. This is a challenging game, in a good way. It is not unbearably hard, but it is much harder than games like it and the games that were inspired by it.
2. There is a grappling hook, which is always good.
3. If you're looking for a game with a little more emphasis on combat, this may be the game for you.

CONS:
1. The game forces you to play in a specific way - that is, in a stealthy way. If you try to get around this, you will have a terribly difficult time.
2. The game can somewhat be played like a rhythm fighting game.
3. Story and gameplay could be improved upon.

Posted: January 14
This is hands down the best game I have ever played. I've never left a review in my life, but this game has me telling friends and family how amazing it is. Before playing Sekiro, I had never really tried a souls-like game. I had played maybe 1 hour of Dark Souls III before returning it--I thought it was too hard, and I didn't enjoy being stressed out. Thanks to a friend, I was encouraged to try Sekiro.

The combat in Sekiro is like nothing else I have ever experienced. I believe it is the closest to sword-to-sword combat we will ever come without VR. It is fast-paced, and trading blows is an essential aspect. I struggled for maybe 30 hours before watching a youtuber beat the game with a dance pad for a controller. It was then I realized that it’s all just a matter of patterns--observe the way the blade sparks, the way it rings--only then will you learn when to strike or defend.

Never in a game have I felt my brain CHANGE like it did when playing Sekiro. The enemies essentially have the same abilities, they're just better than you--to defeat them, you must learn and master the skills. In this regard, the final boss is the most beautiful boss fight I have ever experienced. No game has ever challenged me as much, nor has any game ever rewarded me as greatly for my efforts.

Since beating Sekiro, I have been enjoying Elden Ring. However, it’s just not the same. What I really want is more Sekiro. Throw me into the fire, I want to be transformed again.

Sekiro is a phenomenal, fast paced game that solely teaches the player the basics of the game's combat while subtly teaching the player how to master those mechanics. If you're not into slamming your head against a wall for the sake of mastering satisfying gameplay, this game is not for you. However, if you identify with the previous sentence, there will be few games as good as Sekiro for you to play.

**SLIGHT SPOILERS AHEAD**

Gameplay - The game meshes the feel of being both a shinobi and a samurai very well. You have the option to stealthily take down most enemies in an area and get the drop on bosses, or go in guns (swords? shurikens?) blazing, or some healthy medium between those two options. Both combat options are equally robust. For stealth, there are many ways to isolate targets one by one and re-enter stealth if you're spotted. Learning how to use combat arts effectively and when to use shinobi tools to press your advantage is a phenomenal feeling. I've must've beat sekiro nearly 10 times, and the journey of struggling and learning bosses to becoming the boss yourself and forcing an enemy to desperately ward off your attacks is such a good feeling.

Environments & Enemies - There's a good amount of visual variation between locations and enemies, and the ways they're used against you are refreshing. There's an early game area called Hirata Estate - you face bandits that are initially difficult due to your low stats and their aggression, but eventually you conquer this challenge and move on. Later, once you get to Hidden Forest, you find them again but with a twist - these ones are ghosts, and you won't be able to see them unless you're close enough. It's a refreshing reintroduction to an old enemy that challenges you in a new way. Furthermore, once you're about a third of the way through the game, you're given three objectives, each in different areas. You're free to do those in any order, but the third area you go in will have unique enemies in places they wouldn't be present in if you had done this area earlier. It keeps the game refreshing on further NG cycles, and frankly they gave me a pretty good scare the first couple of times. The only environment/enemy that really soured this experience for me was Fountainhead palace. There's a section where you're forced to use stealth because there are an enemy type that can quickly disable if you're in their sightline, and it leaves in a state where you can barely move or fight, and you'll die in 1-2 hits in that state. I guess I don't like that there's very little room for combat in that section. Besides that section, the environments are super rad.

Story - It's far more on the rails than most of the other FromSoft suite of games. It's not a groundbreaking story, but it's serviceable. There are four unique endings, one of which I doubt most players will find on their own. There is lore attached to items, but most of it serves to allude to future story events or enemy encounters rather than build the world on its own. Again, it's fine - the story is told to you, rather than something like Dark Souls or Bloodborne where to figure out what's going on, you have to investigate and theorize and you still won't get a complete answer. Nevertheless, it still demands to deliver some strong beats (mainly by introducing bad guys with dope intros - I love you, Sword Saint), but people looking for a story driven game in Sekiro will end up disappointed.

I highly recommend this game to anyone looking for a challenging game that is satisfying to master.

Not gonna lie, this game is VERY hard at the beginning. Like, wow, you think Dark Souls or Elden Ring is hard? Well, you are in for quite some adventure.

It takes time to get used to it and until that point it can be frustating and I say this despite I am a souls vet (DS1 - 3, AC6, ER) and at first I really just wanted to get this over with.
My mind started to change after about 30 hours of playtime during farming against normal enemies and I got the feeling for the gameplay right. Now, after 47 hours, I really can say I got the hangs of it because now I consistently can do new bosses without the use of any cheese in a very reasonable amount of tries and I start to like the gameplay I first detested.

There are two things you need to master this game:
-) The will to learn new things. Forget how you fought in previous games from Fromsoftware, this is different. Learn the new style, don't try to force the old one.
-) Time, and this I mean in two different ways. First, you'll just need time to get used to it so be patient. Second, you need to get a feeling for the right timing to attack and deflect. In general, DO NOT hesitate to attack and learn how to deflect as soon as possible.

Sekiro is a souls-like game that is very different from other souls-likes made by From Software. So if you’re expecting the classic souls formula, that might turn you off.

Combat in sekiro revolves around posture and deflection rather than dodging attacks and chipping away at enemy’s health.

Personally I find Sekiro incredibly fun, even though I die a lot (definitely more than twice). Its unique mechanics with emphasis make it feel very rewarding in a way distinct from the Souls series.

So if you want a challenge but feel jaded from playing Dark Souls, this game might be a good fit for you.

Posted: January 11
There is no greater compliment to a game that has won GOTY 2019. So I will point out a few highlights of Sekiro that those who are considering buying this game will have a more relevant view:

1. First of all, this is a difficult game, but it is not "unreasonably" difficult. All the game requires from you is constant effort. Remember this: "I fear not the man who has practiced 10,000 kicks once, but I fear the man who has practiced one kick 10,000 times". So if you really want to play Sekiro, do not be afraid to retry many times.

2. Do not underestimate any enemy, because even the weakest one can defeat you if you are careless.

3. I especially like the Posture Gauge mechanism in Sekiro, you can simply understand that if the Posture is full, anyone can be killed immediately, which makes every match tense and fast. You will not need to force the enemy's HP bar to 0, because if your skills are good enough, you can completely counter all attacks and push the Posture Gauge to the maximum, then kill them

4. As a "tradition" of From Software, a part of the player will soon feel confused because they do not know what to do next because the main quest or side quest is not clearly displayed. So, if you are stuck for too long, do not hesitate to seek help.

5. Sekiro is a game with replay value, although there is still repetition. If you are immersed in the world of Sekiro, you will definitely want to learn all the endings.

6. And finally, this is an extremely valuable game, even without the discount. If you want to conquer all the Bosses, get all the endings and 100% achievements, buy Sekiro and enjoy.

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice stands as one of the most majestic and compelling games ever created, blending intricate combat mechanics with breathtaking environmental design and a gripping narrative. From the very first moment, it immerses players in a world where every sword strike feels weighty, and every move carries consequence. The fluidity of the combat system, with its focus on posture and precision rather than just brute force, brings an unmatched sense of satisfaction and challenge. Set against the backdrop of a beautifully crafted feudal Japan, the game’s stunning visuals, atmospheric landscapes, and haunting music create an unforgettable experience. But it’s the emotional depth of the protagonist, Sekiro, his struggles, and his journey of self-discovery that elevates the game into something truly extraordinary. With its masterful blend of artistry, storytelling, and gameplay, Sekiro not only stands as a technical achievement, but it also remains a triumph of design and ambition in the gaming world.

FromSoft's worst work since AC: Nine Breaker, hands-down. The game is incredibly inconsistent and only the directly plot-related bosses seem to have had any real thought put into that. Couple that with the most punitive death system since Demon's Souls, only the vaguest semblance of a plot (and much shallower world-building than the studio usually manages), a supremely cumbersome system for using any of the special abilities gained through the Shinobi Prosthetic or character progression (most of which end up being completely useless anyway, which is a great laugh) and you've got a game that -- while still better-made than many -- simply isn't that good.

There are other games that have taken the core concepts that Sekiro experimented with and refined them into something beautiful; by comparison, sadly, this feels like a half-baked prototype.

Ah Sekiro. I remember my first experience of this game, in which I reached one of the early mini-bosses, could not avoid his grabs, and died so many times I quit. Then several years later I decided to pick up the game again. Thank god I did.

Sekiro is a masterpiece of combat and difficulty. Never once (well maybe not never but very few times) did I think "Damn that's ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥" after dying. Instead I thought "Damn I messed up". This game is entirely dependant on you and your skill. Unlike other souls-games, you cannot over-level or make a broken build, but it doesn't matter. They give you the most broken ability in the game from the very start: deflect. The fluidity, the simplicity, and the satisfaction gained from beating a boss are the most rewarding I have ever seen from a souls, and I truly believe FromSoftware peaked with Sekiro

FROMSOFT, GIVE ME SEKIRO 2 AND MY LIFE IS YOURS!

However, the game still suffers from a lot of the artificial difficulties other fromsoft games have. regardless of skill level multiple enemies is never fun, and for some reason several of the miniboss fights are designed around that. normal enemy placement in a few certain areas also suffer the same downfall, forcing the player to run pass enemies and go for the next checkpoint.

it's definitely not the hardest fromsoft game like some people say, but it is the most deliberate by having the entire game designed around a few mechanics and it'll be very fun if you can enjoy that.

I've just completed my first run of the game. As a big Souls fan, when I heard people say Sekiro is better than Dark Souls 3, I was in disbelief. Well, now I believe they're right.

One of the biggest examples I can give is Sekiro's exploration of areas made me realise how much of a slog DS 3's travel between bosses is.

But you probably want to hear more about this game. The combat is very difficult, to a point where I felt quite at odds and had to take a break for a week or two. Plus, you can't summon anyone. It's just you vs. the boss. But then one day, the combat just clicks and you start to see the patterns and telegraphs for the bosses' moves. It's incredible because the improvement of the player ties into the improvement of our character Sekiro himself!

I highly recommend this game because of its combination of stunning areas, addicting deflection mechanic, story and overall consistency in quality.

Posted: January 5
Only 100%ed it to invalidate any accusations of sucking at it.

Has nice ideas, but horrible execution. Any of the good parts are overshadowed by the frustration and tedium. There's a part in the game, where to progress, a character tells you to go talk to another character. You go to look for where that character should be, but they aren't there. You then have to walk back to where the initial character is to tell them that the character you have to talk to isn't there. They will then tell you to try finding them at another spot. You go to that spot and the character is there but they are totally and definitely not the one you're looking for (because they have a mask on) and then asks you to go on a side quest for them, where you have to find four characters and off them. Two of these characters can block any and all damage unless you have the one very specific item that can beat them easy. This item can only be found by looking in an area only accessible by talking to another character in another area and getting an item from them.

Most of the ninja tools you have are useless or only useful in very specific, one-time situations. Enemy balance is just awful and grinding xp to unlock all the skills (which are also all useless except for two passive ones) is tedious.
There are two different final bosses, both of which are blocked to you by a different boss. Imagine in DS2, if in order to fight Nashandra, you had to fight the Throne Watcher and Defender, Every. Single. Attempt.

Beating bosses eventually goes from, "Hell yeah" to "It's finally over" to "I have to do this two more times for the other endings".

Sekiro is hard and easy at the same time.

Hard because you have to abandon all the dodging you learned from other souls games. Easy as in if you know how to follow instructions, the game will reward you tremendously. Sekiro's combat system comes in hard set rules, follow these rules and you will beat the game.

Posted: January 4
9/10

Pros:
+ Inspiring level design
+ Perfect Japanese visual aesthetics
+ Challenging combat system
+ Not a Dark Souls III in Japanese Setting
+ Awarding exploration
+ Strangely interesting plot

+ Too steep learning curve
+ Not much variation in how you can approach different enemies
+ Sometimes bosses pressure you too much

In a nutshell: difficult, challenging and rewarding. I would have preferred to find different approaches to difficult enemies, like spells or other weapons. And in general, by the end of the playthrough, I wanted to customize the character and switch the weapon. But here is only one way to do things and that is not bad. It requires you to truly master the way of sword. The game forced me to learn the kanji 死 and fully understand the phrase: hesitation is defeat.

Posted: January 4
Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is a masterful blend of precision, challenge, and storytelling, delivering an unforgettable experience that redefines the action-adventure genre. FromSoftware’s take on feudal Japan is as beautiful as it is brutal, immersing you in a world steeped in myth, honor, and relentless danger.

At its core, Sekiro is a game of skill and patience. The combat is razor-sharp, demanding precision, timing, and an understanding of your enemy’s movements. Unlike the dodge-heavy mechanics of other FromSoftware titles, Sekiro emphasizes parries and posture-breaking, creating a dance of blades that feels both intimate and exhilarating. Every duel, from lowly foot soldiers to towering bosses, is a test of your reflexes and discipline. When you finally master an encounter, the sense of accomplishment is unmatched.

The story is delivered with FromSoftware’s signature subtlety but feels more direct and personal than their previous games. You play as Wolf, a loyal shinobi on a quest to rescue his kidnapped lord and restore his honor. The game explores themes of duty, sacrifice, and the cyclical nature of violence, all set against a backdrop of beautifully realized locations, from serene temples to war-torn battlefields.

Sekiro also stands out for its unique mechanics, such as the grappling hook, which opens up vertical exploration and adds a new dimension to combat. The resurrection system provides a fascinating twist, allowing you to cheat death and rethink your strategies without breaking the immersion.

Visually, Sekiro is stunning, with meticulously designed environments that capture the beauty and brutality of its setting. The haunting soundtrack, filled with traditional Japanese instruments and atmospheric melodies, perfectly complements the tone of the game.

While its difficulty is unforgiving, Sekiro never feels unfair. Every failure is a lesson, every death an opportunity to improve. It’s a game that demands your full attention and rewards your persistence with moments of triumph and awe.

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is not just a game; it’s a journey of mastery and growth. It’s challenging, breathtaking, and utterly rewarding—a modern classic that stands as one of FromSoftware’s finest achievements. For those willing to embrace its difficulty, it’s an experience that will leave a lasting impression.

Posted: January 4
Great game. Definitely as hard as people say it is. The difficulty adds to the experience, I must have died to the chained ogre at the beginning of the game at least 50 times, but little by little you really get a hang of the game. For the first 5 hours I honestly thought I was going to hate it, even considering how well everyone speaks of the game, but the thing is after the ogre fight I felt hopeless. I really did not think there was any way I was finishing the game, I honestly did not think I was even going to be able to beat Gyobu in fact. The thing is, it is a game where you do not need to grind to beat the next boss. If you are somehow good enough from the start, you could probably finish the game in 5 hours. Unfortunately, it took me closer to 50. Despite seeming like a massive number of hours, most of those were beating the first half of the bosses. I was also trying to explore every single place I could, and even finished my prayer beads in my first playthrough. I highly advise you explore, you are not going to beat bosses because of prayer beads, but it will definitely help. I will also take the opportunity to say that one of the bosses I was most worried about was the double apes, seeing how a similar type of enemy in the ogre had made me want to rip my hands off. Somehow, I beat them on my first try. I can confirm that after abandoning the game for a year after the ogre fight at 5 hours of gameplay, you should not give up the game. There are points you will feel hopeless. Watch a small guide too, there are sidequests and bead locations which are extremely difficult to find without a little guidance.
It is difficult to formulate a well put together review, but there is no feeling like beating that final boss. You will honestly feel like you can beat anyone at that point. When I started my second playthrough I felt invincible, and instantly got reality checked by the ogre again :")
The story is great, but I also recommend watching a video on it, as it can be confusing keeping up with so many plot points. It probably is not, but the year of abandonment really got me lost in terms of lore. It is really interesting, and it actually leaves you with some form of curiosity, which is only characteristic of a good story.
Overall, I have to say keep in there. I am very late to playing the game, but I know there are people out there refraining from playing the game because of the difficulty. The game is never going to get easy I can tell you that. The thing is, once you actually understand the mechanics of the game, you could die 100 times and still find those sweet parries just as satisfying. Not only that, but if you do die 100 times, once you finally kill the boss, honestly, it is going to feel miraculous (and really cool thanks to the deathblows).

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice – A Second Chance

When I first tried Sekiro back in 2020, I hated it. Every moment felt frustrating! But after giving it another shot and learning the mechanics, I found it to be extremely fun!

The game is well-balanced: you can rely entirely on your sword to fight, or use a variety of extra tools to help you out. If you pay attention to enemy movements, you’ll find each boss fight deeply rewarding.

I finished the game in about 40 hours, taking down most of the mini-bosses and all the main bosses.
By default, the game is locked to 60 FPS (like Elden Ring and other FromSoftware titles), but if you want to play above 60, there’s a mod to unlock the framerate.

Be warned: you’ll die a lot at first, but that’s part of the learning process. Stick with it, and you’ll discover an amazing experience that truly tests your skills.

Possibly the most fun and most challenging game i've played to date. Reasoning as follows:

1. World-Design: Beautiful japanese mountain-landscape with great attention to japanese-horror/folklore (ogres, armor, headless entities etc.) The game easily enables one to immerse themselves in this unique setting.
2. Functionality and diversity of equipment: Grappling-hook enables fast movement (vertically). Unlimited stamina and slide/mantle mechanics really make the horizontal movement pop. Very fast pace, and never tiring.
Upgrades of the prosthetic arm allow for a great (and yet not too vast) array of gameplay options, and really complement the "sword-only" mechanic for fighting.
Addition: The combat system is very unique, heavily relying on perfect timing and concentration (even more than other souls-like games), and never feels dull, due to its nature of trying to keep you focused 24/7.
3. Entities/Opponents/Bosses: Very well put in place in their respective parts of story/world. The first two "mini-bosses" might throw one off, as they really put you in hot water regarding the pace of the fighting system. However I would argue that they perfectly prepare you for whats to come.
--> Old-japanese Warriors, Beasts and a bunch of crazy folklore ♥♥♥♥ inbetween. Everything you would want from a japanese-inspired game is being displayed by FromSoftware.
4. Storyline: Is easily comprehensible, and follows the old "values" of a Shinobi. A fallen warrior trying to follow his masters dying wish and, in doing so, tries to combat the dystopian ways of an old kingdom.

I cannot go into any further detail about the game if I try to avoid spoilers. All I can say is that this was the best time I ever had trying to finish a game, and that I would ABSOLUTELY recommend it to everyone trying to challenge themselves with a high-quality sword-fighting adventure.
Simply put: Hard as ♥♥♥♥, fun as hell & nothing in between.

(Side-note: I never really got frustrated/angered by the enemies or the game itself. If you are a person to rage easily, or who does not like to commit to lengthier trials of error, then this game might not be for you. But thats not the games fault. People suck & people hate. Dont let that stuff phase you)...

Very difficult for my skin. I have never played a game which requires so much concentration and attention; my toughest enemies until this were God Of War's various elites - this is a whole new ballpark.

But, defeating these guys gives an unpresented feeling of gamer-achievement. Really worth the effort.

EDIT: Having finished the game, I can say that by the time it was over, I was just desparately trying to wrap it up and be done with it. I literally could not wait to click Uninstall. It's a good challenge and my comment about feeling achievement after beating a boss still stands. But, story-wise, narration-wise, content-wise, map-wise, it's just several layers below GoW, GoT, HZD etc.

If you're looking for top-tier exploration, any of the above, plus Skyrim, Witcher 3 and Elden Ring will be better. If you're looking for a better story delivery, almost any action adventure will be better. If you're looking for amazingly flowing combat which punishes you for not dancing in tune - Sekiro is your game.

It's a beautifully crafted combat system with difficult bosses. But, that's about it.

Great action/adventure game with light RPG mechanics and a great combat system. Exploration is quite underwhelming but the combat more than makes up for it. The combat initially has a steep learning curve, but once you climb that hill there's nothing quite like it. It's based around parrying attacks and understanding the tempo of enemies (bosses in particular) to know when you can sneak in hits or otherwise exploit weaknesses in their movesets. If you enjoy the process of learning and mastering a combat system this game is for you. However Sekiro doesn't offer much more than that, which might be a problem for some players.

I really wanted to enjoy this game, but the graphics completely ruined it for me. In 2025, you expect games to at least look decent, but this one feels like it came out 10 years ago. The textures are blurry, the character models look stiff and awkward, and the environments are so plain that they don’t feel alive at all.

What’s worse is that even when you crank the settings up, it still doesn’t look any better. I don’t know if the developers just didn’t care or ran out of time, but for a modern game, this is just unacceptable. Graphics aren’t everything, but when they’re this bad, it’s really hard to stay immersed in the game.

If you’re looking for something visually impressive, this isn’t it. I’d recommend spending your money on a game that actually takes advantage of the technology we have today.

Posted: January 1
Hesitation is defeat. Honestly, I had really high expectations for Sekiro and it completely met them. The game has moments where the landscapes just make you stop and say wow. There’s so much beautiful scenery throughout.

I loved this game. At first, it’s super tough and frustrating, but once it clicks, it’s so rewarding. The first time I played, I died a ridiculous number of times to the first mini bosses and almost quit. But pride, determination, and the gorgeous aesthetic made me come back. I’m really glad I did. The story is great, the level design is amazing (especially Fountainhead Palace), and the boss fights are insanely satisfying.

Customization isn’t as deep as the Souls games, but there’s still plenty of room to develop your own style, whether through prosthetics, combat arts, what items you keep in your hot bar, or your play style. Every boss was a blast to beat (except maybe Guardian Ape and Mate), and it felt like I really earned those wins.

Sekiro might look like another Souls-like at first, but it’s a totally different beast. The combat is the biggest change. You get rewarded for perfectly reacting to and punishing every move. Skill is king here. Fighting is all about timing parries, dodges, and jumps to win intense duels. Some enemies like beasts are fought like in Souls games, but the real challenge shines when fighting other humans.

If you think your lightning-fast reflexes from years of online FPS games will make this easy, think again. More than once I thought, “One more dual sword wielding monkey,” or “One more mini-boss,” is gonna make me quit… but nope, here I am, getting my butt kicked again.

All in all, it’s a challenging and satisfying game I’d recommend to anyone willing to put in the work.

Posted: January 1
This is the worst FromSoft game provided in the modern era. The game design is not based around skill but spamming the ♥♥♥♥ out of the deflect or attack button. On top of this, the game is heavily focused on the usage of the Ninja Prosthetic to make the game feel "fair".

The number of reused bosses is excessive and constantly fighting a boss as a regular enemy is not enjoyable. It reminds me of the end of DS1 where the Capra and Taurus demons were standard enemies as a references to show how strong you are currently. In Sekiro, it fails completely to provide this feeling as it does not matter that you have improved the stats of your character.

Additionally, the game difficulty is not as high as various "Souls gamers" would have you believe. The greatest level of difficulty is believing the game would play like any other FromSoft game that was popular at the time. The perfect deflection period is excessive compared to other games and is more of a memorization of when you can spam the attack button compared when you can spam the deflect button.

The game feels the best when it is against "human" enemies that are susceptible to the parry mechanic. Fighting enemies such as the Apes makes no sense with the available health bar sytem. What is the point of a health bar and a stagger bar when they are both effectively zero when you are allowed to stagger kill an enemy. This system is nonsensical and only really works on "human" enemies.

On top of this, the game has "stealth" mechanics. This is the worst implementation of stealth mechanics I have ever seen. Killing an enemy with a silent take down behind a building will still alert an enemy 90% of the time and makes no sense. The amount of time required for the "stealth system" to reset after being seen is also ridiculous. There is no reason for it beyond being an artificial time lengthener.

The repetition of bosses, the terrible level design, and its complete failure as a stealth game are the reasons I despise this game and wish it was better.

P.S. Adding an enemy under water sucked in Kingdom Hearts and is even worse in this game.

Posted: January 1
IS THIS GAME FOR YOU? You will love this game if you DEEPLY love the Souls game and play them at a high level where you have beaten them multiple times to get all the endings. You have to understand these games at a HIGH HIGH HIGH level! Gameplay here only favors aggressive attacks and parry system. There are not multiple character builds…. You have to parry!

So there are many pretentious gamer who do not know how to critically think about Soul-like games leading to the misconception that these games are only good because they are hard. That is not true... Soul games are good because they are hard but they want you to learn how to fight and give you time to learn the mechanics and as you progress you feel that your victories are earned. This game does not give you the time to learn anything when every boss/mini-boss kills you in 1-hit OR 1-combos; This is not an exaggeration! Out of the 24 bosses/mini bosses I have beaten I have only felt that I earned it 2 times and the rest felt like luck. That is really a symptom of being a bad game but this game is so mechanically flawed it does not deserve the praise it gets.

THE STORY: This game tries to copy Dark Soul’s ambiguity and openness for to interoperation... and fails miserably if you pay attention. There is NO reason for the story to be as ambitious as it is and should have done more to stand out and make the characters more memorable. Parts of the story do not make sense as the main character fights and kills his own countrymen for NO REASON!

STEALTH COMBAT: This is the most fun part of the game where you sneak around enemies and kill enemies that are unaware of you. The stealth is done very well and feels good. Some would argue that it can be brainless because you can sprint at an enemy and backstab them with no issues, but it does feel fun! However, once you are spotted enemies will always psychically even when you break line of sight… making the stealth aspects feel very poorly implemented! Most of the times you learn the best paths to take to always stay in stealth. Stealth is always favored over combat. Towards the VERY end of the game stealth is made almost made impossible where enemies will see you way to easily… it feels poorly done.

MELEE COMBAT: Fighting feels too slow to respond and your sword often feels too short. When you go to fight punish your enemies it never feels like you have enough time to respond. The best way to think about the battles is to think of them as a “dance.” Your goal is to learn when it is your turn attack. The game wants to you keep the pressure of attacks up and favors a system where you parry attacks over dodging. Through the implementation of the “Posture Meter” the player has a limit on how much they can block and deflect enemy attacks. Likewise, enemies also have a “Posture Meter” which when filled can cause a deathblow, allowing you to fully empty an enemy health bar one level. If the players meter is ever full they are knocked down and unable to defend. Towards the end of the game the meter fills up too quickly and it is very unfair… you have to learn to perfect parry. No matter what it makes the game feel unfun.

COUNTERS: During battles you will have pop-ups warning you of the ability to counter attacks and it never feels like you have enough time to respond to counters. Enemies do too many different moves making them hard to predict and learn. Everything about this game is made to feel hard and nothing was really done implemented well.

AWFUL MENU: The worst fight I had in this game is with the lock-on system and menu. Which is exemplified in every boss encounter. The lock-on system randomly drops for NO reason or refuse to lock-on for no reason. When you go to run the lock-on also drops and running is an important part of battles.

The item menu is insanely slow to search through items to use in a fight... there is never enough time scroll through everything and it was just a bad idea implementation.

SKILL SYSTEM: None of the skills you get feel useful OR comprehensible on how to best use them aside from maybe 2 skills you get. Weapon arts are mostly useless and make you a sitting duck.

Leveling up is so slow it really makes the game feel like a mobile game when it comes to acquiring skills… I think this is made to increase

The bosses themselves are garbage that you cannot learn because they kill you in mostly in 1-hit sometimes 2-hits. This red flash mechanic is the worst because it does not let you know what unblockable attack you are responding to... a grab, thrust, sweep. The red letter that shows up gives you no indication which attack is being use and even worse it distracts your eyes away from the positioning of the weapon; that is bad game design!

The pacing of the rate in which you earn skills is so slow... the game feels like a miserable draw... and I rarely died to non-bosses and would never face a boss when you lose half a skill point EXP Gauge on death. SO miserable slow and unfun.

The good: Environments are amazing looking, no game breaking glitches that make the game unplayable. The game MOSTLY runs great with a very noticeable slowdowns!

TL;DR. The game does not suck because it is too hard it sucks because there are too many mechanics, the player character is too slow, and bosses all kill you in one combo so you do not have time to learn their moves and lock on is poorly done. The more I play this game the more I hate it and it is NOT because it is hard but because this game does not give you a chance to learn and grow.

After hearing so many great things about this game from rave reviews to gamers on social media that can't sing enough praises for this game, I decided to give it a try, and this is just plain not for me.

Now, if you have plenty of free time to play games and enjoy extreme challenges, then I'm sure you would love this game like most others do. As a gamer over 40 years old that works full time, has a family and dogs to take care of, I do not get as much time to game as I'd like, so this game was simply too much. For all I know, this game, the Dark Souls series, Elden Ring, etc are all fantastic experiences, I simply don't have the time to "get gud" as most like to suggest.

So, if you have the time, energy and patience, this may very well live up to all the accolades the game has earned.

But if you are like me and don't have the time for the upper echelon of challenging games, you might want to skip it. I know I am in the minority of people that refunded this game, but I know I can't be the only one that lacks the proper time/attention/focus/etc it takes to build the skill needed to get through this one, and it's not like these types of games get different difficulty levels that would make this playable for the types of gamers like myself. I get it, the high level challenge is one of the defining traits of the genre. Perhaps I could have enjoyed this kind of a game 15 years ago (although this wasn't out then), but be it my age, amount of responsibilities in life, or simply my overall lack of patience makes this (and all Souls like games) too much to handle.

If you read this review and found yourself resonating with ANY portion of how I described my life and/or gaming habits, there is a good chance that like me, this title may be too much for you to handle. If you are still in the prime of your life and/or have a high amount of free time and/or patience, or are highly skilled at games in general, then you will likely benefit more from other reviews from people that aren't grumpy old men that look to squeeze in an hour of game time every couple of days when the trash is already out, the dogs are walked, and the family has gotten plenty of attention.

And for anyone that throws out that piece of advice that is the opposite of useful: "just get gud" I respond with this: I truly wish I had the time to. This game looks great graphically, the story intrigues me, and I'm sure it is a great experience, but Ghost of Tsushima is much more my speed.

Posted: December 31, 2024
When you think of Souls-like or FromSoftware's games, you think of stamina management, hard-hitting enemies, and squishy player characters. Those mechanics alone makes things hard, which is, arguably, the appeal of such games. You make things easier by optimising your builds and perhaps summoning cooperators. Sekiro does not have such mechanics.

Instead of dodging around multiple combos, waiting for the moment you can deliver a single attack before dodging dozens of times again, Sekiro empowers you to stand your ground and defend yourself, facing the enemy head-on, and parrying and deflecting their attacks.

Instead of choosing the right weapons, rings, talismans, spells, armour, and whatnot, you are only given access to a single sword that you HAVE to master. No one-shots, no ranged spell combat. Only a single katana, capable of blocking almost any attack, against theirs.

Instead of a myriad of tools such as cooperators, bombs, shields, or other items that could make things significantly easier, You are only given a limited amount of tools that MAY ease things. But ultimately, your composure and your mastery of defending your ground is what wins battles in Sekiro.

No other souls-like has given me this feeling of being powerful. While, yes, I may get that feeling by using weapons forged from the souls of ancient warriors, wearing armor that belonged to a powerful king, and weaving ancient spells lost to time, Sekiro gives me the feeling of overcoming hardship by my own skills and sheer willpower alone. I conquered the challenges by actually "getting good".

If you're tired of seeing the enemy do a myriad of combos while your stuck dodging around them, being squishy and playing it safe by attacking from range, delivering a single dodge attack that has no real feeling of actually killing an enemy. Try Sekiro. You will face your enemy head-on, you will stand your ground, you will see yourself performing a cinematic swordfight with even the toughest and monstrous bosses, you will kill them with the most satisfying animations that reward your skill, you will feel satisfied, you will feel like the most badass warrior.

You will feel powerful.

Yes the art work and graphics are great and the development team should be proud of that.
However the game mechanics are atrocious (lag between input key and actual action, non responsive and inconsistent dodge, easy to fall out of the map, etc.).

Considering the importance of these 3 factors for a game, Sekiro does not get a pass mark with a score of 45 out of 100 (33/33 for graphics, 7/33 for the mechanics, and 5/33 for the story).

Posted: December 30, 2024
I finished Dark Souls 3, Elden Ring, and all their DLCs, so maybe I had high expectations and ended up quite disappointed.

The fight system of Sekiro feels very underwhelming compared to the other Souls games. Only one weapon, that is it. No armour, no rings, no spells, no attack types. Just the off-hand tools, which are mostly situational and not a part of the core gameplay. The game railroads you into one single fight system, and you have to stick to it.
It takes away the enjoyment of figuring out a good build against a boss and changing weapons to try something new.

The enemy diversity also pales in comparison to other Souls games. It is mostly guys with swords, and it feels repetitive very quickly.

The hitboxes do not represent what is on screen. I cannot count how many times I thought that a grab did not get me or a strike did not hit me. You will get hit through walls, and archers can snipe you through debris, fire, and smoke.

Several bosses require specific consumables, which means after dying and using those, you will have to go farm more of them.You cannot attempt a boss several times in a row but will have to alternate boss attempts with tedious farming sessions to get these consumables back.

The layout is confusing. On the main path -not the path to find consumables- you will have to scout sometimes every inch of the map to find where you need to go next.

It is a game whose difficulty has been inflated through gimmicks and not enough through actual gameplay innovation.

On the bright side, the graphics are beautiful, and the OST is great.

Posted: December 29, 2024
Been on my wishlist for atleast 1.5 years finally pulled the trigger for Christmas.

I put in 70 hrs in 6 days to 100% game. it took me about 27 hrs to finish it first time and my 6th ng+ cycle took me 1hr20mins with potential to go faster. To put it bluntly it's a excellent game and is now the GOAT fromsoftware game for me (Tied with BloodBorne depending on my mood)

You will be shoe-horned and molded into the optimal "Sekiro" player with a particular playstyle which takes some getting used to, but well worth the learning curve.

There are no other main weapons which allows FromSoftware to focus on making combat in all scenarios the player encounters very polished and consistent, without the need to cater for other potential weapon types. i.e every fight is made with the sword and prosthetic in mind.

The story is told to you outright by just watching cut-scenes, reading and listening to the dialogue of the NPCs. The two obvious endings are enjoyable and satisfactory and you are extremely likely to get these by playing the game. The harder endings need more work, luck and thought to get by yourself without a wiki. The "P" ending is definitely doable without a wiki if you get lucky with a single requirement to start the chain its also tied for my favourite ending.

Sekiro's bosses are great. I did the Gauntlets of strength (Boss rush) for the skins and it was very enjoyable to refight the boss cast with your skills vastly improved. Not much else to say, I will come back to the game every so often to just to attempt mortal journey again.

As for the things I didn't really enjoy, the music for me is like a 6/10 and doesn't do it for me like DS1 (8/10) and the king BloodBorne (10/10). Replay value is lower then other souls games, by nature of its single main weapon, but this is what makes the combat so polished.

For those who enjoy challenge runs they are limited to from the top of my head: no prosthetic, no death, no upgrades/items, speedruns, deflect only and no hit.

BTW do your own research and unlock the fps cap, makes the game sexy as balls to play

Absolutely wonderful. Tons of great content, well-made across the board, really attentive design, smooth gameplay, looks consistently good, AND full of quality-of-life features and optional fun like a boss revisit mechanic.

This game is so good that there's virtually nothing to criticise about it that's actually worth mentioning in a review, because it absolutely shouldn't diminish your interest in playing the game.
The one thing I will note is that this is a singleplayer-focused experience and doesn't feature covenants or invasion or coop like Dark Souls- But also, it absoluuuutely doesn't need it, and it allows the main character's movements and combat mechanics to be as fluid, dynamic and versatile as you need to feel like a proper ninja. So I'm counting this as a significant net positive.

Finally, this game is a contender for the single best implementation of a melee-parry mechanic that i've ever seen in any game. Ever. A very challenging mechanic to include as a core component of combat, yet they did a really great job.

Sequel when?

Posted: December 26, 2024
The mini bosses (which there are many of) are terrible. Most of the time half of their health can be removed with a sneak attack, which can sometimes be extremely slow and tedious to have to do every single attempt. Most of these mini bosses are surrounded generic enemies which are tedious at best to deal with. Most of these are also just the same enemy over and over again. Even some of the ones that are alone or mostly alone suck because they have really bad animations mislead the player into parrying at the wrong time like Lone Shadow Longswordsman who weirdly floats in the air slowly when kicking. One of the mini bosses I was slowly getting better and better each attempt, and then one time, right after I got the first bar down, he turned away from me, and I just stunlocked him by hitting him from the back until he died. I can't even be satisfied when I win.

The camera is terrible. Being near a wall in a fight makes the camera go above the character. Running through areas to retry fights is tedious and time consuming. Grinding for consumables is tedious and time consuming. Hitboxes are extremely terrible sometimes, especially grabs and fire. It's reminiscent of DS2 in the worst ways. There's also really dumb things like enemies with straw hats or wooden shields that block 100% of damage when guys in plate armor have worse defenses. Those shield enemies aren't even very aggressive so it's hard to wait for them to give you the option to parry and attack them. Enemies are sometimes alerted through walls yet other times they miss very obvious signs.

There are many flaws in the movement. The player can't always dodge off of ledges and will frequently be glued. Grappling is jank. Sometimes the icon is properly highlighted and it still won't work. Sometimes the icon goes offscreen. At least once, I didn't even land on the platform I was supposed to. Often I'll be really close to what I'm supposed to grapple to, and it just doesn't give me the option. Having to retry something because the game didn't activate a jump kill even though you're falling right over the enemy and he's not alerted is so annoying.

Still not as bad as Nioh or Elden Ring, but worse than DS3 and Bloodborne.

Posted: December 24, 2024
I'm surprised I enjoyed this game as much as I did.

Without a shadow of a doubt (no pun intended), Sekiro is one of the best games I've played in a long time. For the first ten hours or so, I was really dissatisfied with the game, "this game is just not for me" I thought, but once I actually mastered the mechanics of the game and started treating it as its own thing, rather than another Dark Souls game, it became an immensely satisfying experience that I enjoyed from beginning to end.

I really liked the story and the characters, which was probably my favorite part of the game, and the atmosphere I thought was pretty cool, even though a fantasy setting is someting I prefer much more than ancient Japan. Nevertheless, I really liked it, and I also enjoyed the different enemy variety and the incredible bosses the game has to offer, although I thought the minibosses were a low point, especially the Headless and Shichimen Warrior enemies, which are probably some of the worst enemies in all of soulsborne.

In the end, Sekiro is a great game that while I think not everyone will enjoy due to its incredibly demanding precision, patience and reflexes, it's an incredibly satisfying and, above all else, incredibly cool game, one that I think is definitely worth your time if you can find the strength to get through its toughest challenges. Just don't forget: hesitate, and you lose.

Posted: December 24, 2024
Probably the least enjoyable, most obtuse, awkward controlling game I have ever played. There is not one mechanic or bit of gameplay that is not contradictory to everything else it touches. Every second is friction. Every moment is a feeling of having no idea what is going on or why it's going wrong. It would seem to be handwaved as being "on purpose" to promote "difficulty" but it really just feels like bland incompetence biting off more than it could chew.

There's no emotion here to be had either. It doesn't even make me angry when the controls eat themselves alive with the most absurd button placements just trying to do the most simple things. It's not even that frustrating when an enemy catches me in a jump and their hitbox (that is multiple times the size of their weapon) connects with a pixel of my character's foot to one-shot me. There's no rhyme or reason to anything going on, it just happens, as if the game is trying to simulate how a neurotic, depressed person who is deep into "the spectrum" sees the world.

It could be that they were trying to make a different genre game in the "dark souls" engine that can barely hold itself together for it's intended genre instead of a totally different one. It could be that the game is (as many have described it) "a rhythm game without music." I think that even with a more suited engine and proper musical cues it would still be a mess because it's the developers not the engine that can only do dark souls, and the developers not the telegraphing that's inadequate.

There's a reason rhythm and fighting games lock your movement, and why their controls must be purposely simple, easy to reach, little input delay or animation startup, and animations must be frame perfect. This game failed at the design stage, there's no fixing it because of that. This game couldn't be fixed at the design stage, because there's an absolute zero chance these devs could do better than what they already did and failed at.

I can't even come up with a philosophical excuse for it all either. It just feels like the game is unfinished, or more accurately the developers didn't care enough to try, or even more accurately they are pretending like they didn't care enough to try to cover up the fact that they couldn't try even if they wanted to. Like all the nihilism and "get good" awkwardness is there because if this played like a typical arpg, then there would be zero Substance on tap and nothing to set it apart from the rest. Reminds me of a cross between a school term paper where the student fills the page with incorrectly-used $5 words to cover up the fact they have no idea what they are doing and all the pre-alpha "minimum viable product" early access con games that litter this platform. Better example would be the "scene" or "emo" or "theater" kid who relies on a pre-packed rhetoric, hive personality, and corporate aesthetic they bought at a specialty store to cover up the fact that they are a truly unimpressive person who has nothing to set them apart outside of their bought-and-paid-for "vibe." Or the loner who pretends to be mysterious because he knows he'd be just as alone if he put effort in, so at least found a way to look cool while running into expected failure. The internet troll that screams "I was trying to be stupid on purpose!" when his gimmick fails. Or the "atheist" who chest-beats about "rationality" and "enlightenment" to cover up that they can't say no to temptation or follow simple rules. Take your pick. It's a ruse to try to spin the obvious flaw into a "cool guy" master plan excuse.

It has to inspire rage, it has to inspire a sentiment of "I'm better" than you in the fans, because without either there would be no emotion to be had and no reason to play this over any other game in the genre!

I played and beat sifu in one sitting in 2 hours just yesterday on the steam deck, so it's not input lag that makes this whole experience a mess. It's not "skill" because I have beaten nearly every classic platinum game on the hardest difficulty with all platinum trophies. Of course the controls and mechanics of those games aren't purposely designed to be at odds with the genre and gameplay.

It's just that this game isn't made well. I refunded it the first time I bought it and decided to give it a second chance, but I should have trusted my gut because this is not acceptable!

Posted: December 24, 2024
Souls games have a knack for making challenging fights that, while high in difficulty, feel both fair and rewarding. Sekiro seems to do the opposite. While many people love its combat, and I can partially see why (getting in a groove with deflects and counters is very satisfying), I cannot fathom how the parts one loves outweigh the "BS factors". Complaints listed below:

1. Gank (groups of enemies) combat is handled poorly. You can be hit immediately coming out of an execution animation with no window for deflect. You must be directly facing an enemy to deflect, and when two attack one after the other, the window can be such that you cannot deflect both attacks. While the game looks to provide paths to avoid enemies, there are plenty of scenarios where getting ganked is all but unavoidable.
2. Combat tells are so much poorer than in ER/DS, it is hard to believe it is from the same developer. You will see a red kanji that means an enemy is about to do a sweep, thrust, or grab, which all have unique ways of being avoided (and two of which cannot be deflected). Which one will they do? Sometimes they have a wind up animation, sometimes they don't. The same enemy can use more than one of the three, and sometimes an enemy will initiate an undeflectable sweep without even showing a red symbol!
3. The general "rules of combat" are not intuitive and specific to each enemy. In ER/DS, if you know the general souls formula, you know how to approach a new enemy without putting yourself in a bad position, even if you don't know their move set. In Sekiro? Some attacks can be deflected, some can't, some enemies you can be aggressive with, some have an auto-damage move if you swing twice and end up on top of them; the game feels more like an exercise in concentration and binding the correct response to your reflexes than it does an exercise of conscientious decision making and skill exhibition.
4. Picture this: Some enemies can practically only be dealt with using an Ash of War (prosthetic equipment in Sekiro). The shieldbearers and the axe for example. Now tie that Ash of War's uses to a consumable that is not replenished when resting at a checkpoint. Now space them in between you and the boss you're about to fight 15 times... see the issue?


All of this ise combined with less RPG elements (equipment and attribute variety), and a higher base difficulty outside of the BS factors (enemies do more damage, you do less) than a traditional DS/ER game. While I haven't reached a boss that's taken me more than a dozen tries so far, I simply have not felt a sense of achievement from any win, but only dread for what punishment comes next. If you are a masochist, this is the game for you,

Sekiro is my first Fromsoft game, my friend told me to get it and so I did. But I played a few hours and put it aside for half a year because I became afraid of it due constantly getting slammed by the Chained Ogre. Then I came back again and left for a few months, but only after a few more tries did it finally "click" as they say.

The rapid deflection and Mikiri counter from enemy attacks was very hard to get used to for a FS newbie but when I finally got used to it for the very first time was incredibly satisfying.
It took me almost a year to finally play this game properly but it was all worth it.
The world design was very gorgeous and the boss soundtracks are solid as well.

I love being abused by Sekiro 10/10

Posted: December 23, 2024
Art. That's the only way to describe what Sekiro is. Everything, from the setting and story, to the combat system and gameplay loop, is done to perfection. The story explores the perfect mix of a historically-accurate Sengoku era and the fantastical mythologies and folktales of Japan. I just finished a course at University about the mortality of the soul, and the philosophical themes surrounding immortality make the story so well written it's not even funny.

The game teaches you to get better; there are few exploits and little grinding you can do to become overpowered, the only way to do so is to learn from your mistakes and improve, and trust me, you will. It's beyond satisfying to watch yourself become more skilled the more you fail.

Sekiro is, without a doubt, a masterpiece.

The ol fromsoft souls formula that's beloved by many myself included, but faster, and more skill intensive as a result. Just gonna leave my original review up, as these issues pretty much stays the entire game.
Other things that is pretty terrible in this game:
-Camera is a disaster. Worst in any souls game. And that's kinda saying allot. Many of these bosses are hard simply because of the camera and small rooms/arenas you are given to fight them in.
- Grapple system is pretty trash. When it works, it's pretty cool. but i've had some many instances of it simply just not working.
- Stuck in anything in this game. And good luck getting out if you're fighting a boss. You are simply stuck. This kinda goes hand in hand with the camera issues aswell. Bundle these together, and you're gonna have a terrible time.
-Combat system: Everyone saying this combat is so hard, but in reality it's really not. You can cheese most bosses, and u can simply just spam the parry button, to either block or get a parry for free. it's just silly.
-Many of the bosses are just gimmick fights. Where u can trivialize the entire fight with a simple tool.
-Bosses straight up ignoring your attacks. they got a poise of 999 it seems. just smacking you thru your attacks. Feels terrible.
-Talent system is absolute garbage. You can't respec this for some reason, and it's a pretty big deal if u spend your points on useless stuff, compared to the other broken stuff u can obtain.

Good things about the game:
-A couple of the bosses was pretty well made, and fun to fight.
Exploration and lore is something fromsoft does very well, and Sekiro is no different, wich is what made me complete the game.

Finishing Sekiro 100% was an unforgettable journey. The game’s brutal difficulty had many players dropping off midway, but people just need patience to learn the game, and it becomes an amazing experience instead of giving up. The sense of achievement after conquering those punishing bosses was incredible. Sekiro is a true masterpiece, and for me, it's the GOAT. I’ll never stop playing it, even after finishing it 100%. I hope FromSoftware gives us a sequel to Sekiro, especially after the Return ending or even a prequel exploring the past of Isshin, Lady Tomoe, and the Sculptor(Most Sekiro fans want to play as young Isshin). definition of a "get gud" game. you can start it, and beat it in under 20 minutes if youre a speed runner... i have 60 hours in and am barely 20% through. its fun, you can grind it to get better skills to help you out, but really if you understand the mechanics and have the skill... you can parry all but 2 attack styles in the game, its pretty insane. dont run, dont doge.. dont drain the hp bar (for the most part) you want to stay in their face, deflect and use your shinobi reflexes and you will deliver that deathblow. This game revolves around the parry and the ability to parry all but sweep and grab perilous attacks indicated by red icons. there is a third, which is thrusts but you can counter those or perfect parry them.... but like i said, its like guitar hero... you have the skill, you can win. If you dont, like me, the game takes a bit longer. you cant blame the game... it is very fine polished.. its a player, a character with a katana, and a mission. you win or you don't. game has like a 20% completion rate of those who attempt it. Its fun, get it on sale if you can. Ive played a lot of souls and RPG games, this is not one of those.... theres not really character building, theres no armor sets to collect. You can level up some combat skills but thats about it. you just play, and in that aspect it does it flawlessly. If you want to feel like a real shinobi, and evicerate your foes... this is the game. dont give up, theres a ton of videos online to help you

Posted: December 20, 2024
What an amazing game! Souls games are my absolute favourite genre, because I guess I'm addicted to the suffering and frustration you feel until you finally work things out and feel immense satisfaction that you finally defeated that enemy or boss. Even the mobs in this game can be a problem to begin with, but as with a lot of souls games you learn to either deal with them or avoid them in due time, and mobs that were once a guaranteed death will become nothing more than mere inconvenience on your way to have another take at that boss that is really ruining your day!

The game has beautiful visuals without trying to be something they're not or trying too hard. I'm playing on my Steam Deck and they hold up just as well in very nearly 2025 as they did when I first played it in 2019. From software definitely focus on gameplay first with graphics coming second, but that's not to say that the graphics aren't beautiful and just what they need to be. The graphics still hold strong on the verge of 2025 as they did on 2019.

This game can be brutally difficult to new players but if you have the percerverance to stick with it then you can get a lot of satisfaction from beating the bosses which, at first, seem impossible, and having to pass through the mobs which will majorly hinder your performance with the upcoming boss will become nothing more than minor inconvenience.

Every enemy and every boss has a weakness, whether it be through your items or skills, or even gameplay events that you will discover along the way.

This game is very difficult but 100% doable and the satisfaction that you get from just defeating just the first boss, for example, is like no other game I have ever played.

In every nook and cranny there are dangers waiting for you, but a lot of these dangers are what you need to truly progress. Yes they're difficult, yes they're frustrating but once you defeat them, feel the satisfaction of doing so, and receive a reward of an item that will help you going forward, you know that you can beat this game, no matter how hard it is going forward.

TLDR;Very difficult but incredibly rewarding game, pay attention to what the game tells you and use it. Satisfaction is like no other.

It's 2024 and if you're still on the fence about this game because you've never played a Souls game and you've heard they're hard - I'll give it to you straight.

It is an endeavor, and will take awhile to learn. You will struggle until you learn to use all of the tools that are available to you. And then that boss finally dies, and it's a feeling unlike any other.

I've been video games for over 30 years across every console known to man and I've never felt that way in gaming before, ever. It's pure adrenaline and exhilaration.

Put in the time to learn it, it's worth it and incredibly rewarding.

This game is an absolute masterclass of game design, easily the best combat I've ever experienced in any game ever. Beautiful scenery juxtaposes the grotesque enemy design, as well as the challenge that's expected of any FromSoftware game. This games difficulty is strange, as I didn't find it nearly as hard as some people have described. Nearly everything is intuitive and the game teaches you everything you need to know to face its more difficult encounters, so it's only a matter of time before you learn what you need to know and you can get the ball rolling with this phenomenal experience. None of this is to mention the stellar music, JP voice acting (though the ENG dub sounds great too from the little I've heard), the area design, or the cinematic cutscenes that always made the experience that much more worthwhile. Don't let this games difficulty scare you away, as long as you're committed, success is only a matter of time. 10/10.
